### [1] [A High-Accuracy Optical Music Recognition Method Based on Bottleneck Residual Convolutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16446)
*Junwen Ma,Huhu Xue,Xingyuan Zhao,and Weicheng Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: End-to-end Optical Music Recognition framework using ResNet-v2-style CNN with BiGRU for sequence modeling achieves high accuracy on music score datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optical Music Recognition (OMR) needs efficient conversion of printed/handwritten music scores to symbolic representations. Existing methods may lack integration of fine-grained symbol details with global structural information.

Method: Combines CNN with ResNet-v2-style residual bottleneck blocks and multi-scale dilated convolutions for feature extraction, followed by BiGRU for sequence modeling. Uses Connectionist Temporal Classification loss for end-to-end training without alignment annotations.

Result: On Camera-PrIMuS: SeER 7.52%, SyER 0.45%, pitch accuracy 99.33%, type accuracy 99.60%, note accuracy 99.28%. On PrIMuS: SeER 8.11%, SyER 0.49%, pitch accuracy 99.27%, type accuracy 99.58%, note accuracy 99.21%. Training time 1.74s per epoch.

Conclusion: The proposed end-to-end framework effectively combines CNN and BiGRU for OMR, achieving high recognition accuracy with computational efficiency, as validated on benchmark datasets.

Abstract: Optical Music Recognition (OMR) aims to convert printed or handwritten music score images into editable symbolic representations. This paper presents an end-to-end OMR framework that combines residual bottleneck convolutions with bidirectional gated recurrent unit (BiGRU)-based sequence modeling. A convolutional neural network with ResNet-v2-style residual bottleneck blocks and multi-scale dilated convolutions is used to extract features that encode both fine-grained symbol details and global staff-line structures. The extracted feature sequences are then fed into a BiGRU network to model temporal dependencies among musical symbols. The model is trained using the Connectionist Temporal Classification loss, enabling end-to-end prediction without explicit alignment annotations. Experimental results on the Camera-PrIMuS and PrIMuS datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. On Camera-PrIMuS, the proposed method achieves a sequence error rate (SeER) of $7.52\%$ and a symbol error rate (SyER) of $0.45\%$, with pitch, type, and note accuracies of $99.33\%$, $99.60\%$, and $99.28\%$, respectively. The average training time is 1.74~s per epoch, demonstrating high computational efficiency while maintaining strong recognition performance. On PrIMuS, the method achieves a SeER of $8.11\%$ and a SyER of $0.49\%$, with pitch, type, and note accuracies of $99.27\%$, $99.58\%$, and $99.21\%$, respectively. A fine-grained error analysis further confirms the effectiveness of the proposed model.

</details>


### [2] [From Inheritance to Saturation: Disentangling the Evolution of Visual Redundancy for Architecture-Aware MLLM Inference Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16462)
*Jiaqi Shi,Yuechan Li,Xulong Zhang,Xiaoyang Qu,Jianzong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HalfV reduces computational costs in high-resolution MLLMs by identifying and pruning visual redundancy in two stages, achieving 4.1× FLOPs speedup with minimal performance loss across diverse architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution MLLMs suffer from prohibitive computational costs due to explosion of visual tokens. Existing acceleration methods have "backbone dependency" - they work well on some architectures (Vicuna, Mistral) but degrade significantly on others like Qwen.

Method: Uses truncated matrix entropy to discover a universal three-stage inference lifecycle, decoupling visual redundancy into Intrinsic Visual Redundancy (IVR) and Secondary Saturation Redundancy (SSR). HalfV first mitigates IVR via unified pruning, then adaptively handles SSR based on its specific manifestation.

Result: HalfV achieves superior efficiency-performance trade-offs across diverse backbones. On Qwen25-VL, retains 96.8% performance at 4.1× FLOPs speedup, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses backbone dependency in MLLM acceleration by identifying universal redundancy patterns and providing adaptive pruning strategies, enabling efficient high-resolution multimodal inference across diverse architectures.

Abstract: High-resolution Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face prohibitive computational costs during inference due to the explosion of visual tokens. Existing acceleration strategies, such as token pruning or layer sparsity, suffer from severe "backbone dependency", performing well on Vicuna or Mistral architectures (e.g., LLaVA) but causing significant performance degradation when transferred to architectures like Qwen. To address this, we leverage truncated matrix entropy to uncover a universal three-stage inference lifecycle, decoupling visual redundancy into universal Intrinsic Visual Redundancy (IVR) and architecture-dependent Secondary Saturation Redundancy (SSR). Guided by this insight, we propose HalfV, a framework that first mitigates IVR via a unified pruning strategy and then adaptively handles SSR based on its specific manifestation. Experiments demonstrate that HalfV achieves superior efficiency-performance trade-offs across diverse backbones. Notably, on Qwen25-VL, it retains 96.8\% performance at a 4.1$\times$ FLOPs speedup, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Our code is available at https://github.com/civilizwa/HalfV.

</details>


### [3] [Latent-Compressed Variational Autoencoder for Video Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16479)
*Jiarui Guan,Wenshuai Zhao,Zhengtao Zou,Juho Kannala,Arno Solin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A latent compression method that removes high-frequency components in video VAEs instead of reducing channels, improving video reconstruction quality while maintaining compression ratio.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video VAEs need many latent channels for high-quality reconstruction, but too many channels can hurt diffusion model convergence and generative performance, even when reconstruction is good.

Method: Proposes a latent compression method that removes high-frequency components in video latent representations rather than directly reducing the number of channels, which often compromises reconstruction fidelity.

Result: Experimental results show the method achieves superior video reconstruction quality compared to strong baselines while maintaining the same overall compression ratio.

Conclusion: Removing high-frequency components in video latent representations is more effective than channel reduction for maintaining reconstruction quality while enabling efficient latent diffusion training.

Abstract: Video variational autoencoders (VAEs) used in latent diffusion models typically require a sufficiently large number of latent channels to ensure high-quality video reconstruction. However, recent studies have revealed that an excessive number of latent channels can impede the convergence of latent diffusion models and deteriorate their generative performance, even when reconstruction quality remains high. We propose a latent compression method that removes high-frequency components in video latent representations rather than directly reducing the number of channels, which often compromises reconstruction fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior video reconstruction quality compared to strong baselines while maintaining the same overall compression ratio.

</details>


### [4] [Positioning radiata pine branches requiring pruning by drone stereo vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16480)
*Yida Lin,Bing Xue,Mengjie Zhang,Sam Schofield,Richard Green*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A drone-mounted stereo vision system for detecting and localizing radiata pine branches to enable autonomous pruning, using segmentation models and depth estimation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an automated system for forestry pruning using low-cost stereo vision, addressing the need for efficient branch detection and localization in autonomous forestry operations.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: 1) Branch segmentation using YOLOv8, YOLOv9, and Mask R-CNN variants on custom stereo dataset; 2) Depth estimation comparing traditional SGBM with WLS filtering against deep learning methods (PSMNet, ACVNet, GWCNet, MobileStereoNet, RAFT-Stereo, NeRF-Supervised Deep Stereo), with centroid-based triangulation and MAD outlier rejection for distance computation.

Result: Deep learning-based disparity maps produce more coherent depth estimates than traditional SGBM at 1-2m distances, demonstrating feasibility of low-cost stereo vision for automated branch positioning.

Conclusion: The proposed stereo vision system shows promise for autonomous pruning applications in forestry, with deep learning methods outperforming traditional approaches for branch localization.

Abstract: This paper presents a stereo-vision-based system mounted on a drone for detecting and localising radiata pine branches to support autonomous pruning. The proposed pipeline comprises two stages: branch segmentation and depth estimation. For segmentation, YOLOv8, YOLOv9, and Mask R-CNN variants are compared on a custom dataset of 71 stereo image pairs captured with a ZED Mini camera. For depth estimation, both a traditional method (SGBM with WLS filtering) and deep-learning-based methods (PSMNet, ACVNet, GWCNet, MobileStereoNet, RAFT-Stereo, and NeRF-Supervised Deep Stereo) are evaluated. A centroid-based triangulation algorithm with MAD outlier rejection is proposed to compute branch distance from the segmentation mask and disparity map. Qualitative evaluation at distances of 1-2 m indicates that the deep learning-based disparity maps produce more coherent depth estimates than SGBM, demonstrating the feasibility of low-cost stereo vision for automated branch positioning in forestry.

</details>


### [5] [Erasing Thousands of Concepts: Towards Scalable and Practical Concept Erasure for Text-to-Image Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16481)
*Hoigi Seo,Byung Hyun Lee,Jaehyun Cho,Sungjin Lim,Se Young Chun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ETC is a scalable framework that erases thousands of concepts from text-to-image diffusion models while preserving generation quality, using t-distribution modeling and Mixture-of-Experts erasure with robustness to attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale T2I diffusion models can reproduce undesirable content like copyrighted material, posing safety risks. Existing concept erasure methods are limited in scalability, precision, and robustness, typically handling only a few hundred concepts.

Method: 1) Model low-rank concept distributions via Student's t-distribution Mixture Model (tMM); 2) Perform pin-point erasure via affine optimal transport while preserving other concepts by anchoring distribution boundaries; 3) Train MoEraser (Mixture-of-Experts module) to remove target embeddings while preserving anchor embeddings; 4) Inject noise into text embedding projector and fine-tune MoEraser for recovery to achieve robustness against attacks.

Result: Extensive experiments on over 2,000 concepts across heterogeneous domains and diffusion models demonstrate state-of-the-art scalability and precision in large-scale concept erasure.

Conclusion: ETC provides a scalable framework for erasing thousands of concepts from diffusion models while maintaining generation quality and robustness to attacks, addressing limitations of previous approaches.

Abstract: Large-scale text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models deliver remarkable visual fidelity but pose safety risks due to their capacity to reproduce undesirable content, such as copyrighted ones. Concept erasure has emerged as a mitigation strategy, yet existing approaches struggle to balance scalability, precision, and robustness, which restricts their applicability to erasing only a few hundred concepts. To address these limitations, we present Erasing Thousands of Concepts (ETC), a scalable framework capable of erasing thousands of concepts while preserving generation quality. Our method first models low-rank concept distributions via a Student's t-distribution Mixture Model (tMM). It enables pin-point erasure of target concepts via affine optimal transport while preserving others by anchoring the boundaries of target concept distributions without pre-defined anchor concepts. We then train a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)-based module, termed MoEraser, which removes target embeddings while preserving the anchor embeddings. By injecting noise into the text embedding projector and fine-tuning MoEraser for recovery, our framework achieves robustness to white-box attack such as module removal. Extensive experiments on over 2,000 concepts across heterogeneous domains and diffusion models demerate state-of-the-art scalability and precision in large-scale concept erasure.

</details>


### [6] [LOD-Net: Locality-Aware 3D Object Detection Using Multi-Scale Transformer Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16696)
*Mustaqeem Khan,Aidana Nurakhmetova,Wail Gueaieb,Abdulmotaleb El Saddik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 3DETR with Multi-Scale Attention improves 3D object detection by better capturing local geometry and global context through upsampling for high-resolution features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D object detection in point clouds is challenging due to data sparsity and lack of global structure. Existing methods need better integration of local geometric details and global contextual information.

Method: Proposes Multi-Scale Attention (MSA) mechanism integrated into 3DETR architecture. Introduces upsampling operation to generate high-resolution feature maps, enabling better detection of smaller and semantically related objects.

Result: On ScanNetv2 dataset, 3DETR+MSA achieves gains of almost 1% in mAP@25 and 4.78% in mAP@50 over baseline. Limited improvement on 3DETR-m variant suggests need for adapted upsampling strategies for lightweight models.

Conclusion: Combining hierarchical feature extraction with attention mechanisms effectively enhances 3D scene understanding. The MSA mechanism successfully addresses challenges of sparse point cloud data by capturing both local geometry and global context.

Abstract: 3D object detection in point cloud data remains a challenging task due to the sparsity and lack of global structure inherent in the input. In this work, we propose a novel Multi-Scale Attention (MSA) mechanism integrated into the 3DETR architecture to better capture both local geometry and global context. Our method introduces an upsampling operation that generates high-resolution feature maps, enabling the network to better detect smaller and semantically related objects. Experiments conducted on the ScanNetv2 dataset demonstrate that our 3DETR + MSA model improves detection performance, achieving a gain of almost 1% in mAP@25 and 4.78% in mAP@50 over the baseline. While applying MSA to the 3DETR-m variant shows limited improvement, our analysis reveals the importance of adapting the upsampling strategy for lightweight models. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining hierarchical feature extraction with attention mechanisms in enhancing 3D scene understanding.

</details>


### [7] [A Survey of Spatial Memory Representations for Efficient Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16482)
*Ma. Madecheen S. Pangaliman,Steven S. Sison,Erwin P. Quilloy,Rowel Atienza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Survey analyzes spatial memory efficiency in robotics, introduces α ratio (peak runtime memory / saved map size), reveals 100x variation in neural methods, proposes standardized evaluation protocol, and provides α-aware budgeting for deployment feasibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-based robots face unbounded spatial memory growth that exhausts computational resources on embedded platforms (8-16GB shared memory, <30W), where hardware upgrades aren't feasible. Need to understand memory efficiency across different mapping paradigms.

Method: Survey of 88 references spanning 52 systems (1989-2025) from occupancy grids to neural implicit representations. Introduces α = M_peak / M_map ratio to measure deployment cost gap. Independent profiling on NVIDIA A100 GPU. Proposes standardized evaluation protocol with memory growth rate, query latency, memory-completeness curves, and throughput degradation metrics.

Result: α spans two orders of magnitude within neural methods alone (2.3 for Point-SLAM to 215 for NICE-SLAM). Pareto frontier analysis shows no single paradigm dominates: 3DGS methods achieve best accuracy at 90-254MB map size on Replica, while scene graphs provide semantic abstraction at predictable cost. Provides first independently measured α reference values.

Conclusion: Memory architecture, not paradigm label, determines deployment feasibility. Paper provides α-aware budgeting algorithm enabling practitioners to assess deployment feasibility on target hardware before implementation. Standardized evaluation protocol needed to capture memory efficiency metrics missing from current benchmarks.

Abstract: As vision-based robots navigate larger environments, their spatial memory grows without bound, eventually exhausting computational resources, particularly on embedded platforms (8-16GB shared memory, $<$30W) where adding hardware is not an option. This survey examines the spatial memory efficiency problem across 88 references spanning 52 systems (1989-2025), from occupancy grids to neural implicit representations. We introduce the $α= M_{\text{peak}} / M_{\text{map}}$, the ratio of peak runtime memory (the total RAM or GPU memory consumed during operation) to saved map size (the persistent checkpoint written to disk), exposing the gap between published map sizes and actual deployment cost. Independent profiling on an NVIDIA A100 GPU reveals that $α$ spans two orders of magnitude within neural methods alone, ranging from 2.3 (Point-SLAM) to 215 (NICE-SLAM, whose 47,MB map requires 10GB at runtime), showing that memory architecture, not paradigm label, determines deployment feasibility. We propose a standardized evaluation protocol comprising memory growth rate, query latency, memory-completeness curves, and throughput degradation, none of which current benchmarks capture. Through a Pareto frontier analysis with explicit benchmark separation, we show that no single paradigm dominates within its evaluation regime: 3DGS methods achieve the best absolute accuracy at 90-254,MB map size on Replica, while scene graphs provide semantic abstraction at predictable cost. We provide the first independently measured $α$ reference values and an $α$-aware budgeting algorithm enabling practitioners to assess deployment feasibility on target hardware prior to implementation.

</details>


### [8] [Unified Ultrasound Intelligence Toward an End-to-End Agentic System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16914)
*Chen Ma,Yunshu Li,Junhu Fu,Shuyu Liang,Yuanyuan Wang,Yi Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: USTri is a three-stage ultrasound AI pipeline that achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple organs and tasks by combining universal pretraining, specialized fine-tuning, and workflow-based inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical ultrasound analysis requires models that generalize across heterogeneous organs, views, and devices while supporting interpretable workflow-level analysis. Existing methods struggle with task-wise adaptation and cross-task interference, making workflow-level outputs difficult to achieve.

Method: USTri uses a tri-stage approach: 1) USGen - universal generalist pretraining across domains for robust priors, 2) USpec - frozen USGen with dataset-specific heads for task-aligned performance, 3) USAgent - orchestrates USpec specialists for multi-step inference mimicking clinician workflows.

Result: On FMC_UIA validation set, achieves best overall performance across 4 task types and 27 datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. USAgent produces clinically structured reports with high accuracy and interpretability.

Conclusion: USTri provides a scalable path to ultrasound intelligence that generalizes across heterogeneous ultrasound tasks and supports consistent end-to-end clinical workflows.

Abstract: Clinical ultrasound analysis demands models that generalize across heterogeneous organs, views, and devices, while supporting interpretable workflow-level analysis. Existing methods often rely on task-wise adaptation, and joint learning may be unstable due to cross-task interference, making it hard to deliver workflow-level outputs in practice. To address these challenges, we present USTri, a tri-stage ultrasound intelligence pipeline for unified multi-organ, multi-task analysis. Stage I trains a universal generalist USGen on different domains to learn broad, transferable priors that are robust to device and protocol variability. To better handle domain shifts and reach task-aligned performance while preserving ultrasound shared knowledge, Stage II builds USpec by keeping USGen frozen and finetuning dataset-specific heads. Stage III introduces USAgent, which mimics clinician workflows by orchestrating USpec specialists for multi-step inference and deterministic structured reports. On the FMC\_UIA validation set, our model achieves the best overall performance across 4 task types and 27 datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, qualitative results show that USAgent produces clinically structured reports with high accuracy and interpretability. Our study suggests a scalable path to ultrasound intelligence that generalizes across heterogeneous ultrasound tasks and supports consistent end-to-end clinical workflows.

</details>


### [9] [Dynamic Eraser for Guided Concept Erasure in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16483)
*Qinghui Gong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DSS is a training-free framework for concept erasure in T2I diffusion models that uses semantic boundary modeling and guidance to achieve 91% erasure rate with minimal fidelity loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inference-time methods for concept erasure in T2I diffusion models have limitations: feature-correction causes uncontrolled over-correction, token-level interventions struggle with semantic granularity, and both suffer from semantic drift or representation collapse.

Method: Dynamic Semantic Steering (DSS) introduces: 1) Sensitive Semantic Boundary Modeling (SSBM) to automate discovery of safe semantic anchors, and 2) Sensitive Semantic Guidance (SSG) which uses cross-attention features for precise detection and performs correction via a closed-form solution from a well-posed objective.

Result: DSS achieves an average erasure rate of 91.0%, significantly outperforming SOTA methods (which range from 18.6% to 85.9%) with minimal impact on output fidelity.

Conclusion: DSS provides a lightweight, training-free framework for interpretable and controllable concept erasure that optimally suppresses sensitive content while preserving benign semantics, addressing key limitations of existing methods.

Abstract: Concept erasure in Text-To-Image (T2I) diffusion models is vital for safe content generation, but existing inference-time methods face significant limitations. Feature-correction approaches often cause uncontrolled over-correction, while token-level interventions struggle with semantic granularity and context. Moreover, both types of methods are prone to severe semantic drift or even complete representation collapse. To address these challenges, we present Dynamic Semantic Steering (DSS), a lightweight, training-free framework for interpretable and controllable concept erasure. DSS introduces: 1) Sensitive Semantic Boundary Modeling (SSBM) to automate the discovery of safe semantic anchors, and 2) Sensitive Semantic Guidance (SSG), which leverages cross-attention features for precise detection and performs correction via a closed-form solution derived from a well-posed objective. This ensures optimal suppression of sensitive content while preserving benign semantics. DSS achieves an average erasure rate of 91.0\%, significantly outperforming SOTA methods (from 18.6\% to 85.9\%) with minimal impact on output fidelity.

</details>


### [10] [Hyperspectral Unmixing Hierarchies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16969)
*Joseph L. Garrett,P. S. Vishnu,Pauliina Salmi,Daniela Lupu,Nitesh Kumar Singh,Ion Necoara,Tor Arne Johansen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BLUTHs (Binary Linear Unmixing Tactile Hierarchies) is a hierarchical unmixing method using Deep Nonnegative Matrix Factorization with hierarchical abundance constraints to address spectral variability, endmember number determination, and clarity degradation in hyperspectral image analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperspectral unmixing faces three key challenges: spectral variability inhibits performance, determining the number of endmembers is ambiguous, and endmember clarity degrades as more are included. Hierarchical structure is proposed as a solution to all three problems.

Method: Hierarchical unmixing is defined by imposing hierarchical abundance sum constraints on Deep Nonnegative Matrix Factorization. BLUTHs solve this with a simple network architecture, and sparsity modulation unmixing growth tailors the topology of a BLUTH to each scene.

Result: BLUTHs outperform state-of-the-art unmixing algorithms on laboratory scenes, particularly for abundance estimation, while remaining competitive on remote sensing scenes. Successful ocean color unmixing is demonstrated on hyperspectral scenes from HYPSO and PACE satellites.

Conclusion: Hierarchical unmixing via BLUTHs effectively addresses key challenges in hyperspectral analysis by revealing endmembers with varying spectral contrast levels, mitigating spectral variability while maintaining competitive performance across different scene types.

Abstract: Unmixing reveals the spatial distribution and spectral details of different constituents, called endmembers, in a hyperspectral image. Because unmixing has limited ground truth requirements, can accommodate mixed pixels, and is closely tied to light propagation, it is a uniquely powerful tool for analyzing hyperspectral images. However, spectral variability inhibits unmixing performance, the proper way to determine the number of endmembers is ambiguous, and the clarity of the endmembers degrades as more are included. Hierarchical structure is a possible solution to all three problems.
  Here, hierarchical unmixing is defined by imposing a hierarchical abundance sum constraint on Deep Nonnegative Matrix Factorization. Binary Linear Unmixing Tactile Hierarchies (BLUTHs) solve the hierarchical unmixing problem with a simple network architecture. Sparsity modulation unmixing growth tailors the topology of a BLUTH to each scene. The structure imposed by BLUTHs allows endmembers with varying levels of spectral contrast to be revealed, mitigating the challenge of spectral variability.
  The performance of BLUTHs exceeds state-of-the-art unmixing algorithms on laboratory scenes, particularly with regard to abundance estimation, while their performance remains competitive on remote sensing scenes. In addition, ocean color unmixing by BLUTHs is demonstrated on hyperspectral scenes from the HYPSO and PACE satellites.

</details>


### [11] [DexWorldModel: Causal Latent World Modeling towards Automated Learning of Embodied Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16484)
*Yueci Deng,Guiliang Liu,Kui Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CLWM introduces a causal latent world model using DINOv3 features for robust manipulation, with O(1) memory scaling, speculative inference to reduce latency, and EmbodiChain for scaling via physics-grounded trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generative world-action models for manipulation suffer from redundant pixel-level reconstruction, O(T) memory scaling with horizon length, and sequential inference latency that bottlenecks deployment.

Method: Uses DINOv3 features as generative targets to disentangle interaction semantics from visual noise. Features Dual-State Test-Time Training Memory for O(1) memory footprint, Speculative Asynchronous Inference to mask diffusion denoising behind execution, and EmbodiChain framework for scaling with physics-grounded trajectories.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in complex dual-arm simulation and unprecedented zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on physical robots, outperforming baselines explicitly finetuned on real-world data.

Conclusion: CLWM addresses key bottlenecks in deploying generative world models for manipulation through semantic feature targets, constant memory scaling, reduced inference latency, and scalable training with physics-grounded data.

Abstract: Deploying generative World-Action Models for manipulation is severely bottlenecked by redundant pixel-level reconstruction, $\mathcal{O}(T)$ memory scaling, and sequential inference latency. We introduce the Causal Latent World Model (CLWM), which employs DINOv3 features as generative targets to disentangle interaction semantics from visual noise, yielding highly robust domain generalization. To overcome memory scaling, CLWM features a Dual-State Test-Time Training (TTT) Memory that guarantees a strict $\mathcal{O}(1)$ footprint for long-horizon tasks. To overcome deployment latency, we propose Speculative Asynchronous Inference (SAI) to mask partial diffusion denoising behind physical execution, cutting blocking latency by about $50\%$. To scale robust policies, we present EmbodiChain, an online framework that establishes the Efficiency Law by injecting an infinite flow of physics-grounded trajectories during training. Extensive experiments validate that CLWM achieves state-of-the-art performance in complex dual-arm simulation and unprecedented zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on physical robots, outperforming baselines explicitly finetuned on real-world data.

</details>


### [12] [Saccade Attention Networks: Using Transfer Learning of Attention to Reduce Network Sizes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16485)
*Marc Estafanous*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Saccade Attention Network reduces transformer computation by 80% using learned sparse attention on key image features, inspired by human saccadic eye movements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers suffer from quadratic attention complexity with sequence length. Human visual processing uses sparse attention (saccades) focusing on key features, suggesting computational efficiency can be achieved through similar sparse attention mechanisms.

Method: Propose Saccade Attention Network that learns where to attend from a large pre-trained model, using it to pre-process images by selecting only key features, thereby reducing input sequence length to just attended regions.

Result: Can reduce calculations by close to 80% while producing similar results compared to full attention approaches.

Conclusion: Sparse attention inspired by human saccades enables significant computational savings in transformers while maintaining performance, offering a practical solution to the quadratic attention complexity problem.

Abstract: One of the limitations of transformer networks is the sequence length due to the quadratic nature of the attention matrix. Classical self attention uses the entire sequence length, however, the actual attention being used is sparse. Humans use a form of sparse attention when analyzing an image or scene called saccades. Focusing on key features greatly reduces computation time. By using a network (Saccade Attention Network) to learn where to attend from a large pre-trained model, we can use it to pre-process images and greatly reduce network size by reducing the input sequence length to just the key features being attended to. Our results indicate that you can reduce calculations by close to 80% and produce similar results.

</details>


### [13] [Towards Generalizable Deepfake Image Detection with Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17376)
*Kaliki V Srinanda,M Manvith Prabhu,Hemanth K Mogilipalem,Jayavarapu S Abhinai,Vaibhav Santhosh,Aryan Herur,Deepu Vijayasenan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ensemble of fine-tuned vision transformers (DINOv2, AIMv2, OpenCLIP) achieves state-of-the-art deepfake detection on DF-Wild dataset, winning IEEE SP Cup 2025 with 96.77% AUC and 9% EER.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of detecting deepfake images due to rapid evolution of generative models and poor generalization of existing methods.

Method: Use ensemble of fine-tuned vision transformers (DINOv2, AIMv2, OpenCLIP's ViT-L/14) on DF-Wild dataset, starting with CNN classifiers trained on spatial features.

Result: Ensemble outperforms individual models and CNN baselines, achieving 96.77% AUC and 9% EER on DF-Wild test set, beating state-of-the-art Effort algorithm by 7.05% AUC and 8% EER.

Conclusion: The ensemble approach provides a generalizable method for deepfake detection, winning IEEE SP Cup 2025 and presented at ICASSP 2025.

Abstract: In today's day and age, we face a challenge in detecting deepfake images because of the fast evolution of modern generative models and the poor generalization capability of existing methods. In this paper, we use an ensemble of fine-tuned vision transformers like DINOv2, AIMv2 and OpenCLIP's ViT-L/14 to create generalizable method to detect deepfakes. We use the DF-Wild dataset released as part of the IEEE SP Cup 2025, because it uses a challenging and diverse set of manipulations and generation techniques. We started our experiments with CNN classifiers trained on spatial features. Experimental results show that our ensemble outperforms individual models and strong CNN baselines, achieving an AUC of 96.77% and an Equal Error Rate (EER) of just 9% on the DF-Wild test set, beating the state-of-the-art deepfake detection algorithm Effort by 7.05% and 8% in AUC and EER respectively. This was the winning solution for SP Cup, presented at ICASSP 2025.

</details>


### [14] [Aletheia: Physics-Conditioned Localized Artifact Attention (PhyLAA-X) for End-to-End Generalizable and Robust Deepfake Video Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16486)
*Devendra Ghori*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PhyLAA-X integrates physics-based features (optical flow, specular reflection, rPPG) into deepfake detection to improve robustness against cross-generator shifts, compression, and adversarial attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deepfake detectors fail under cross-generator shifts, compression, and adversarial perturbations because they decouple semantic artifact learning from physical invariants like optical-flow discontinuities, specular-reflection inconsistencies, and cardiac-modulated reflectance.

Method: PhyLAA-X injects three physics-derived feature volumes (optical-flow curl, specular-reflectance skewness, spatially-upsampled rPPG power spectra) into LAA-X attention via cross-attention gating and resonance consistency loss, forcing learning of manipulation boundaries where semantic and physical inconsistencies co-occur.

Result: Achieves 97.2% accuracy on FaceForensics++ (c23), 94.9% on Celeb-DF v2, 90.8% on DFDC, outperforming LAA-Net by 4.1-7.3% in cross-generator settings, and maintains 79.4% accuracy under adversarial attacks (epsilon=0.02 PGD-10).

Conclusion: Physics-conditioned attention significantly improves deepfake detection robustness across diverse challenging scenarios, with the full system open-sourced for reproducibility.

Abstract: State-of-the-art deepfake detectors achieve near-perfect in-domain accuracy yet degrade under cross-generator shifts, heavy compression, and adversarial perturbations. The core limitation remains the decoupling of semantic artifact learning from physical invariants: optical-flow discontinuities, specular-reflection inconsistencies, and cardiac-modulated reflectance (rPPG) are treated either as post-hoc features or ignored.
  We introduce PhyLAA-X, a novel physics-conditioned extension of Localized Artifact Attention (LAA-X). PhyLAA-X injects three end-to-end differentiable physics-derived feature volumes - optical-flow curl, specular-reflectance skewness, and spatially-upsampled rPPG power spectra - directly into the LAA-X attention computation via cross-attention gating and a resonance consistency loss. This forces the network to learn manipulation boundaries where semantic inconsistencies and physical violations co-occur - regions inherently harder for generative models to replicate consistently.
  PhyLAA-X is embedded across an efficient spatiotemporal ensemble (EfficientNet-B4+BiLSTM, ResNeXt-101+Transformer, Xception+causal Conv1D) with uncertainty-aware adaptive weighting. On FaceForensics++ (c23), Aletheia reaches 97.2% accuracy / 0.992 AUC-ROC; on Celeb-DF v2, 94.9% / 0.981; on DFDC, 90.8% / 0.966 - outperforming the strongest published baseline (LAA-Net [1]) by 4.1-7.3% in cross-generator settings and maintaining 79.4% accuracy under epsilon = 0.02 PGD-10 attacks. Single-backbone ablations confirm PhyLAA-X alone delivers a 4.2% cross-dataset AUC gain. The full production system is open-sourced at https://github.com/devghori1264/Aletheia (v1.2, April 2026) with pretrained weights, the adversarial corpus (referred to as ADC-2026 in this work), and complete reproducibility artifacts.

</details>


### [15] [Multi-Camera Self-Calibration in Sports Motion Capture: Leveraging Human and Stick Poses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17567)
*Fan Yang,Changsoo Jung,Ryosuke Kawamura,Hon Yung Wong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Tool-free multi-camera calibration method for sports using human keypoints and stick-like implements of known length.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-camera calibration in sports is costly and labor-intensive, requiring dedicated calibration tools. There's a need for efficient, tool-free calibration methods.

Method: Three-stage optimization pipeline that jointly exploits human body keypoints (unknown metric scale) and rigid stick-like implements of known length from synchronized multi-camera videos.

Result: Achieves accurate extrinsic calibration without tools, with state-of-the-art performance (low rotation/translation errors). First dataset created for multi-camera self-calibration in stick-based sports.

Conclusion: Proposed method enables efficient, tool-free multi-camera calibration for sports with stick-like implements, reducing setup costs and labor.

Abstract: Multi-camera systems are widely employed in sports to capture the 3D motion of athletes and equipment, yet calibrating their extrinsic parameters remains costly and labor-intensive. We introduce an efficient, tool-free method for multi-camera extrinsic calibration tailored to sports involving stick-like implements (e.g., golf clubs, bats, hockey sticks). Our approach jointly exploits two complementary cues from synchronized multi-camera videos: (i) human body keypoints with unknown metric scale and (ii) a rigid stick-like implement of known length. We formulate a three-stage optimization pipeline that refines camera extrinsics, reconstructs human and stick trajectories, and resolves global scale via the stick-length constraint. Our method achieves accurate extrinsic calibration without dedicated calibration tools. To benchmark this task, we present the first dataset for multi-camera self-calibration in stick-based sports, consisting of synthetic sequences across four sports categories with 3 to 10 cameras. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our method delivers SOTA performance, achieving low rotation and translation errors. Our project page: https://fandulu.github.io/sport_stick_multi_cam_calib/.

</details>


### [16] [Geometry-Aware CLIP Retrieval via Local Cross-Modal Alignment and Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16487)
*Nirmalendu Prakash,Narmeen Fatimah Oozeer,Xin Su,Phillip Howard,Shaan Shah,Zoe Wanying He,Shuang Wu,Shivam Raval,Roy Ka-Wei Lee,Meenakshi Khosla,Amir Abdullah*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes two inference-time methods to improve CLIP retrieval by addressing local geometric inconsistencies: neighborhood-level re-ranking via Hungarian matching and query-conditioned local steering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLIP retrieval suffers from local geometric inconsistencies where nearby items are incorrectly ordered, leading to systematic confusions and diffuse result sets. While CLIP achieves strong global cross-modal alignment, these local failures persist.

Method: Two inference-time methods: (1) Neighborhood-level re-ranking via Hungarian matching that rewards structural consistency, and (2) Query-conditioned local steering where directions derived from contrastive neighborhoods around the query reshape retrieval.

Result: The techniques improve retrieval performance on attribute-binding and compositional retrieval tasks without requiring retraining.

Conclusion: Retrieval quality and controllability depend critically on local structure, which can be exploited at inference time through neighborhood alignment methods without retraining the base model.

Abstract: CLIP retrieval is typically framed as a pointwise similarity problem in a shared embedding space. While CLIP achieves strong global cross-modal alignment, many retrieval failures arise from local geometric inconsistencies: nearby items are incorrectly ordered, leading to systematic confusions (e.g., pentagon vs. hexagon) and produces diffuse, weakly controlled result sets. Prior work largely optimizes for point wise relevance or finetuning to mitigate these problems. We instead view retrieval as a problem of neighborhood alignment. Our work introduces (1) neighborhood-level re-ranking via Hungarian matching, which rewards structural consistency; (2) query-conditioned local steering, where directions derived from contrastive neighborhoods around the query reshape retrieval. We show that these techniques improve retrieval performance on attribute-binding and compositional retrieval tasks. Together, these methods operate on local neighborhoods but serve different roles: re-ranking rewards alignment whereas local steering controls neighborhood structure. This shows that retrieval quality and controllability depend critically on local structure, which can be exploited at inference time without retraining.

</details>


### [17] [An Uncertainty-Aware Loss Function Incorporating Fuzzy Logic: Application to MRI Brain Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16490)
*Hanuman Verma,Akshansh Gupta,Pranabesh Maji,Saurav Mandal,Vijay Kumar Pandey*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel loss function combining categorical cross-entropy with fuzzy entropy improves brain MRI segmentation by handling uncertainty in pixel classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Brain image segmentation is crucial for neurological disease diagnosis, but existing loss functions don't adequately handle uncertainty in pixel classification inherent in MRI images.

Method: Proposed a novel loss function integrating categorical cross-entropy (CCE) with fuzzy entropy based on fuzzy logic to account for uncertainty in pixel classifications during brain tissue segmentation.

Result: The proposed loss function outperformed standard CCE on IBSR and OASIS datasets using U-Net and U-Net++ architectures across various performance metrics, while effectively handling uncertainty during training.

Conclusion: The fuzzy logic-enhanced loss function improves segmentation accuracy and enhances model prediction reliability by better handling uncertainty in brain MRI tissue segmentation.

Abstract: Accurate brain image segmentation, particularly for distinguishing various tissues from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) images, plays a pivotal role in finding the neurological dis ease and medical image computing. In deep learning approaches, loss functions are very crucial for optimizing the model. In this study, we introduce a novel loss function integrating fuzzy logic to deals uncertainty issues in brain image segmentation into various tissues. It integrates the well-known categorical cross-entropy (CCE) loss function and fuzzy entropy based on fuzzy logic. By employing fuzzy logic, this loss function accounts for the inherent uncertainties in pixel classifications. The proposed loss function has been evaluated on two publicly available benchmark datasets, IBSR and OASIS, using two widely recognised architectures, U-Net and U-Net++. Experimental results demonstrate that the trained model with proposed loss function provided better results in comparison to the CCE optimisation function in terms of various performance metrics. Additionally, it effectively enhances segmentation performance while handling meaningful uncer tainty during training. The findings suggest that this approach not only improves segmentation outcomes but also contributes to the reliability of model predictions.

</details>


### [18] [A Lightweight Transformer for Pain Recognition from Brain Activity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16491)
*Stefanos Gkikas,Christian Arzate Cruz,Yu Fang,Lu Cao,Muhammad Umar Khan,Thomas Kassiotis,Giorgos Giannakakis,Raul Fernandez Rojas,Randy Gomez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight transformer architecture for pain recognition from fNIRS data using unified tokenization of multiple signal representations, achieving competitive performance with low computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pain assessment is clinically important but challenging, requiring reliable automated methods. Current approaches may be computationally heavy or lack effective fusion of complementary signal representations from fNIRS data.

Method: Proposes a lightweight transformer that fuses multiple fNIRS representations (raw waveform and power spectral density) through unified tokenization. Uses token-mixing strategy to preserve spatial, temporal, and time-frequency characteristics via structured segmentation for local aggregation and global interaction.

Result: Demonstrates competitive pain recognition performance on AI4Pain dataset while remaining computationally compact. Suitable for real-time inference on both GPU and CPU hardware.

Conclusion: The approach provides an effective and efficient solution for automated pain assessment from fNIRS data, balancing performance with computational efficiency for practical deployment.

Abstract: Pain is a multifaceted and widespread phenomenon with substantial clinical and societal burden, making reliable automated assessment a critical objective. This paper presents a lightweight transformer architecture that fuses multiple fNIRS representations through a unified tokenization mechanism, enabling joint modeling of complementary signal views without requiring modality-specific adaptations or increasing architectural complexity. The proposed token-mixing strategy preserves spatial, temporal, and time-frequency characteristics by projecting heterogeneous inputs onto a shared latent representation, using a structured segmentation scheme to control the granularity of local aggregation and global interaction. The model is evaluated on the AI4Pain dataset using stacked raw waveform and power spectral density representations of fNIRS inputs. Experimental results demonstrate competitive pain recognition performance while remaining computationally compact, making the approach suitable for real-time inference on both GPU and CPU hardware.

</details>


### [19] [LayerCache: Exploiting Layer-wise Velocity Heterogeneity for Efficient Flow Matching Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16492)
*Guandong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LayerCache improves Flow Matching model inference by implementing layer-aware caching that exploits heterogeneous velocity dynamics in Transformer layers, achieving better quality-speed trade-offs than monolithic caching approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow Matching models have high inference costs due to iterative denoising through large Transformers. Existing caching methods treat the entire Transformer as a monolithic unit, failing to exploit the observation that different layer groups exhibit markedly heterogeneous velocity dynamics - shallow layers are stable while deep layers change significantly.

Method: LayerCache partitions the Transformer into layer groups and makes independent, per-group caching decisions at each denoising step. It introduces adaptive JVP span K selection using per-group stability measurements to balance accuracy and computational savings. The approach formulates a three-dimensional scheduling problem over timesteps, layer groups, and JVP span, solved with a greedy budget allocation algorithm.

Result: On Qwen-Image (1024x1024, 50 steps), LayerCache achieves PSNR 37.46 dB (+5.38 dB over MeanCache), SSIM 0.9834, and LPIPS 0.0178 (70% reduction over MeanCache) at 1.37x speedup, dominating all prior caching methods on the quality-speed Pareto frontier.

Conclusion: LayerCache demonstrates that layer-aware caching can significantly improve the quality-speed trade-off for Flow Matching models by exploiting the heterogeneous velocity dynamics across Transformer layers, outperforming monolithic caching approaches.

Abstract: Flow Matching models achieve state-of-the-art image generation quality but incur substantial inference cost due to iterative denoising through large Transformer networks. We observe that different layer groups within a Transformer exhibit markedly heterogeneous velocity dynamics: shallow layers are highly stable and amenable to aggressive caching, while deep layers undergo large velocity changes that demand full computation. Existing caching methods, however, treat the entire Transformer as a monolithic unit, applying a single caching decision per timestep and thus failing to exploit this heterogeneity. Based on this finding, we propose LayerCache, a layer-aware caching framework that partitions the Transformer into layer groups and makes independent, per-group caching decisions at each denoising step. LayerCache introduces an adaptive JVP span K selection mechanism that leverages per-group stability measurements to balance estimation accuracy and computational savings. We formulate a three-dimensional scheduling problem over timesteps, layer groups, and JVP span, and solve it with a greedy budget allocation algorithm. On Qwen-Image (1024x1024, 50 steps), LayerCache achieves PSNR 37.46 dB (+5.38 dB over MeanCache), SSIM 0.9834, and LPIPS 0.0178 (a 70% reduction over MeanCache) at 1.37x speedup, dominating all prior caching methods on the quality-speed Pareto frontier.

</details>


### [20] [HQA-VLAttack: Towards High Quality Adversarial Attack on Vision-Language Pre-Trained Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16499)
*Han Liu,Jiaqi Li,Zhi Xu,Xiaotong Zhang,Xiaoming Xu,Fenglong Ma,Yuanman Li,Hong Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HQA-VLAttack is a black-box adversarial attack framework for vision-language models that generates high-quality adversarial examples through text and image perturbation stages, achieving better attack success rates than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Black-box adversarial attacks on vision-language models are challenging due to simultaneous text/image perturbations and limited access to model internals. Existing methods either use complex iterative search (high query cost) or only reduce positive pair similarity while ignoring negative pairs, limiting attack effectiveness.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) Text perturbation uses counter-fitting word vectors to generate semantically consistent substitute words. 2) Image perturbation initializes adversarial examples via layer-importance guided strategy, then optimizes using contrastive learning to decrease positive pair similarity while increasing negative pair similarity.

Result: Experimental results on three benchmark datasets show HQA-VLAttack significantly outperforms strong baselines in attack success rate.

Conclusion: HQA-VLAttack provides an effective black-box adversarial attack framework for vision-language models that balances attack effectiveness with query efficiency by properly handling both positive and negative similarity relationships.

Abstract: Black-box adversarial attack on vision-language pre-trained models is a practical and challenging task, as text and image perturbations need to be considered simultaneously, and only the predicted results are accessible. Research on this problem is in its infancy, and only a handful of methods are available. Nevertheless, existing methods either rely on a complex iterative cross-search strategy, which inevitably consumes numerous queries, or only consider reducing the similarity of positive image-text pairs but ignore that of negative ones, which will also be implicitly diminished, thus inevitably affecting the attack performance. To alleviate the above issues, we propose a simple yet effective framework to generate high-quality adversarial examples on vision-language pre-trained models, named HQA-VLAttack, which consists of text and image attack stages. For text perturbation generation, it leverages the counter-fitting word vector to generate the substitute word set, thus guaranteeing the semantic consistency between the substitute word and the original word. For image perturbation generation, it first initializes the image adversarial example via the layer-importance guided strategy, and then utilizes contrastive learning to optimize the image adversarial perturbation, which ensures that the similarity of positive image-text pairs is decreased while that of negative image-text pairs is increased. In this way, the optimized adversarial images and texts are more likely to retrieve negative examples, thereby enhancing the attack success rate. Experimental results on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that HQA-VLAttack significantly outperforms strong baselines in terms of attack success rate.

</details>


### [21] [Semantically Stable Image Composition Analysisvia Saliency and Gradient Vector Flow Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16500)
*Armin Dadras,Robert Sablatnig,Franziska Proksa,Markus Seidl*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VFCNet uses gradient vector flow fields combining saliency and edge information to assess photographic composition, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the PICD benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current computational assessment of photographic composition needs features that are discriminative of spatial layout while being robust to semantic content. The paper is motivated by the assumption that composition can be understood as the flow of visual attention across geometric structure.

Method: Proposes VFCNet which fuses saliency and edge information into gradient vector flow (GVF) fields. The model computes dual-stream GVF representations, integrates them via attention mechanisms, and extracts multi-scale flow features using a DINOv3 backbone.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on PICD benchmark with CDA-1: 0.683 and CDA-2: 0.629, improving by 33.1% and 36.1% over previous best method. Also shows that simple classifier on self-supervised DINOv3 features outperforms more sophisticated composition-specialized models.

Conclusion: VFCNet's approach of modeling composition as visual attention flow across geometric structure provides an effective low-level representation for computational assessment of photographic composition, with significant performance improvements over existing methods.

Abstract: The reliable computational assessment of photographic composition requires features that are discriminative of spatial layout yet robust to semantic content. This paper proposes a low-level representation grounded in the assumption that composition can be understood as the flow of visual attention across geometric structure. We introduce VFCNet, which fuses saliency and edge information into a gradient vector flow (GVF) field. The model computes dual-stream GVF representations, integrates them via attention, and extracts multi-scale flow features with a DINOv3 backbone. VFCNet achieves state-of-the-art performance on the PICD benchmark (CDA-1: 0.683, CDA-2: 0.629), improving by 33.1\% and 36.1\% over the previous best method. We also show that a simple classifier on self-supervised DINOv3 features substantially outperforms more sophisticated, composition-specialized models. Code is available at https://github.com/ADadras/VFCNet

</details>


### [22] [Topology-Aware Layer Pruning for Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16502)
*Pengcheng Zheng,Chaoning Zhang,Ya Wen,Wang Liu,Qigan Sun,Jiarong Mo,Jiaquan Zhang,Jewon Lee,Tae-Ho Kim,Kuien Liu,Tianyu Li,Caiyan Qin,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Topology-aware layer pruning framework for Large Vision-Language Models using simplicial complexes and zigzag persistent homology to preserve critical representational transitions while reducing computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have high computational and memory costs that hinder deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Existing pruning methods fail to capture the global and dynamic evolution of representations across model depth, often removing transition-critical layers.

Method: Proposes a topology-aware layer pruning framework that represents layer-wise hidden states as point clouds and models their evolution using simplicial complexes. Uses zigzag persistent homology to quantify inter-layer topological consistency and enable adaptive pruning that preserves critical representational transitions.

Result: Extensive experiments on diverse multimodal benchmarks show the proposed framework consistently outperforms existing pruning methods across a wide range of sparsity ratios.

Conclusion: The topology-aware pruning approach effectively reduces computational costs of LVLMs while maintaining performance by preserving critical representational transitions through topological analysis of layer evolution.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in natural language understanding and reasoning, while recent extensions that incorporate visual inputs enable them to process multimodal information. Despite these advances, Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) incur substantial computational and memory costs, hindering deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Existing layer pruning methods typically rely on local similarity metrics or static proxy signals, failing to capture the global and dynamic evolution of representations across model depth, which often leads to the removal of transition-critical layers. To address this limitation, we propose a topology-aware layer pruning framework for LVLMs. Specifically, we represent layer wise hidden states as point clouds and models their evolution using \textit{simplicial complexes}. By leveraging \textit{zigzag persistent homology}, we quantify inter-layer topological consistency and enable adaptive pruning that preserves critical representational transitions. Extensive experiments on diverse multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms existing pruning methods across a wide range of sparsity ratios. Our code is available at https://github.com/zpc456/TopoVLM.

</details>


### [23] [Motif-Video 2B: Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16503)
*Junghwan Lim,Wai Ting Cheung,Minsu Ha,Beomgyu Kim,Taewhan Kim,Haesol Lee,Dongpin Oh,Jeesoo Lee,Taehyun Kim,Minjae Kim,Sungmin Lee,Hyeyeon Cho,Dahye Choi,Jaeheui Her,Jaeyeon Huh,Hanbin Jung,Changjin Kang,Dongseok Kim,Jangwoong Kim,Youngrok Kim,Hyukjin Kweon,Hongjoo Lee,Jeongdoo Lee,Junhyeok Lee,Eunhwan Park,Yeongjae Park,Bokki Ryu,Dongjoo Weon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Motif-Video 2B achieves strong text-to-video quality with only 10M clips and <100k GPU hours by using specialized architecture rather than just scaling up, outperforming larger models with 7× fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video generation models require massive datasets, parameters, and compute. The paper explores whether strong quality is possible with much smaller budgets by focusing on how model capacity is organized rather than just scaling up.

Method: Uses architectural separation of roles: Shared Cross-Attention for text control with long sequences, and a three-part backbone separating early fusion, joint representation learning, and detail refinement. Combined with efficient training using dynamic token routing and early-phase feature alignment to a frozen pretrained video encoder.

Result: Achieves 83.76% on VBench, surpassing Wan2.1 14B while using 7× fewer parameters and substantially less training data. Later blocks develop clearer cross-frame attention structure than standard single-stream baselines.

Conclusion: Careful architectural specialization combined with efficiency-oriented training can narrow or exceed the quality gap typically associated with much larger video models, suggesting that organization of capacity matters more than just scale.

Abstract: Training strong video generation models usually requires massive datasets, large parameter counts, and substantial compute. In this work, we ask whether strong text-to-video quality is possible at a much smaller budget: fewer than 10M clips and less than 100,000 H200 GPU hours. Our core claim is that part of the answer lies in how model capacity is organized, not only in how much of it is used. In video generation, prompt alignment, temporal consistency, and fine-detail recovery can interfere with one another when they are handled through the same pathway. Motif-Video 2B addresses this by separating these roles architecturally, rather than relying on scale alone. The model combines two key ideas. First, Shared Cross-Attention strengthens text control when video token sequences become long. Second, a three-part backbone separates early fusion, joint representation learning, and detail refinement. To make this design effective under a limited compute budget, we pair it with an efficient training recipe based on dynamic token routing and early-phase feature alignment to a frozen pretrained video encoder. Our analysis shows that later blocks develop clearer cross-frame attention structure than standard single-stream baselines. On VBench, Motif-Video~2B reaches 83.76\%, surpassing Wan2.1 14B while using 7$\times$ fewer parameters and substantially less training data. These results suggest that careful architectural specialization, combined with an efficiency-oriented training recipe, can narrow or exceed the quality gap typically associated with much larger video models.

</details>


### [24] [From Handwriting to Structured Data: Benchmarking AI Digitisation of Handwritten Forms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16504)
*Nicholas Pather,Joshua Fouché,Sitwala Mundia,Karl-Günter Technau,Thokozile Malaba,Alex Welte,Ushma Mehta,Bruce A. Bassett*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Benchmarking 17 multimodal LLMs on challenging medical form digitization shows latest models achieve ~85% accuracy, with Gemini 3.1 performing best overall and prompt optimization significantly improving classification metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual digitization of structured handwritten documents is slow and costly, especially problematic for low- and middle-income countries. There's a need to automate this process using advanced AI models.

Method: Benchmarked 17 leading frontier multimodal large language models (including Google, OpenAI, and open-source models) on a challenging real-world medical form containing mixed content: dates, structured printed text, handwritten responses, and significant variability. Used prompt optimization techniques to improve performance.

Result: Latest Google and OpenAI models achieved ~85% accuracy with weighted F1 scores ~90% on discrete/predefined fields. GPT 5.4 excelled in noisy date extraction with lowest hallucination rate (6%). Claude Sonnet 4.6 performed best on formatted fields. Gemini 3.1 delivered best overall performance with lowest free text error rates (WER=0.50, CER=0.31). Prompt optimization improved macro precision, recall and F1 by over 60%, but only modestly improved weighted metrics (2-5%).

Conclusion: Rapid improvements in multimodal LLMs offer a compelling pathway toward fully automated digitization of complex handwritten workflows, particularly relevant for low- and middle-income countries where manual digitization is especially burdensome.

Abstract: Manual digitisation of structured handwritten documents is slow and costly. We benchmark 17 leading frontier multi-modal large language models and open-source models against a very challenging real-world medical form that mixes dates; structured, printed text; hand-written responses and significant variability challenges. None of the smaller or older models perform well but the latest Google and OpenAI models reach accuracies around $85\%$ with weighted F1 scores $\simeq 90\%$ across the discrete or predefined fields despite the very challenging nature of the responses. Clear task specific strengths emerge: GPT 5.4 excels in noisy date extraction as well as reliability with the lowest hallucination rate ($6\%$). Claude Sonnet 4.6 had the best average performance across formatted fields (dates and numerical values), while Gemini 3.1 delivered the best overall performance, with the lowest free text error rates (WER = $0.50$ and CER = $0.31$) and the strongest results across discrete classification metrics. We further show that prompt optimisation dramatically improves macro precision, recall and F1 by over $60\%$, but has little impact on weighted metrics (only $\sim2-5\%$ improvement). These results provide evidence that the rapid improvements of multimodal large language models offer a compelling pathway toward fully automated digitisation of complex handwritten workflows that is particularly relevant in low- and middle-income countries.

</details>


### [25] [Predicting Blastocyst Formation in IVF: Integrating DINOv2 and Attention-Based LSTM on Time-Lapse Embryo Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16505)
*Zahra Asghari Varzaneh,Niclas Wölner-Hanssen,Reza Khoshkangini,Thomas Ebner,Magnus Johnsson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hybrid DINOv2-LSTM model predicts blastocyst formation from limited daily embryo images with 96.4% accuracy, outperforming existing methods and handling missing frames for IVF clinics without full time-lapse systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual inspection of time-lapse imaging for embryo selection in IVF is challenging and time-consuming. Many clinics lack complete time-lapse systems, making full videos unavailable. There's a need to predict blastocyst formation from limited daily images to improve embryo selection efficiency.

Method: Proposes a novel hybrid model combining DINOv2 (transformer-based vision model) with an enhanced LSTM network featuring multi-head attention. DINOv2 extracts features from embryo images, then LSTM analyzes temporal development patterns to predict blastocyst formation.

Result: Achieved 96.4% accuracy on a real dataset of 704 embryo videos, surpassing existing methods. The model performs well even with missing frames, making it suitable for clinics with limited imaging systems.

Conclusion: The proposed approach can assist embryologists in selecting better embryos more efficiently and confidently, particularly valuable for IVF laboratories with incomplete time-lapse imaging systems.

Abstract: The selection of the optimal embryo for transfer is a critical yet challenging step in in vitro fertilization (IVF), primarily due to its reliance on the manual inspection of extensive time-lapse imaging data. A key obstacle in this process is predicting blastocyst formation from the limited number of daily images available. Many clinics also lack complete time-lapse systems, so full videos are often unavailable. In this study, we aimed to predict which embryos will develop into blastocysts using limited daily images from time-lapse recordings. We propose a novel hybrid model that combines DINOv2, a transformer-based vision model, with an enhanced long short-term memory (LSTM) network featuring a multi-head attention layer. DINOv2 extracts meaningful features from embryo images, and the LSTM model then uses these features to analyze embryo development over time and generate final predictions. We tested our model on a real dataset of 704 embryo videos. The model achieved 96.4% accuracy, surpassing existing methods. It also performs well with missing frames, making it valuable for many IVF laboratories with limited imaging systems. Our approach can assist embryologists in selecting better embryos more efficiently and with greater confidence.

</details>


### [26] [Generative Semantic Communication via Alternating Dual-Domain Posterior Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16796)
*Shunpu Tang,Qianqian Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ADDPS: A diffusion-based semantic communication receiver using alternating dual-domain posterior sampling to achieve superior perceptual quality in wireless image transmission.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative semantic communication receivers rely on MAP estimation which cannot preserve data distribution, limiting perceptual quality. Current diffusion-based approaches using single-domain guidance have limitations: latent-domain guidance is sensitive to channel noise, while image-domain guidance inherits decoder bias. Simply combining both domains yields overconfident pseudo-posterior.

Method: Formulate semantic decoding as Bayesian inverse problem and prove posterior sampling achieves optimal perceptual quality. Propose alternating dual-domain posterior sampling (ADDPS) - a diffusion-based SemCom receiver that alternately enforces latent-domain and image-domain consistency during sampling process. This alternating strategy decomposes joint posterior sampling into simpler subproblems.

Result: Experiments on FFHQ demonstrate that ADDPS achieves superior perceptual quality compared with existing methods.

Conclusion: Posterior sampling preserves data distribution for optimal perceptual quality. Alternating dual-domain approach avoids gradient conflicts while retaining complementary strengths of both domains, overcoming limitations of single-domain guidance methods.

Abstract: Generative semantic communication (SemCom) harnesses pretrained generative priors to improve the perceptual quality of wireless image transmission. Existing generative SemCom receivers, however, rely on maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation, which fundamentally cannot preserve the data distribution and thus limits achievable perceptual quality. Moreover, current diffusion-based approaches using single-domain guidance face significant limitations: latent-domain guidance is sensitive to channel noise, while image-domain guidance inherits decoder bias. Simply combining both domains simultaneously yields an overconfident pseudo-posterior. In this paper, we formulate semantic decoding as a Bayesian inverse problem and prove that posterior sampling achieves optimal perceptual quality by preserving the data distribution. Building on this insight, we propose alternating dual-domain posterior sampling (ADDPS), a diffusion-based SemCom receiver that alternately enforces latent-domain and image-domain consistency during the sampling process. This alternating strategy decomposes joint posterior sampling into simpler subproblems, avoiding gradient conflicts while retaining the complementary strengths of both domains. Experiments on FFHQ demonstrate that the proposed ADDPS achieves superior perceptual quality compared with existing methods.

</details>


### [27] [Medical thinking with multiple images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16506)
*Zonghai Yao,Benlu Wang,Yifan Zhang,Junda Wang,Iris Xia,Zhipeng Tang,Shuo Han,Feiyun Ouyang,Zhichao Yang,Arman Cohan,Hong Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MedThinkVQA is a new benchmark for multi-image medical reasoning requiring models to interpret multiple images, combine cross-view evidence, and answer diagnostic questions with intermediate supervision. Current models struggle significantly, with top models only reaching ~57% accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real clinical reasoning requires integrating evidence across multiple medical images rather than interpreting single views, but existing benchmarks focus on single-image tasks. There's a need to evaluate models' ability to perform grounded multi-image reasoning in medical contexts.

Method: Created MedThinkVQA, an expert-annotated benchmark with 8,067 cases (720 test cases) averaging 6.62 images per case. Includes intermediate supervision and step-level evaluation. Evaluated various closed-source (Claude, Gemini, GPT) and open-source models (Qwen). Analyzed errors through step-level analysis and ablation studies with expert cues vs. self-generated intermediates.

Result: Best models perform poorly: Claude-4.6-Opus (57.2%), Gemini-3-Pro (55.3%), GPT-5.2-xhigh (54.9%). Smaller models perform worse. Open-source models lag behind. Over 70% of errors come from image reading and cross-view integration. Expert cues improve performance while self-generated intermediates reduce accuracy. Additional computation only helps when visual grounding is reliable.

Conclusion: The main bottleneck is grounded multi-image reasoning - models fail to extract, align, and compose evidence across views. The key challenge is not reasoning length alone but reliable mechanisms for grounding, aligning, and composing distributed evidence across real-world multimodal clinical inputs.

Abstract: Large language models perform well on many medical QA benchmarks, but real clinical reasoning often requires integrating evidence across multiple images rather than interpreting a single view. We introduce MedThinkVQA, an expert-annotated benchmark for thinking with multiple images, where models must interpret each image, combine cross-view evidence, and answer diagnostic questions with intermediate supervision and step-level evaluation. The dataset contains 8,067 cases, including 720 test cases, with an average of 6.62 images per case, substantially denser than prior work, whose expert-level benchmarks use at most 1.43 images per case. On the test set, the best closed-source models, Claude-4.6-Opus, Gemini-3-Pro, and GPT-5.2-xhigh, reach only 57.2%, 55.3%, and 54.9% accuracy, while GPT-5-mini and GPT-5-nano reach 39.7% and 30.8%. Strong open-source models lag behind, led by Qwen3.5-397B-A17B at 52.2% and Qwen3.5-27B at 50.6%. Further analysis identifies grounded multi-image reasoning as the main bottleneck: models often fail to extract, align, and compose evidence across views before higher-level inference can help. Providing expert single-image cues and cross-image summaries improves performance, whereas replacing them with self-generated intermediates reduces accuracy. Step-level analysis shows that over 70% of errors arise from image reading and cross-view integration. Scaling results further show that additional inference-time computation helps only when visual grounding is already reliable; when early evidence extraction is weak, longer reasoning yields limited or unstable gains and can amplify misread cues. These results suggest that the key challenge is not reasoning length alone, but reliable mechanisms for grounding, aligning, and composing distributed evidence across real-world multimodal clinical inputs.

</details>


### [28] [Medial Axis Aware Learning of Signed Distance Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16512)
*Samuel Weidemaier,Christoph Norden-Smoch,Martin Rumpf*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel variational method using phase field approximation to compute accurate global signed distance functions from point clouds by explicitly modeling the medial axis discontinuity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for computing signed distance functions from point clouds often lack global accuracy, especially near the medial axis where the SDF gradient has discontinuities. There's a need for a method that explicitly handles these discontinuities to achieve both local and global accuracy.

Method: The method uses a higher-order variational formulation that explicitly accounts for the jump set of the SDF gradient (medial axis) by enforcing linear growth along gradient directions away from discontinuities. It employs a phase field approximation (Ambrosio-Tortorelli type) to make the problem computationally tractable, with neural networks approximating both the SDF and phase field functions for unoriented point clouds.

Result: Experiments demonstrate high accuracy both in near-field regions and globally. Quantitative and qualitative comparisons with other approaches show advantages of the proposed method in terms of accuracy.

Conclusion: The proposed variational method with phase field approximation successfully computes highly accurate global signed distance functions from point clouds by explicitly handling medial axis discontinuities, outperforming existing approaches.

Abstract: We propose a novel variational method to compute a highly accurate global signed distance function (SDF) to a given point cloud. To this end, the jump set of the gradient of the SDF, which coincides with the medial axis of the surface, is explicitly taken into account through a higher-order variational formulation that enforces linear growth along the gradient direction away from this discontinuity set. The eikonal equation and the zero-level set of the SDF are enforced as constraints. To make this variational problem computationally tractable, a phase field approximation of Ambrosio-Tortorelli type is employed. The associated phase field function implicitly describes the medial axis. The method is implemented for surfaces represented by unoriented point clouds using neural network approximations of both the SDF and the phase field. Experiments demonstrate the method's accuracy both in the near field and globally. Quantitative and qualitative comparisons with other approaches show the advantages of the proposed method.

</details>


### [29] [SynthPID: P&ID digitization from Topology-Preserving Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16513)
*Suraj Prasad,Pinak Mahapatra*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SynthPID: Synthetic P&ID dataset with real topology seeding achieves 63.8% edge mAP, closing within 8% of real-data performance, showing structural realism matters more than visual fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
