Motivation: Automating P&ID digitization is bottlenecked by lack of public annotated data (only 12 images available), and previous synthetic methods fail because they produce unrealistic random graphs with poor edge detection (~33% accuracy).

Method: Created SynthPID corpus of 665 synthetic P&IDs where pipe topology is seeded directly from real drawings, paired with patch-based Relationformer adapted for high-resolution diagrams.

Result: Model trained only on SynthPID achieves 63.8 ± 3.1% edge mAP on PID2Graph OPEN100 benchmark, closing within 8 percentage points of real-data oracle. Gains flatten beyond ~400 synthetic images, indicating seed diversity is the limiting factor.

Conclusion: Structural realism in synthetic data generation drives performance more than model choice, and seeding synthetic data with real topology is crucial for effective P&ID digitization when real annotated data is scarce.

Abstract: Automating the digitization of Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs) into structured process graphs would unlock significant value in plant operations, yet progress is bottlenecked by a fundamental data problem: engineering drawings are proprietary, and the entire community shares a single public benchmark of just 12 annotated images. Prior attempts at synthetic augmentation have fallen short because template-based generators scatter symbols at random, producing graphs that bear little resemblance to real process plants and, accordingly, yield only approximately 33% edge detection accuracy under synth-only training. We argue the failure is structural rather than visual and address it by introducing SynthPID, a corpus of 665 synthetic P&IDs whose pipe topology is seeded directly from real drawings. Paired with a patch-based Relationformer adapted for high-resolution diagrams, a model trained on SynthPID alone achieves 63.8 +/- 3.1% edge mAP on PID2Graph OPEN100 without seeing a single real P&ID during training, closing within 8 pp of the real-data oracle. These gains hold up under a controlled comparison against the template-based regime, confirming that generation quality drives performance rather than model choice. A scaling study reveals that gains flatten beyond roughly 400 synthetic images, pointing to seed diversity as the binding constraint.

</details>


### [30] [BARD: Bridging AutoRegressive and Diffusion Vision-Language Models Via Highly Efficient Progressive Block Merging and Stage-Wise Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16514)
*Baoyou Chen,Hanchen Xia,Peng Tu,Haojun Shi,Shan Mu,Weihao Yuan,Siyu Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BARD converts pretrained autoregressive VLMs into efficient diffusion VLMs with 3× faster decoding while maintaining performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive VLMs have inference bottlenecks due to token-by-token decoding, while diffusion VLMs offer parallel decoding but suffer quality degradation when directly converted from autoregressive models.

Method: BARD uses progressive supervised block merging to enlarge decoding blocks, stage-wise intra-diffusion distillation from a small-block anchor, mixed noise scheduler for robustness, token revision during denoising, and memory-friendly training for long sequences.

Result: BARD-VL achieves SOTA among comparable-scale open diffusion VLMs at 4B and 8B scales, with ≤4.4M training data, and achieves up to 3× decoding throughput speedup compared to source autoregressive models.

Conclusion: Intra-diffusion distillation is more effective than direct autoregressive-to-diffusion distillation, enabling efficient conversion of autoregressive VLMs to high-performance diffusion VLMs with significant speed improvements.

Abstract: Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) deliver strong multimodal capability, but their token-by-token decoding imposes a fundamental inference bottleneck. Diffusion VLMs offer a more parallel decoding paradigm, yet directly converting a pretrained autoregressive VLM into a large-block diffusion VLM (dVLM) often leads to substantial quality degradation. In this work, we present BARD, a simple and effective bridging framework that converts a pretrained autoregressive VLM into a same-architecture, decoding-efficient dVLM. Our approach combines progressive supervised block merging, which gradually enlarges the decoding block size, with stage-wise intra-dVLM distillation from a fixed small-block diffusion anchor to recover performance lost at larger blocks. We further incorporate a mixed noise scheduler to improve robustness and token revision during denoising, and memory-friendly training to enable efficient training on long multimodal sequences. A key empirical finding is that direct autoregressive-to-diffusion distillation is poorly aligned and can even hurt performance, whereas distillation within the diffusion regime is consistently effective. Experimental results show that, with $\leq 4.4M$ data, BARD-VL transfers strong multimodal capability from Qwen3-VL to a large-block dVLM. Remarkably, BARD-VL establishes a new SOTA among comparable-scale open dVLMs on our evaluation suite at both 4B and 8B scales. At the same time, BARD-VL achieves up to \textbf{3$\times$} decoding throughput speedup compared to the source model.

</details>


### [31] [Penny Wise, Pixel Foolish: Bypassing Price Constraints in Multimodal Agents via Visual Adversarial Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16515)
*Jiachen Qian,Zhaolu Kang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Paper introduces PriceBlind, a stealthy adversarial attack that exploits visual dominance in MLLMs to override textual price evidence in financial transactions, achieving 80% attack success rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLMs are increasingly used for high-stakes financial transactions, but their adversarial robustness is underexplored. The paper identifies Visual Dominance Hallucination (VDH) where imperceptible visual cues can override textual price evidence, leading to irrational decisions.

Method: Proposes PriceBlind, a white-box adversarial attack framework that exploits modality gaps in CLIP-based encoders using Semantic-Decoupling Loss. This aligns image embeddings with low-cost value anchors while preserving pixel-level fidelity.

Result: On E-ShopBench: 80% ASR in white-box evaluation. Under simplified single-turn protocol: 35-41% ASR transfer across GPT-4o, Gemini-1.5-Pro, and Claude-3.5-Sonnet. Robust encoders and Verify-then-Act defenses reduce ASR but with clean-accuracy trade-offs.

Conclusion: PriceBlind demonstrates significant vulnerability in MLLMs for financial applications via visual dominance attacks. While defenses exist, they involve accuracy trade-offs, highlighting the need for more robust multimodal systems in high-stakes domains.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has enabled mobile agents to execute high-stakes financial transactions, but their adversarial robustness remains underexplored. We identify Visual Dominance Hallucination (VDH), where imperceptible visual cues can override textual price evidence in screenshot-based, price-constrained settings and lead agents to irrational decisions. We propose PriceBlind, a stealthy white-box adversarial attack framework for controlled screenshot-based evaluation. PriceBlind exploits the modality gap in CLIP-based encoders via a Semantic-Decoupling Loss that aligns the image embedding with low-cost, value-associated anchors while preserving pixel-level fidelity. On E-ShopBench, PriceBlind achieves around 80% ASR in white-box evaluation; under a simplified single-turn coordinate-selection protocol, Ensemble-DI-FGSM transfers with roughly 35-41% ASR across GPT-4o, Gemini-1.5-Pro, and Claude-3.5-Sonnet. We also show that robust encoders and Verify-then-Act defenses reduce ASR substantially, though with some clean-accuracy trade-off.

</details>


### [32] [Operationalizing Fairness in Text-to-Image Models: A Survey of Bias, Fairness Audits and Mitigation Strategies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16516)
*Megan Smith,Venkatesh Thirugnana Sambandham,Florian Richter,Laura Crompton,Matthias Uhl,Torsten Schön*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper provides a systematic review of fairness in Text-to-Image generation, addressing conceptual ambiguity in bias/fairness terminology, proposing a taxonomy of bias types and fairness notions, and introducing a framework for operationalizing fairness beyond descriptive metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: T2I models are widely used but criticized for exhibiting societal stereotypes. Current research suffers from conceptual ambiguity where terms like "bias" and "fairness" lack clear operational definitions, making systematic evaluation and mitigation challenging.

Method: The paper conducts a comprehensive systematic review of T2I fairness literature, organizing existing work into a taxonomy of bias types and fairness notions. It critically assesses the gap between "target fairness" (normative ideals) and "threshold fairness" (actionable decision rules), and surveys mitigation strategies from prompt engineering to diffusion process manipulation.

Result: The review organizes T2I fairness research into a structured taxonomy, identifies the conceptual gap between ideal fairness goals and practical implementation standards, and maps the landscape of existing mitigation approaches.

Conclusion: The paper proposes a new framework for operationalizing fairness that moves beyond descriptive metrics towards rigorous, target-based testing, offering an approach for more accountable generative AI development by bridging the gap between normative ideals and actionable standards.

Abstract: Text-to-Image (T2I) generation models have been widely adopted across various industries, yet are criticized for frequently exhibiting societal stereotypes. While a growing body of research has emerged to evaluate and mitigate these biases, the field at present contends with conceptual ambiguity, for example terms like "bias" and "fairness" are not always clearly distinguished and often lack clear operational definitions. This paper provides a comprehensive systematic review of T2I fairness literature, organizing existing work into a taxonomy of bias types and fairness notions. We critically assess the gap between "target fairness" (normative ideals in T2I outputs) and "threshold fairness" (normative standards with actionable decision rules). Furthermore, we survey the landscape of mitigation strategies, ranging from prompt engineering to diffusion process manipulation. We conclude by proposing a new framework for operationalizing fairness that moves beyond descriptive metrics towards rigorous, target-based testing, offering an approach for more accountable generative AI development.

</details>


### [33] [SmoGVLM: A Small, Graph-enhanced Vision-Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16517)
*Debjyoti Mondal,Rituraj Singh,Subhadarshi Panda*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SmoGVLM is a small graph-enhanced vision-language model that integrates structured knowledge with visual and textual data using Graph Neural Networks, achieving up to 16.24% performance gains and surpassing larger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large vision-language models often suffer from hallucination and poor grounding in knowledge-intensive reasoning tasks, despite their strong performance on multimodal tasks.

Method: Proposes SmoGVLM, a small graph-enhanced VLM that integrates structured knowledge with visual and textual modalities using Graph Neural Networks. Investigates effects across model sizes from 1.3B to 13B parameters.

Result: Small models trained with this approach achieve performance gains up to 16.24% and surpass larger counterparts, outperforming larger VLMs and strong fine-tuned baselines.

Conclusion: Structured knowledge augmentation shows significant potential for developing efficient, smaller-scale multimodal reasoning systems that can outperform larger models.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong performance on multimodal tasks but often suffer from hallucination and poor grounding in knowledge-intensive reasoning. We propose SmoGVLM, a small, graph-enhanced VLM that integrates structured knowledge with visual and textual modalities, using Graph Neural Networks. We investigate the effects of our method across a range of model sizes, from tiny (1.3B) to large (13B) models. Our results demonstrate that, when trained using our approach, a small model can achieve performance gains upto 16.24%, and surpass its larger counterparts, outperforming larger VLMs and strong fine-tuned baselines. These results highlight the potential of structured knowledge augmentation for efficient, smaller-scale multimodal reasoning systems.

</details>


### [34] [Fast Online 3D Multi-Camera Multi-Object Tracking and Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16522)
*Linh Van Ma,Tran Thien Dat Nguyen,Moongu Jeon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Fast online 3D multi-object tracking and pose estimation using multiple monocular cameras with only 2D detections, no 3D training needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create an efficient 3D tracking and pose estimation system that avoids costly 3D training data and computationally expensive deep learning models, while maintaining real-time performance.

Method: Uses only 2D bounding box and pose detections from multiple monocular cameras, implements efficient Bayes-optimal multi-object tracking filter, leverages publicly available pre-trained 2D detection models.

Result: Significantly faster than state-of-the-art methods without compromising accuracy, robust to camera disconnection/reconnection scenarios during operation.

Conclusion: Proposed method enables efficient 3D multi-object tracking and pose estimation using only 2D detections, eliminating need for 3D training while maintaining accuracy and robustness.

Abstract: This paper proposes a fast and online method for jointly performing 3D multi-object tracking and pose estimation using multiple monocular cameras. Our algorithm requires only 2D bounding box and pose detections, eliminating the need for costly 3D training data or computationally expensive deep learning models. Our solution is an efficient implementation of a Bayes-optimal multi-object tracking filter, enhancing computational efficiency while maintaining accuracy. We demonstrate that our algorithm is significantly faster than state-of-the-art methods without compromising accuracy, using only publicly available pre-trained 2D detection models. We also illustrate the robust performance of our algorithm in scenarios where multiple cameras are intermittently disconnected or reconnected during operation.

</details>


### [35] [Privacy-Preserving Semantic Segmentation without Key Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16523)
*Mare Hirose,Shoko Imaizumi,Hitoshi Kiya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Novel privacy-preserving semantic segmentation method using client-specific encryption keys for each image, with encryption applied during both training and inference to maintain performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable privacy-preserving semantic segmentation where each client can use independent encryption keys for their images, protecting sensitive visual data while maintaining model utility.

Method: Both model creator and clients encrypt images using locally generated keys, with encryption applied during model training (not just inference) to mitigate performance degradation. Uses vision transformer-based SETR model.

Result: Effectiveness confirmed on Cityscapes dataset using SETR model, demonstrating the method works with independent client keys and maintains segmentation performance.

Conclusion: Proposed method enables privacy-preserving semantic segmentation with client-specific encryption while maintaining model performance through encryption during both training and inference phases.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel privacy-preserving semantic segmentation method that can use independent keys for each client and image. In the proposed method, the model creator and each client encrypt images using locally generated keys, and model training and inference are conducted on the encrypted images. To mitigate performance degradation, an image encryption method is applied to model training in addition to the generation of test images. In experiments, the effectiveness of the proposed method is confirmed on the Cityscapes dataset under the use of a vision transformer-based model, called SETR.

</details>


### [36] [Expert-Annotated Embryo Image Dataset with Natural Language Descriptions for Evidence-Based Patient Communication in IVF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16528)
*Nicklas Neu,Thomas Ebner,Jasmin Primus,Bernhard Schenkenfelder,Raphael Zefferer,Mathias Brunbauer,Florian Kromp*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces an expert-annotated dataset of embryo images with natural language descriptions to enable interpretable AI for embryo selection in IVF, addressing current limitations in automated embryo assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI methods for embryo selection have limited impact due to: 1) difficulty adapting to custom clinical data, 2) reliance on time-lapse incubators, 3) lack of interpretability for AI reasoning. Modern patients question expert decisions, especially when treatment fails, creating need for evidence-based decision justification and transparent patient communication.

Method: The authors create an expert-annotated dataset of embryo images paired with natural language descriptions containing information on embryonic cell cycle, developmental stage, and morphological features. This dataset enables fine-tuning of foundational vision-language models to learn and improve over time with high accuracy.

Result: The proposed dataset supports research in language-based, interpretable, and transparent automated embryo assessment. Predicted embryo descriptions can be leveraged to automatically extract scientific evidence from literature, facilitating evidence-based decision-making and transparent patient communication.

Conclusion: This dataset has the potential to significantly enhance the embryo selection decision-making process and improve patient outcomes over time by enabling interpretable AI that supports transparent, evidence-based decision justification in IVF treatment.

Abstract: Embryo selection is one of multiple crucial steps in in-vitro fertilization, commonly based on morphological assessment by clinical embryologists. Although artificial intelligence methods have demonstrated their potential to support embryo selection by automated embryo ranking or grading methods, the overall impact of AI-based solutions is still limited. This is mainly due to the required adaptation of automated solutions to custom clinical data, reliance on time lapse incubators and a lack of interpretability to understand AI reasoning. The modern, informed patient is questioning expert decisions, particularly if the treatment is not successful. Thus, evidence-based decision justification in tasks like embryo selection would support transparent decision making and respectful patient communication. To support this aim, we hereby present an expert-annotated dataset consisting of embryo images and corresponding morphological description using natural language. The description contains relevant information on embryonic cell cycle, developmental stage and morphological features. This dataset enables the finetuning of modern foundational vision-language models to learn and improve over time with high accuracy. Predicted embryo descriptions can then be leveraged to automatically extract scientific evidence from literature, facilitating well-informed, evidence-based decision-making and transparent communication with patients. Our proposed dataset supports research in language-based, interpretable, and transparent automated embryo assessment and has the potential to enhance the decision-making process and improve patient outcomes significantly over time.

</details>


### [37] [Beyond Attack Success Rate: A Multi-Metric Evaluation of Adversarial Transferability in Medical Imaging Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16532)
*Emily Curl,Kofi Ampomah,Md Erfan,Sayanton Dibbo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Current adversarial vulnerability evaluations in medical AI rely too heavily on Attack Success Rate (ASR), which fails to account for perturbation strength, image quality, and cross-architecture transferability, leading to incomplete assessments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As deep learning systems become more prevalent in medical image analysis, their vulnerabilities to adversarial attacks raise serious clinical concerns. The current reliance on ASR as a binary metric provides incomplete interpretation since it ignores factors like perturbation strength, perceptual image quality, and cross-architecture attack transferability. This gap is particularly important as Vision Transformers challenge CNN dominance with different learning mechanisms.

Method: Systematic empirical study on four medical image datasets (PathMNIST, DermaMNIST, RetinaMNIST, CheXpert) evaluating seven models (VGG-16, ResNet-50, DenseNet-121, Inception-v3, DeiT, Swin Transformer, ViT-B/16) against seven attack methods at five perturbation budgets. Measured ASR, PSNR, SSIM, and L2 perturbation magnitude to analyze relationships between metrics.

Result: Perceptual and distortion metrics (PSNR, SSIM, L2) are strongly associated with each other but exhibit minimal correlation with ASR. This pattern holds consistently across both CNNs and Vision Transformers, demonstrating that ASR alone is an inadequate indicator of adversarial robustness and transferability.

Conclusion: A thorough assessment of adversarial risk in medical AI requires multi-metric frameworks that encompass not only attack efficacy (ASR) but also methodology and associated overheads (perturbation strength, perceptual quality, transferability). Single-metric evaluations like ASR are insufficient for comprehensive vulnerability assessment.

Abstract: While deep learning systems are becoming increasingly prevalent in medical image analysis, their vulnerabilities to adversarial perturbations raise serious concerns for clinical deployment. These vulnerability evaluations largely rely on Attack Success Rate (ASR), a binary metric that indicates solely whether an attack is successful. However, the ASR metric does not account for other factors, such as perturbation strength, perceptual image quality, and cross-architecture attack transferability, and therefore, the interpretation is incomplete. This gap requires consideration, as complex, large-scale deep learning systems, including Vision Transformers (ViTs), are increasingly challenging the dominance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). These architectures learn differently, and it is unclear whether a single metric, e.g., ASR, can effectively capture adversarial behavior. To address this, we perform a systematic empirical study on four medical image datasets: PathMNIST, DermaMNIST, RetinaMNIST, and CheXpert. We evaluate seven models (VGG-16, ResNet-50, DenseNet-121, Inception-v3, DeiT, Swin Transformer, and ViT-B/16) against seven attack methods at five perturbation budgets, measuring ASR, Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM), and $L_2$ perturbation magnitude. Our findings show a consistent pattern: perceptual and distortion metrics are strongly associated with one another and exhibit minimal correlation with ASR. This applies to both CNNs and ViTs. The results demonstrate that ASR alone is an inadequate indicator of adversarial robustness and transferability. Consequently, we argue that a thorough assessment of adversarial risk in medical AI necessitates multi-metric frameworks that encompass not only the attack efficacy but also its methodology and associated overheads.

</details>


### [38] [PoInit-of-View: Poisoning Initialization of Views Transfers Across Multiple 3D Reconstruction Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16540)
*Weijie Wang,Songlong Xing,Zhengyu Zhao,Nicu Sebe,Bruno Lepri*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PoInit-of-View is a novel attack method that targets the SfM initialization module in 3D reconstruction systems by introducing cross-view gradient inconsistencies to corrupt pose estimation and triangulation, achieving transferable poisoning effects across different reconstruction systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing poisoning attacks on 3D reconstruction systems backpropagate adversarial gradients through the entire pipeline without targeting specific vulnerable modules. The authors identify that structure-from-motion (SfM) initialization, as the geometric core of many reconstruction systems, presents a new vulnerability that can be exploited for transferable attacks across diverse systems.

Method: PoInit-of-View optimizes adversarial perturbations to intentionally introduce cross-view gradient inconsistencies at projections of corresponding 3D points. These inconsistencies disrupt keypoint detection and feature matching, which corrupts pose estimation and triangulation within SfM initialization. The method includes theoretical analysis connecting cross-view inconsistency to correspondence collapse.

Result: Experimental results show PoInit-of-View is effective across diverse 3D reconstruction systems and datasets. In black-box transfer settings (e.g., 3DGS to NeRF), it surpasses single-view baselines by 25.1% in PSNR and 16.5% in SSIM, demonstrating strong transferability and poisoning effectiveness.

Conclusion: Targeting the SfM initialization module enables transferable poisoning attacks across different 3D reconstruction systems. The proposed PoInit-of-View method effectively corrupts 3D reconstruction by exploiting cross-view gradient inconsistencies, revealing a new vulnerability in reconstruction pipelines that existing attacks overlook.

Abstract: Poisoning input views of 3D reconstruction systems has been recently studied. However, we identify that existing studies simply backpropagate adversarial gradients through the 3D reconstruction pipeline as a whole, without uncovering the new vulnerability rooted in specific modules of the 3D reconstruction pipeline. In this paper, we argue that the structure-from-motion (SfM) initialization, as the geometric core of many widely used reconstruction systems, can be targeted to achieve transferable poisoning effects across diverse 3D reconstruction systems. To this end, we propose PoInit-of-View, which optimizes adversarial perturbations to intentionally introduce cross-view gradient inconsistencies at projections of corresponding 3D points. These inconsistencies disrupt keypoint detection and feature matching, thereby corrupting pose estimation and triangulation within SfM, eventually resulting in low-quality rendered views. We also provide a theoretical analysis that connects cross-view inconsistency to correspondence collapse. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our PoInit-of-View on diverse 3D reconstruction systems and datasets, surpassing the single-view baseline by 25.1% in PSNR and 16.5% in SSIM in black-box transfer settings, such as 3DGS to NeRF.

</details>


### [39] [BOOKAGENT: Orchestrating Safety-Aware Visual Narratives via Multi-Agent Cognitive Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16541)
*Bo Gao,Chang Liu,Yuyang Miao,Siyuan Ma,Ser-Nam Lim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BookAgent is a safety-aware multi-agent framework for generating illustrated storybooks with end-to-end planning, scripting, illustrating, and consistency repair, outperforming existing methods in narrative coherence, visual consistency, and safety compliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current storybook generation methods decompose the task into separate stages, limiting holistic multi-modal grounding. Additionally, existing safety alignment approaches rarely integrate child-specific safety constraints into narrative planning and multi-modal verification.

Method: BookAgent uses a multi-agent collaboration framework for end-to-end storybook synthesis from user drafts. It jointly handles planning, scripting, illustrating, and global inconsistency repair. The system dynamically calibrates page-level alignment between text and visual layouts, and verifies-then-rectifies global inconsistencies in character identity and storytelling logic.

Result: Extensive experiments show BookAgent significantly outperforms current methods in narrative coherence, visual consistency, and safety compliance. The framework offers a robust paradigm for reliable agents in complex multi-modal creation.

Conclusion: BookAgent addresses key limitations in storybook generation by providing an end-to-end, safety-aware framework with dynamic multi-modal grounding and consistency verification, representing an advancement in reliable multi-agent systems for complex creative tasks.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Large Generative Models (LGMs) have revolutionized multi-modal generation. However, generating illustrated storybooks remains an open challenge, where prior works mainly decompose this task into separate stages, and thus, holistic multi-modal grounding remains limited. Besides, while safety alignment is studied for text- or image-only generation, existing works rarely integrate child-specific safety constraints into narrative planning and sequence-level multi-modal verification. To address these limitations, we propose BookAgent, a safety-aware multi-agent collaboration framework designed for high-quality, safety-aware visual narratives. Different from prior story visualization models that assume a fixed storyline sequence, BookAgent targets end-to-end storybook synthesis from a user draft by jointly planning, scripting, illustrating, and globally repairing inconsistencies. To ensure precise multi-modal grounding, BookAgent dynamically calibrates page-level alignment between textual scripts and visual layouts. Furthermore, BookAgent calibrates holistic consistency from the temporal dimension, by verifying-then-rectifying global inconsistencies in character identity and storytelling logic. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BookAgent significantly outperforms current methods in narrative coherence, visual consistency, and safety compliance, offering a robust paradigm for reliable agents in complex multi-modal creation. The implementation will be publicly released at https://github.com/bogao-code/BookAgent/tree/main.

</details>


### [40] [A B-Spline Function Based 3D Point Cloud Unwrapping Scheme for 3D Fingerprint Recognition and Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16546)
*Mohammad Mogharen Askarin,Jiankun Hu,Min Wang,Xuefei Yin,Xiuping Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel method for 3D fingerprint recognition that unwraps 3D point clouds using B-spline curve fitting, converts them to grayscale images, and applies conventional 2D recognition techniques, achieving superior performance over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D fingerprint recognition offers advantages over 2D systems including contactless operation (improved hygiene/security) and richer depth/shape information. However, challenges remain: topological height variations complicate ridge extraction, and registration issues require consistent orientation across samples.

Method: 1) Unwrap 3D fingerprints (represented as point clouds) using B-spline curve fitting to mitigate height variation and reduce registration limitations. 2) Convert unwrapped point cloud to grayscale image by mapping relative heights. 3) Apply conventional 2D fingerprint identification methods to the grayscale image.

Result: Superior performance in 3D fingerprint recognition with EERs of 0.2072%, 0.26%, and 0.22% across three experiments, outperforming existing methods. Also surpassed 3D fingerprint flattening technique in cross-session experiments, achieving EER of 1.50% with varying registrations.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses key challenges in 3D fingerprint recognition by transforming 3D point clouds into 2D representations suitable for conventional recognition, demonstrating significant performance improvements over existing approaches.

Abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) fingerprint recognition and identification offer several advantages over traditional two-dimensional (2D) recognition systems. The contactless nature of 3D fingerprints enhances hygiene and security, reducing the risk of contamination and spoofing. In addition to surface ridge and valley patterns, 3D fingerprints capture depth, curvature, and shape information, enabling the development of more precise and robust authentication systems. Despite recent advancements, significant challenges remain. The topological height of fingerprint pixels complicates the extraction of ridge and valley patterns. Furthermore, registration issues limit the acquisition process, requiring consistent direction and orientation across all samples. To address these challenges, this paper introduces a method that unwraps 3D fingerprints, represented as 3D point clouds, using B-spline curve fitting to mitigate height variation and reduce registration limitations. The unwrapped point cloud is then converted into a grayscale image by mapping the relative heights of the points. This grayscale image is subsequently used for recognition through conventional 2D fingerprint identification methods. The proposed approach demonstrated superior performance in 3D fingerprint recognition, achieving Equal Error Rates (EERs) of 0.2072%, 0.26%, and 0.22% across three experiments, outperforming existing methods. Additionally, the method surpassed 3D fingerprint flattening technique in both recognition and identification during cross-session experiments, achieving an EER of 1.50% when fingerprints with varying registrations were included.

</details>


### [41] [Co-generation of Layout and Shape from Text via Autoregressive 3D Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16552)
*Zhenggang Tang,Yuehao Wang,Yuchen Fan,Jun-Kun Chen,Yu-Ying Yeh,Kihyuk Sohn,Zhangyang Wang,Qixing Huang,Alexander Schwing,Rakesh Ranjan,Dilin Wang,Zhicheng Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 3D-ARD+: A sequential text-to-scene generation model that creates detailed 3D scenes with objects following complex spatial layouts and semantics from text instructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing text-to-scene methods either generate only layouts or only objects, produce overly simple scenes even with LLMs, and often fail to match non-trivial text descriptions of shape, appearance, and spatial arrangements.

Method: Proposes 3D-ARD+, a 3D Autoregressive Diffusion model that unifies autoregressive generation over multimodal tokens with diffusion generation of 3D latents. It generates scenes sequentially: first coarse-grained 3D latents in scene space, then fine-grained object geometry/appearance in object space.

Result: Trained on 230K indoor scenes with text instructions. The 7B parameter model generates and places objects following non-trivial spatial layouts and semantics prescribed by text instructions.

Conclusion: Introduces a new sequential text-to-scene generation paradigm that successfully creates detailed 3D scenes matching complex text descriptions through unified autoregressive-diffusion modeling.

Abstract: Recent text-to-scene generation approaches largely reduced the manual efforts required to create 3D scenes. However, their focus is either to generate a scene layout or to generate objects, and few generate both. The generated scene layout is often simple even with LLM's help. Moreover, the generated scene is often inconsistent with the text input that contains non-trivial descriptions of the shape, appearance, and spatial arrangement of the objects. We present a new paradigm of sequential text-to-scene generation and propose a novel generative model for interactive scene creation. At the core is a 3D Autoregressive Diffusion model 3D-ARD+, which unifies the autoregressive generation over a multimodal token sequence and diffusion generation of next-object 3D latents. To generate the next object, the model uses one autoregressive step to generate the coarse-grained 3D latents in the scene space, conditioned on both the current seen text instructions and already synthesized 3D scene. It then uses a second step to generate the 3D latents in the smaller object space, which can be decoded into fine-grained object geometry and appearance. We curate a large dataset of 230K indoor scenes with paired text instructions for training. We evaluate 7B 3D-ARD+, on challenging scenes, and showcase the model can generate and place objects following non-trivial spatial layout and semantics prescribed by the text instructions.

</details>


### [42] [PA-TCNet: Pathology-Aware Temporal Calibration with Physiology-Guided Target Refinement for Cross-Subject Motor Imagery EEG Decoding in Stroke Patients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16554)
*Xiangkai Wang,Yun Zhao,Dongyi He,Qingling Xia,Gen Li,Nizhuan Wang,Ningxiao Peng,Bin Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PA-TCNet is a pathology-aware temporal calibration framework for stroke patient EEG decoding that addresses lesion-related abnormal temporal dynamics and inter-patient heterogeneity through rhythmic state decomposition and physiology-guided target refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stroke patient EEG decoding for motor imagery BCI faces challenges due to lesion-related abnormal temporal dynamics and inter-patient heterogeneity, which undermine generalization. Existing adaptation methods are misled by pathological slow-wave activity and unstable target-domain pseudo-labels.

Method: PA-TCNet integrates two components: 1) Pathology-aware Rhythmic State Mamba (PRSM) decomposes EEG features into slow rhythmic context and fast transient perturbations, injecting pathological context into state propagation; 2) Physiology-Guided Target Calibration (PGTC) constructs source-domain sensorimotor region templates and imposes physiological consistency constraints to refine target-domain pseudo-labels.

Result: Leave-one-subject-out experiments on XW-Stroke and 2019-Stroke datasets achieved mean accuracies of 66.56% and 72.75%, respectively, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling pathological temporal dynamics with physiology-constrained pseudo-supervision provides robust cross-subject initialization for personalized post-stroke MI-BCI rehabilitation.

Abstract: Stroke patient cross-subject electroencephalography (EEG) decoding of motor imagery (MI) brain-computer interface (BCI) is essential for motor rehabilitation, yet lesion-related abnormal temporal dynamics and pronounced inter-patient heterogeneity often undermine generalization. Existing adaptation methods are easily misled by pathological slow-wave activity and unstable target-domain pseudo-labels. To address this challenge, we propose PA-TCNet, a pathology-aware temporal calibration framework with physiology-guided target refinement for stroke motor imagery decoding. PA-TCNet integrates two coordinated components. The Pathology-aware Rhythmic State Mamba (PRSM) module decomposes EEG spatiotemporal features into slowly varying rhythmic context and fast transient perturbations, injecting the fused pathological context into selective state propagation to more effectively capture abnormal temporal dynamics. The Physiology-Guided Target Calibration (PGTC) module constructs source-domain sensorimotor region-of-interest templates, imposing physiological consistency constraints and dynamically refining target-domain pseudo-labels, thereby improving adaptation reliability. Leave-one-subject-out experiments on two independent stroke EEG datasets, XW-Stroke and 2019-Stroke, yielded mean accuracies of 66.56\% and 72.75\%, respectively, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. These results indicate that jointly modeling pathological temporal dynamics and physiology-constrained pseudo-supervision can provide more robust cross-subject initialization for personalized post-stroke MI-BCI rehabilitation. The implemented code is available at https://github.com/wxk1224/PA-TCNet.

</details>


### [43] [See Through the Noise: Improving Domain Generalization in Gaze Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16562)
*Yanming Peng,Shijing Wang,Yaping Huang,Yi Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SeeTN framework improves gaze estimation generalization by addressing label noise through semantic embedding space construction and affinity regularization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing gaze estimation methods overlook label noise effects on generalization, despite the inherent difficulty of obtaining precise gaze annotations in real-world applications.

Method: Constructs semantic embedding space via prototype-based transformation to preserve topological structure between gaze features and labels. Measures feature-label affinity consistency to distinguish noisy/clean samples, and introduces affinity regularization in semantic manifold to transfer gaze-related information from clean to noisy samples.

Result: Extensive experiments show SeeTN effectively mitigates source-domain noise impact, achieving superior cross-domain generalization without compromising source-domain accuracy.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates importance of explicitly handling label noise in generalized gaze estimation, promoting semantic structure alignment and domain-invariant gaze relationships for improved robustness.

Abstract: Generalizable gaze estimation methods have garnered increasing attention due to their critical importance in real-world applications and have achieved significant progress. However, they often overlook the effect of label noise, arising from the inherent difficulty of acquiring precise gaze annotations, on model generalization performance. In this paper, we are the first to comprehensively investigate the negative effects of label noise on generalization in gaze estimation. Further, we propose a novel solution, called See-Through-Noise (SeeTN) framework, which improves generalization from a novel perspective of mitigating label noise. Specifically, we propose to construct a semantic embedding space via a prototype-based transformation to preserve a consistent topological structure between gaze features and continuous labels. We then measure feature-label affinity consistency to distinguish noisy from clean samples, and introduce a novel affinity regularization in the semantic manifold to transfer gaze-related information from clean to noisy samples. Our proposed SeeTN promotes semantic structure alignment and enforces domain-invariant gaze relationships, thereby enhancing robustness against label noise. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our SeeTN effectively mitigates the adverse impact of source-domain noise, leading to superior cross-domain generalization without compromising the source-domain accuracy, and highlight the importance of explicitly handling noise in generalized gaze estimation.

</details>


### [44] [Classification of systolic murmurs in heart sounds using multiresolution complex Gabor dictionary and vision transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16563)
*Mahmoud Fakhry,Abeer FathAllah Brery*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Automatic systolic murmur classification system using multiresolution complex Gabor basis functions for feature extraction and vision transformer for classification, achieving 95.96% accuracy on CirCor DigiScope dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systolic murmurs indicate heart abnormalities but vary in intensity, pitch, and quality, requiring precise identification for accurate cardiac disorder diagnosis. Manual classification is challenging due to this variability.

Method: Two-stage approach: 1) Feature extraction using complex orthogonal matching pursuit with multiresolution complex Gabor basis functions to create variable-resolution time-frequency feature matrices from murmur segments. 2) Classification using vision transformer with CNN patch tokenization, multihead attention, and residual connections to process multiple resolution matrices.

Result: Achieved 95.96% classification accuracy for four types of systolic murmurs on the CirCor DigiScope dataset, demonstrating effective integration of multiresolution feature extraction with transformer-based classification.

Conclusion: The proposed system successfully combines advanced signal processing (complex Gabor basis functions) with modern deep learning (vision transformer) to accurately classify systolic murmurs, addressing variability challenges through shared dictionary learning and multiresolution analysis.

Abstract: Systolic murmurs are extra heart sounds that occur during the contraction phase of the cardiac cycle, often indicating heart abnormalities caused by turbulent blood flow. Their intensity, pitch, and quality vary, requiring precise identification for the accurate diagnosis of cardiac disorders. This study presents an automatic classification system for systolic murmurs using a feature extraction module, followed by a classification model. The feature extraction module employs complex orthogonal matching pursuit to project single or multiple murmur segments onto a redundant dictionary composed of multiresolution complex Gabor basis functions (GBFs). The resulting projection weights are split and reshaped into variable-resolution time--frequency feature matrices. Processing multiple segments of a single recording using a shared dictionary mitigates murmur variability. This is achieved by learning the weights for each segment while enforcing that they correspond to the same set of basis functions in the dictionary, promoting consistent time--frequency feature matrices. The classification model is built based on a vision transformer to process multiple input matrices of different resolutions by passing each through a convolutional neural network for patch tokenization. All embedding tokens are then concatenated to form a matrix and forwarded to an encoder layer that includes multihead attention, residual connections, and a convolutional network with a kernel size of one. This integration of multiresolution feature extraction with transformer-based feature classification enhances the accuracy and reliability of heart murmur identification. An experimental analysis of four types of systolic murmurs from the CirCor DigiScope dataset demonstrates the effectiveness of the system, achieving a classification accuracy of $95.96\%$.

</details>


### [45] [Multilevel neural networks with dual-stage feature fusion for human activity recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16577)
*Abeer FathAllah Brery,Ascensión Gallardo-Antolín,Israel Gonzalez-Carrasco,Mahmoud Fakhry*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel two-level network architecture with dual-stage feature fusion (late + intermediate) for human activity recognition outperforms single fusion methods and baseline models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an optimal multilevel hybrid model for human activity recognition by strategically integrating multiple neural networks to leverage their complementary strengths, with a focus on the structural arrangement of components.

Method: Proposed a two-level network architecture with dual-stage feature fusion: late fusion (combining outputs from first level) and intermediate fusion (integrating features from both first and second levels). Evaluated 15 different architectures of CNNs, LSTMs, and convolutional LSTMs with various fusion configurations.

Result: Architectures incorporating both late and intermediate fusion achieve higher accuracy than those using late fusion alone. The optimal configuration outperforms baseline models on two public benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed two-level architecture with dual-stage feature fusion is effective for HAR, demonstrating that strategic integration of multiple networks with proper structural arrangement significantly improves recognition performance.

Abstract: Human activity recognition (HAR) refers to the process of identifying human actions and activities using data collected from sensors. Neural networks, such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs), long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, convolutional LSTM, and their hybrid combinations, have demonstrated exceptional performance in various research domains. Developing a multilevel individual or hybrid model for HAR involves strategically integrating multiple networks to capitalize on their complementary strengths. The structural arrangement of these components is a critical factor influencing the overall performance. This study explores a novel framework of a two-level network architecture with dual-stage feature fusion: late fusion, which combines the outputs from the first network level, and intermediate fusion, which integrates the features from both the first and second levels. We evaluated $15$ different network architectures of CNNs, LSTMs, and convolutional LSTMs, incorporating late fusion with and without intermediate fusion, to identify the optimal configuration. Experimental evaluation on two public benchmark datasets demonstrates that architectures incorporating both late and intermediate fusion achieve higher accuracy than those relying on late fusion alone. Moreover, the optimal configuration outperforms baseline models, thereby validating its effectiveness for HAR.

</details>


### [46] [Camo-M3FD: A New Benchmark Dataset for Cross-Spectral Camouflaged Pedestrian Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16582)
*Henry O. Velesaca,Andrea Mero,Guillermo A. Castillo,Angel D. Sappa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Camo-M3FD, a new benchmark for cross-spectral camouflaged pedestrian detection using registered visible-thermal image pairs with high foreground-background similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pedestrian detection remains challenging due to occlusions and poor visibility. While multispectral detection helps with visibility issues, camouflaged pedestrians (where targets blend into surroundings) are largely unexplored, especially in safety-critical human detection applications.

Method: Created Camo-M3FD benchmark derived from M3FD dataset, consisting of registered visible-thermal image pairs curated using quantitative metrics to ensure high foreground-background similarity. Provided pixel-level masks and established standardized evaluation framework using state-of-the-art COD models.

Result: Results show thermal signals provide essential localization cues, but multispectral fusion is necessary for refining structural details in camouflaged pedestrian detection.

Conclusion: Camo-M3FD serves as a foundational resource for developing robust safety-critical detection systems, addressing the gap in camouflaged human detection benchmarks.

Abstract: Pedestrian detection is fundamental to autonomous driving, robotics, and surveillance. Despite progress in deep learning, reliable identification remains challenging due to occlusions, cluttered backgrounds, and degraded visibility. While multispectral detection-combining visible and thermal sensors-mitigates poor visibility, the challenge of camouflaged pedestrians remains largely unexplored. Existing Camouflaged Object Detection (COD) benchmarks focus on biological species, leaving a gap in safety-critical human detection where targets blend into their surroundings. To address this, we introduce Camo-M3FD (derived from the M3FD dataset), a novel benchmark for cross-spectral camouflaged pedestrian detection, consisting of registered visible-thermal image pairs. The dataset is curated using quantitative metrics to ensure high foreground-background similarity. We provide high-quality pixel-level masks and establish a standardized evaluation framework using state-of-the-art COD models. Our results demonstrate that while thermal signals provide indispensable localization cues, multispectral fusion is essential for refining structural details. Camo-M3FD serves as a foundational resource for developing robust and safety-critical detection systems. The dataset is available on GitHub: https://cod-espol.github.io/Camo-M3FD/

</details>


### [47] [Real-Time Visual Attribution Streaming in Thinking Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16587)
*Seil Kang,Woojung Han,Junhyeok Kim,Jinyeong Kim,Youngeun Kim,Seong Jae Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Amortized framework enables real-time visual attribution streaming in multimodal thinking models, achieving faithfulness comparable to exhaustive causal methods without costly repeated computations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal thinking models (generating code from screenshots, solving math from images) need visual grounding evidence, but current methods are either too slow (faithful causal methods require repeated backward passes) or lack validity (raw attention maps). Need real-time, faithful attribution.

Method: Introduce amortized approach that learns to estimate causal effects of semantic regions directly from attention features. Uses lightweight learning instead of brute-force computation.

Result: Across five diverse benchmarks and four thinking models, achieves faithfulness comparable to exhaustive causal methods while enabling visual attribution streaming (users see grounding evidence as model reasons).

Conclusion: Real-time, faithful attribution in multimodal thinking models is achievable through lightweight learning, enabling visual attribution streaming where users observe grounding evidence during reasoning.

Abstract: We present an amortized framework for real-time visual attribution streaming in multimodal thinking models. When these models generate code from a screenshot or solve math problems from images, their long reasoning traces should be grounded in visual evidence. However, verifying this reliance is challenging: faithful causal methods require costly repeated backward passes or perturbations, while raw attention maps offer instant access, they lack causal validity. To resolve this, we introduce an amortized approach that learns to estimate the causal effects of semantic regions directly from the rich signals encoded in attention features. Across five diverse benchmarks and four thinking models, our approach achieves faithfulness comparable to exhaustive causal methods while enabling visual attribution streaming, where users observe grounding evidence as the model reasons, not after. Our results demonstrate that real-time, faithful attribution in multimodal thinking models is achievable through lightweight learning, not brute-force computation.

</details>


### [48] [MambaKick: Early Penalty Direction Prediction from HAR Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16588)
*Henry O. Velesaca,David Freire-Obregon,Abel Reyes-Angulo,Steven Araujo,Angel Sappa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MambaKick is a learning-based framework that uses pretrained human action recognition embeddings with Mamba temporal modeling to predict penalty kick direction from short video segments, achieving up to 64.5% accuracy for binary classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Penalty kicks in soccer require goalkeepers to anticipate shot direction under extreme time constraints. Current approaches often rely on explicit kinematic reconstruction or handcrafted biomechanical features, which may be inefficient or limited. The paper aims to develop a more practical approach using transferable representations for low-latency intention prediction.

Method: MambaKick leverages pretrained human action recognition (HAR) embeddings extracted from contact-centered short video segments around ball contact. It combines these with a lightweight temporal predictor using selective state-space models (Mamba) for efficient sequence aggregation. Simple contextual metadata (field side and footedness) are also incorporated as complementary cues to reduce ambiguity in real-world footage.

Result: Across various HAR backbones, MambaKick consistently improves or matches strong embedding baselines, achieving up to 53.1% accuracy for three-class prediction and 64.5% accuracy for two-class prediction under the proposed methodology.

Conclusion: Combining pretrained HAR representations with efficient state-space temporal modeling (Mamba) provides a practical direction for low-latency intention prediction in real-world sports video, offering a viable alternative to explicit kinematic reconstruction or handcrafted biomechanical features.

Abstract: Penalty kicks in soccer are decided under extreme time constraints, where goalkeepers benefit from anticipating shot direction from the kickers motion before or around ball contact. In this paper, MambaKick is presented as a learning-based framework for penalty direction prediction that leverages pretrained human action recognition (HAR) embeddings extracted from contact-centered short video segments and combines them with a lightweight temporal predictor. Rather than relying on explicit kinematic reconstruction or handcrafted biomechanical features, the approach reuses transferable spatiotemporal representations and utilizes selective state-spare models (Mamba) for efficient sequence aggregation. Simple contextual metadata (e.g., field side and footedness) are also considered as complementary cues that may reduce ambiguity in real-world footage. Across a range of HAR backbones, MambaKick consistently improves or matches strong embedding baselines, achieving up to 53.1% accuracy for three classes and 64.5% for two classes under the proposed methodology. Overall, the results indicate that combining pretrained HAR representations with efficient state-space temporal modeling is a practical direction for low-latency intention prediction in real-world sports video. The code will be available at GitHub: https://github.com/hvelesaca/MambaKick/

</details>


### [49] [IncepDeHazeGAN: Novel Satellite Image Dehazing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16609)
*Tejeswar Pokuri,Shivarth Rai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IncepDeHazeGAN: A novel GAN with Inception blocks and multi-layer feature fusion for single-image dehazing, achieving state-of-the-art results with explainable AI analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dehazing is crucial for enhancing visual quality of images captured in cloudy/foggy conditions, especially for remote sensing data. Existing methods need improvement in feature extraction and reuse for better dehazing performance.

Method: Proposes IncepDeHazeGAN - a Generative Adversarial Network incorporating Inception blocks for multi-scale feature extraction and multi-layer feature fusion for efficient feature reuse. Uses Grad-CAM XAI technique to analyze network focus regions.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on several datasets. Grad-CAM analysis shows the network effectively focuses on relevant regions for dehazing and adapts to different haze conditions.

Conclusion: IncepDeHazeGAN successfully addresses single-image dehazing with improved feature extraction and reuse, demonstrating superior performance and providing explainable insights into its dehazing process.

Abstract: Dehazing is a technique in computer vision for enhancing the visual quality of images captured in cloudy or foggy conditions. Dehazing helps to recover clear, high-quality images from haze-affected remote sensing data. In this study, we introduce IncepDeHazeGAN, a novel Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) involving Inception block and multi-layer feature fusion for the task of single-image dehazing. Utilizing the Inception block allows for multi-scale feature extraction. On the other hand, the multi-layer feature fusion design achieves efficient reuse of features as the features extracted at different convolution layers are fused several times. Grad-CAM XAI technique has been applied to our network, highlighting the regions focused on by the network for dehazing and its adaptation to different haze conditions. Experiments demonstrate that our network achieves state-of-the-art results in several datasets.

</details>


### [50] [AVRT: Audio-Visual Reasoning Transfer through Single-Modality Teachers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16617)
*Edson Araujo,Saurabhchand Bhati,M. Jehanzeb Mirza,Brian Kingsbury,Samuel Thomas,Rogerio Feris,James R. Glass,Hilde Kuehne*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AVRT is a framework that generates high-quality audio-visual reasoning traces from single-modality teacher models, enabling effective multimodal reasoning training through supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While reasoning models have advanced in text domains, transferring these capabilities to multimodal settings (audio-visual) remains challenging due to limited availability of high-quality multimodal reasoning data.

Method: Generate independent vision- and audio-reasoning traces using modality-specialized models, merge them with an LLM merger model, use the multimodal traces for supervised fine-tuning (cold start), then train with reinforcement learning on larger-scale data.

Result: 3B and 7B parameter models achieve state-of-the-art results on seven audio-visual and audio benchmarks, outperforming comparable models like OmniBench, DailyOmni, and MMAR, with cross-modal training transferring to single-modality tasks.

Conclusion: AVRT establishes a new training pipeline for multimodal reasoning models that effectively addresses the data scarcity problem in audio-visual reasoning through trace generation and two-stage training.

Abstract: Recent advances in reasoning models have shown remarkable progress in text-based domains, but transferring those capabilities to multimodal settings, e.g., to allow reasoning over audio-visual data, still remains a challenge, in part because of the limited availability of high-quality reasoning data in targeted multimodal combinations. To address this problem, we introduce AVRT, a novel framework that generates high-quality audio-visual reasoning traces from single-modality teacher models. We generate independent vision- and audio-reasoning traces via models specialized to reason over their respective modalities and merge the resulting traces with an LLM merger model. The resulting multimodal traces are used in a supervised fine-tuning (SFT) cold start to adapt the target model to audio-visual reasoning traces first, before training it in a second reinforcement learning stage on larger-scale data. Evaluated on seven audio-visual and audio benchmarks, our 3B and 7B parameter models achieve state-of-the-art results among models of comparable size including OmniBench and DailyOmni for audio-visual and MMAR for audio-only reasoning, showing that cross-modal training also transfers to single-modality tasks and establishing a new training pipeline for multimodal reasoning models.

</details>


### [51] [Amortized Inverse Kinematics via Graph Attention for Real-Time Human Avatar Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16629)
*Muhammad Saif Ullah Khan,Chen-Yu Wang,Tim Prokosch,Michael Lorenz,Bertram Taetz,Didier Stricker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IK-GAT is a lightweight graph-attention network that reconstructs full-body joint orientations from 3D joint positions in a single forward pass, outperforming traditional iterative IK solvers in speed and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional inverse kinematics (IK) solvers for recovering joint orientations from sparse 3D joint positions are slow, iterative, and sensitive to noise. The problem is underconstrained due to twist ambiguity, making real-time human avatar animation challenging.

Method: IK-GAT uses graph-attention networks to perform message passing over the skeletal parent-child graph. It predicts rotations in a bone-aligned world-frame representation anchored to rest-pose frames, uses continuous 6D rotation representation, and is trained with geodesic loss on SO(3) plus optional forward-kinematics consistency regularization.

Result: The model has only 374K parameters and achieves over 650 FPS on CPU, outperforming VPoser-based iterative optimization without warm-start. It produces animation-ready local rotations robust to initial pose and input noise.

Conclusion: IK-GAT provides a fast, robust solution for real-time orientation reconstruction from sparse joint positions, enabling direct driving of rigged avatars and conversion to SMPL-like body model parameters for online applications.

Abstract: Inverse kinematics (IK) is a core operation in animation, robotics, and biomechanics: given Cartesian constraints, recover joint rotations under a known kinematic tree. In many real-time human avatar pipelines, the available signal per frame is a sparse set of tracked 3D joint positions, whereas animation systems require joint orientations to drive skinning. Recovering full orientations from positions is underconstrained, most notably because twist about bone axes is ambiguous, and classical IK solvers typically rely on iterative optimization that can be slow and sensitive to noisy inputs. We introduce IK-GAT, a lightweight graph-attention network that reconstructs full-body joint orientations from 3D joint positions in a single forward pass. The model performs message passing over the skeletal parent-child graph to exploit kinematic structure during rotation inference. To simplify learning, IK-GAT predicts rotations in a bone-aligned world-frame representation anchored to rest-pose bone frames. This parameterization makes the twist axis explicit and is exactly invertible to standard parent-relative local rotations given the kinematic tree and rest pose. The network uses a continuous 6D rotation representation and is trained with a geodesic loss on SO(3) together with an optional forward-kinematics consistency regularizer. IK-GAT produces animation-ready local rotations that can directly drive a rigged avatar or be converted to pose parameters of SMPL-like body models for real-time and online applications. With 374K parameters and over 650 FPS on CPU, IK-GAT outperforms VPoser-based per-frame iterative optimization without warm-start at significantly lower cost, and is robust to initial pose and input noise

</details>


### [52] [Tri-Modal Fusion Transformers for UAV-based Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16630)
*Craig Iaboni,Pramod Abichandani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Tri-modal RGB-thermal-event fusion framework for UAV object detection with Modality-Aware Gated Exchange (MAGE) and Bidirectional Token Exchange (BiTE) modules, evaluated on new 10,489-frame dataset showing improved performance over dual-modal baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAV object detection needs robustness to illumination changes, motion blur, and dynamic scenes where RGB cues fail. Thermal LWIR preserves contrast in low light, event cameras capture microsecond temporal edges, but integrating all three modalities hasn't been systematically studied.

Method: Dual-stream hierarchical vision transformer processes RGB, thermal, and event data. Uses Modality-Aware Gated Exchange (MAGE) for inter-sensor channel/spatial gating and Bidirectional Token Exchange (BiTE) for bidirectional token-level attention with depthwise-pointwise refinement, producing fused maps for feature pyramid and two-stage detector.

Result: Tri-modal fusion outperforms all dual-modal baselines. Fusion depth significantly affects performance. Lightweight CSSA variant recovers most benefits at minimal cost. 61 controlled ablations evaluate fusion placement, mechanisms, modality subsets, and backbone capacity.

Conclusion: First systematic benchmark and modular backbone for tri-modal UAV-based object detection. Provides comprehensive evaluation framework and demonstrates effectiveness of RGB-thermal-event fusion for robust UAV perception.

Abstract: Reliable UAV object detection requires robustness to illumination changes, motion blur, and scene dynamics that suppress RGB cues. Thermal long-wave infrared (LWIR) sensing preserves contrast in low light, and event cameras retain microsecond-level temporal edges, but integrating all three modalities in a unified detector has not been systematically studied. We present a tri-modal framework that processes RGB, thermal, and event data with a dual-stream hierarchical vision transformer. At selected encoder depths, a Modality-Aware Gated Exchange (MAGE) applies inter-sensor channel and spatial gating, and a Bidirectional Token Exchange (BiTE) module performs bidirectional token-level attention with depthwise-pointwise refinement, producing resolution-preserving fused maps for a standard feature pyramid and two-stage detector.
  We introduce a 10,489-frame UAV dataset with synchronized and pre-aligned RGB-thermal-event streams and 24,223 annotated vehicles across day and night flights. Through 61 controlled ablations, we evaluate fusion placement, mechanism (baseline MAGE+BiTE, CSSA, GAFF), modality subsets, and backbone capacity. Tri-modal fusion improves over all dual-modal baselines, with fusion depth having a significant effect and a lightweight CSSA variant recovering most of the benefit at minimal cost. This work provides the first systematic benchmark and modular backbone for tri-modal UAV-based object detection.

</details>


### [53] [A Benchmark Study of Segmentation Models and Adaptation Strategies for Landslide Detection from Satellite Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16663)
*Md Kowsher,Weiwei Zhan,Chen Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Systematic benchmarking of CNN, transformer, and foundation models for landslide detection using GDCLD dataset, comparing full vs parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA/AdaLoRA).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's insufficient understanding of the relative effectiveness of modern segmentation architectures and fine-tuning strategies for landslide detection from high-resolution satellite imagery, which is critical for disaster response and risk assessment.

Method: Systematic benchmarking study comparing CNN-based segmentation models, transformer-based segmentation models, and large pre-trained foundation models using the Globally Distributed Coseismic Landslide Dataset (GDCLD). Evaluation includes consistent training protocols and comparison of full fine-tuning vs parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods (LoRA and AdaLoRA).

Result: Transformer-based models achieve strong segmentation performance. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning reduces trainable parameters by up to 95% while maintaining comparable accuracy to full fine-tuning. The study also analyzes generalization under distribution shift by comparing validation and held-out test performance.

Conclusion: The systematic benchmarking provides insights into effective segmentation architectures and fine-tuning strategies for landslide detection, showing that transformer models perform well and parameter-efficient methods offer significant efficiency gains with minimal accuracy loss.

Abstract: Landslide detection from high resolution satellite imagery is a critical task for disaster response and risk assessment, yet the relative effectiveness of modern segmentation architectures and finetuning strategies for this problem remains insufficiently understood. In this work, we present a systematic benchmarking study of convolutional neural networks, transformer based segmentation models, and large pre-trained foundation models for landslide detection. Using the Globally Distributed Coseismic Landslide Dataset (GDCLD) dataset, we evaluate representative CNN- and transformer-based segmentation models alongside large pretrained foundation models under consistent training and evaluation protocols. In addition, we compare full fine-tuning with parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, including LoRA and AdaLoRA, to assess their performance efficiency tradeoffs. Experimental results show that transformer-based models achieve strong segmentation performance, while parameter efficient finetuning reduces trainable parameters by up to 95% with comparable accuracy to full finetuning. We further analyze generalization under distribution shift by comparing validation and held-out test performance.

</details>


### [54] [Appearance-free Action Recognition: Zero-shot Generalization in Humans and a Two-Pathway Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16675)
*Prerana Kumar,Martin A. Giese*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Humans and a novel two-stream 3D CNN model both generalize zero-shot to appearance-free action videos, showing motion-based representations are crucial for this generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether humans and vision models can generalize zero-shot to appearance-free transformations of real-world action videos, without explicit training on such degraded stimuli.

Method: 1) Human psychophysics experiment: 22 participants trained on naturalistic videos (UCF5) and tested zero-shot on two appearance-free transformations (AFD5 dense-noise and random-dot videos). 2) Computational modeling: Developed a two-pathway 3D CNN combining RGB (form) and optical flow (motion) streams with coherence-gating inspired by Gestalt common-fate grouping.

Result: Humans recognize actions in appearance-free videos well above chance (though reduced accuracy vs. naturalistic videos). The two-stream model generalizes to both appearance-free datasets, outperforms contemporary video models, and narrows the human-performance gap. Motion pathway is critical for appearance-free generalization; form pathway improves naturalistic video performance.

Conclusion: Motion-based representations are essential for generalization to appearance-free videos, supporting multi-stream architectures for modeling video-based action recognition and highlighting the importance of motion cues in human action perception.

Abstract: Action recognition is a fundamental ability for social species. Yet, its underlying computations are not well understood. Classical psychophysical studies using simplified stimuli have shown that humans can perceive body motion even under degradation of relevant shape cues. Recent work using real-world action videos and their appearance-free counterparts (that preserve motion but lack static shape cues) included explicit training of humans and models on the appearance-free videos. Whether humans and vision models generalize in a zero-shot manner to appearance-free transformations of real-world action videos is not yet known. To measure this generalization in humans, we conducted a laboratory-based psychophysics experiment. 22 participants were trained to recognize five action categories using naturalistic videos (UCF5 dataset), and tested zero-shot on two types of appearance-free transformations: (i) dense-noise motion videos from an existing dataset (AFD5) and (ii) random-dot appearance-free videos. We find that participants recognize actions in both types of appearance-free videos well above chance, albeit with reduced accuracy compared to naturalistic videos. To model this behavior, we developed a two-pathway 3D CNN-based model combining an RGB (form) stream and an optical flow (motion) stream, including a coherence-gating mechanism inspired by Gestalt common-fate grouping. Our model generalizes to both appearance-free datasets and outperforms contemporary video classification models, narrowing the gap to human performance. We find that the motion pathway is critical for generalization to appearance-free videos, while the form pathway improves performance on naturalistic videos. Our findings highlight the importance of motion-based representations for generalization to appearance-free videos, and support the use of multi-stream architectures to model video-based action recognition.

</details>


### [55] [C-GenReg: Training-Free 3D Point Cloud Registration by Multi-View-Consistent Geometry-to-Image Generation with Probabilistic Modalities Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16680)
*Yuval Haitman,Amit Efraim,Joseph M. Francos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: C-GenReg is a training-free 3D point cloud registration framework that combines world-scale generative priors with Vision Foundation Models to achieve robust cross-domain generalization without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning-based 3D point cloud registration methods struggle with generalization across different sensing modalities, sampling variations, and environmental conditions, limiting their practical applicability in real-world scenarios.

Method: The framework transfers the 3D matching problem to an auxiliary image domain using a World Foundation Model to generate multi-view-consistent RGB representations from input geometry. A Vision Foundation Model extracts dense correspondences from these generated views, which are then lifted back to 3D. A "Match-then-Fuse" probabilistic cold-fusion scheme combines correspondences from the generated-RGB branch with the raw geometric branch.

Result: Extensive experiments on indoor (3DMatch, ScanNet) and outdoor (Waymo) benchmarks demonstrate strong zero-shot performance and superior cross-domain generalization. The framework successfully operates on real outdoor LiDAR data without requiring any imagery data.

Conclusion: C-GenReg presents the first generative registration framework that effectively handles real outdoor LiDAR data, offering a plug-and-play, zero-shot solution that preserves modality-specific inductive biases while providing calibrated confidence without additional learning.

Abstract: We introduce C-GenReg, a training-free framework for 3D point cloud registration that leverages the complementary strengths of world-scale generative priors and registration-oriented Vision Foundation Models (VFMs). Current learning-based 3D point cloud registration methods struggle to generalize across sensing modalities, sampling differences, and environments. Hence, C-GenReg augments the geometric point cloud registration branch by transferring the matching problem into an auxiliary image domain, where VFMs excel, using a World Foundation Model to synthesize multi-view-consistent RGB representations from the input geometry. This generative transfer, preserves spatial coherence across source and target views without any fine-tuning. From these generated views, a VFM pretrained for finding dense correspondences extracts matches. The resulting pixel correspondences are lifted back to 3D via the original depth maps. To further enhance robustness, we introduce a "Match-then-Fuse" probabilistic cold-fusion scheme that combines two independent correspondence posteriors, that of the generated-RGB branch with that of the raw geometric branch. This principled fusion preserves each modality inductive bias and provides calibrated confidence without any additional learning. C-GenReg is zero-shot and plug-and-play: all modules are pretrained and operate without fine-tuning.
