It synergistically combines integrated geometric prior (IGP) with gated spatial modulation (GSM) and centerline-aware topology (CAT) loss supervision, ensuring structural continuity. (ii) An adaptive noise module (ANM) within our coronary background restoration network (CBResNet). Unlike standard restoration, ANM uniquely models the stochastic nature of clinical X-ray noise, bridging the domain gap to enable seamless background intensity estimation and the complete elimination of boundary artifacts. The final subtraction is obtained by removing the restored background from the raw angiogram. Quantitatively, it significantly outperformed state-of-the-art methods in vascular intensity correlation and perceptual quality. A 25.6% improvement in morphology assessment efficiency and a 42.9% gain in hemodynamic evaluation speed set a new benchmark for utility in interventional cardiology, while maintaining diagnostic results consistent with raw angiograms. The project code is available at https://github.com/DrThink-ai/CDSA-Net.

</details>


### [136] [EmbodiedHead: Real-Time Listening and Speaking Avatar for Conversational Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17211)
*Yu Zhang,Kaiyuan Shen,Yang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EmbodiedHead is a real-time speech-driven talking-head framework that uses Rectified-Flow Diffusion Transformer with differentiable renderer for high-quality avatar generation in just 4 steps, featuring single-stream audio processing with explicit state conditioning for seamless turn-taking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create practical embodied avatars that simultaneously achieve real-time generation, unified listening-speaking behavior, and high visual quality for LLM conversations, overcoming limitations of prior dual-stream audio methods that create interlocutor look-ahead dependencies incompatible with causal user-LLM interaction.

Method: Uses Rectified-Flow Diffusion Transformer (DiT) with differentiable renderer for efficient generation (4 sampling steps). Implements single-stream audio interface with explicit per-frame listening-speaking state conditioning and Streaming Audio Scheduler to suppress spurious mouth motion during listening. Employs two-stage training: coefficient-space pretraining followed by joint image-domain refinement.

Result: State-of-the-art visual quality and motion fidelity in both speaking and listening scenarios, achieving real-time generation with seamless turn-taking capabilities.

Conclusion: EmbodiedHead successfully addresses the key challenges of real-time talking-head generation for LLM avatars, providing a practical solution that balances visual quality, motion fidelity, and real-time performance while enabling natural conversation flow.

Abstract: We present EmbodiedHead, a speech-driven talking-head framework that equips LLMs with real-time visual avatars for conversation. A practical embodied avatar must achieve real-time generation, unified listening-speaking behavior, and high rendered visual quality simultaneously. Our framework couples the first Rectified-Flow Diffusion Transformer (DiT) for this task with a differentiable renderer, enabling diverse, high-fidelity generation in as few as four sampling steps. Prior listening-speaking methods rely on dual-stream audio, introducing an interlocutor look-ahead dependency incompatible with causal user--LLM interaction. We instead adopt a single-stream interface with explicit per-frame listening-speaking state conditioning and a Streaming Audio Scheduler, suppressing spurious mouth motion during listening while enabling seamless turn-taking. A two-stage training scheme of coefficient-space pretraining and joint image-domain refinement further closes the gap between motion-level supervision and rendered quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art visual quality and motion fidelity in both speaking and listening scenarios.

</details>


### [137] [Cross-Modal Attention Analysis and Optimization in Vision-Language Models: A Study on Visual Reliability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17217)
*Lijie Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vision-Language Models over-rely on text and under-use visual evidence (text shortcut learning). An adversarial evaluation framework with conflicting text-image pairs shows optimized fine-tuning reduces this dependency by 64.4% while maintaining high accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs often exhibit "text shortcut learning" - they over-rely on textual descriptions while under-utilizing visual evidence, which can lead to poor performance when text and images conflict.

Method: Proposed adversarial evaluation framework with four strategies (shape_swap, color_swap, position_swap, random_text) on geometric shapes dataset. Compared three configurations: Baseline CLIP, LoRA fine-tuning, and LoRA Optimized (with Hard Negative Mining, Label Smoothing, layer-wise learning rates, Cosine Restarts, curriculum learning, and data augmentation).

Result: Optimized model reduced average accuracy degradation (Drop) from 27.5% to 9.8% (64.4% relative improvement, p<0.001) while maintaining 97% normal accuracy. Attention visualization and embedding-space analysis confirmed better visual attention and cross-modal alignment.

Conclusion: The optimized fine-tuning approach effectively mitigates text shortcut learning in VLMs, forcing models to rely more on visual evidence while maintaining strong performance, demonstrating the importance of addressing cross-modal dependency biases.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) achieve strong cross-modal performance, yet recent evidence suggests they over-rely on textual descriptions while under-utilizing visual evidence -- a phenomenon termed ``text shortcut learning.'' We propose an adversarial evaluation framework that quantifies this cross-modal dependency by measuring accuracy degradation (Drop) when semantically conflicting text is paired with unchanged images. Four adversarial strategies -- shape\_swap, color\_swap, position\_swap, and random\_text -- are applied to a controlled geometric-shapes dataset ($n{=}1{,}000$). We compare three configurations: Baseline CLIP (ViT-B/32), LoRA fine-tuning, and LoRA Optimized (integrating Hard Negative Mining, Label Smoothing, layer-wise learning rates, Cosine Restarts, curriculum learning, and data augmentation). The optimized model reduces average Drop from 27.5\% to 9.8\% (64.4\% relative improvement, $p{<}0.001$) while maintaining 97\% normal accuracy. Attention visualization and embedding-space analysis confirm that the optimized model attends more to visual features and achieves tighter cross-modal alignment.

</details>


### [138] [Enhancing Zero-shot Personalized Image Aesthetics Assessment with Profile-aware Multimodal LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17233)
*Chun Wang,Chenfeng Wei,Chenyang Liu,Weihong Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: P-MLLM: A profile-aware multimodal LLM for zero-shot personalized image aesthetics assessment using user profiles as contextual signals instead of historical ratings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PIAA methods rely on historical user ratings and struggle when such data is unavailable (zero-shot setting). Need to address personalization without requiring prior rating data.

Method: Introduces P-MLLM, a profile-aware multimodal LLM that augments a frozen LLM with selective fusion modules. These modules selectively integrate visual information into hidden states during profile-conditioned reasoning, enabling profile-aware visual integration.

Result: Achieves competitive zero-shot performance on recent PIAA benchmarks. Remains effective even with coarse profile information, demonstrating robustness.

Conclusion: Profile-based personalization shows strong potential for zero-shot PIAA, offering an effective alternative to historical rating-dependent methods.

Abstract: Personalized image aesthetics assessment (PIAA) aims to predict an individual user's subjective rating of an image, which requires modeling user-specific aesthetic preferences. Existing methods rely on historical user ratings for this modeling and therefore struggle when such data are unavailable. We address this zero-shot setting by using user profiles as contextual signals for personalization and adopting a profile-based personalization paradigm. We introduce P-MLLM, a profile-aware multimodal LLM that augments a frozen LLM with selective fusion modules for controlled visual integration. These modules selectively integrate visual information into the model's evolving hidden states during profile-conditioned reasoning, allowing visual information to be incorporated in a profile-aware manner. Experiments on recent PIAA benchmarks show that P-MLLM achieves competitive zero-shot performance and remains effective even with coarse profile information, highlighting the potential of profile-based personalization for zero-shot PIAA.

</details>


### [139] [RemoteShield: Enable Robust Multimodal Large Language Models for Earth Observation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17243)
*Rui Min,Liang Yao,Shiyu Miao,Shengxiang Xu,Yuxuan Liu,Chuanyi Zhang,Shimin Di,Fan Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RemoteShield: A robust Multimodal Large Language Model for Earth Observation that maintains consistent interpretation under realistic input perturbations like clouds, fog, and diverse textual variations through preference learning over semantic equivalence clusters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Remote Sensing MLLMs fail to maintain consistent interpretation under realistic input variations because they're trained on clean datasets and learn brittle mappings that don't generalize to noisy operational conditions, leading to performance degradation with imperfect inputs.

Method: RemoteShield uses preference learning over semantic equivalence clusters where each clean sample is paired with its image-text perturbed variants. The model is optimized by comparing responses to clean and corrupted inputs, encouraging stable responses over perturbation-induced failures through cross-condition alignment.

Result: Experiments on three Earth Observation tasks show RemoteShield consistently delivers stronger robustness and cross-condition consistency than representative baselines under realistic multimodal perturbations, significantly outperforming current RS foundation models that are impaired by these perturbations.

Conclusion: RemoteShield addresses the vulnerability of current Remote Sensing MLLMs to realistic input variations by learning to maintain consistent outputs through preference learning over semantic equivalence clusters, enabling more reliable Earth Observation applications in operational settings.

Abstract: A robust Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) for Earth Observation should maintain consistent interpretation and reasoning under realistic input variations. However, current Remote Sensing MLLMs fail to meet this requirement. Trained on carefully curated clean datasets, they learn brittle mappings that do not generalize to noisy conditions in operational Earth Observation. Consequently, their performance degrades when confronted with imperfect inputs in deployment. To quantify this vulnerability, we construct a realistic set of multimodal perturbations, including visual degradations such as cloud and fog cover, together with diverse human-centric textual variations ranging from colloquialisms to vague or omitted instructions. Empirical evaluations show that these perturbations significantly impair the visual-semantic reasoning capabilities of leading RS foundation models. To address this limitation, we introduce RemoteShield, a robust Remote Sensing MLLM trained to maintain consistent outputs across realistic input variations. During training, each clean sample is paired with its image-text perturbed variants to form a semantic equivalence cluster. Rather than directly fitting noisy samples, RemoteShield is optimized through preference learning over clean and perturbed conditions within the same cluster. By comparing model responses to clean and corrupted inputs, the model is encouraged to favor stable responses over perturbation-induced failures. This cross-condition alignment helps the model focus on underlying task semantics despite visual degradations and textual noise. Experiments on three Earth Observation tasks show that RemoteShield consistently delivers stronger robustness and cross-condition consistency than representative baselines under realistic multimodal perturbations.

</details>


### [140] [Fractal Characterization of Low-Correlation Signals in AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17268)
*Wenwei Xie,Jie Yin,Lu Ma,Xuansong Zhang,Wenjing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new deepfake detection method using low-correlation signal analysis based on fractal theory, showing superior robustness in open-world scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-generated imagery poses significant threats to information security and societal trust, but existing deepfake detection methods lack robustness in real-world scenarios.

Method: Analyzes intrinsic signal-level discrepancies between synthetic and authentic images, focusing on low-correlation signals as distinctive markers. Introduces a novel quantification method based on fractal theory to capture statistical anomalies in the synthesis process.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the method's robustness and superior detection performance compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: The work emphasizes shifting research focus to signal-level analysis for deepfake detection, provides a new research direction, and shows the approach is broadly applicable to all AI-generated image detection tasks beyond just face images.

Abstract: AI-generated imagery has reached near-photorealistic fidelity, yet this technology poses significant threats to information security and societal trust. Existing deepfake detection methods often exhibit limited robustness in open-world scenarios. To address this limitation, this paper investigates intrinsic discrepancies between synthetic and authentic images from a signal-level perspective. Our analysis reveals that low-correlation signals serve as distinctive markers for differentiating AI-generated imagery from real photographs. Building on this insight, we introduce a novel method for quantifying these signals based on fractal theory. By analyzing the fractal characteristics of low-correlation signals, our method effectively captures the subtle statistical anomalies inherent to the synthesis process. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the method's robustness and superior detection performance. This work emphasizes the need to shift research focus to a new signal-level direction for deepfake detection. Theoretically, this proposed approach is not limited to face image identification but can be applied to all AI-generated image detection tasks. This study provides a new research direction for deepfake detection.

</details>


### [141] [Instinct vs. Reflection: Unifying Token and Verbalized Confidence in Multimodal Large Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17274)
*Yunkai Dang,Yifan Jiang,Yizhu Jiang,Anqi Chen,Wenbin Li,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework to address instinct-reflection misalignment in MLLMs' confidence estimation by merging token-level and verbal confidence signals with cross-channel consistency and order-preserving alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs need reliable confidence estimation for practical deployment, but prior work focuses on text-only LLMs using expensive self-consistency sampling. There's a significant misalignment between models' implicit token-level support and their verbal self-assessment confidence.

Method: Proposes a monotone confidence fusion framework to merge dual-channel signals (token-level and verbal confidence) using cross-channel consistency to estimate correctness, followed by order-preserving mean alignment to correct global bias while preserving risk-coverage trade-off.

Result: Experiments on diverse open-source and closed-source MLLMs show the method consistently yields more reliable confidence estimates and improves both calibration and failure prediction.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses instinct-reflection misalignment in MLLMs, providing more reliable confidence estimation for practical deployment while improving calibration and failure prediction capabilities.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in various perception and reasoning tasks. Despite this success, ensuring their reliability in practical deployment necessitates robust confidence estimation. Prior works have predominantly focused on text-only LLMs, often relying on computationally expensive self-consistency sampling. In this paper, we extend this to multimodal settings and conduct a comprehensive evaluation of MLLMs' response confidence estimation. Our analysis reveals a significant instinct-reflection misalignment: the model's implicit token-level support frequently diverges from its verbal self-assessment confidence. To address this misalignment, we propose a monotone confidence fusion framework to merge dual-channel signals and cross-channel consistency to estimate correctness. Subsequently, an order-preserving mean alignment step is applied to correct global bias, which improves calibration while preserving the risk-coverage trade-off for selective prediction. Experiments on diverse open-source and closed-source MLLMs show that our method consistently yields more reliable confidence estimates and improves both calibration and failure prediction. Code will be available at https://github.com/Yunkaidang/Instinct-vs.-Reflection.

</details>


### [142] [PestVL-Net: Enabling Multimodal Pest Learning via Fine-grained Vision-Language Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17278)
*Xueheng Li,Tao Hu,Ke Cao,Runsheng Qi,Huixin Zhang,Rui Li,Jie Zhang,Chengjun Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PestVL-Net: A vision-language framework using RWKV architecture and MLLM priors for fine-grained pest recognition, addressing challenges in real-world agricultural pest data collection and diverse species characteristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world pest data collection is challenging due to diverse species with complex morphological characteristics. Existing methods struggle to model fine-grained visual and semantic features, limiting practical agricultural applications.

Method: PestVL-Net integrates visual and linguistic pathways: visual uses RWKV architecture with saliency-guided adaptive window partitioning; linguistic uses MLLM priors with expert knowledge and multimodal Chain-of-Thought reasoning for precise semantic descriptions.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple pest datasets validate PestVL-Net's superior performance, demonstrating its effectiveness for fine-grained pest learning and real-world pest management.

Conclusion: The synergistic vision-language approach enables effective fine-grained pest recognition, addressing critical challenges in agricultural pest management with potential for practical real-world applications.

Abstract: Effective pest recognition and management are crucial for sustainable agricultural development. However, collecting pest data in real scenarios is often challenging. Compared to other domains, pests exhibit a wide variety of species with complex and diverse morphological characteristics. Existing techniques struggle to effectively model the key visual and high-level semantic features of pests in a fine-grained manner. These limitations hinder the practical application of such methods in real agricultural scenarios. To address these critical challenges, we present a synergistic approach that integrates PestVL-Net, a novel vision-language framework, with two multi-species pest datasets to facilitate fine-grained pest learning. The visual pathway of PestVL-Net utilizes the Recurrent Weighted Key Value (RWKV) architecture, incorporating a saliency-guided adaptive window partitioning scheme to effectively model the fine-grained visual characteristics of pests. Concurrently, the linguistic component generates precise pest semantic descriptions by leveraging Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) priors, critically informed by agricultural expert knowledge and structured via multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. The deep fusion of these complementary visual and textual representations enables fine-grained multimodal pest learning. Extensive experimental evaluations on multiple pest datasets validate the superior performance of PestVL-Net, highlighting its potential for effective real-world pest management.

</details>


### [143] [Depth Adaptive Efficient Visual Autoregressive Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17286)
*Chunliang Li,Tianze Cao,Sanyuan Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DepthVAR is a training-free framework that accelerates Visual Autoregressive models by adaptively allocating per-token computational depth instead of pruning entire tokens, achieving 2.3-3.1× speedup with minimal quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VAR acceleration methods use binary hard-pruning based on frequency maps, which is fundamentally limited and doesn't improve quality even with better frequency estimation. The authors observed that VAR models have significant depth redundancy, suggesting a paradigm shift from pruning tokens to adaptively allocating computational depth per token.

Method: DepthVAR introduces: 1) Adaptive depth scheduler that assigns computational depth via cyclic rotated schedule for balanced, non-static refinement; 2) Dynamic inference process that translates depths into layer-major masks, selectively applies transformer blocks, and blends resulting codes to ensure each token's influence is proportional to its processing depth.

Result: Extensive experiments show DepthVAR achieves 2.3×-3.1× acceleration with minimal quality loss, offering a competitive compute-performance trade-off compared to existing hard-pruning approaches.

Conclusion: DepthVAR provides an effective training-free framework for accelerating VAR models by shifting from token pruning to adaptive depth allocation, demonstrating superior performance-efficiency trade-offs over existing methods.

Abstract: Visual Autoregressive (VAR) modeling inefficiently applies a fixed computational depth to each position when generating high-resolution images. While existing methods accelerate inference by pruning tokens using frequency maps, their binary hard-pruning approach is fundamentally limited and fails to improve quality even with better frequency estimation. Observing that VAR models possess significant depth redundancy, we propose a paradigm shift from pruning entire tokens to adaptively allocating per-token computational depth. To this end, we introduce DepthVAR, a training-free framework that dynamically allocates computation. It integrates an adaptive depth scheduler, which assigns computational depth via a cyclic rotated schedule for balanced, non-static refinement, with a dynamic inference process that translates these depths into layer-major masks, selectively applies transformer blocks, and blends the resulting codes to ensure each token's influence is proportional to its processing depth. Extensive experiments show that DepthVAR achieves 2.3$\times$-3.1$\times$ acceleration with minimal quality loss, offering a competitive compute-performance trade-off compared to existing hard-pruning approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/STOVAGtz/DepthVAR

</details>


### [144] [Spectral Forensics of Diffusion Attention Graphs for Copy-Move Forgery Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17287)
*H. M. Shadman Tabib,Tasriad Ahmed Tias,Nafis Tahmid*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GraphSpecForge detects copy-move forgery by analyzing spectral structure of attention graphs from Stable Diffusion U-Net without training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Copy-move forgery (duplicating image regions) threatens visual media integrity, requiring detection methods that don't require forgery-specific training.

Method: Training-free framework using spectral analysis of self-attention graphs from pretrained Stable Diffusion U-Net. Detects approximate subgraph duplication via normalized graph Laplacian spectral redistribution, using Wasserstein distances between Laplacian spectra and authentic reference distribution.

Result: Achieves AUROC = 0.606 on RecodAI-LUC (5,128 images), outperforming raw attention spectra by +0.057 AUROC. On other benchmarks: MICC-F220 (0.752), CoMoFoD (0.774), COVERAGE (0.673). On CoMoFoD also achieves AUPRC = 0.833, balanced accuracy = 0.712, MCC = 0.499, TPR@1%FPR = 32.5%.

Conclusion: GraphSpecForge provides effective training-free detection of copy-move forgery by leveraging spectral properties of attention graphs, with ablation studies confirming specificity and sensitivity to manipulation strength.

Abstract: Copy-move forgery, where a region within an image is duplicated to hide or fabricate content, remains a persistent threat to visual media integrity. We introduce GraphSpecForge, a training-free framework that detects copy-move forgery by analysing the spectral structure of attention graphs from a pretrained Stable Diffusion U-Net. Our central insight is that copy-move manipulation induces approximate subgraph duplication in the self-attention graph, leading to measurable spectral redistribution in the normalized graph Laplacian. We formalise this link with perturbation-based arguments and build an image-level anomaly detector using Wasserstein distances between per-image Laplacian spectra and an authentic reference distribution. We evaluate GraphSpecForge on four copy-move benchmarks without forgery-specific retraining. On RecodAI-LUC (5,128 images), our best configuration achieves AUROC = 0.606 (95% CI: 0.580-0.638; permutation p = 0.005), and the normalized Laplacian outperforms raw attention spectra by +0.057 AUROC. On MICC-F220, CoMoFoD, and COVERAGE, the same pipeline attains AUROCs of 0.752, 0.774, and 0.673, respectively; on CoMoFoD it also reaches AUPRC = 0.833, balanced accuracy = 0.712, MCC = 0.499, and TPR@1%FPR = 32.5%. Additional ablation and falsification experiments confirm the signal's specificity and sensitivity to manipulation strength, while null-graph controls rule out trivial-statistic explanations.

</details>


### [145] [Frequency-guided Multi-level Reasoning for Scene Graph Generation in Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17298)
*Chenxing Li,Yiping Duan,Xiaoming Tao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FReMuRe model improves video scene graph generation for long-tail relationships using frequency-guided multi-level reasoning and specialized classification heads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video scene graph generation methods struggle with long-tail distributions of relationships, where rare relationships are poorly handled despite being important for comprehensive video understanding.

Method: Proposes Frequency-guided Relational Multi-level Reasoning (FReMuRe) with: 1) relation-specific branches to handle gradient conflicts, 2) frequency-aware dual-branch predicate embedding network for separate modeling of high/low-frequency relationships with gated fusion, and 3) interchangeable relation classification heads (Bayesian Head for uncertainty estimation and Gaussian Mixture Model Head for intra-class diversity).

Result: Experimental results on Action Genome dataset show significant improvements in recall rate for long-tail relationships and overall reasoning robustness compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: FReMuRe effectively addresses long-tail distribution challenges in video scene graph generation through frequency-guided modeling and specialized architectural components, advancing the field's ability to handle rare but semantically important relationships.

Abstract: Video Scene Graph Generation aims to obtain structured semantic representations of objects and their relationships in videos for high-level understanding. However, existing methods still have limitations in handling long-tail distributions. This paper proposes the Frequency-guided Relational Multi-level Reasoning (FReMuRe) model, which enhances the modeling ability of long-tail relationships from a mechanism perspective. We introduce relation-specific branches to deal gradient conflicts, yielding more balanced and tail-aware learning. And we design a frequency-aware dual-branch predicate embedding network to model high-frequency and low-frequency relationships separately and improve the recall rate of tail classes through gated fusion. Meanwhile, we propose two types of interchangeable relation classification heads: Bayesian Head for uncertainty estimation and new Gaussian Mixture Model Head to enhance intra-class diversity. Experimental results show that FReMuRe significantly improves the recall rate of long-tail relationships and overall reasoning robustness on the Action Genome dataset.

</details>


### [146] [The First Challenge on Mobile Real-World Image Super-Resolution at NTIRE 2026: Benchmark Results and Method Overview](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17306)
*Jiatong Li,Zheng Chen,Kai Liu,Jingkai Wang,Zihan Zhou,Xiaoyang Liu,Libo Zhu,Jue Gong,Radu Timofte,Yulun Zhang,Congyu Wang,Zihao Wang,Ke Wu,Xinzhe Zhu,Fengkai Zhang,Zhongbao Yang,Long Sun,Jiangxin Dong,Jinshan Pan,Jiachen Tu,Yaokun Shi,Guoyi Xu,Yaoxin Jiang,Jiajia Liu,Renyuan Situ,Yixin Yang,Zhaorun Zhou,Junyang Chen,Yuqi Li,Chuanguang Yang,Weilun Feng,Chuanyue Yan,Yuedong Tan,Yingli Tian,Zhenzhong Chen,Tongqi Guo,Ruhan Liu,Sangzi Shi,Huazhang Deng,Jie Yang,Wenzhuo Ma,Yuantong Zhang,Daiqin Yang,Tianrun Chen,Deyi Ji,Yuxiao Jiang,Qi Zhu,Lanyun Zhu,Yuwen Pan,Runze Tian,Mingyu Shi,Zhanfeng Feng,Yuanfei Bao,Jiaming Guo,Renjing Pei,Xin Di,Long Peng,Linfeng Jiang,Xueyang Fu,Yang Cao,Zhengjun Zha,Choulhyouc Lee,Shyang-En Weng,Yi-Cheng Liao,Jorge Tyrakowski,Yu-Syuan Xu,Wei-Chen Chiu,Ching-Chun Huang,Yoonjin Im,Jihye Park,Hyungju Chun,Hyunhee Park,MinKyu Park,Xiaoxuan Yu,Jianxing Zhang,Yuxuan Jiang,Chengxi Zeng,Tianhao Peng,Fan Zhang,David Bull,Watchara Ruangsang,Supavadee Aramvith,JiaHao Deng,Wei Zhou,Hongyu Huang,Shaohui Lin,Zihan Wang,Yilin Chen,Yunchen Li,Junbo Qiao,Wei Li,Jiao Xie,Gaoqi He,Wenxi Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Review of NTIRE 2026 mobile real-world image super-resolution challenge focusing on efficient models for mobile devices with unknown degradations at 4x scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance mobile real-world image super-resolution by developing efficient models that can run on mobile devices while handling unknown degradations, addressing the gap between computational constraints and quality requirements.

Method: Challenge-based approach with specific evaluation metrics combining image quality assessment scores and speedup ratios, attracting 108 registrants with 16 teams submitting valid solutions for mobile-optimized super-resolution models.

Result: The challenge successfully attracted significant participation (108 registrants) with 16 teams achieving valid scores, advancing the state-of-the-art in mobile real-world image super-resolution and providing insights into current trends in the field.

Conclusion: The NTIRE 2026 challenge successfully advanced mobile real-world image super-resolution by fostering development of efficient models for mobile devices, providing valuable overview of current trends and collaborative progress in the field.

Abstract: This paper provides a review of the NTIRE 2026 challenge on mobile real-world image super-resolution, highlighting the proposed solutions and the resulting outcomes. The challenge aims to recover high-resolution (HR) images from low-resolution (LR) counterparts generated through unknown degradations with a x4 scaling factor while ensuring the models remain executable on mobile devices. The objective is to develop effective and efficient network designs or solutions that achieve state-of-the-art real-world image super-resolution performance. The track of the challenge evaluates performance using a weighted combination of image quality assessment (IQA) score and speedup ratios. The competition attracted 108 registrants, with 16 teams achieving a valid score in the final ranking. This collaborative effort advances the performance of mobile real-world image super-resolution while offering an in-depth overview of the latest trends in the field.

</details>


### [147] [Generalizable Face Forgery Detection via Separable Prompt Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17307)
*Enrui Yang,Yuezun Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SePL uses CLIP's text modality with separable prompt learning to detect face forgeries by disentangling forgery-specific and irrelevant information, achieving strong generalization across datasets and methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CLIP-based deepfake detection methods focus mainly on the visual encoder while neglecting the instructive potential of the text modality. The authors believe text can provide valuable guidance for deepfake detection through careful design.

Method: Proposes Separable Prompt Learning (SePL) strategy that disentangles forgery-specific and forgery-irrelevant information via two types of prompt learning. Uses cross-modality alignment and dedicated objectives to achieve this disentanglement.

Result: Achieves competitive and superior performance compared to other methods under both cross-dataset and cross-method evaluation, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities.

Conclusion: The text modality of CLIP can be effectively leveraged for deepfake detection through separable prompt learning, offering a simple yet powerful adaptation that outperforms existing approaches.

Abstract: Detecting face forgeries using CLIP has recently emerged as a promising and increasingly popular research direction. Owing to its rich visual knowledge acquired through large-scale pretraining, most existing methods typically rely on the visual encoder of CLIP, while paying limited attention to the text modality. Given the instructive nature of the text modality, we posit that it can be leveraged to instruct Deepfake detection with meticulous design. Accordingly, we shift the focus from the visual modality to the text modality and propose a new Separable Prompt Learning strategy (SePL) that enables CLIP to serve as an effective face forgery detector. The core idea of SePL is to disentangle forgery-specific and forgery-irrelevant information in images via two types of prompt learning, with the former enhancing detection. To achieve this disentangle, we describe a cross-modality alignment strategy and a set of dedicated objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, with this simple adaptation, our method achieves competitive and even superior performance compared to other methods under both cross-dataset and cross-method evaluation, highlighting its strong generalizability. The codes have been released at https://github.com/OUC-YER/SePL-DeepfakeDetection

</details>


### [148] [When Background Matters: Breaking Medical Vision Language Models by Transferable Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17318)
*Akash Ghosh,Subhadip Baidya,Sriparna Saha,Xiuying Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MedFocusLeak: A black-box multimodal attack that generates imperceptible perturbations in medical images to fool VLMs into making clinically plausible but incorrect diagnoses by distracting attention from pathological regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs are increasingly used in clinical diagnostics but their robustness to adversarial attacks is unexplored, posing serious risks. Existing attacks either focus on secondary objectives (model stealing, fine-tuning) or create visible distortions that clinicians can detect.

Method: Proposes MedFocusLeak - a highly transferable black-box multimodal attack that injects coordinated perturbations into non-diagnostic background regions and uses an attention distraction mechanism to shift model focus away from pathological areas while keeping perturbations imperceptible.

Result: Extensive evaluations across six medical imaging modalities show state-of-the-art performance, generating misleading yet realistic diagnostic outputs across diverse VLMs. The attack reveals critical weaknesses in modern clinical VLMs' reasoning capabilities.

Conclusion: MedFocusLeak demonstrates serious vulnerabilities in clinical VLMs through imperceptible attacks that produce clinically plausible but incorrect diagnoses, highlighting the need for improved robustness in medical AI systems.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly used in clinical diagnostics, yet their robustness to adversarial attacks remains largely unexplored, posing serious risks. Existing medical attacks focus on secondary objectives such as model stealing or adversarial fine-tuning, while transferable attacks from natural images introduce visible distortions that clinicians can easily detect. To address this, we propose MedFocusLeak, a highly transferable black-box multimodal attack that induces incorrect yet clinically plausible diagnoses while keeping perturbations imperceptible. The method injects coordinated perturbations into non-diagnostic background regions and employs an attention distraction mechanism to shift the model's focus away from pathological areas. Extensive evaluations across six medical imaging modalities show that MedFocusLeak achieves state-of-the-art performance, generating misleading yet realistic diagnostic outputs across diverse VLMs. We further introduce a unified evaluation framework with novel metrics that jointly capture attack success and image fidelity, revealing a critical weakness in the reasoning capabilities of modern clinical VLMs.

</details>


### [149] [E2E-GMNER: End-to-End Generative Grounded Multimodal Named Entity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17319)
*Meng Zhang,Jinzhong Ning,Xiaolong Wu,Hongfei Lin,Yijia Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: E2E-GMNER is an end-to-end generative framework for Grounded Multimodal Named Entity Recognition that unifies entity recognition, typing, visual grounding, and knowledge reasoning within a single multimodal LLM, using instruction tuning and chain-of-thought reasoning with noise-aware bounding box supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GMNER approaches use pipeline architectures that decouple textual entity recognition and visual grounding, causing error accumulation and suboptimal joint optimization. There's a need for unified end-to-end frameworks that can better integrate multimodal information and handle annotation noise.

Method: Proposes E2E-GMNER: 1) Formulates GMNER as instruction-tuned conditional generation task with chain-of-thought reasoning to adaptively use visual evidence or background knowledge; 2) Introduces Gaussian Risk-Aware Box Perturbation (GRBP) that replaces hard box supervision with probabilistically perturbed soft targets to handle annotation noise and discretization errors.

Result: Extensive experiments on Twitter-GMNER and Twitter-FMNERG benchmarks show E2E-GMNER achieves highly competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods, validating effectiveness of unified end-to-end optimization and noise-aware grounding supervision.

Conclusion: The proposed end-to-end generative framework successfully unifies multiple GMNER subtasks, with chain-of-thought reasoning enabling adaptive multimodal reasoning and GRBP improving robustness to annotation noise, demonstrating advantages over pipeline-based approaches.

Abstract: Grounded Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (GMNER) aims to jointly identify named entity mentions in text, predict their semantic types, and ground each entity to a corresponding visual region in an associated image. Existing approaches predominantly adopt pipeline-based architectures that decouple textual entity recognition and visual grounding, leading to error accumulation and suboptimal joint optimization. In this paper, we propose E2E-GMNER, a fully end-to-end generative framework that unifies entity recognition, semantic typing, visual grounding, and implicit knowledge reasoning within a single multimodal large language model. We formulate GMNER as an instruction-tuned conditional generation task and incorporate chain-of-thought reasoning to enable the model to adaptively determine when visual evidence or background knowledge is informative, reducing reliance on noisy cues. To further address the instability of generative bounding box prediction, we introduce Gaussian Risk-Aware Box Perturbation (GRBP), which replaces hard box supervision with probabilistically perturbed soft targets to improve robustness against annotation noise and discretization errors. Extensive experiments on the Twitter-GMNER and Twitter-FMNERG benchmarks demonstrate that E2E-GMNER achieves highly competitive performance compared with state of the art methods, validating the effectiveness of unified end-to-end optimization and noise-aware grounding supervision. Code is available at:https://github.com/Finch-coder/E2E-GMNER

</details>


### [150] [Towards Joint Quantization and Token Pruning of Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17320)
*Xinqing Li,Xin He,Xindong Zhang,Ming-Ming Cheng,Lei Zhang,Yun Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QUOTA framework combines quantization and token pruning for efficient VLM inference, achieving 95.65% accuracy with only 30% of visual tokens in W4A4 regime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language Models suffer from high inference costs due to long visual-token prefixes and growing KV cache. Existing stage-wise combinations of token pruning and quantization are brittle due to mismatch between calibration and pruning.

Method: Proposes QUOTA framework that unifies low-bit inference and deterministic visual-token pruning. Uses quantization calibration signals to create layer-wise token allocation schedule, evaluating token importance under deployed W4A4 operators with quantized KV cache using activation magnitude, attention cues, and low-bit risk signals.

Result: Achieves 95.65% average retention on standard VLM benchmarks while keeping only 30% of visual tokens, outperforming stage-wise baselines (~94.3% retention).

Conclusion: QUOTA provides a robust collaborative framework for efficient VLM deployment through unified quantization-and-pruning, enabling consistent budgeted token selection in low-bit regimes.

Abstract: Deploying Vision-Language Models (VLMs) under aggressive low-bit inference remains challenging because inference cost is dominated by the long visual-token prefix during prefill and the growing KV cache during autoregressive decoding. Token pruning and low-bit quantization are complementary for reducing these costs, yet naive stage-wise combinations are often brittle due to a mismatch between quantization calibration and pruning execution. We present a collaborative quantization-and-pruning framework that unifies low-bit inference and deterministic visual-token pruning in a single deployable pipeline. The framework introduces the \textbf{Q}uantization \textbf{U}nified \textbf{O}ffline \textbf{T}oken \textbf{A}llocator (\textbf{QUOTA}), which converts low-bit calibration signals into a layer-wise token allocation schedule and materializes it as a pruning recipe. Token importance is evaluated under deployed W4A4 operators with a quantized KV cache by combining activation magnitude, attention cues, and an explicit low-bit risk signal, enabling consistent budgeted top-$k$ selection. Experiments on standard VLM benchmarks show improved robustness over stage-wise baselines under the same low-bit regime, achieving 95.65\% average retention while retaining only 30\% of visual tokens, compared with about 94.3\% retention for representative stage-wise combinations. The code will be released.

</details>


### [151] [R-FLoRA: Residual-Statistic-Gated Low-Rank Adaptation for Single-Image Face Morphing Attack Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17321)
*Raghavendra Ramachandra*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new single-image face morphing attack detection framework that combines high-frequency Laplacian residual statistics with frozen foundation vision transformer features using gated adapters and fusion mechanisms, achieving state-of-the-art performance and cross-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Face morphing attacks threaten face recognition systems in passport issuance, border control, and digital identity verification. Single-image detection is challenging due to lack of trusted reference and diverse attack generation methods.

Method: Integrates high-frequency Laplacian residual statistics with frozen foundation-scale vision transformer representations. Uses residual-statistic-gated low-rank adapters (R-FLoRA) and feature-wise residual fusion (Res-FiLM) to enhance sensitivity to local morphing artifacts while preserving semantic context. Includes residual-contrastive alignment loss to regularize fused token space.

Result: Outperforms nine recent state-of-the-art S-MAD algorithms across four ICAO-compliant datasets with seven morph generation techniques. Achieves superior detection accuracy and cross-domain/dataset generalization. With frozen backbone and minimal trainable parameters, achieves real-time efficiency and interpretability.

Conclusion: The proposed S-MAD framework effectively detects face morphing attacks from single images, offering robust performance, generalization capability, and practical suitability for real-world biometric verification systems with real-time efficiency.

Abstract: Face morphing attacks pose a substantial risk to the reliability of face recognition systems used in passport issuance, border control, and digital identity verification. Detecting morphing attacks from a single facial image remains challenging owing to the lack of a trusted reference and the diversity of attack generation methods. This paper presents a new Single-Image Face Morphing Attack Detection (S-MAD) framework that integrates high-frequency Laplacian residual statistics with representations from a frozen, foundation-scale vision transformer. The approach employs residual-statistic-gated low-rank adapters (R-FLoRA) and feature-wise residual fusion (Res-FiLM) to enhance sensitivity to local morphing artefacts while preserving the semantic context of the backbone. A novel residual-contrastive alignment loss further regularises the fused token space, improving discrimination under unseen morphing conditions. Comprehensive experiments on four ICAO-compliant datasets, encompassing seven morph generation techniques, demonstrate that the proposed method consistently surpasses nine recent state-of-the-art S-MAD algorithms in detection accuracy and cross-domain (or dataset) generalisation. With a frozen backbone and minimal trainable parameters, the model achieves real-time efficiency and interpretability, making it suitable for real-life scenarios in biometric verification systems.

</details>


### [152] [Robust Diabetic Retinopathy Grading Using Dual-Resolution Attention-Based Deep Learning with Ordinal Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17341)
*Afshan Hashmi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dual-resolution deep learning framework with attention-based feature fusion and ordinal regression improves cross-dataset generalization for diabetic retinopathy grading.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a leading cause of vision impairment, but deep learning models often degrade when deployed across datasets with different imaging conditions, highlighting the need for robust cross-dataset generalization.

Method: Uses two parallel EfficientNet backbones at different spatial resolutions, learnable attention mechanism for feature fusion, ordinal regression (CORAL) for ordered severity levels, and preprocessing (circular cropping, contrast enhancement, histogram matching) to mitigate domain discrepancies.

Result: Achieved quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) of 0.88 on APTOS 2019 validation set and 0.68 on external Messidor-2 test set, demonstrating improved robustness for cross-dataset DR grading.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-resolution framework with attention-based fusion and ordinal regression effectively improves cross-dataset generalization for diabetic retinopathy grading, making it suitable for real-world screening applications.

Abstract: Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a leading cause of vision impairment worldwide, and automated grading systems play a crucial role in large-scale screening programs. However, deep learning models often exhibit degraded performance when deployed across datasets acquired under different imaging conditions. This study presents a robust dual-resolution deep learning framework for DR grading that integrates attention-based feature fusion with ordinal regression to improve cross-dataset generalization. The proposed method employs two parallel EfficientNet backbones operating at different spatial resolutions to capture complementary retinal features. A learnable attention mechanism adaptively fuses multi-resolution representations, while an ordinal regression formulation based on the cumulative link model (CORAL) explicitly accounts for the ordered nature of DR severity levels. To mitigate domain discrepancies between datasets, a preprocessing strategy combining circular cropping, contrast enhancement, and histogram matching is applied. The model was trained on the APTOS 2019 dataset and evaluated on both an internal validation split and an external Messidor-2 test set. Experimental results demonstrate strong grading performance, achieving a quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) of 0.88 on the APTOS validation set and 0.68 on the unseen Messidor-2 dataset, indicating improved robustness for cross-dataset DR grading applications.

</details>


### [153] [When Text Hijacks Vision: Benchmarking and Mitigating Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination in Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17375)
*Cui Yakun,Xingqun Qi,TianTian Geng,Yuyao Zhang,Sirui Han,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Paper introduces VisualTextTrap benchmark for measuring text overlay-induced hallucinations in VLMs, and proposes VTHM-MoE framework to mitigate this issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Vision-Language Models (VLMs) systematically hallucinate when on-screen text contradicts visual scenes, prioritizing textual semantics over actual visual content. This Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination (TOIH) problem is critical but overlooked in current multimodal video understanding benchmarks.

Method: 1) Creates VisualTextTrap benchmark with 6,057 human-validated samples across 88 fine-grained attributes in four dimensions (temporal, action, object, spatial), with hallucination intensity quantified on 5-level scale. 2) Proposes VTHM-MoE framework with dual-encoder architecture and four dimension-specialized expert modules pre-trained to identify cross-modal discrepancies. 3) Uses Adaptive Token Routing Strategy for dynamic expert allocation.

Result: VTHM-MoE outperforms state-of-the-art counterparts on diverse video question answering tasks, demonstrating robust resistance to TOIH while preserving performance on uncontaminated videos.

Conclusion: The paper addresses the critical TOIH problem in VLMs, provides a comprehensive benchmark for evaluation, and proposes an effective mitigation framework that successfully disentangles vision and text information to reduce hallucinations.

Abstract: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have substantially enhanced their ability across multimodal video understanding benchmarks spanning temporal, action, object, and spatial understanding. However, we identify a critical yet overlooked issue: when embedded on-screen text contradicts the visual scene, existing VLMs systematically hallucinate, prioritizing overlay textual semantics over the actual visual content. We define this phenomenon as Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination (TOIH). In this work, we propose VisualTextTrap, the first comprehensive benchmark, including large-scale human-validated samples with specifically designed evaluation metrics. In particular, we construct VisualTextTrap from widely-used public datasets using a scalable hybrid pipeline of VLMs assisted text generation and rigorous manual verification. The benchmark features 6,057 samples annotated across 88 fine-grained attributes within four dimensions, with hallucination intensity quantified on a five-level scale (L1--L5) that reflects the semantic contradiction between overlay text and visual reality. Moreover, we propose Visual Text Hallucination Mitigation Mixture-of-Experts (VTHM-MoE), a novel Vision-Text Disentanglement framework that employs a dual-encoder architecture. Concretely, four dimension-specialized expert modules spanning Temporal, Action, Object, and Spatial reasoning are first pre-trained to identify and leverage cross-modal discrepancies between textual semantics and actual video content. We develop an Adaptive Token Routing Strategy to enable dynamic expert allocation, conferring robust resistance to TOIH while preserving performance on uncontaminated videos. Extensive experiments conducted on our VisualTextTrap benchmark verify the effectiveness of VTHM-MoE, outperforming state-of-the-art counterparts with diverse video question answering tasks.

</details>


### [154] [SpatialImaginer: Towards Adaptive Visual Imagination for Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17385)
*Yian Li,Yang Jiao,Bin Zhu,Tianwen Qian,Shaoxiang Chen,Jingjing Chen,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpatialImaginer is a multimodal framework that combines text reasoning with visual imagination to improve spatial intelligence in MLLMs, achieving SOTA performance on spatial reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLMs struggle with spatial intelligence tasks due to a mismatch between spatial recognition needs and text-only reasoning. Textual representations abstract away critical geometric details needed for consistent spatial state tracking.

Method: Proposes SpatialImaginer framework with divide-and-conquer strategy: text chain-of-thought for high-level semantic planning, and visual imagination for geometry-sensitive state transformation and consistency preservation. Uses difficulty-aware data engine with closed-loop verification to train selective visual imagination invocation.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on diverse spatial intelligence benchmarks and substantially improves robustness on complex multi-step spatial reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Integrating textual reasoning with visual imagination addresses the fundamental mismatch in MLLMs' spatial intelligence capabilities, enabling more robust and consistent spatial reasoning through faithful preservation of geometric structure.

Abstract: Spatial intelligence, which refers to the ability to reason about geometric and physical structure from visual observations, remains a core challenge for multimodal large language models. Despite promising performance, recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often exhibit fragile reasoning traces in spatial intelligence tasks that involve consistent spatial state recognition. We argue that these failures stem from a mismatch between the spatial recognition mechanism and the text-only reasoning behavior of these MLLMs. Effective spatial reasoning requires low-level geometric structure to be faithfully preserved and updated throughout the reasoning process, whereas textual representations tend to abstract away precisely these critical details. To address this issue, we propose SpatialImaginer, a unified multimodal generation framework that integrates textual reasoning with visual imagination. Our framework adopts a divide-and-conquer strategy, using text chain-of-thought for high-level semantic planning and the visual imagination for geometry-sensitive state transformation and consistency preservation. To support this capability, we further introduce a difficulty-aware data engine with closed-loop verification to train the model to invoke visual imagination selectively when stable spatial state tracking is required. Extensive experiments on diverse spatial intelligence benchmarks show that SpatialImaginer achieves state-of-the-art performance and substantially improves robustness on complex multi-step spatial reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [155] [Deep learning based Non-Rigid Volume-to-Surface Registration for Brain Shift compensation Using Point Cloud](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17389)
*Eashrat Jahan Muniya,Gernot Kronreif,Ander Biguri,Wolfgang Birkfellner,Sepideh Hatamikia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Deep learning framework for non-rigid volume-to-surface registration to estimate brain shift from sparse intra-operative cortical surface observations without requiring intra-operative volumetric imaging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Brain shift during neurosurgery causes anatomical deviations from pre-operative images, compromising navigation accuracy. Existing compensation methods using intra-operative MRI/CT/ultrasound are disruptive to surgical workflow. Partial cortical surfaces from microscopes/LRS offer a practical alternative but present challenges due to sparsity and noise.

Method: Deep learning-based framework for non-rigid volume-to-surface registration using multi-scale point-based feature extraction and hierarchical deformation decoder. Integrates partial intra-operative surface information into full pre-operative point cloud domain for implicit correspondence learning and dense deformation recovery without explicit point correspondences.

Result: Achieved accurate recovery of fine-scale deformations with Endpoint Error (EPE) of 1.13 ± 0.75 mm and RMSE of 1.33 ± 0.81 mm under challenging partial-surface conditions.

Conclusion: Proposed approach enables automatic, workflow-compatible brain-shift compensation from sparse surface observations without disrupting surgery, addressing limitations of existing intra-operative imaging methods.

Abstract: Soft-tissue deformation remains a major limitation in image-guided neurosurgery, where intra-operative anatomy can deviate substantially from pre-operative imaging due to brain shift, compromising navigation accuracy and surgical safety. Existing compensation methods often rely on intra-operative MRI, CT, or ultrasound, which are disruptive and difficult to integrate repeatedly into the surgical workflow. In contrast, partial 3D cortical surfaces can be reconstructed as point clouds from stereoscopic microscopes or laser range scanners (LRS), capturing only a limited portion of the exposed cortex. This makes point cloud registration a practical alternative without interrupting surgery; however, such partial and noisy observations make deformation estimation highly challenging. In this study, we propose a deep learning-based framework for non-rigid volume-to-surface registration, enabling dense displacement field estimation from sparse intra-operative surface observations without explicit point correspondences or volumetric intra-operative imaging. The network leverages multi-scale point-based feature extraction and a hierarchical deformation decoder to capture both global and local deformations. The key contribution lies in integrating partial intra-operative surface information into the full pre-operative point cloud domain, enabling implicit correspondence learning and dense deformation recovery under limited visibility. Quantitative results demonstrate accurate recovery of fine-scale deformations, achieving an Endpoint Error (EPE) of 1.13 +/- 0.75 mm and RMSE of 1.33 +/- 0.81 mm under challenging partial-surface conditions. The proposed approach supports automatic, workflow-compatible brain-shift compensation from sparse surface observations.

</details>


### [156] [MESA: A Training-Free Multi-Exemplar Deep Framework for Restoring Ancient Inscription Textures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17390)
*Vasileios Toulatzis,Ioannis Fudos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MESA is an image restoration method for damaged inscriptions that uses well-preserved exemplar inscriptions to guide reconstruction, employing VGG19 features, Gram matrices, and OCR-based weighting to preserve style and structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ancient inscriptions often have missing or corrupted regions due to fragmentation and erosion, making them difficult to read and analyze. Existing restoration methods have limitations in handling the unique challenges of inscription recovery.

Method: MESA uses VGG19 convolutional features encoded as Gram matrices to capture exemplar texture, style, and stroke structure. It selects exemplars minimizing Mean-Squared Displacement to damaged input, uses OCR-estimated character widths for layer-wise weighting, and employs a training mask to restrict synthesis to damaged areas only.

Result: Comparative experiments demonstrate MESA's advantages over prior methods. The paper provides a practical roadmap for choosing restoration strategies based on available exemplars and metadata.

Conclusion: MESA effectively addresses limitations of previous restoration approaches by leveraging exemplar inscriptions to guide reconstruction while preserving original style and structure, offering a robust solution for epigraphic image restoration.

Abstract: Ancient inscriptions frequently suffer missing or corrupted regions from fragmentation, erosion, or other damage, hindering reading, and analysis. We review prior image restoration methods and their applicability to inscription image recovery, then introduce MESA (Multi-Exemplar, Style-Aware) -an image-level restoration method that uses well-preserved exemplar inscriptions (from the same epigraphic monument, material, or similar letterforms) to guide reconstruction of damaged text. MESA encodes VGG19 convolutional features as Gram matrices to capture exemplar texture, style, and stroke structure; for each neural network layer it selects the exemplar minimizing Mean-Squared Displacement (MSD) to the damaged input. Layer-wise contribution weights are derived from Optical Character Recognition-estimated character widths in the exemplar set to bias filters toward scales matching letter geometry, and a training mask preserves intact regions so synthesis is restricted to damaged areas. We also summarize prior network architectures and exemplar and single-image synthesis, inpainting, and Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) approaches, highlighting limitations that MESA addresses. Comparative experiments demonstrate the advantages of MESA. Finally, we provide a practical roadmap for choosing restoration strategies given available exemplars and metadata.

</details>


### [157] [Speculative Decoding for Autoregressive Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17397)
*Yuezhou Hu,Jintao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SDVG adapts speculative decoding from LLMs to autoregressive video diffusion by using an image-quality router instead of token verification, achieving 1.59-2.09x speedup while maintaining 95.7-98.1% of target model quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speculative decoding is dominant for LLM acceleration but hasn't been effectively adapted to autoregressive video generation because video blocks are continuous spatiotemporal tensors without token-level distributions for exact rejection sampling.

Method: SDVG uses a 1.3B drafter to propose candidate blocks via 4 denoising steps, then VAE-decodes and scores them with ImageReward using worst-frame aggregation (minimum per-frame reward). Blocks above threshold τ are accepted into the 14B target's KV cache; others are regenerated by the target. Key design: first block always force-rejected to anchor scene composition.

Result: On 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts (832x480), SDVG retains 98.1% of target-only VisionReward quality (0.0773 vs. 0.0788) at 1.59x speedup with τ=-0.7, and reaches 2.09x speedup with 95.7% quality retention. Consistently outperforms draft-only generation by over +17%.

Conclusion: SDVG successfully brings speculative decoding to block-based autoregressive video diffusion, offering training-free acceleration without architectural changes that can be seamlessly integrated into existing pipelines, with τ providing a smooth quality-speed Pareto frontier.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion is emerging as a promising paradigm for streaming video synthesis, with step distillation serving as the primary means of accelerating inference. Whether speculative decoding, the dominant acceleration strategy for large language models, can be effectively adapted to autoregressive video generation remains an open question, because video blocks are continuous spatiotemporal tensors with no token-level distribution for exact rejection sampling. We introduce SDVG, which brings speculative decoding to block-based autoregressive video diffusion by replacing token verification with an image-quality router. A 1.3B drafter proposes candidate blocks via four denoising steps; each block is VAE-decoded and scored by ImageReward using worst-frame aggregation--taking the minimum per-frame reward to catch single-frame artifacts that averaging would mask. Blocks scoring above a fixed threshold tau are accepted into the 14B target's KV cache; the rest are regenerated by the target. Two additional design choices prove critical: the first block is always force-rejected to anchor scene composition, and tau serves as a single knob that traces a smooth quality-speed Pareto frontier. On 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts (832x480), SDVG retains 98.1% of target-only VisionReward quality (0.0773 vs. 0.0788) at a 1.59x speedup with tau=-0.7, and reaches 2.09x at 95.7% quality retention--while consistently outperforming draft-only generation by over +17%. The framework is training-free, requires no architectural changes, and can be seamlessly integrated into existing autoregressive video generation pipelines.

</details>


### [158] [Where to Focus: Query-Modulated Multimodal Keyframe Selection for Long Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17422)
*Shaoguang Wang,Weiyu Guo,Ziyang Chen,Xuming Hu,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Q-Gate: A plug-and-play, training-free framework for long video understanding that dynamically routes modalities during keyframe selection based on query intent, outperforming SOTA methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLMs struggle with long video understanding due to computational costs. Existing keyframe selection methods use either visual-only metrics (ineffective for narrative queries) or static fusion of heuristics (introduces modal noise for visual tasks), creating a "one-size-fits-all" bottleneck.

Method: Q-Gate treats keyframe selection as dynamic modality routing with three expert streams: Visual Grounding (local details), Global Matching (scene semantics), and Contextual Alignment (subtitle-driven narratives). A Query-Modulated Gating Mechanism uses LLM reasoning to assess query intent and dynamically allocate attention weights across experts, activating relevant modalities while muting irrelevant ones.

Result: Extensive experiments on LongVideoBench and Video-MME across multiple MLLM backbones show Q-Gate substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by effectively suppressing modality-specific noise.

Conclusion: Q-Gate provides a robust, highly interpretable solution for scalable video reasoning by intelligently routing modalities based on query intent, breaking the bottleneck of current "one-size-fits-all" approaches.

Abstract: Long video understanding remains a formidable challenge for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) due to the prohibitive computational cost of processing dense frame sequences. Prevailing solutions, which select a keyframe subset, typically rely on either a single visual-centric metric (e.g., CLIP similarity) or a static fusion of heuristic scores. This ``one-size-fits-all'' paradigm frequently fails: visual-only metrics are ineffective for plot-driven narrative queries, while indiscriminately incorporating textual scores introduces severe ``modal noise'' for purely visual tasks. To break this bottleneck, we propose Q-Gate, a plug-and-play and training-free framework that treats keyframe selection as a dynamic modality routing problem. We decouple the retrieval process into three lightweight expert streams: Visual Grounding for local details, Global Matching for scene semantics, and Contextual Alignment for subtitle-driven narratives. Crucially, Q-Gate introduces a Query-Modulated Gating Mechanism that leverages the in-context reasoning of an LLM to assess the query's intent and dynamically allocate attention weights across the experts. This mechanism intelligently activates necessary modalities while ``muting'' irrelevant ones, thereby maximizing the signal-to-noise ratio. Extensive experiments on LongVideoBench and Video-MME across multiple MLLM backbones demonstrate that Q-Gate substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. By effectively suppressing modality-specific noise, it provides a robust, highly interpretable solution for scalable video reasoning.

</details>


### [159] [Long-CODE: Isolating Pure Long-Context as an Orthogonal Dimension in Video Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17428)
*Zhijiang Tang,Jiaxin Qi,Bing Zhao,Jianqiang Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new evaluation framework for long videos, arguing that current metrics designed for short videos fail to capture long-range characteristics like narrative consistency. It introduces Long-CODE benchmark and shot-based metrics that better align with human judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As video generation models advance to produce longer videos, existing evaluation metrics (designed for short videos) are inadequate. They focus on frame-level visual quality and short-term smoothness but fail to assess essential long-range attributes like narrative richness and global causal consistency.

Method: 1) Introduces long-video attribute corruption tests to expose limitations of existing metrics; 2) Designs novel long-video metrics based on shot dynamics sensitive to long-range characteristics; 3) Creates Long-CODE dataset with human annotations specifically for genuine long-range attributes; 4) Conducts extensive experiments comparing proposed metrics with human judgments.

Result: The proposed metrics achieve state-of-the-art correlation with human judgments for long-video evaluation. The Long-CODE benchmark successfully isolates and measures long-range characteristics that traditional metrics miss.

Conclusion: Long-video evaluation should be disentangled from short-video assessment as they measure orthogonal dimensions. The proposed framework complements existing short-video standards, establishing a holistic and unbiased evaluation paradigm for video generation models.

Abstract: As video generation models achieve unprecedented capabilities, the demand for robust video evaluation metrics becomes increasingly critical. Traditional metrics are intrinsically tailored for short-video evaluation, predominantly assessing frame-level visual quality and localized temporal smoothness. However, as state-of-the-art video generation models scale to generate longer videos, these metrics fail to capture essential long-range characteristics, such as narrative richness and global causal consistency. Recognizing that short-term visual perception and long-context attributes are fundamentally orthogonal dimensions, we argue that long-video metrics should be disentangled from short-video assessments. In this paper, we focus on the rigorous justification and design of a dedicated framework for long-video evaluation. We first introduce a suite of long-video attribute corruption tests, exposing the critical limitations of existing hort-video metrics from their insensitivity to structural inconsistencies, such as shot-level perturbations and narrative shuffling. To bridge this gap, we design a novel long-video metric based on shot dynamics, which is highly sensitive to the long-range testing framework. Furthermore, we introduce Long-CODE (Long-Context as an Orthogonal Dimension for video Evaluation), a specialized dataset designed to benchmark long-video evaluation, with human annotations isolated specifically to genuine long-range characteristics. Extensive experiments show that our proposed metrics achieve state-of-the-art correlation with human judgments. Ultimately, our metric and benchmark seamlessly complement existing short-video standards, establishing a holistic and unbiased evaluation paradigm for video generation models.

</details>


### [160] [DEM Refinement and Validation on the Lunar Surface Using Shape-from-Shading with Chandrayaan-2 OHRC Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17436)
*Aaranay Aadi,Jai Gopal Singla,Nitant Dube*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Shape from Shading (SfS) framework enhances lunar DEM resolution using Chandrayaan-2 OHRC imagery, providing new topographic data beyond stereo limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve sub-metre resolution lunar digital elevation models (DEMs) by overcoming stereo baseline limitations and revealing fine-scale topographic features that are currently unresolved.

Method: Apply SfS to independent OHRC images of lunar regions, using a three-stage parameter sweep on SfS smoothness weight across three test sites (Cyrillus crater, Vikram landing region, lunar south pole).

Result: Measurable topographic enhancement, especially in surface slope statistics, revealing previously unresolved fine-scale crater morphology. Identified limitations include reduced SfS sensitivity with large pitch angle separation and variable enhancement quality due to partial footprint coverage.

Conclusion: SfS can effectively enhance lunar DEMs as both a refinement tool and source of new topographic data, though performance depends on imaging geometry and coverage.

Abstract: This study presents a Shape from Shading (SfS) framework to enhance sub-metre resolution lunar digital elevation models (DEMs) using imagery from the Orbiter High Resolution Camera (OHRC) aboard Chandrayaan-2. The framework applies SfS to an independent OHRC image of the same region, enabling SfS not just as a refinement tool, but as a source of new topographic data, unconstrained by stereo baseline limitations. The method is applied across three lunar sites, including the Cyrillus crater, the Vikram landing region, and the lunar south pole (Mons Mouton), with a systematic three-stage parameter sweep on the SfS smoothness weight. Results show measurable topographic enhancement, particularly in surface slope statistics, revealing fine-scale crater morphology previously unresolved. A limiting case is also characterized, where large pitch angle separation between the shading image and stereo pair reduces SfS sensitivity, and partial footprint coverage of the shading image is identified as a factor influencing spatially variable enhancement quality.

</details>


### [161] [Attention Is not Everything: Efficient Alternatives for Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17439)
*Nur Mohammad Kazi,Ibteshum Khaled,Md. Luthful Hasan Galib,Ali Faruk Shihab,Md. Rakibul Islam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A comprehensive review paper that categorizes and analyzes non-Transformer computer vision methods, examining their efficiency, scalability, interpretability, and robustness compared to Transformer-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the dominance of Transformer-based models in recent computer vision advancements, many non-Transformer methods remain competitive. The paper aims to provide a systematic overview of these alternative approaches to understand their current state and future potential.

Method: The authors conduct a literature review of 40 selected papers, organizing non-Transformer methods into categories including convolution-based models, MLP-based models, state-space-based approaches, and others. They analyze these methods across multiple dimensions: computational efficiency, scalability, interpretability, and robustness.

Result: The review presents a comprehensive taxonomy of non-Transformer computer vision methods, identifying their strengths and weaknesses relative to Transformer-based approaches. It highlights that many non-Transformer methods remain viable competitors in terms of efficiency, scalability, interpretability, and robustness.

Conclusion: Non-Transformer methods continue to offer valuable alternatives to Transformer-based models in computer vision. The review provides insights into current challenges and opportunities for future research, suggesting that diverse architectural approaches beyond Transformers merit continued exploration and development.

Abstract: Recently computer vision has seen advancements mainly thanks to Transformer-based models. However many non-Transformer methods are still doing well being a direct competition of Transformer-based models. This review tries to present a comprehensive taxonomy of such methods and organize these methods into categories like convolution-based models, MLP-based models, state-space-based and more. These methods are looked at in terms of how efficient they are, how well they scale, how easy they are to understand and how robust they are. A total of 40 papers were chosen for this study. The goal is to give a view of non-Transformer methods and find out what challenges and opportunities exist for future computer vision research.

</details>


### [162] [HyKey: Hyperspectral Keypoint Detection and Matching in Minimally Invasive Surgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17446)
*Alexander Saikia,Chiara Di Vece,Zhehua Mao,Sierra Bonilla,Chloe He,Joao Ramalhinho,Tobias Czempiel,Sophia Bano,Danail Stoyanov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HyKey model uses hyperspectral imaging to improve keypoint detection and matching in minimally invasive surgery, outperforming RGB-based methods in texture-poor surgical environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RGB-based keypoint detection struggles in surgical scenes due to poor texture and complex illumination. The authors investigate whether hyperspectral imaging can provide better results for 3D reconstruction in minimally invasive surgery.

Method: Developed HyKey, a hybrid 3D-2D CNN that extracts spatial-spectral features from hyperspectral images. Trained using synthetic homographic augmentation and epipolar geometry constraints on a dual-camera RGB-HSI laparoscopic dataset of ex-vivo organs with calibrated poses.

Result: HSI-based model outperformed RGB baselines (SuperPoint, ALIKE) on registered RGB frames, achieving 96.62% mean matching accuracy and 67.18% mean average accuracy at 10 degrees on pose estimation.
