However, existing datasets and estimation methods lack the high-resolution granularity required for complex aerial spaces. Additionally, current schemes suffer from poor generalization and heavy reliance on environmental priors. To address these gaps, this paper introduces FARM, a pioneering foundation model for unified aerial radio map estimation. This model is supported by a newly curated, high-resolution dataset featuring multi-band and multi-antenna configurations specifically for low-altitude environments. FARM utilizes a masked autoencoder to extract deep latent representations of the aerial radio environment, which then guide a diffusion-based decoder to generate high-fidelity signal distributions through iterative refinement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FARM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks and exhibits superior generalization capabilities across unseen scenarios. Ultimately, FARM serves as a critical infrastructure for low-altitude economy by enabling autonomous aerial logistics and intelligent urban networking.

</details>


### [426] [Leveraging Kernel Symmetry for Joint Compression and Error Mitigation in Edge Model Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17371)
*Anis Hamadouche,Mathini Sellathurai*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A DoF-based neural network transmission method that exploits symmetry constraints in convolutional kernels to reduce bandwidth while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable communication-efficient neural network transmission over constrained links by leveraging structured symmetry in convolutional kernels rather than transmitting all parameters.

Method: Proposes a degrees-of-freedom (DoF) based codec that transmits only unique coefficients implied by chosen symmetry groups, enabling deterministic reconstruction at receiver. Includes projection step for robustness against transmission impairments.

Result: DoF-based transmission achieves substantial bandwidth reduction while preserving significantly higher accuracy than pruning-based baselines. Central-skew symmetry provides best accuracy-compression tradeoff on MNIST and CIFAR-10 with DeepCNN.

Conclusion: Structured symmetry constraints can be effectively leveraged for reliable and efficient neural model delivery over constrained communication links, outperforming traditional pruning approaches.

Abstract: This paper investigates communication-efficient neural network transmission by exploiting structured symmetry constraints in convolutional kernels. Instead of transmitting all model parameters, we propose a degrees-of-freedom (DoF) based codec that sends only the unique coefficients implied by a chosen symmetry group, enabling deterministic reconstruction of the full weight tensor at the receiver. The proposed framework is evaluated under quantization and noisy channel conditions across multiple symmetry patterns, signal-to-noise ratios, and bit-widths. To improve robustness against transmission impairments, a projection step is further applied at the receiver to enforce consistency with the symmetry-invariant subspace, effectively denoising corrupted parameters. Experimental results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 using a DeepCNN architecture demonstrate that DoF-based transmission achieves substantial bandwidth reduction while preserving significantly higher accuracy than pruning-based baselines, which often suffer catastrophic degradation. Among the tested symmetries, \textit{central-skew symmetry} consistently provides the best accuracy-compression tradeoff, confirming that structured redundancy can be leveraged for reliable and efficient neural model delivery over constrained links.

</details>


### [427] [Physics-Aware Query-Conditioned Graph Attention Networks for Radio Map Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17414)
*Ang Li,Chengyu Liu,Yue Wang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes a physics-aware hierarchical graph attention network for transmitter-specific point-wise radio map estimation from sparse measurements, with three operating regimes for different deployment scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning-based approaches use dense map completion over predefined grids, but practical deployments need transmitter-specific RSS estimation at queried locations or refinement of existing maps after local changes.

Method: Physics-aware query-conditioned hierarchical graph attention network with local graphs for reference-to-query evidence aggregation using transmitter-referenced geometric descriptors, and global graphs for neighborhood consistency. Three regimes: direct RSS estimation, prior-conditioned residual correction, and post-hoc gated attenuation.

Result: In simulations on DeepMIMO scenario, HGAT achieves lowest RMSE and MAE among learning-based baselines in direct regime. Residual and gated regimes further reduce prior error when conventional prior estimate is available.

Conclusion: Proposed framework enables flexible transmitter-resolved point-wise radio map estimation using only measurement-side quantities, addressing practical deployment needs beyond dense grid completion.

Abstract: Radio map estimation from sparse measurements is fundamental to wireless network planning, optimization, and localized map updating. Most recent learning-based approaches formulate the problem as dense map completion over a predefined grid, whereas many practical deployments require estimating transmitter-specific received signal strength only at queried locations or refining an existing map after local changes. This paper proposes a physics-aware query-conditioned hierarchical graph attention network for transmitter-resolved point-wise radio map estimation. For each queried target--transmitter pair, the proposed encoder constructs a bounded local graph over sampled reference observations and aggregates reference-to-query evidence through transmitter-referenced geometric descriptors. A global graph then exchanges representation-level context among nearby target locations to improve neighborhood consistency without revisiting a large number of reference measurements. On top of this shared architecture, we instantiate three operating regimes: direct RSS estimation, prior-conditioned residual correction, and post-hoc gated attenuation of the learned correction. The framework uses only measurement-side quantities and does not rely on environment-side inputs. Simulations on the DeepMIMO scenario show that, in the direct regime, the proposed HGAT achieves the lowest RMSE and MAE among the evaluated learning-based baselines on all reported sites. When conventional prior estimate is available, the residual and gated regimes further reduce the prior error.

</details>


### [428] [Integrated Sensing, User Location and Orientation Estimation in RIS-Assisted Dynamic Rich Scattering Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17474)
*Anum Umer,Ivo Müürsepp,Muhammad Mahtab Alam*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: BiLSTM-based adaptive sensing and beamforming for UE localization in RIS-assisted MIMO systems in rich-scattering indoor environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Localization in rich-scattering environments is challenging due to multiple signal interactions with RIS and dynamic scattering objects, requiring adaptive sensing and beamforming strategies.

Method: Proposes a bidirectional LSTM network controller that captures temporal dependencies to adaptively sense the environment and sequentially design RIS configuration, BS beamforming, and UE beamforming vectors to progressively focus signals onto the UE.

Result: The approach achieves low localization error with robustness across various RSE conditions including different RIS installations, varying numbers of moving scattering objects, and different numbers of sensing RIS elements.

Conclusion: The biLSTM-based adaptive sensing approach effectively enables accurate UE localization in challenging rich-scattering indoor environments with dynamic scattering conditions.

Abstract: This paper investigates an uplink user equipment (UE) location and orientation estimation problem in an indoor rich-scattering environment (RSE) for a multiple-input-multiple-output (MIMO) narrowband reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS)-assisted communication system. The localization problem in RSE is challenging as the uplink pilot signal undergoes multiple interactions with the RIS and dynamic scattering objects (SOs). This paper proposes an approach where base station (BS) adaptively senses the environment with the help of RIS. Based on this sensing, it sequentially designs RIS configuration, BS beamforming and UE beamforming vectors, using the sequence of pilot transmissions from the UE to the BS, with an objective of progressively focusing them onto the UE. Towards this end, we train a bidirectional long-short term memory (biLSTM) network based controller to capture the temporal dependencies between measurements to first adaptively sense the RSE and then design RIS, BS and UE beamforming vectors to localize the UE. We evaluate the proposed approach under various RSE conditions such as various distributed RIS installations, varying number of randomly moving SOs and sensing RIS elements. Simulation results illustrate that it effectively enables adaptive sensing to achieve low localization error with robustness in various RSEs.

</details>


### [429] [Adaptive RIS Configuration Design with Environmental Sensing for User Localization in Dynamic Rich Scattering Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17485)
*Anum Umer,Ivo Müürsepp,Muhammad Mahtab Alam*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes adaptive RIS configuration using biLSTM for user localization in rich-scattering environments, achieving lower RMSE than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the challenge of accurate user localization in rich-scattering environments where electromagnetic waves undergo multiple interactions with dynamic scatterers and RIS elements, requiring adaptive configuration.

Method: Uses bidirectional LSTM model with two neural sub-networks: Bi-SEN for scattering estimation and Bi-ARULN for RIS configuration and localization. Employs Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter tuning and adaptive sensing with sequential pilot transmissions.

Result: Achieves significantly lower localization root mean squared error compared to random configuration, prestored codebook look-ups, and adaptive baselines in both SISO and MIMO RIS-assisted networks. Scales well with RIS size and network dimensions.

Conclusion: Demonstrates strong potential of RIS deployment and the proposed approach for enabling reliable location services in rich-scattering environments through adaptive learning-based localization.

Abstract: This paper addresses the problem of adaptive reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) configuration design for user localization in rich-scattering environment (RSE), where electromagnetic waves undergo multiple interactions with dynamic scatterers and RIS elements. We propose an adaptive learning-based localization approach for a distributed RIS-assisted network in a RSE using a bidirectional long-short term memory (biLSTM) model that captures temporal correlations between observations. The proposed approach actively senses the environment using sequential pilot transmissions from the base station (BS), accounting for scattering effects, and adaptively updates the RIS configuration based on prior measurements to eventually accurately estimate and minimize the user localization error. The proposed model comprises two neural sub-networks: Scattering Estimation Network (Bi-SEN), for estimation of scattering in the environment, and Adaptive RIS-Assisted User Localization Network (Bi-ARULN), for RIS configuration and localization. Bayesian optimization is used for hyperparameter tuning of the model. The simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, achieving significantly lower localization root mean squared error (RMSE compared to random configuration, prestored codebook look-ups, and adaptive baselines in both single-input-single-output (SISO) and multiple-input-multiple output(MIMO) RIS-assisted networks in RSE. The design is generalized across configurations and scales with RIS size and network dimensions. The results highlight the strong potential of RIS deployment and of the proposed approach to enable reliable location services in RSE.

</details>


### [430] [A Novel 3D Antenna Architecture with Spatial Resource Allocation for Massive MIMO HAPS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17533)
*Rozita Shafie,Omid Abbasi,Halim Yanikomeroglu,Mohammad Javad Omidi*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposed integrated spatial resource allocation and user clustering approach to mitigate spatial correlation in massive MIMO HAPS systems, using sectorized antennas and joint optimization of power/resource allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatial correlation in massive MIMO HAPS systems causes high correlation and interference among users' channel gains, which degrades system performance and needs to be addressed.

Method: Integrated approach combining spatial resource allocation and user clustering: assign same resource blocks to users with orthogonal channel gains, different blocks to non-orthogonal users; plus sectorized antenna architecture for 3D beamforming to reduce spatial correlation.

Result: Simulation results show the effectiveness of spatial resource allocation in managing spatial correlation and interference among users in massive MIMO HAPS systems.

Conclusion: The proposed integrated approach successfully addresses spatial correlation challenges in massive MIMO HAPS systems through intelligent resource allocation, user clustering, and sectorized antenna design.

Abstract: Spatial correlation poses a significant challenge in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) high-altitude platform station (HAPS) systems. The inherent spatial correlation among antenna elements on the HAPS induces high correlation and interference among users' channel gains. To mitigate this issue, we propose an integrated approach that combines spatial resource allocation and user clustering. In our proposed solution, we assign the same resource blocks to users with orthogonal channel gains, while users with non-orthogonal channel gains receive different resource blocks. Additionally, we propose a sectorized antenna architecture for the massive MIMO HAPS base station, specifically designed to directly transmit three-dimensional beams to users and reduce spatial correlation among antenna elements. This work addresses the joint optimization problem of power allocation and resource allocation to maximize the overall data rate of the massive MIMO HAPS system. Simulation results revealed the role of spatial resource allocation in managing spatial correlation and interference among users.

</details>


### [431] [Active MIMO Sensing With Exploration-Exploitation Tradeoff](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17582)
*Nadim Ghaddar,Kareem M. Attiah,Wei Yu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Active sensing framework for MIMO radar with adaptive transmit/receive beamformers designed by minimizing Bayesian Cramér-Rao Bound at each stage, addressing exploration-exploitation tradeoff through two optimization variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an adaptive beamforming strategy for MIMO radar that optimally designs transmit and receive beamformers at each sensing stage based on previous measurements, addressing the fundamental exploration-exploitation tradeoff in active sensing.

Method: Proposes BCRB minimization via Lagrangian dual optimization with two variants: exploration-centric (ensures multiple orthogonal beamforming directions) and exploitation-centric (no restriction on beamformers). Uses alternating optimization algorithm between transmit and receive beamformers, with analysis in Lagrangian dual domain showing global optimality under certain eigenvalue conditions.

Result: Algorithm converges to stationary point when each optimization is solved globally. Global optimality guaranteed under conditions related to eigenvalue multiplicity of direction matrix. Simulation results show benefits over state-of-the-art adaptive beamforming strategies.

Conclusion: The proposed BCRB-based active sensing framework effectively addresses exploration-exploitation tradeoff in MIMO radar beamforming, with theoretical guarantees of convergence and optimality under certain conditions, demonstrating practical advantages over existing methods.

Abstract: This paper develops an active sensing framework for designing the transmit and receive beamformers of a multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) radar system. In the proposed technique, the beamformers are adaptively designed in each sensing stage based on the measurements made in the previous sensing stages. The beamformers are determined by minimizing the Bayesian Cram{é}r-Rao bound (BCRB) for the estimation of the unknown sensing parameters at each stage via Lagrangian dual optimization. To address the exploration-exploitation tradeoff that is inherent to such an adaptive design, this paper proposes two variants of the BCRB optimization problem: an exploration-centric variant, that ensures that multiple orthogonal beamforming directions are probed in each sensing stage, and an exploitation-centric variant, that does not restrict the number of optimal beamformers. Each variant of the optimization problem is solved via an alternating optimization algorithm that alternates between solving for the transmit beamformers and solving for the receive beamformers. The algorithm is shown to converge to a stationary point provided that each optimization problem is solved to global optimality. Moreover, this paper studies each of the two BCRB optimization sub-problems in the Lagrangian dual domain and shows that despite the non-convexity, global optimality is guaranteed provided that certain sufficient conditions hold. The conditions pertain to the multiplicity of the eigenvalues of a specific direction matrix that can be analytically written in terms of the optimal dual variables. These conditions further imply the tightness of the semidefinite relaxation of the optimization problems. Simulation results demonstrate the benefits of the proposed BCRB-based design compared to state-of-the-art adaptive beamforming strategies.

</details>


### [432] [Conjugate Beamforming Variants for Multicasting in Cell-Free Massive MIMO Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17631)
*Alejandro de la Fuente,Adrián Espinosa,Jan García-Morales,Guillem Femenias,Felip Riera-Palou*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes scalable conjugate beamforming variants for physical-layer multicasting in cell-free massive MIMO systems, comparing classical CB, normalized CB, and enhanced CB in different user deployment scenarios to determine optimal low-complexity distributed precoding strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for efficient multicast transmission in cell-free massive MIMO systems to support broadband and multimedia services, requiring low-complexity distributed precoding solutions that can handle different user deployment scenarios.

Method: The study uses a subgroup-centric multicast framework where users are partitioned based on large-scale fading similarity. It analyzes three conjugate beamforming variants: classical CB, normalized CB (NCB), and enhanced CB (ECB). Performance is evaluated through Monte Carlo simulations of aggregated spectral efficiency under various user geometries including uniform distributions, clustered deployments, and heterogeneous scenarios.

Result: Performance strongly depends on spatial geometry: unicast is better for uniform deployments, while subgroup-based multicasting is essential for clustered and heterogeneous scenarios. NCB offers the best performance-complexity trade-off across most scenarios, while ECB provides additional gains only when sufficient channel hardening is present.

Conclusion: The study provides practical insights for selecting low-complexity distributed precoders and multicast transmission modes in CF-mMIMO systems, with NCB emerging as the most robust choice for most deployment scenarios.

Abstract: This paper studies scalable conjugate beamforming (CB) variants for physical-layer multicasting in cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (CF-mMIMO) systems. Focusing on fully distributed precoding, we analyze classical CB, normalized CB (NCB), and enhanced CB (ECB) within a subgroup-centric multicast framework. Multicast users are partitioned into subgroups based on large-scale fading similarity, which enables composite channel estimation, pilot reuse, and distributed precoding with low complexity. The performance of the different CB variants is evaluated in terms of aggregated spectral efficiency (ASE) under representative user geometries, including uniformly distributed users, spatially clustered deployments, and heterogeneous scenarios combining hotspots with more dispersed users. Monte Carlo simulations reveal a strong spatial geometry-dependent behavior: unicast transmission is preferable in uniform deployments, while subgroup-based multicasting becomes essential in clustered and heterogeneous scenarios. Among the CB-based precoders, NCB offers a robust performance-complexity trade-off across most scenarios, whereas ECB provides additional gains only when sufficient channel hardening is present. These results provide practical insights into the selection of low-complexity distributed precoders and multicast transmission modes in CF-mMIMO systems supporting broadband and multimedia services.

</details>


### [433] [Joint Detection and Velocity Estimation in OFDM-ISAC Cell-Free Massive MIMO Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18056)
*Maryam Darabi,Sergi Liesegang,Emanuele Grossi,Stefano Buzzi*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Doppler-aware sensing framework for cell-free massive MIMO networks using OFDM-based ISAC with 3D-bistatic Doppler geometry and GLRT detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address sensing challenges in high-mobility scenarios for cell-free massive MIMO networks, particularly dealing with Doppler effects and scalability issues in distributed access point environments.

Method: Develops a Doppler-aware GLRT detector incorporating 3D-bistatic Doppler geometry, uses user-target-centric AP association for scalability, estimates 3D tangential velocity components, and employs search strategies (coarse grid search, gradient-based refinement, PSO).

Result: PSO-aided detector achieves best accuracy-complexity trade-off; Doppler mismatch causes significant sensing-SNR degradation in high-mobility scenarios; more OFDM subcarriers improve frequency-domain diversity and sensing-SNR gains.

Conclusion: The proposed Doppler-aware framework effectively handles sensing in high-mobility CF-mMIMO networks, with PSO optimization providing optimal performance trade-offs, while highlighting the importance of Doppler compensation and frequency diversity.

Abstract: This paper develops a Doppler-aware sensing framework for cell-free massive MIMO (CF-mMIMO) networks operating under OFDM-based integrated sensing and communication (ISAC). The framework explicitly incorporates the 3D-bistatic Doppler geometry across distributed access points (APs) into a generalized likelihood ratio test (GLRT) detector. To address the scalability, a user-target-centric AP association approach is utilized. The 3D tangential components of the target's velocity vector are estimated, and several search and optimization strategies, including coarse grid search, gradient-based refinement, and particle swarm optimization (PSO), are developed and evaluated. The Doppler-aware GLRT statistic and receive sensing signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) are derived. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed PSO-aided detector achieves the most favorable accuracy-complexity trade-off, while Doppler mismatch can cause substantial sensing-SNR degradation in high-mobility scenarios. Additionally, leveraging more OFDM subcarriers enhances frequency-domain diversity and yields further sensing-SNR gains.

</details>


### [434] [RIS-Assisted Cell-Free Massive MIMO: RIS-MS Selection in FR1 and FR3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17634)
*Alejandro de la Fuente,Fernando Galindo,Uriel García-Bárbulo,Sandra-Noemy Arana-Alegre,Jan García-Morales*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO with intelligent user association improves spectral efficiency in FR1/FR3 bands, but training overhead can offset gains with high RIS density.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance spectral efficiency in RIS-assisted CF-mMIMO networks by addressing challenges of frequency-dependent propagation, RIS configuration optimization, and training overhead management across FR1 and FR3 frequency bands.

Method: Proposed a novel RIS-user association algorithm that assigns each RIS to a single user based on LoS connectivity, using spatially correlated Ricean fading channels and scalable P-MMSE combining in a comprehensive framework for RIS-assisted CF-mMIMO systems.

Result: The proposed RIS-user selection strategy significantly improves spectral efficiency compared to random/exhaustive configurations, especially with moderate RIS numbers. However, excessive pilot requirements can offset benefits when RIS density/element count increases, highlighting the importance of advanced channel estimation techniques.

Conclusion: Intelligent RIS-user pairing and scalable estimation methods are crucial for future 6G deployments, with FR3 bands showing particular potential for RIS-assisted CF-mMIMO when combined with advanced channel estimation to mitigate training overhead.

Abstract: This paper explores the integration of reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) into cell-free massive multiple-input-multiple-output (CF-mMIMO) networks operating in FR1 and FR3 frequency bands. We present a comprehensive framework for analyzing RIS-assisted CF-mMIMO systems under realistic propagation conditions, accounting for frequency-dependent characteristics and RIS configurations. A novel RIS-user association algorithm is proposed to optimize phase-shift settings by assigning each RIS to a single user based on line of sight (LoS) connectivity. The system model incorporates spatially correlated Ricean fading channels and employs scalable partial-minimum mean square error (P-MMSE) combining. The numerical results demonstrate that the proposed RIS-user selection strategy significantly improves the spectral efficiency compared to random or exhaustive RIS configurations, particularly when the number of RISs is moderate. We also analyze the trade-off between training overhead and performance gains, showing that excessive pilot requirements can offset benefits when RIS density or element count increases. The results highlight the potential of the FR3 bands for RIS-assisted CF-mMIMO, provided advanced channel estimation techniques are adopted to mitigate overhead. These findings emphasize the importance of intelligent RIS-user pairing and scalable estimation methods for future 6G deployments.

</details>


### [435] [Low-Complexity Tone Injection via Candidate Ranking for PAPR Reduction in OFDM and AFDM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18060)
*Yupeng Zheng,Ang Li,Jinfei Wang,Yi Ma,Rahim Tafazolli*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Novel tone injection schemes for OFDM/AFDM systems using iterative candidate ranking with time-domain peak guidance and depth-first search for improved PAPR reduction with low complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tone injection (TI) schemes for PAPR reduction suffer from fundamental limitations in performance - random candidate generation and clipping noise spectrum approaches don't achieve optimal PAPR reduction despite being distortionless techniques.

Method: Proposes iterative TI schemes that update the TI sequence via candidate ranking guided by time-domain local peaks, with depth-first search integration to exploit the tree structure of the process. Achieves complexity comparable to FFT.

Result: Simulations show over 1 dB PAPR gain compared to baseline TI schemes at comparable complexity. Consistent performance across various subcarrier numbers under controlled per-iteration complexities.

Conclusion: The proposed schemes achieve superior performance-complexity trade-off for both OFDM and AFDM systems, making them practical for real-world applications requiring efficient PAPR reduction.

Abstract: Tone injection (TI) is a promising distortionless PAPR reduction technique that incurs no spectral efficiency loss. However, state-of-the-art TI schemes based on random candidate generation or clipping noise spectrum suffer from fundamental limitations in PAPR performance. In this paper, we propose novel TI schemes compatible with both OFDM and AFDM systems. The proposed schemes iteratively update the TI sequence via a candidate ranking procedure guided by time-domain local peaks. This accurately selects effective candidates while achieving a complexity comparable to that of the fast Fourier transform. Depth-first search is further integrated to enhance PAPR performance by exploiting the tree structure of the process. Simulations demonstrate that the proposed schemes achieve over 1 dB PAPR gain over baseline TI schemes at comparable complexity. The gain is consistent across various numbers of subcarriers under controlled per-iteration complexities, confirming a superior performance-complexity trade-off for both OFDM and AFDM.

</details>


### [436] [Building Low-Altitude Communication Networks: A Digital Twin-Based Optimization Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17781)
*Boqun Huang,Yancheng Wang,Wei Guo,Zhaojie Guo,Di Wu,Ran Li,Dayang Liu,Wanshun Lan,Chuan Huang,Shuguang Cui*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: DT-MOO: A Digital Twin-based Multi-Objective Optimization framework for Low-Altitude Communication Networks that jointly optimizes coupled performance objectives like coverage, interference, and handover management through high-fidelity virtual replicas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-altitude communication networks (LACNs) operate in dynamic 3D environments with strong coupling among key performance objectives (coverage, interference, handover, sensing). Conventional experience-driven tuning and field trials are inefficient and costly due to inability to capture cross-objective interactions.

Method: DT-MOO constructs a high-fidelity digital twin that integrates realistic environmental models, electromagnetic propagation, and traffic dynamics in a unified environment. This enables joint evaluation and systematic optimization of interdependent network parameters, scoring candidate configurations by their combined effect on multiple objectives.

Result: In real-world 5G-enabled LACN experiments focusing on coverage-interference co-optimization, DT-MOO increased high-quality coverage rate from 14.0% to 52.9% across all evaluated altitudes compared to operator-provisioned baseline, while achieving net SINR gain despite local spatial trade-offs.

Conclusion: DT-MOO effectively handles coupled objectives in practical LACN deployment, demonstrating superior performance over conventional approaches and validating the framework's ability to systematically optimize interdependent network parameters in dynamic 3D environments.

Abstract: Low-altitude communication networks (LACNs) serve as the critical infrastructure of the emerging low-altitude economy (LAE), supporting services such as drone delivery and infrastructure inspection. However, LACNs operate in highly dynamic three-dimensional (3D) environments characterized by high mobility and predominantly line-of-sight (LoS) propagation, creating strong coupling among key performance objectives including coverage, interference mitigation, handover management, and sensing capability. Isolated tuning of individual objectives cannot capture these cross-objective interactions, rendering conventional approaches based on experience-driven tuning and repeated field trials inefficient and costly. To address these challenges, we propose DT-MOO, a Digital Twin-based Multi-Objective Optimization framework for LACNs. By constructing a high-fidelity virtual replica that integrates realistic environmental models, electromagnetic (EM) propagation, and traffic dynamics within a unified environment, DT-MOO enables joint evaluation and systematic optimization of interdependent network parameters, scoring candidate configurations by their combined effect on multiple objectives. As the foundational validation of the framework, we report real-world experiments in a 5G-enabled LACN focusing on coverage-interference co-optimization, where DT-MOO increases the high-quality coverage rate from 14.0% to 52.9% across all evaluated altitudes compared to an operator-provisioned, experience-based baseline, while achieving a net SINR gain under stringent criteria despite local spatial trade-offs, confirming its ability to handle coupled objectives in practical LACN deployment.

</details>


### [437] [Movable-Antenna Enabled Robust Vehicular Consumer Networks Under Imperfect CSI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17791)
*Xuhui Zhang,Chunjie Wang,Wenchao Liu,Huijun Xing,Jinke Ren,Zheng Xing,Yanyan Shen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a movable-antenna (MA) system for consumer vehicular networks to combat CSI uncertainty caused by vehicle mobility, using joint optimization of antenna positioning and beamforming to maximize worst-case sum rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High mobility of vehicular users in consumer-oriented vehicular networks creates significant channel state information uncertainty, which degrades performance of conventional fixed-position antenna systems.

Method: Developed a joint optimization framework that dynamically coordinates spatial positioning of movable antennas and transmit beamforming at base station. Used alternating optimization strategy to decompose the non-convex max-min problem into tractable subproblems solved via S-Procedure, Schur complement, and successive convex approximation techniques.

Result: Numerical evaluations confirm the proposed approach achieves substantial gains over existing benchmarks in terms of worst-case throughput.

Conclusion: Movable antennas can effectively enhance communication robustness in consumer vehicular networks under imperfect CSI conditions by dynamically adapting to channel uncertainties through coordinated optimization of antenna positioning and beamforming.

Abstract: The accelerating advancement of intelligent transportation systems has established consumer-oriented vehicular networks (CVNs) as a critical infrastructure for next-generation connected mobility. However, the high mobility of vehicular users (VUs) introduces significant channel state information (CSI) uncertainty, which severely undermines the performance of conventional fixed-position antenna systems. To address this, this paper explores the deployment of movable-antennas (MAs) to enhance communication robustness in CVNs under imperfect CSI conditions. We develop a joint optimization framework that dynamically coordinates the spatial positioning of MAs and transmit beamforming at the base station, with the objective of maximizing the worst-case sum rate across all VUs. The problem is formulated as a non-convex max-min optimization problem, subject to bounded CSI estimation errors, transmit power limits, and physical constraints on antenna displacement. By adopting an alternating optimization strategy, the original problem is decomposed into tractable subproblems, solved via techniques including the S-Procedure, Schur complement, and successive convex approximation. Numerical evaluations confirm that the proposed approach achieves substantial gains over existing benchmarks in terms of worst-case throughput.

</details>


### [438] [Low-Complexity Learning-Based Beamforming for Ultra-Massive MIMO THz Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17845)
*Sourabh Solanki,Abuzar Babikir Mohammad Adam,Chandan Kumar Sheemar,Zaid Abdullah,Eva Lagunas,George C. Alexandropoulos,Symeon Chatzinotas*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A neural network architecture for low-complexity beam training in ultra-massive MIMO THz systems that eliminates the need for constant feedback links between transmitter and receiver.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: THz communications face severe propagation losses requiring ultra-massive MIMO with highly directional beams. Codebook-based analog beamforming is practical but leads to high computational complexity due to the large number of codewords to test in beam training and alignment.

Method: Proposes an inception and residual neural network architecture trained on received signal powers using transmit/receive codewords from predefined hierarchical codebooks. The approach doesn't require constant feedback links between transmitter and receiver.

Result: Numerical investigations show the machine learning approach significantly reduces complexity compared to standard exhaustive and hierarchical beam searching methods for UM-MIMO beamforming design.

Conclusion: The neural network-based beam training provides an efficient solution for THz UM-MIMO systems, addressing the computational complexity challenges of traditional beam alignment methods while eliminating feedback requirements.

Abstract: Terahertz (THz) communications have emerged as a key technology for escalating data rates in future generation wireless networks. However, severe propagation losses at THz frequencies pose significant challenges, which can be mitigated via ultra-massive multiple-input multiple-output (UM-MIMO) systems employing highly directional transmissions. To this end, codebook-based analog beamforming constitutes a realistic solution, eliminating the need for explicit channel estimation. However, in UM-MIMO systems, the use of extremely narrow beams makes beam training and alignment increasingly challenging, leading to a substantial increase in the number of codewords to be tested and, thus, to high computational complexity. In this paper, a novel artificial neural network architecture for low-complexity beam training in UM-MIMO THz systems is presented, which does not require a constant feedback link between transmitter and receiver to obtain the best beamformer and combiner pair. An inception and residual network, which is trained based on the received signal powers using the transmit and receive codewords generated from predefined hierarchical codebooks, is designed. Our numerical investigations demonstrate that the proposed machine learning approach significantly reduces the complexity of UM-MIMO transmit and receive beamforming design, as compared to the standard exhaustive and hierarchical beam searching methods.

</details>


### [439] [Symbol-Level Mask-Compliant Hybrid Precoding for Multi-User MIMO-OFDM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17853)
*Navid Reyhanian,Parisa Ramezani,Emil Björnson*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Robust hybrid precoding design for mmWave MU-MIMO-OFDM systems with practical constraints including phase errors, OOB emissions, and power limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: mmWave MIMO systems need hybrid precoding due to hardware costs, but face challenges: OFDM causes OOB emissions, phase shifters have errors, and practical constraints like power limits and spectral masks must be addressed.

Method: Developed MMSE-based block coordinate descent algorithm that alternates between updating transmitter-side digital-RF precoders and user-side digital-RF combiners, with closed-form solutions for each subproblem.

Result: Proposed method shows clear performance improvements over benchmark schemes through extensive simulations, with computationally efficient and scalable solutions suitable for practical implementation.

Conclusion: The robust joint digital-RF precoder design effectively addresses practical mmWave system constraints while maintaining performance, offering a practical solution for next-generation networks.

Abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) technology is a crucial enabler for next-generation networks because it offers substantially greater available bandwidth. mmWave multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems cannot rely solely on fully digital precoding due to hardware costs. As a result, hybrid precoding, which combines digital baseband processing with RF precoding, has emerged as a practical solution that balances performance and implementation complexity. As mmWave links typically operate over wideband, frequency-selective channels, orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) is commonly used to mitigate dispersive effects, yet OFDM introduces practical drawbacks, including out-of-band (OOB) emissions from abrupt spectral transitions among subcarriers and additional spectral leakage induced by windowing. Moreover, nonideal phase shifters (PS) in the RF transmit precoder and the user combiner impose inherent implementation limits that result in phase errors. We investigate robust joint digital--RF precoder design for minimizing the downlink sum mean-squared error (MSE) in hybrid multi-user (MU) MIMO--OFDM systems subject to maximum transmit-power, clipping, and OOB spectral-mask constraints. The resulting optimization is nonconvex and challenging to solve. To address this, we develop a minimum mean-squared error (MMSE) based block coordinate descent (BCD) algorithm that alternates between updating the transmitter-side digital--RF precoders and the user-side digital--RF combiners. For each BCD subproblem, we propose computationally efficient and scalable, closed-form solution strategies suitable for practical implementation. Extensive simulations validate the proposed methods and show clear performance improvements over established benchmark schemes.

</details>


### [440] [Joint Phase Noise and Off-Grid Channel Estimation for AFDM Systems via Sparse Bayesian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17858)
*You Xu,Huaijin Zhang,Lixia Xiao,Guanghua Liu,Zilong Liu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A joint phase noise and channel estimation method (JPNCE-SBL) using sparse Bayesian learning for AFDM systems that addresses the coupling of oscillator phase noise and fractional shifts, avoiding high-SNR error floors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In practical AFDM systems, conventional estimators suffer from severe high-SNR error floors due to the intricate coupling between oscillator phase noise and off-grid fractional shifts, which traps them in performance limitations.

Method: Proposes JPNCE-SBL with: 1) reduced-rank subspace projection to capture dominant eigen-energy of Wiener PN process, 2) dynamic grid evolution strategy to iteratively eliminate off-grid errors without global grid densification, both integrated into a unified EM framework for joint channel and PN estimation updates.

Result: Simulation results show JPNCE-SBL significantly outperforms existing benchmarks in both NMSE and BER, closely approaching perfect channel state information performance under practical phase noise conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed JPNCE-SBL method effectively addresses the coupling challenges of phase noise and fractional shifts in AFDM systems, achieving near-optimal performance without prohibitive computational complexity.

Abstract: In practical affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) systems, the intricate coupling of oscillator phase noise (PN) and off-grid fractional shifts traps conventional estimators in a severe high-SNR error floor. To address these challenges, we propose a joint PN and channel estimation method based on sparse Bayesian learning (JPNCE-SBL). Specifically, a reduced-rank subspace projection is first introduced to capture the dominant eigen-energy of the Wiener PN process. Concurrently, a dynamic grid evolution strategy is designed to iteratively eliminate off-grid errors without requiring computationally prohibitive global grid densification. Both components are integrated into a unified Expectation-Maximization (EM) framework, where the channel and PN estimates are jointly updated at each iteration to prevent error propagation. Simulation results demonstrate that JPNCE-SBL significantly outperforms existing benchmarks in both NMSE and BER, closely approaching the perfect channel state information case under practical PN conditions.

</details>


### [441] [Ray Tracing-Enabled Digital Twin for RIS Phase Optimization: Implementation and Experimental Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17929)
*Ömer Lütfü Karakelle,Sefa Kayraklık,İbrahim Hökelek,Ali Görçin,Halim Yanıkömeroğlu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: DT-driven RIS optimization eliminates channel estimation overhead by using ray-tracing in a digital twin to compute optimal phase shifts based on location info.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RIS optimization requires complex channel estimation with high pilot overhead, creating deployment bottlenecks in time-varying wireless environments.

Method: Build digital twin using NVIDIA Sionna ray-tracing library based on 3D map, compute optimal RIS phase shifts using transceiver location info, transfer configurations to physical RIS prototype.

Result: DT-generated phase configurations significantly enhance received signal power in physical environment, validating ray-tracing model fidelity and optimization feasibility.

Conclusion: DT-driven framework successfully eliminates signaling overhead for RIS optimization, enabling real-time deployment in dynamic wireless environments through accurate ray-tracing models.

Abstract: Determining the optimal phase configurations of reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) elements typically requires complex channel estimation procedures with high pilot overhead, creating a bottleneck for real-time deployment in time-varying wireless environments. In this paper, we propose a digital twin (DT)-driven framework for RIS phase shift optimization that eliminates extensive signaling overhead associated with estimating high-dimensional RIS channels. Leveraging the NVIDIA Sionna ray-tracing library, we construct a DT of the physical environment based on a three-dimensional map. The proposed system utilizes the location information of the transceivers to compute the optimal RIS phase shift configurations within the DT. These computationally generated configurations are then transferred to a physical RIS prototype. Experimental results demonstrate that the phase configurations obtained from the DT significantly enhance the received signal power in the physical environment, validating the fidelity of the ray-tracing model and the feasibility of the proposed optimization strategy.

</details>


### [442] [A Novel CSI-RS Reporting Scheme for RIS Optimization in O-RAN-based NextG Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17938)
*Ali Fuat Sahin,Sefa Kayraklik,Ali Gorcin,Ibrahim Hokelek,Ertugrul Basar,Halim Yanikomeroglu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes a CSI-RS-based reporting scheme for RIS optimization in O-RAN architecture, experimentally validated on real testbed showing improved signal power.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RIS technology enables dynamic shaping of wireless propagation, but practical operation requires efficient CSI acquisition and reporting for real-time control in O-RAN systems.

Method: Develops CSI-RS-based reporting scheme for downlink complex channel information, establishes CCI extraction and reporting procedures, integrates RIS optimization algorithms (Hadamard and OMP) into O-RAN architecture, and validates on real testbed with open-source O-RAN and RIS prototype.

Result: Experimental validation shows notable improvements in received signal power for both near and far users, demonstrating effectiveness and practical viability of the proposed scheme.

Conclusion: The proposed CSI-RS-based reporting framework successfully enables RIS optimization in O-RAN environments, providing a practical solution for real-time control of RIS-assisted wireless systems.

Abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) technology is a promising enabler for next-generation (NextG) wireless systems, capable of dynamically shaping the propagation environment. Integrating RIS within the open radio access network (O-RAN) architecture enables flexible and intelligent control of wireless links. However, practical RIS-assisted operation requires efficient acquisition and reporting of channel state information (CSI) to support real-time control from the base station side. This paper proposes a CSI reference signal (CSI-RS)-based reporting scheme for downlink complex channel information (CCI) to facilitate RIS optimization in an O-RAN-compliant environment. The proposed framework establishing CCI extraction and CSI-RS reporting procedures is experimentally validated on a real-world testbed integrating an open-source O-RAN system with an RIS prototype operating in the n78 frequency band. Existing channel estimation-based RIS optimization algorithms, including Hadamard and orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP), are tailored for integration into the O-RAN architecture. Experimental results demonstrate notable improvements in received signal power for both near and far users, highlighting the effectiveness and practical viability of the proposed scheme.

</details>


### [443] [Paradigm Shift from Statistical Channel Modeling to Digital Twin Prediction: An Environment-Generalizable ChannelLM for 6G AI-enabled Air Interface](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18021)
*Yichen Cai,Yuelong Qiu,Jianhua Zhang,Li Yu,Yuxiang Zhang,Zhen Zhang,Guangyi Liu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: ChannelLM-driven Digital Twin Channel architecture uses AI to create real-time wireless channel predictions with strong environmental generalization, achieving 4.23 dB error reduction and 70ms latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 6G's ubiquitous connectivity demands accurate real-time channel acquisition in diverse environments. Traditional statistical models fail in online applications, and existing AI models lack both environmental generalization and end-to-end real-time prediction capabilities.

Method: Three-module architecture: 1) Low-complexity environment reconstruction using dynamic object detection and multimodal image-point cloud alignment, 2) Physically interpretable environment feature extraction, 3) ChannelLM core that maps features to generalized environment representations for multi-task channel prediction.

Result: In unseen test environments, ChannelLM reduces prediction errors by 4.23 dB in channel state information prediction compared to small-scale AI models, while achieving end-to-end inference latency of 70 milliseconds in real-world scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed ChannelLM-driven DTC architecture successfully addresses the challenges of environmental generalization and real-time prediction for 6G wireless channels, offering a promising solution for accurate and timely channel acquisition in diverse environments.

Abstract: As 6G advances, ubiquitous connectivity and higher capacity requirements of the air interface pose substantial challenges for accurate and real-time wireless channel acquisition in diverse environments. Conventional statistical channel modeling relies on offline measurement data from limited environments, struggling to support online applications facing diverse environments. To this end, the digital twin channel (DTC) has emerged as a novel paradigm that constructs a digital replica of the physical environment through high-fidelity sensing and predicts corresponding channel in real time utilizing artificial intelligence (AI) models. As the engine of DTC, existing AI models struggle to simultaneously achieve strong environmental generalization in real-world and end-to-end channel prediction for real time tasks. Therefore, this paper proposes a channel large model (ChannelLM)-driven DTC architecture comprising three modules: low-complexity and high-accuracy environment reconstruction based on dynamic object detection and multimodal alignment of image and point cloud data, physically interpretable environment feature extraction, and a ChannelLM core to mapping these features into generalized environment representations for multi-task channel prediction. Simulation results demonstrate that, in unseen test environments, compared with small-scale AI models, ChannelLM reduces prediction errors by 4.23 dB in channel state information prediction while achieving an end-to-end inference latency of 70 milliseconds in the real world.

</details>


### [444] [User Mobility Demands Near-Field Communications in Terahertz Band Wireless Networks Beyond 6G](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18040)
*Peng Zhang,Vitaly Petrov,Arjun Singh,Emil Björnson,Josep Miquel Jornet*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Mobile THz links cannot operate exclusively in far-field while maintaining broadband performance; near-field-aware designs are essential for practical mobile THz access systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: THz systems often operate in near-field due to large apertures needed for gain, but near-field operation complicates mobile system design. The paper investigates whether mobile THz links can achieve broadband performance while operating exclusively in far-field.

Method: Developed a proof-by-contradiction feasibility framework that jointly enforces far-field requirement (based on Fraunhofer distance) and reliability requirement (target SNR at worst-case distance). Derived closed-form upper bounds on far-field-feasible bandwidth for stationary and mobile links, incorporating practical misalignment through UE rotation and mobility scenarios.

Result: Stationary THz links can remain far-field-only with physically realizable apertures while supporting extremely large bandwidths. However, practical mobile THz systems cannot - far-field-feasible bandwidth becomes a severe limiting factor requiring unrealistically high UE transmit power for tens-of-GHz targets. Cross-band comparison shows far-field-only operation is attainable at sub-6GHz and mmWave for moderate bandwidths.

Conclusion: Near-field-aware designs become essential for mobile THz access systems, as exclusive far-field operation is infeasible for practical mobile THz links while maintaining broadband performance.

Abstract: Near-field propagation is often unavoidable at terahertz (THz) frequencies due to the large apertures needed for sufficient array gain, yet near-field operation complicates practical system design, especially under user mobility. This paper asks whether a mobile THz link can remain broadband, achieve the desired high rates and coverage, while operating exclusively in the radiative far field. To answer this question, we develop a proof-by-contradiction feasibility framework that jointly enforces (i) a far-field requirement based on the Fraunhofer distance and (ii) a reliability requirement specified by a target SNR at the worst-case link distance. We derive closed-form upper bounds on the far-field-feasible bandwidth for stationary and mobile links. We further incorporate practical misalignment through several UE rotation and mobility scenarios. Numerical results show that stationary THz links can remain far-field-only with physically realizable apertures while supporting extremely large bandwidths, whereas practical mobile THz systems cannot. In practically relevant mobile THz access settings, the far-field-feasible bandwidth becomes a severe limiting factor: achieving tens-of-GHz targets would require unrealistically high UE transmit power. A cross-band comparison further shows that far-field-only operation is largely attainable at sub-6~GHz and, to a significant extent, at mmWave for moderate bandwidths, while near-field-aware designs become essential for mobile THz access.

</details>


### [445] [Semi-Blind Receivers for RIS-Aided Fluid Antenna Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18138)
*Josué V. de Araújo,Gilderlan T. de Araújo,André L. F. de Almeida*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Semi-blind tensor-based receivers for RIS-aided fluid antenna systems that jointly estimate channels and symbols with reduced training overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Channel acquisition in RIS-aided FA systems is challenging due to cascaded links depending on time-varying antenna-port selections and RIS configurations, leading to high training overhead in conventional pilot-based methods.

Method: Propose semi-blind estimation framework with two hierarchical transmission protocols: Protocol 1 uses two-time-scale structure yielding PARAFAC model, Protocol 2 uses single-time-scale structure with blockwise spatial variations yielding Nested PARAFAC2 model. Develop semi-blind receivers based on trilinear alternating least squares to jointly estimate user-to-RIS channels, RIS-to-BS channels, and transmitted symbols.

Result: Derived identifiability conditions and computational complexity, revealing trade-off: PF receiver (Protocol 1) more aggressively exploits joint RIS/FA reconfiguration for stronger robustness, while NPF receiver (Protocol 2) offers flexible, lower-complexity alternative. Simulations show accurate recovery with significantly reduced training overhead.

Conclusion: Tensor-based semi-blind processing is effective for RIS-aided fluid antenna communications, achieving accurate channel and symbol recovery with reduced training overhead through exploitation of spatio-temporal diversity from FA and RIS reconfiguration.

Abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) and fluid antennas (FAs) are key technologies for enhancing spatial degrees of freedom in future wireless networks. However, channel acquisition in RIS-aided FA systems is challenging as cascaded links depend on time-varying antenna-port selections and RIS configurations, leading to high training overhead in conventional pilot-based methods. We propose a semi-blind estimation framework for this joint architecture to estimate channels and symbols concurrently. Two hierarchical transmission protocols are introduced, resulting in distinct tensor models. Protocol 1 uses a two-time-scale structure yielding a PARAFAC (PF) model, while Protocol 2 employs a single-time-scale structure with blockwise spatial variations, leading to a Nested PARAFAC2 (NPF) model. For both, we develop semi-blind receivers based on trilinear alternating least squares to jointly estimate user-to-RIS channels, RIS-to-BS channels, and transmitted symbols by exploiting spatio-temporal diversity from FA and RIS reconfiguration. We derive identifiability conditions and computational complexity, revealing a fundamental trade-off: the PF receiver (Protocol 1) more aggressively exploits joint RIS/FA reconfiguration for stronger robustness, whereas the NPF receiver (Protocol 2) offers a flexible, lower-complexity alternative. Simulations show the proposed receivers achieve accurate recovery with significantly reduced training overhead, demonstrating the effectiveness of tensor-based semi-blind processing for RIS-aided fluid antenna communications.

</details>


### [446] [Geometry-Aware Networking for Low-Altitude Economy: Movable Antennas in Space-Air-Ground Integrated Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18156)
*Heyou Liu,Bang Huang,Mohamed-Slim Alouini*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes using movable antenna (MA) technology in space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGINs) to actively adapt spatial geometry rather than passively reacting to it, addressing limitations of traditional approaches in dynamic low-altitude environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAGIN performance is increasingly limited by inability to adapt to rapidly changing spatial geometry (relative configurations of nodes, obstacles, targets) in dense low-altitude operations, shifting bottlenecks from static resource allocation to real-time geometry maintenance.

Method: Proposes a geometry-aware layered SAGIN architecture incorporating movable antenna technology: LEO provides macro-scale coverage/coordination, HAPS enables regional continuity/backhaul, and MA enables fine-grained geometry adaptation at UAV/terrestrial layers where channel dynamics are most pronounced.

Result: MA technology introduces spatial degree of freedom allowing direct adaptation of local spatial geometry at fine granularity, enhancing robustness, supporting multi-functional operations (communication, sensing, control, navigation), and enabling flexible spatial cooperation across layers.

Conclusion: Movable antenna technology provides a fundamentally new perspective for SAGIN design by enabling active geometry control rather than passive adaptation, addressing critical limitations in dynamic low-altitude environments and supporting future NTN and low-altitude economy services.

Abstract: Space--air--ground integrated networks (SAGINs) are emerging as a key foundation for future non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) and low-altitude economy services. However, their performance is increasingly limited not only by communication resources, but by the inability to adapt to rapidly changing spatial geometry. Here, spatial geometry refers to the relative configuration among network nodes, obstacles, and targets, which directly determines propagation conditions, blockage states, interference patterns, and sensing observability.This trend becomes more pronounced as low-altitude operations grow in density and complexity, causing the dominant bottleneck to shift from static resource allocation toward real-time maintenance of favorable spatial geometry across layers.In this article, we argue that movable antenna (MA) technology provides a fundamentally new perspective for SAGIN design. By enabling controlled antenna displacement, MA introduces a spatial degree of freedom that allows the network to directly adapt local spatial geometry at fine granularity, rather than passively reacting to it through beamforming or platform mobility.We present a geometry-aware, layered SAGIN architecture, where Low-Earth-Orbit (LEO) provides macro-scale coverage and coordination, High-Altitude Platform Stations (HAPS) enables regional continuity and backhaul support, and MA is incorporated into the layered design to enable fine-grained geometry adaptation, particularly at unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and terrestrial layers where local channel dynamics are most pronounced. We further discuss how such geometry control enhances robustness, supports multi-functional operation spanning communication, sensing, control, and navigation, and enables more flexible spatial cooperation across layers.

</details>


### [447] [Cramér-Rao Bound Optimization for Near-Field ISAC with Extended Targets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18166)
*Zongyao Zhao,Zhaolin Wang,Lincong Han,Liang Xu,Jing Jin,Yuanwei Liu,Kaibin Huang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Geometry-aware transmit design for extended targets in near-field ISAC using parametric modeling and reduced-dimensional SDR optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Near-field ISAC requires moving beyond point-target models when targets have non-negligible spatial extent, necessitating geometry-aware approaches for extended targets.

Method: Developed parametric extended target model with center, orientation, and size parameters; formulated CRB for geometric parameters; identified exact ET-aware reduced subspace for lifted covariance; implemented reduced-dimensional SDR under SINR and power constraints.

Result: Simulations show lower CRB values than point-target and geometry-agnostic baselines, with substantially reduced runtime for large arrays.

Conclusion: The proposed geometry-aware transmit design effectively handles extended targets in near-field ISAC, achieving better estimation performance and computational efficiency compared to traditional approaches.

Abstract: Near-field integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) requires target models beyond the point-target abstraction when the target has a non-negligible spatial extent. In this letter, a geometry-aware transmit design is developed for a parametric extended target (ET) described by its center, orientation, and size under spherical-wave propagation. The CRB for the geometric parameters is formulated around a nominal ET state, an exact ET-aware reduced subspace is identified for the lifted covariance formulation, and a reduced-dimensional semidefinite relaxation (SDR) is developed under signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) and power constraints. Simulation results show lower CRB values than point-target and geometry-agnostic baselines together with substantially reduced runtime for large arrays.

</details>


### [448] [A Novel Piecewise Atmospheric Attenuation Model for Free Space Optical Links in Vertical Heterogeneous Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18207)
*Eylem Erdogan,Mohammed Elamassie,Ibrahim Altunbas,Gunes Karabulut Kurt,Murat Uysal,Halim Yanikomeroglu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A unified atmospheric attenuation model for FSO communications in VHetNets that accounts for multiple weather phenomena across vertical paths, validated against MODTRAN simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional single-coefficient Beer-Lambert models are too simplistic for FSO communications in VHetNets, as they handle atmospheric conditions like clouds, fog, rain, and aerosols in isolation rather than accounting for their cumulative effects across multiple atmospheric layers.

Method: Developed a unified attenuation model that incorporates aerosols, fog, rain, cloud layers, and drizzle while accounting for zenith angle, providing holistic estimates of cumulative power loss across atmospheric layers in vertical and slant paths.

Result: The model shows several-decibel attenuation variations across representative weather scenarios, with predictions staying within 1 dB of layer-resolved MODTRAN simulations, validating its accuracy.

Conclusion: The proposed unified model provides accurate and practical attenuation estimates for VHetNet link-budget studies, addressing the limitations of conventional simplified atmospheric models for FSO communications.

Abstract: Free-space optical (FSO) communication is emerging as a key backhaul technology for next-generation vertical heterogeneous networks (VHetNets), whose architecture spans satellites, high-altitude platform stations (HAPS), unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), and terrestrial nodes. Along these vertical and slant paths, optical beams traverse successive atmospheric layers that may contain clouds, fog, rain, and aerosols, conditions that conventional single-coefficient Beer-Lambert models typically handle only in isolation. Instead of such simplified formulas, we present a unified attenuation model that incorporates aerosols, fog, rain, cloud layers, and drizzle, accounts for the zenith angle, and provides a holistic estimate of the cumulative power loss across atmospheric layers. Numerical results show several-decibel attenuation variations across representative weather scenarios, while the difference between the proposed model predictions and the layer-resolved MODTRAN simulations remains within 1 dB, thereby validating the accuracy of the proposed model and its practical relevance for VHetNet link-budget studies.

</details>


### [449] [WiFo-MiSAC: A Wireless Foundation Model for Multimodal Sensing and Communication Integration via Synesthesia of Machines (SoM)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18255)
*Xuanyu Liu,Shijian Gao,Boxun Liu,Xiang Cheng,Liuqing Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: WiFo-MiSAC is a task-agnostic foundation model that tokenizes heterogeneous wireless signals into unified space using self-supervised pre-training with SS-DMoE architecture, achieving SOTA performance across beam prediction and channel estimation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning-based wireless methods struggle with generalization due to fragmented processing of communication and sensing data, lacking unified approaches for integrated sensing and communication systems.

Method: Proposes WiFo-MiSAC foundation model with tokenization of heterogeneous signals into unified space, using shared-specific disentangled mixture-of-experts (SS-DMoE) architecture to decouple modality-shared and modality-specific representations, combined with masked reconstruction and contrastive alignment for self-supervised pre-training.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across downstream tasks including beam prediction and channel estimation, demonstrates robust few-shot adaptation, and enables seamless integration of new modalities.

Conclusion: WiFo-MiSAC serves as a scalable backbone for future integrated sensing and communication systems, addressing generalization challenges through unified signal processing and flexible modality integration.

Abstract: Current learning-based wireless methods struggle with generalization due to the fragmented processing of communication and sensing data. WiFo-MiSAC addresses this as a task-agnostic foundation model that tokenizes heterogeneous signals into a unified space for self-supervised pre-training. A shared-specific disentangled mixture-of-experts (SS-DMoE) architecture is employed to decouple modality-shared and modality-specific representations, facilitating interaction without cross-modal interference. By combining masked reconstruction with contrastive alignment, the model achieves state-of-the-art performance across downstream tasks, including beam prediction and channel estimation. Experimental results demonstrate robust few-shot adaptation and seamless integration of new modalities, positioning WiFo-MiSAC as a scalable backbone for future integrated sensing and communication systems.

</details>


### [450] [Passive RIS Is Not Silent: Revisiting Performance Limits Under Thermal Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18263)
*Farjam Karim,Deepak Kumar,Prathapasinghe Dharmawansa,Nurul Huda Mahmood,Arthur Sousa de Sena,Matti-Latva-aho*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Passive RIS thermal noise, often neglected, significantly impacts 6G wireless system performance; analytical framework with closed-form expressions reveals substantial discrepancies when noise is ignored.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While passive RIS architectures are assumed virtually noiseless due to lack of active components, this idealized assumption can lead to misleading performance evaluations in 6G networks. The paper revisits this assumption to demonstrate that thermal noise from passive RIS elements, though often neglected, significantly affects system performance.

Method: Proposes a tractable approximated analytical framework that incorporates RIS-induced thermal noise into the system and derives closed-form expressions for key performance metrics such as outage probability and throughput.

Result: Simulation results validate the approximated analysis and highlight substantial performance discrepancies that arise when RIS thermal noise is ignored. The framework reveals important trade-offs between receiver and RIS noise.

Conclusion: The results offer valuable insights into the trade-offs between receiver and RIS noise, guiding the development of robust and efficient 6G communication systems by properly accounting for passive RIS thermal noise effects.

Abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) have emerged as a promising solution for enabling energy-efficient and flexible spectrum usage in wireless communication, particularly in the context of sixth-generation (6G) networks. While passive RIS architectures are widely regarded as virtually noiseless due to the lack of active components, this idealized assumption can lead to misleading performance evaluations. In this paper, we revisit this assumption and demonstrate that the thermal noise generated by passive RIS elements, though often neglected, can significantly affect system performance. We propose a tractable approximated analytical framework that incorporates RIS-induced thermal noise into the system and derive closed-form expressions for key performance metrics, such as outage probability and throughput. Simulation results validate our approximated analysis and highlight the substantial performance discrepancies that arise when RIS thermal noise is ignored.
  Our results offer valuable insights into the trade-offs between receiver and RIS noise, guiding the development of robust and efficient 6G communication systems.

</details>


### [451] [Impact of CSIR, SIC, and Hardware Impairments on the Ergodic Rate of Downlink RSMA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18269)
*Farjam Karim,Deepak Kumar,Prathapasinghe Dharmawansa,Nurul Huda Mahmood,Arthur Sousa de Sena,Matti Latva-aho*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: RSMA outperforms NOMA in downlink systems under practical impairments like imperfect CSIR, SIC, and hardware issues, with hardware impairments becoming the main bottleneck at high power.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze RSMA's performance under real-world impairments (imperfect CSIR, SIC, hardware issues) and compare it with NOMA for next-generation wireless networks.

Method: Derived closed-form expressions for ergodic rate, energy efficiency, sum-rate, and Jain's fairness index under practical impairments, validated through numerical simulations.

Result: At low power, imperfect CSIR is dominant; at high power, hardware impairments become primary bottleneck. RSMA consistently outperforms NOMA in rate, fairness, and sum-rate even under severe impairments.

Conclusion: RSMA is a robust multiple access candidate for next-gen networks, emphasizing the importance of incorporating fairness alongside rate and energy efficiency in design objectives.

Abstract: This work investigates the ergodic rate performance analysis of rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) in a downlink communication system under practical impairments. Closed-form expressions are derived for key performance metrics such as ergodic rate, energy efficiency, sum-rate, and Jains fairness index, capturing the joint effects of imperfect channel state information at the receiver (CSIR), imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC), and hardware impairments. Numerical simulations validate the accuracy of the analytical expressions and reveal several insightful trends. At low transmit powers, imperfect CSIR is the dominant performance-limiting factor, followed by hardware impairments and imperfect SIC. However, as the transmit power increases, hardware impairments become the primary bottleneck, with the impact of imperfect CSIR gradually diminishing, and imperfect SIC becoming a more prominent bottleneck. Moreover, RSMA consistently outperforms non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) in terms of ergodic rate, fairness, and sum-rate, even under severe non-idealities. These findings underscore the importance of incorporating fairness as a core design objective alongside rate and energy efficiency, positioning RSMA as a robust and strong multiple access candidate for next-generation wireless networks.

</details>


### [452] [RSMA-Aided Full-Duplex Networks Under Imperfect CSI and SIC: Performance Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18279)
*Farjam Karim,Nurul Huda Mahmood,Deepak Kumar,Arthur Sousa de Sena,Matti-Latva-aho*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Analysis of FD-enhanced RSMA system under practical constraints shows imperfect CSI hurts low-power performance while imperfect SIC degrades high-power performance, with CCI and SI cancellation being critical factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze the performance of full-duplex enhanced rate-splitting multiple access systems under realistic practical constraints including imperfect channel state information, imperfect successive interference cancellation, co-channel interference, and self-interference.

Method: Derived closed-form expressions for key performance metrics (outage probability, throughput) for both uplink and downlink users, considering CCI from uplink to downlink and modeling SI channel as random variable. Validated with Monte Carlo simulations.

Result: Imperfect CSI significantly affects system at low transmit power but effect weakens as power increases. Imperfect SIC becomes more detrimental at high power, causing severe degradation. Neglecting CCI and assuming perfect SI cancellation leads to substantial performance overestimation. SI cancellation factor must be carefully selected.

Conclusion: Practical imperfections significantly impact FD-RSMA performance. System designers must carefully consider imperfect CSI at low power, imperfect SIC at high power, and properly account for CCI and SI cancellation to avoid performance overestimation and achieve FD technology's full potential.

Abstract: This work investigates a full-duplex (FD)-enhanced Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) system under practical constraints, including imperfect channel state information (CSI) and successive interference cancellation (SIC). We derive closed-form expressions for key performance metrics, such as outage probability and throughput, for both uplink and downlink users. The analysis considers co-channel interference (CCI) from uplink to downlink users and models the self-interference (SI) channel as a random variable. Monte Carlo simulations validate the analytical results and highlight the impact of system imperfections on RSMA-FD performance. At low transmit power, imperfect CSI significantly affects the system, though this effect weakens as power increases. In contrast, imperfect SIC becomes more detrimental at high transmit power, causing severe degradation. Additionally, neglecting CCI and assuming perfect SI cancellation leads to substantial overestimation of performance. Lastly, we demonstrate that the SI cancellation factor must be carefully selected to suppress interference effectively. Otherwise, a poor choice limits the full potential of FD technology.

</details>


### [453] [Quasi-Constant Modulus Design for Nonlinearity-Tolerant Geometric Shaped Four Dimensional Modulation Format](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18435)
*Junzhe Xiao,Zekun Niu,Lyu Li,Minghui Shi,Weisheng Hu,Lilin Yi*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes QCM-QAM 4D modulation formats with high spectral efficiencies (9-13 bit/4D-sym) that improve nonlinear tolerance in fiber optic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design nonlinearity-tolerant 4D modulation formats for fiber optic communications by leveraging quasi-constant modulus properties to combat fiber nonlinearities.

Method: Analyzes QCM property and designs QCM-based QAM constellations (QCM-QAM) with spectral efficiencies of 9, 11, and 13 bit/4D-sym. Evaluates in unrepeatered WDM systems over SSMF and NZDSF fibers.

Result: QCM-QAM shows robust nonlinear tolerance with higher optimal launch power and improved SNR. Achieves GMI gains of 0.22-0.24 bit/4D-sym and transmission reach extensions of 0.9-1.8% across different SE levels and fiber types.
