Conclusion: QCM-QAM modulation formats effectively enhance nonlinear tolerance in fiber optic systems, providing performance improvements in both SSMF and NZDSF fibers across multiple spectral efficiency levels.

Abstract: In this paper, the quasi-constant modulus (QCM) property is analyzed and leveraged in the design of nonlinearity-tolerant four-dimensional (4D) modulation formats. Accordingly, we propose a family of QCM-based quadrature amplitude modulation (QCM-QAM) constellations with high spectral efficiencies (SEs) of 9, 11, and 13 bit/4D-sym, respectively. The quasi-constant modulus design theoretically enhances tolerance to fiber nonlinearities. Meanwhile, QCM-QAM is evaluated in an unrepeatered wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) system over both standard single-mode fiber (SSMF) and non-zero dispersion-shifted fiber (NZDSF). Across all SEs, QCM-QAM demonstrates robust nonlinear tolerance in both SSMF and NZDSF. This is evidenced by a consistent shift of the optimal launch power toward higher values and a significant improvement in effective signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). QCM-QAM also delivers generalized mutual information (GMI) gains of 0.22, 0.09, and 0.21 bit/4D-sym in SSMF, and 0.24, 0.10, and 0.22 bit/4D-sym, in NZDSF at the optimal transmission power, corresponding to the SEs of 9, 11, and 13 bit/4D-sym. Furthermore, QCM-QAM achieves transmission reach extensions of 1.6%, 0.9%, and 1.7% in SSMF, and 1.7%, 1.5%, and 1.8% in NZDSF, respectively, for the three SE levels.

</details>


### [454] [Warm-Start Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm for QAM MIMO Data Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18479)
*Soumyadip Paul,Sourav Banerjee,Debanjan Bhowmik,Neel Kanth Kundu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Hybrid quantum-classical framework using warm-start linear-ramp QAOA for MIMO data detection with higher-order QAM, achieving near-ML performance with faster convergence than standard QAOA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data detection in large-scale MIMO systems with higher-order QAM is challenging due to exponential complexity of ML detectors, especially with Gray-coded modulation that creates nonlinear symbol-to-bit mappings and HUBO problems.

Method: Proposes WSLR-QAOA (warm-start linear-ramp Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm) with structured warm-start based on low-rank semidefinite relaxation solved via block coordinate descent, plus linear ramp parameterization for QAOA optimization.

Result: Outperforms classical methods in SER, converges faster than standard QAOA, achieves near-ML performance. Validated on IBM quantum hardware with near-ML performance at low SNR and competitive accuracy at higher SNR despite hardware noise.

Conclusion: Demonstrates practical potential of HUBO-based WSLR-QAOA for large-scale MIMO data detection, showing quantum advantage in solving complex optimization problems with hybrid quantum-classical approaches.

Abstract: Data detection in large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems with higher-order quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) remains a challenging problem due to the exponential complexity of the classical maximum likelihood (ML) detector. This challenge is further amplified by Gray-coded modulation, which introduces nonlinear symbol-to-bit mappings and transforms the problem into a higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) formulation. To address this problem, this paper presents a hybrid quantum-classical detection framework that leverages a warm-start linear-ramp Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (WSLR-QAOA) for solving the resulting HUBO problem. A structured warm-start based on a low-rank semidefinite relaxation, solved via a block coordinate descent (BCD) method, provides an efficient and high-quality initialization, while a linear ramp parameterization guides the QAOA optimization. Simulation results show that the proposed framework outperforms classical methods in terms of symbol error rate (SER) and converges faster than standard QAOA, while achieving performance close to the optimal ML detector. Furthermore, the WSLR-QAOA algorithm is validated on actual IBM quantum hardware, where it achieves near-ML performance at low SNR and maintains competitive accuracy at higher SNR despite moderate degradation due to hardware noise. This demonstrates the practical potential of the HUBO-based WSLR-QAOA algorithm for large-scale MIMO data detection.

</details>


### [455] [Joint Scheduling of Multi-Band Radar Sensing and DNN Inference for Cross-Stage Parallelism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18520)
*Yanan Du,Sai Xu,Kezhi Wang,Yansha Deng*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a joint scheduling framework for multi-band radar sensing and DNN inference that exploits cross-stage parallelism to minimize end-to-end latency, unlike conventional sequential approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional designs treat radar sensing and DNN inference as two sequential stages, which leads to inefficiencies. The authors aim to minimize end-to-end latency by exploiting parallelism between sensing and inference operations across different frequency bands.

Method: The authors formulate a joint scheduling problem coupling sensing-time allocation, branch release timing, and non-preemptive multi-core DAG execution. They develop a release-aware heuristic that evaluates sensing decisions based on downstream DAG makespan impact, combined with a greedy list scheduler for multi-core DAG execution under release times.

Result: Simulation results show the proposed design effectively exploits cross-stage parallelism and reduces end-to-end latency compared to decoupled baselines in heterogeneous sensing scenarios. The study also identifies operating regimes where latency gains become limited.

Conclusion: The joint scheduling framework successfully minimizes latency by enabling parallel execution of sensing and inference operations, demonstrating significant improvements over traditional sequential approaches while providing insights into performance boundaries.

Abstract: This paper studies end-to-end latency minimization for a multi-band radar sensing and deep neural network (DNN) inference pipeline. Unlike conventional stage-wise designs that treat radar sensing and DNN inference as two sequential stages, the proposed framework exploits cross-stage parallelism by allowing the inference branch associated with a sensed band to start as soon as that band completes sensing, without waiting for all bands to finish. To characterize this interaction, we formulate a joint scheduling problem that couples sensing-time allocation, branch release timing, and non-preemptive multi-core execution of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) under sensing-feasibility, precedence, and core-capacity constraints. Since the resulting problem is combinatorial and strongly time-coupled, we further develop a release-aware heuristic that evaluates each sensing decision according to its downstream impact on the DAG makespan, together with a greedy list scheduler for multi-core DAG execution under release times. Simulation results show that the proposed design can effectively exploit cross-stage parallelism and reduce end-to-end latency relative to a decoupled baseline in many heterogeneous sensing scenarios, while also clarifying the operating regimes in which the latency gain becomes limited.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [456] [Quantized Zero-Energy RIS: Residual Phase Modeling and Outage Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16638)
*Dimitrios Tyrovolas,Sotiris A. Tegos,Kunrui Cao,Yue Xiao,Panagiotis D. Diamantoulakis,Nikos C. Sagias,Stylianos D. Asimonis,Christos K. Liaskos,George K. Karagiannidis*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper develops an analytical framework for zero-energy RIS systems with quantized phase shifts, analyzing joint energy-data rate outage probability and energy efficiency under different harvest-and-reflect schemes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-energy RISs offer energy-efficient programmable wireless environments but face challenges due to the coupling between energy harvesting and signal reflection, especially under practical hardware limitations like finite-resolution phase control.

Method: Developed a comprehensive analytical framework for zeRIS-assisted systems with quantized phase shifts and harvest-and-reflect schemes. Analyzed joint energy-data rate outage probability and energy efficiency under time switching and element splitting schemes for both transmitter-side and user-side deployments. Explicitly modeled residual phase error from quantization and incorporated its statistical properties.

Result: Quantization jointly affects both energy harvesting and signal reflection, creating non-trivial trade-offs. The framework enables accurate performance evaluation and reveals critical design trade-offs for phase resolution selection and HaR scheme choice in zeRIS-assisted networks.

Conclusion: The analytical framework provides essential insights for designing practical zeRIS-assisted wireless networks by quantifying the impact of quantization on the fundamental energy-data rate trade-offs and guiding optimal system configuration decisions.

Abstract: Zero-energy reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (zeRISs) have recently emerged as a promising solution for enabling energy-efficient and scalable programmable wireless environments (PWEs) by harvesting their operational energy from impinging radio-frequency signals. However, the operation of zeRIS-assisted systems is inherently constrained by the coupling between energy harvesting and signal reflection, a dependency that becomes more intricate under practical hardware limitations such as finite-resolution phase control. In this paper, we develop a comprehensive analytical framework for zeRIS-assisted communication systems operating under quantized phase shifts and harvest-and-reflect (HaR) schemes. Specifically, we analyze the joint energy-data rate outage probability and the energy efficiency under time switching and element splitting schemes, considering both transmitter-side and user-side deployment scenarios. By explicitly modeling the residual phase error induced by quantization and incorporating its statistical properties into the analysis, we show that quantization jointly affects energy harvesting and signal reflection, thereby inducing non-trivial trade-offs. As a result, the presented framework enables accurate performance evaluation and reveals critical design trade-offs for the selection of the phase resolution, and the applied HaR scheme in zeRIS-assisted wireless networks.

</details>


### [457] [Beyond Covariance: Generative Spatial Correlation Modeling and Channel Interpolation for Fluid Antenna Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16639)
*Zhentian Zhang,Hao Jiang,Kai-Kit Wong,Hyundong Shin,Ross Murch*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: FAS channel interpolation using AR(p) Gauss-Markov modeling achieves optimal MMSE performance with linear complexity O(N), enabling efficient channel reconstruction from sparse observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FAS requires CSI for many antenna ports, but conventional estimation has high overhead. Existing correlation-based interpolation lacks generalization ability, and fundamental limits of sparse CSI interpolation are poorly understood.

Method: Model FAS channel as pth-order autoregressive Gauss-Markov process, derive optimal MMSE estimator, establish theoretical bounds, and develop Kalman filtering/smoothing algorithm for linear-complexity interpolation.

Result: Achieves optimal MMSE performance with linear complexity O(N), provides tight lower bound on minimum observations needed for target reconstruction error, and offers scalable framework for practical FAS channel reconstruction.

Conclusion: AR(p) Gauss-Markov modeling provides principled, tunable tradeoff between complexity and accuracy, enabling theoretically grounded, efficient FAS channel interpolation that outperforms conventional correlation-based methods.

Abstract: Fluid antenna systems (FAS) enable unprecedented spatial diversity within a compact form factor by flexibly switching among high-density antenna ports. To activate this capability, channel state information (CSI) over the ports is required, which implies high estimation overhead because the number of ports is usually very large. Conventional estimation schemes tend to first estimate the CSI for a small number of ports and then infer the CSI for the remaining antenna ports by interpolation exploiting correlation characteristics. However, existing correlation-based techniques lack generalization ability, and the fundamental limits of interpolating the CSI from sparse observations remain poorly understood. This paper adopts a generative modeling framework for characterizing the channel correlation among the FAS ports that departs fundamentally from covariance-descriptive models. Specifically, we represent the spatially sampled channel as a $p$th-order autoregressive (AR) Gauss-Markov process, which provides a principled and tunable tradeoff between model complexity and approximation accuracy via the AR order. In so doing, we can characterize the limits of channel interpolation by deriving the globally optimal minimum mean-square error (MMSE) estimator and establishing a tight lower bound on the minimum number of observations required to meet a prescribed reconstruction error. To reduce the complexity of MMSE estimation, we then exploit the state-space structure due to the ${\rm AR}(p)$ model and develop a Kalman filtering/smoothing-based interpolation algorithm. The resulting method attains the optimal MMSE performance with strictly linear complexity $\mathcal{O}(N)$ with $N$ denoting the number of ports, resulting in a scalable, efficient, and theoretically grounded framework for practical FAS channel reconstruction.

</details>


### [458] [Maximal quadrics over finite fields and minimal codewords of projective Reed-Muller codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16676)
*Alain Couvreur,Rati Ludhani*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper characterizes minimal codewords of projective Reed-Muller codes of order 2 by studying maximal quadrics over finite fields, showing that except one case over F2, inclusion of rational points implies equality of quadrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the structure of projective Reed-Muller codes of order 2 by classifying their minimal codewords, which has applications in coding theory and finite geometry.

Method: The authors study quadrics over finite fields and their rational points, proving that except for one special case over F2, if two absolutely irreducible quadrics have sets of rational points where one is contained in the other, then the quadrics must be equal as projective varieties.

Result: A precise characterization of minimal codewords of projective Reed-Muller codes of order 2 is obtained, along with exact counts of minimal codewords for each possible weight.

Conclusion: The classification problem for minimal codewords of projective Reed-Muller codes of order 2 is solved by connecting it to the study of maximal quadrics, providing complete structural information about these codes.

Abstract: We study the classification of minimal codewords of projective Reed-Muller codes of order $2$. This problem is equivalent to identifying quadrics over finite fields whose set of rational points is maximal with respect to the inclusion. We prove that except one particular case over $\mathbb{F}_2$, any two absolutely irreducible quadrics whose sets of rational points are contained within one another should be equal as projective varieties. We deduce a precise characterisation of the minimal codewords of projective Reed-Muller codes of order $2$ and further give their exact number for each possible weight.

</details>


### [459] [Zak-OTFS: A Predictable Physical Layer for Communications and Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16781)
*Sandesh Rao Mattu,Nishant Mehrotra,Venkatesh Khammammetti,Robert Calderbank*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper derives mathematical foundations for predictable, non-selective carrier waveforms in Zak-OTFS, showing they are geometric modes of the Heisenberg-Weyl group and providing conditions for predictable I/O relations in wireless channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish mathematical foundations for understanding what makes carrier waveforms predictable and non-selective in wireless communications, particularly focusing on Zak-OTFS waveforms for 6G systems.

Method: Derives mathematical properties of Zak-OTFS carrier waveforms as geometric modes of the Heisenberg-Weyl group, analyzes them as common eigenvectors of maximal commutative subgroups, and establishes conditions for predictable input-output relations based on ambiguity properties.

Result: Shows that Zak-OTFS carriers are predictable and non-selective when channel delay/Doppler spreads are within specific periods, and identifies a common structure (pulse train modulated by Hadamard matrix) shared by multiple 6G waveform families including Zak-OTFS, AFDM, OTSM, and ODDM.

Conclusion: Provides necessary and sufficient conditions for predictable, non-selective wireless carriers based on ambiguity properties, revealing a unified mathematical structure underlying several proposed 6G waveforms beyond just geometric modes of the Heisenberg-Weyl group.

Abstract: This tutorial derives the mathematical foundations of what it means for a carrier waveform to be predictable and non-selective. We focus on Zak-OTFS, where each carrier waveform is a pulse in the delay-Doppler (DD) domain, formally a quasi-periodic localized function with specific periods along delay and Doppler. Viewed in the time domain, the Zak-OTFS carrier is realized as a pulse train modulated by a tone (termed a pulsone).
  We start by providing physical intuition, describing what it means for the Zak-OTFS carrier waveforms to be geometric modes of the Heisenberg-Weyl (HW) group of discrete delay and Doppler shifts that define the discrete-time communication model. In fact, we show that these geometric modes are common eigenvectors of a maximal commutative subgroup of our discrete HW group.
  When the channel delay spread is less than the delay period, and the channel Doppler spread is less than the Doppler period, we show that the Zak-OTFS input-output (I/O) relation is predictable and non-selective. Given the I/O response at one DD point in a frame, it is possible to predict the I/O response at all other points, without recourse to some mathematical model of the channel. While it may be intuitive that geometric modes of the HW group are predictable and non-selective wireless carriers, this is not a requirement. We provide a necessary and sufficient condition that depends on the ambiguity properties of the basis of carrier waveforms. In fact, we show that the structure of a pulse train modulated by a Hadamard matrix is common to several families of waveforms proposed for 6G, including Zak-OTFS, AFDM, OTSM and ODDM.

</details>


### [460] [Anti-Jamming Optimization for EM-Compliant Active RIS via Decoupling Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16885)
*Yang Cao,Wenchi Cheng,Jingqing Wang,Lifeng Wang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: EM-compliant active RIS model for anti-jamming systems that accounts for mutual coupling effects, channel correlation, and discrete phase constraints, with a low-complexity alternating optimization algorithm for practical implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wireless systems face sophisticated jamming attacks, but traditional anti-jamming techniques have limited performance. Existing RIS studies use simplified EM models that ignore critical hardware imperfections like mutual coupling and impedance mismatches, limiting practical effectiveness.

Method: Proposes an EM-compliant active RIS model incorporating EM and physical properties (mutual coupling, channel correlation, discrete phase). Develops a low-complexity alternating optimization algorithm based on decoupling architecture to eliminate mutual coupling effects and transform the coupled system into a tractable uncoupled representation.

Result: The DA-based AO algorithm significantly reduces modeling and optimization complexity, efficiently solves the problem with alternating optimization, and substantially reduces iteration overhead while maintaining anti-jamming performance.

Conclusion: The proposed EM-compliant active RIS model with decoupling architecture provides a practical, hardware-aware solution for anti-jamming systems that addresses real-world EM imperfections while maintaining computational efficiency.

Abstract: Wireless communication systems are increasingly vulnerable to sophisticated jamming attacks with the rapid evolution of jamming technologies and advanced signal processing techniques. While traditional anti-jamming techniques offer limited performance gains, active reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) have emerged as a promising channel-domain solution for improving resilience against jamming. Nonetheless, existing studies often rely on simplified electromagnetic (EM) models that do not fully capture mutual coupling (MC) and impedance mismatches in RIS hardware. In this paper, we propose an EM-compliant active (EMC-Active) RIS model for anti-jamming systems, explicitly incorporating the EM and physical properties at active RIS, such as MC effects, channel correlation, and discrete phase. To evaluate the anti-jamming performance of the proposed EMC-Active RIS, we develop a low-complexity alternating optimization (AO) algorithm based on the decoupling architecture (DA) to maximize the ergodic achievable rate. By leveraging the DA to explicitly eliminate MC effects among REs, the original coupled system is transformed into a tractable and scalable uncoupled representation. Numerical results demonstrate that the DA-based AO algorithm can significantly reduce the modeling and optimization complexity and efficiently solve the problem in an alternating manner with substantially reduced iteration overhead.

</details>


### [461] [Jointly Correlated Dual-Side Fluid Antenna System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16942)
*Zhentian Zhang,Yuanhui Wu,Kai-Kit Wong,Hao Jiang,An Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the ergodic capacity of dual-side fluid antenna systems (FAS) with joint correlation, deriving closed-form upper bounds and optimal power allocation algorithms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FAS research focuses on one-sided configurations (FAS at either transmitter or receiver only), but there's a need to understand the performance of dual-side FAS where both ends use fluid antennas to exploit spatial diversity through mutual correlation.

Method: Developed a jointly correlated dual-side channel model, derived ergodic capacity with tight closed-form upper bound, studied optimal power allocation, and proposed a practical iterative algorithm for implementation.

Result: The paper provides analytical expressions for ergodic capacity in dual-side FAS systems, establishes performance upper bounds, and develops efficient power allocation algorithms for practical deployment.

Conclusion: Dual-side FAS with joint correlation significantly enhances wireless system capacity compared to one-sided configurations, and the proposed analytical framework and algorithms enable practical implementation of these performance gains.

Abstract: Fluid antenna systems (FASs) have introduced a new paradigm for wireless system design by revealing how mutual correlation can be exploited to harvest inherent spatial diversity. While existing studies have mainly focused on one-sided FAS configurations, i.e., with FAS deployed at either the transmitter or the receiver, this work investigates the ergodic capacity of a jointly correlated dual-side FAS under statistical eigenmode transmission. Specifically, a jointly correlated dual-side channel model is developed, and the corresponding ergodic capacity together with a tight closed-form upper bound is derived. In addition, the optimal power allocation is studied, and a practical iterative algorithm is proposed for its implementation.

</details>


### [462] [Dynamic Parameter Scheduling in Soft-Hard BPGD for Lossy Source Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17124)
*Masoumeh Alinia,David G. M. Mitchell*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Dynamic scheduling of softness parameters in BPGD encoder improves LDGM code performance without exhaustive parameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The performance of soft-hard BPGD encoder for LDGM codes is highly sensitive to fixed softness parameters (β,μ), requiring exhaustive empirical sweeps for tuning, which is computationally expensive.

Method: Introduce dynamic scheduling framework where softness parameters (β,μ) change during decimation process, starting soft for exploration and hardening for convergence, similar to simulated annealing. Use linear and exponential schedules integrated with soft-hard BPGD without increasing complexity.

Result: Numerical experiments show improved rate-distortion performance and reduced non-convergence compared to constant-parameter baselines, while eliminating expensive grid searches for optimal parameter pairs.

Conclusion: Dynamic scheduling of softness parameters in soft-hard BPGD encoder provides better performance and eliminates the need for exhaustive parameter tuning, making the approach more practical for LDGM-based lossy source coding.

Abstract: We investigate lossy source coding based on a soft-decision belief propagation guided decimation (BPGD) encoder for low-density generator matrix (LDGM) codes, referred to as \emph{soft-hard BPGD}. The performance of this encoder is highly sensitive to the choice of ``softness'' parameters, typically denoted by $(β,μ)$, which are conventionally tuned via exhaustive empirical sweeps. To reduce this burden and to better align the algorithm with the evolving graphical structure during decimation, we introduce a \emph{dynamic scheduling} framework in which $(β,μ)$ are not fixed globally but change as decimation progresses. The schedule starts in a softer regime to encourage exploration and gradually hardens toward the end to promote convergence, similar to simulated annealing. We consider linear and exponential schedules, discuss their physical interpretation via an effective temperature viewpoint, and explain how they integrate with soft-hard BPGD without changing the order of magnitude of its complexity. Numerical experiments with irregular and semi-regular LDGM ensembles indicate improved rate-distortion performance and reduced non-convergence compared to constant-parameter baselines, while largely eliminating expensive grid searches for a single best pair $(β,μ)$.

</details>


### [463] [MAGRPO: Accelerated MARL Training for Fluid Antenna-Assisted Wireless Network Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17379)
*Wanzhe Wang,Tong Zhang,Hao Xu,Shuai Wang,Rui Wang,Kai-Kit Wong*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes MAGRPO algorithm for optimizing fluid antenna positions, beamforming, and power allocation in wireless networks, achieving comparable performance to MAPPO with 30-40% faster training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fluid antenna systems enable position-flexible elements for better channel conditions, but optimizing FA positions, beamforming, and power allocation is challenging due to non-convexity and lack of BS coordination.

Method: Formulates the problem as decentralized partially observable Markov decision process, proposes multi-agent group relative policy optimization (MAGRPO) under CTDE paradigm, replacing critic network with group relative advantage estimation to reduce computational complexity.

Result: FA-assisted networks achieve multiple-fold sum-rate enhancement over fixed antenna networks. MAGRPO attains sum-rates comparable to MAPPO while reducing training time by 30-40%.

Conclusion: MAGRPO provides efficient optimization for FA-assisted wireless networks with significant performance gains over traditional fixed antenna systems and reduced computational overhead compared to existing methods.

Abstract: Fluid antenna system (FAS) becomes a promising paradigm for next-generation wireless networks, which enables position-flexible antenna elements that can dynamically adjust to more favorable channel conditions. However, the optimization of fluid antenna (FA) positions, beamforming, and power allocation in FA-assisted wireless networks is challenging, due to the non-convexity and the lack of base station (BS) coordination. In this paper, we first formulate this challenging optimization problem as a decentralized partially observable Markov decision process, and then propose a multi-agent group relative policy optimization (MAGRPO) algorithm under the centralized training decentralized execution (CTDE) paradigm. Compared with multi-agent proximal policy optimization (MAPPO), MAGRPO replaces the critic network with group relative advantage estimation. This design reduces computational complexity by nearly half under parameter sharing. Furthermore, we derive a variance upper bound of the cumulative reward, which scales with network parameters, e.g., the number of BSs, users, and FAs. Simulation results show that compared with wireless networks with fixed antenna positions, FA-assisted wireless networks achieve multiple-fold sum-rate enhancement. Moreover, the proposed MAGRPO attains sum-rates comparable to those of MAPPO in testing, while reducing training time by $30\% \sim 40\%$.

</details>


### [464] [CSI Compression for Massive MIMO-OFDM: Mismatch-Aware Rate-Distortion Trade-offs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17426)
*Bumsu Park,Youngmok Park,Chanho Park,Namyoon Lee*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes a robust reverse water-filling (RRWF) algorithm for CSI compression in wideband massive MIMO systems when there's covariance mismatch at the decoder, improving distortion performance over conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In wideband frequency division duplex massive MIMO systems, channel state information (CSI) compression is crucial, but conventional compression methods assume perfect covariance knowledge at both encoder and decoder. In practice, the base station reconstructs CSI using imperfect covariance models, leading to performance degradation with covariance mismatch.

Method: The authors derive an achievable mismatched Gaussian rate-distortion characterization using a Gaussian test channel and mismatched MMSE reconstruction rule. For the shared-eigenvector regime (common eigenbasis with mismatched eigenvalues), the problem decouples across modes, enabling a robust reverse water-filling (RRWF) allocation computed via bisection and per-mode root finding.

Result: Simulations using wideband massive MIMO covariance models demonstrate that RRWF consistently improves reconstruction distortion and end-to-end mean square error compared to conventional reverse water-filling under covariance mismatch conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed RRWF algorithm provides a robust solution for CSI compression in practical massive MIMO systems where covariance mismatch exists, offering significant performance gains over traditional compression methods that assume perfect statistical knowledge.

Abstract: We study channel state information (CSI) compression for wideband frequency division duplex massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) when the base station (BS) reconstructs CSI using an imperfect covariance model. Under matched second-order statistics, remote rate--distortion theory yields transform coding with reverse water-filling (RWF) over covariance eigenmodes. With decoder-side covariance mismatch, however, this allocation is no longer end-to-end optimal. We derive an achievable mismatched Gaussian rate--distortion characterization based on a Gaussian test channel and a mismatched minimum mean square error (MMSE) reconstruction rule. In a shared-eigenvector regime (common eigenbasis, mismatched eigenvalues), the problem decouples across modes and leads to a robust reverse water-filling (RRWF) allocation computable via bisection and per-mode root finding. Simulations using wideband massive MIMO covariance models show that RRWF consistently improves reconstruction distortion and end-to-end mean square error relative to conventional RWF under mismatch.

</details>


### [465] [About Optimal Prefix Codes over Countably Infinite Alphabets: Probabilistic Intervals for the Codeword Lengths Assignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17443)
*Hongyang Liu,Wei Yan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: For infinite-alphabet sources, the paper establishes probability intervals where optimal code length equals k, and provides a simpler criterion for distributions with code lengths l_i = i.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous work on optimal coding for infinite alphabets had limitations, particularly for anti-uniform sources where verification required extensive information. The authors aim to develop more efficient criteria for determining optimal code lengths in infinite-alphabet scenarios.

Method: The authors prove mathematical theorems for discrete memoryless sources with countably infinite alphabets. They establish probability intervals for the largest symbol probability that guarantee specific optimal code lengths, and develop a verification criterion for distributions following the pattern l_i = i.

Result: For any positive integer k, there exists a probability interval such that if the largest symbol probability p₁ falls in this interval, the optimal code length equals k. Additionally, a simpler criterion is provided for verifying distributions with code lengths l_i = i, requiring less information than existing methods.

Conclusion: The paper provides improved theoretical tools for analyzing optimal coding of infinite-alphabet sources, with more efficient verification criteria that reduce the information needed compared to existing approaches for anti-uniform sources.

Abstract: For the discrete memoryless sources with a countably infinite alphabet, we prove that for any positive integer $k$, there exists a corresponding probability interval such that if the largest symbol probability $p_{1}$ falls in this interval, the optimal code length for the symbol equals $k$. Furthermore, for infinite sources, we provide a criterion to determine probability distributions whose optimal code length assignment follows the pattern $l^{best}_{i}=i$, for $i\ge 1$. Compared with the existing conclusion for anti-uniform sources, the proposed criterion requires less information for verification.

</details>


### [466] [Node-Based Soft-Output Fast Successive Cancellation List Decoding of Polar Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17482)
*Li Shen,Yongpeng Wu,Zhen Gao,Yin Xu,Xiaohu You,Xiqi Gao,Wenjun Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: SO-FSCL decoder reduces latency by 81.8% while maintaining similar soft-output performance as SO-SCL by incorporating node-based fast decoding into soft-output successive cancellation list framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The sequential decoding nature of conventional SCL introduces high decoding latency to soft-output SCL (SO-SCL) decoders, creating a need for faster soft-output polar decoders.

Method: Incorporated node-based fast decoding into SO-SCL framework, addressed soft output extraction challenges in special node decoding, proposed SO-FSCL algorithm with log-domain implementation and hardware-friendly version.

Result: SO-FSCL reduces decoding time steps by 81.8%, number of additions by 41.3%, and comparisons by 46.4% while delivering almost same soft-output performance as SO-SCL, outperforming other soft-output polar decoders in iterative decoding scenarios.

Conclusion: SO-FSCL decoder serves as an add-on extension to FSCL decoder, enabling autonomous choice between hard decisions or soft outputs while significantly reducing latency and maintaining performance.

Abstract: The soft-output successive cancellation list (SO-SCL) decoder provides a methodology for estimating the a-posteriori probability log-likelihood ratios by only leveraging the conventional SCL decoder of polar codes. However, the sequential decoding nature of SCL introduces high decoding latency to SO-SCL. In this paper, we incorporate node-based fast decoding into the SO-SCL framework. After addressing the challenge of soft output extraction in special node decoding, we proposed the soft-output fast SCL (SO-FSCL) decoding algorithm, along with its log-domain implementation and hardware-friendly version. The proposed SO-FSCL decoder can be regarded as an add-on extension to FSCL decoder, enabling us to autonomously choose whether to output only hard decisions like FSCL or to provide additional soft outputs. Latency and complexity analyses demonstrate that SO-FSCL can significantly reduce, for example, decoding time steps by 81.8\% (with unlimited resources), the number of additions by 41.3\%, and the number of comparisons by 46.4\%. Meanwhile, simulation results indicate that SO-FSCL delivers almost the same soft-output performance as SO-SCL, outperforming other soft-output polar decoders, especially in scenarios involving iterative decoding.

</details>


### [467] [WISV: Wireless-Informed Semantic Verification for Distributed Speculative Decoding in Device-Edge LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17701)
*Zixuan Liu,Zhiyong Chen,Nan Xue,Shengkang Chen,Jiangchao Yao,Meixia Tao,Wenjun Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: WISV is a distributed speculative decoding framework that uses channel-aware semantic verification instead of strict token matching, improving performance in wireless edge environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional token-level verification in distributed device-edge speculative decoding causes excessive rejections, reduces accepted sequence length, and increases interaction rounds under fluctuating wireless conditions.

Method: WISV integrates a lightweight decision head into the edge-side target LLM to dynamically evaluate speculative tokens by combining high-dimensional hidden representations with instantaneous channel state information (CSI). It uses two communication protocols: full-hidden upload and mismatch-first selective-hidden upload.

Result: WISV achieves up to 60.8% increase in accepted length, 37.3% reduction in interaction rounds, and 31.4% improvement in end-to-end latency compared to vanilla speculative decoding, with <1% task accuracy drop. Validated on hardware testbed with NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin and A40 server.

Conclusion: WISV effectively accelerates edge-deployed LLM inference by replacing rigid token-level verification with channel-aware semantic acceptance, optimizing the trade-off between verification fidelity and communication overhead in wireless environments.

Abstract: While distributed device-edge speculative decoding enhances resource utilization across heterogeneous nodes, its performance is often bottlenecked by conventional token-level verification strategies. Such rigid alignment leads to excessive rejections, significantly diminishing the accepted sequence length and increasing interaction rounds under fluctuating wireless conditions. In this paper, we propose WISV (Wireless-Informed Semantic Verification), a novel distributed speculative decoding framework that goes beyond strict token-level matching via a channel-aware semantic acceptance policy. WISV integrates a lightweight decision head into the edge-side target LLM to dynamically evaluate speculative tokens by synthesizing high-dimensional hidden representations with instantaneous channel state information (CSI). To optimize the trade-off between verification fidelity and communication overhead, we further design two tailored communication protocols: full-hidden upload and mismatch-first selective-hidden upload. Extensive simulations using a 1B drafter and an 8B target model demonstrate that WISV achieves up to a 60.8% increase in accepted length, a 37.3% reduction in interaction rounds, and a 31.4% improvement in end-to-end latency compared to vanilla speculative decoding across tested settings, while maintaining a negligible task accuracy drop (<1%). Finally, we validate WISV on a hardware testbed comprising an NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin and an A40-equipped server, confirming its real-world efficacy in accelerating edge-deployed LLM inference.

</details>


### [468] [The dimensions of Schur squares of HRS codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17864)
*Haojie Gu,Zhihao Zhu,Jun Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the Schur square dimensions of Hyperderivative Reed-Solomon (HRS) codes, providing bounds and exact formulas for specific parameter ranges, with applications to code-based cryptography.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Schur square operation is fundamental in classical and quantum coding theory, and understanding its properties for HRS codes is important for both theoretical analysis and practical applications in cryptography.

Method: The authors solve special determinants to derive lower and upper bounds for Schur square dimensions of HRS codes, then prove exact dimension formulas for specific parameter ranges where the dimension reaches the upper bound.

Result: When p ≥ t ≥ 2s and t ≤ (r+2s-1)/2, the Schur square dimension of HRS codes reaches the upper bound (2t-2s+1)s. For p ≥ t=2s and r ≥ t+1, the dimension equals t(t+1)/2, matching random codes.

Conclusion: HRS codes with specific parameter settings have Schur square dimensions that match random codes, making them potentially resistant to Schur square distinguisher attacks in code-based cryptography.

Abstract: The Schur square of linear codes over a finite field has emerged as a fundamental operation in both classical and quantum coding theory. In this paper, we investigate the Schur square problem of Hyperderivative Reed-Solomon (HRS) codes. By solving certain special determinants, we first give a lower bound and an upper bound for the dimensions of Schur squares of HRS codes, and then prove that when $p\geq t\geq 2s$ and $t\leq \frac{r+2s-1}{2}$, the dimension of the Schur square of the HRS code $HRS_{t}(\{α_{1},\dots,α_{r}\},s)$ (with length $rs$ and dimension $t$) reaches the upper bound $(2t-2s+1)s$. In particular, when $p \ge t=2s$ and $r\geq t+1$, the dimension of the Schur square equals $\frac{t(t+1)}{2}$ which is the dimension of the Schur squares of random codes with high probability. As an application in code-based cryptography, HRS codes with specific parameter settings might resist the attack of Schur square distinguisher.

</details>


### [469] [Mismatch Capacity under Stochastic Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17964)
*Francesc Molina,Albert Guillen i Fabregas*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Derives channel capacity formulas for mismatched stochastic likelihood decoding using information-spectrum approach, showing Csiszár-Narayan conjecture is tight for this case.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand channel capacity under mismatched stochastic decoding scenarios where the decoder uses a different likelihood function than the true channel, extending classical capacity results to practical mismatched situations.

Method: Uses Feinstein- and Verdú-Han-style bounds on error probability, develops information-spectrum approach to derive capacity formula as supremum over input distributions of limit inferior in probability of normalized mismatched information densities.

Result: Obtains general capacity formula for mismatched stochastic decoding, shows it reduces to classical Verdú-Han formula in matched case, proves upper-bound via expectations is achievable for discrete-memoryless channels with product metrics, confirming Csiszár-Narayan conjecture tightness.

Conclusion: Establishes comprehensive capacity theory for mismatched stochastic decoding, bridging classical information theory with practical decoding scenarios where perfect channel knowledge is unavailable.

Abstract: This manuscript investigates channel capacity under mismatched stochastic likelihood decoding. We derive Feinstein- and Verdú-Han-style bounds on the error probability coded communication. These are used to obtain a general information-spectrum formula for the channel capacity under mismatched stochastic decoding. The mismatch capacity formula is expressed as the supremum over all input distribution sequences of the limit inferior in probability of the sequence of normalized mismatched information densities. The resulting capacity formula is the mismatched analog of the channel capacity formula for the matched case by Verdú and Han. We also show that when the sequence of normalized mismatched information densities is uniformly integrable, the capacity formula admits an upper-bound as the limit of the corresponding sequence of expectations. This upper-bound is shown to be achievable for discrete-memoryless channels and product decoding metrics, showing that the Csiszár-Narayan conjecture is tight for mismatched stochastic decoders.

</details>


### [470] [Polar and Convolutional Codes for the Unequal Message Protection Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17999)
*Alexander Sauter,Riccardo Schiavone,Lucía Balsa Picado,Gianluigi Liva*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Polar and convolutional coset codes designed for unequal message protection in short blocklengths, addressing rate loss from preamble-based solutions with a two-step decoding approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the rate loss introduced by preamble-based solutions for unequal message protection (UMP) in the short blocklength regime, where traditional approaches suffer efficiency penalties.

Method: Designs polar and convolutional coset codes for UMP with conditions for message class disjointness. Proposes two-step decoding: 1) message class identification via likelihood ratio test (exact for convolutional codes, approximated for polar codes), 2) maximum/near-likelihood decoding within chosen class.

Result: Construction closely tracks finite-length benchmarks. CRC-aided polar codes perform comparably to existing polar code approaches without requiring specific code design, offering robust and spectrally efficient UMP solutions.

Conclusion: The proposed polar and convolutional coset codes with two-step decoding provide an effective solution for unequal message protection in short blocklength regimes, addressing rate loss issues while maintaining performance comparable to existing approaches.

Abstract: This paper proposes the design of polar and convolutional coset codes for the unequal message protection (UMP) in the short blocklength regime, to overcome the rate loss introduced by preamble-based solutions. After providing conditions to ensure message class disjointness, a two-step decoding architecture is proposed: it first identifies the message class via a likelihood ratio test--computable exactly for convolutional codes and approximated for polar codes--and subsequently performs maximum (or near) likelihood decoding among the codewords of the chosen message class. Numerical results show that our construction closely tracks finite-length benchmarks. Specifically, the analyzed CRC-aided polar codes perform comparable to existing polar code approaches, without requiring specific code design, while offering a robust and spectrally efficient solution for UMP scenarios.

</details>


### [471] [Order Optimal Task Allocation in Distributed Computing via Interweaved Cliques](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18232)
*Javad Maheri,K. K. Krishnan Namboodiri,Petros Elia*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes an Interweaved Clique (IC) design for distributed computing with d-uniform decomposable functions, achieving near-optimal communication and computation costs within constant factors of fundamental limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design efficient distributed computing systems where a master coordinates workers to evaluate functions that admit d-uniform decompositions, minimizing worst-case communication and computational loads across workers.

Method: First shows optimal allocation corresponds to Steiner systems S(d, k, n), then proposes Interweaved Clique (IC) design as a universal deterministic allocation framework that relaxes strict Steiner system structure while maintaining near-optimal performance.

Result: IC design achieves communication cost within constant factor 4e of the converse bound while maintaining order-optimal computation cost, establishing fundamental scaling laws for general distributed computing problems.

Conclusion: The proposed IC design provides a practical near-optimal solution for distributed computing with d-uniform decomposable functions, overcoming limitations of Steiner systems and establishing fundamental performance limits.

Abstract: We consider a distributed computing system in which a master node coordinates $N$ workers to evaluate a function over $n$ input files, where this function accepts general decomposition. In particular, we focus on the general case where the requested function admits a $d$-uniform decomposition, meaning that it can be decomposed into a set of subfunctions that each depends on a unique $d$-tuple of the $n$ files. Our objective is to design file and task allocations that minimize the worst-case communication from the master to any worker and the worst-case computational load across workers. We first show that the optimal file and task allocation with minimum communication and computation costs admits a natural characterization within combinatorial design theory: it corresponds to a Steiner system $S(t, k, v)$ with $t=d$, $v=n$, and $k \approx \frac{n}{N^{1/d}}$. However, Steiner systems are known to exist only for very restricted parameter regimes. To overcome this limitation, we propose the information-theoretic-inspired \emph{Interweaved Clique (IC) design}, a universal and deterministic allocation framework that relaxes the strict structure of Steiner systems by allowing slight variations in worker file loads. Although slightly suboptimal, the IC design achieves a communication cost within a constant factor $4e$ from our converse, while also maintaining an order-optimal computation cost, thus allowing this work to derive the fundamental scaling laws of this general distributed computing problem for a large range of parameters.

</details>


### [472] [Near-Codewords Aware Bit Flipping Decoding of QC-MDPC Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18247)
*Alessio Baldelli,Marco Baldi,Davide De Zuane,Paolo Santini*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: BF decoders for QC-MDPC codes can be modified to detect and recover from near-codeword trapping sets, achieving significant DFR reduction with minimal computational overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: BF decoders suffer from trapping sets (near-codewords) that cause decoding failures at low DFR values in QC-MDPC code-based cryptosystems like BIKE.

Method: Modify existing BF decoders to make them "aware" of near-codewords by enabling recognition and recovery from bad configurations caused by these trapping sets.

Result: Drastic reductions in DFR with minimal computational overhead; BF-Max variant outperforms BIKE's NIST competition decoders.

Conclusion: Near-codeword-aware BF decoders significantly improve decoding reliability for QC-MDPC code-based cryptography with minimal performance impact.

Abstract: Bit-Flipping (BF) decoders are a family of decoders widely employed in post-quantum cryptographic schemes based on Quasi-Cyclic Moderate-Density Parity-Check (QC-MDPC) codes, such as BIKE. BF decoders suffer from trapping sets, corresponding to low-weight error patterns that likely lead to decoding failures. For QC-MDPC codes, the most relevant family of trapping sets is that of near-codewords, which are error patterns associated to low-weight syndromes. Indeed, recent works show that error patterns having a large overlap with near-codewords are the main culprits for decoding failures at very low Decoding Failure Rate (DFR) values. In this paper, we show that any BF decoder can be tweaked and made somehow aware of near-codewords, which means being able to recognize, and recover from, bad configurations due to near-codewords. We show that this modification results in minimal computational overhead. Through intensive numerical simulations, we evaluate the effectiveness of this approach on several BF decoders, considering both toy code parameters and BIKE parameters for NIST security category 1. Our results show drastic reductions in the DFR. We also find that, with this modification, a recently proposed BF variant called BF-Max outperforms the two decoders used by BIKE within the NIST competition.

</details>


### [473] [Polar Coded Quantization for Distributed Source Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18335)
*Muhammed Yusuf Sener,Gerhard Kramer,Shlomo Shamai,Ronald Böhnke,Wen Xu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Scalar quantization + probabilistic shaping achieves Berger-Tung region corner points for Gaussian source coding; demonstrated with 5G polar codes for Wyner-Ziv polar coded quantization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve distributed source coding of Gaussian sources with mean-square error distortion by combining scalar quantization and probabilistic shaping techniques.

Method: Uses coding scheme with modulo interval, dithering, and truncated Gaussian shaping; implements with short-block-length multilevel 5G polar codes for Wyner-Ziv polar coded quantization.

Result: Achieves corner points of Berger-Tung region; Wyner-Ziv polar coded quantization substantially reduces total distortion compared to separate polar coded quantization of source blocks.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively combines quantization and shaping for distributed Gaussian source coding, demonstrating practical benefits with modern 5G polar codes.

Abstract: Scalar quantization and probabilistic shaping are applied to the distributed source coding of Gaussian sources, with mean-square error distortion. A coding scheme with a modulo interval, dithering, and truncated Gaussian shaping is shown to achieve the corner points of the Berger-Tung region. The theory is illustrated by designing short-block-length multilevel 5G polar codes for Wyner-Ziv (WZ) polar coded quantization (PCQ). WZ-PCQ substantially reduces the total distortion compared to separate PCQ of the source blocks.

</details>


### [474] [Feedforward Phase Noise Compensation for Intersymbol Interference Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18391)
*Alex Jäger,Gerhard Kramer*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Non-iterative phase noise compensation using sum-product algorithm outperforms linear MMSE filtering for ISI channels with similar complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Phase noise compensation in communication systems typically requires iterative methods. The paper aims to develop a non-iterative approach that maintains performance while reducing computational complexity.

Method: Apply sum-product algorithm (SPA) to ISI channel outputs modeled as independent Gaussian variables. Use mismatched processing with von Mises statistics at the receiver.

Result: SPA achieves higher information rates than linear minimum-mean-square-error (MMSE) filtering for three channel types: ISI-free, standard single-mode fiber, and multipath OFDM channels, while maintaining similar complexity.

Conclusion: The non-iterative SPA-based phase noise compensation method provides superior performance to linear MMSE filtering across various channel types without increasing computational complexity, making it a practical solution for real-world communication systems.

Abstract: A non-iterative phase noise compensation method based on the sum-product algorithm (SPA) is applied to the outputs of intersymbol interference (ISI) channels. The outputs are modeled as independent Gaussian random variables, and the receiver applies mismatched processing with von Mises statistics. The performance is compared with that of linear minimum-mean-square-error filtering. The SPA achieves higher information rates at similar complexity for three channel types: ISI-free, standard single-mode fiber, and multipath channels with orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing.

</details>


### [475] [Bayesian experimental design: grouped geometric pooled posterior via ensemble Kalman methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18505)
*Huchen Yang,Xinghao Dong,Jinlong Wu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes grouped geometric pooled posterior framework for Bayesian experimental design to improve accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency of amortized approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian experimental design for complex systems is limited by nested inference for expected information gain estimation. Existing methods sacrifice either accuracy (per-outer-sample inference) or efficiency (amortized inference), especially under posterior heterogeneity.

Method: Grouped geometric pooled posterior framework that partitions outer samples into groups and constructs pooled proposal for each group. Uses tailored ensemble Kalman inversion to generate proposal samples without extra forward-model evaluation cost. Includes conservative diagnostic to assess importance-sampling quality for grouping guidance.

Result: Grouping strategy improves within-group proposal-target alignment, yielding more accurate and stable estimators while keeping computational cost comparable to amortized approaches. Validated on Gaussian-linear and high-dimensional network-based model discrepancy calibration problems.

Conclusion: Proposed framework addresses accuracy-efficiency trade-off in Bayesian experimental design by grouping outer samples, improving inference quality while maintaining computational efficiency of amortized methods.

Abstract: Bayesian experimental design (BED) for complex physical systems is often limited by the nested inference required to estimate the expected information gain (EIG) or its gradients. Each outer sample induces a different posterior, creating a large and heterogeneous set of inference targets. Existing methods have to sacrifice either accuracy or efficiency: they either perform per-outer-sample posterior inference, which yields higher fidelity but at prohibitive computational cost, or amortize the inner inference across all outer samples for computational reuse, at the risk of degraded accuracy under posterior heterogeneity. To improve accuracy and maintain cost at the amortized level, we propose a grouped geometric pooled posterior framework that partitions outer samples into groups and constructs a pooled proposal for each group. While such grouping strategy would normally require generating separate proposal samples for different groups, our tailored ensemble Kalman inversion (EKI) formulation generates these samples without extra forward-model evaluation cost. We also introduce a conservative diagnostic to assess importance-sampling quality to guide grouping. This grouping strategy improves within-group proposal-target alignment, yielding more accurate and stable estimators while keeping the cost comparable to amortized approaches. We evaluate the performance of our method on both Gaussian-linear and high-dimensional network-based model discrepancy calibration problems.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [476] [BASIS: Balanced Activation Sketching with Invariant Scalars for "Ghost Backpropagation"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16324)
*Vladimer Khasia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BASIS is a memory-efficient backpropagation algorithm that decouples activation memory from batch/sequence dimensions using compressed rank-R tensors while preserving exact gradient flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional backpropagation requires O(L*BN) activation memory, creating a spatial bottleneck that limits scaling of deep neural networks. Randomized AD methods suffer from catastrophic variance.

Method: BASIS uses compressed rank-R tensors for weight updates while preserving exact error signals. Introduces Balanced Hashing to eliminate off-diagonal collision variance and Invariant Scalars to preserve spatial geometry energy norms.

Result: Reduces activation memory to O(L*RN). GPT training with R=32 achieves parity with exact backpropagation (6.575 vs 6.616 validation loss). Even extreme compression (R=1) allows convergence, proving robustness.

Conclusion: BASIS provides a practical solution to activation memory bottleneck while maintaining training stability and performance, enabling more scalable deep neural network training.

Abstract: The activation memory required for exact backpropagation scales linearly with network depth, context length, and feature dimensionality, forming an O(L * BN ) spatial bottleneck (where B is the sequence-batch cardinality and N is the feature dimension). This constraint historically throttles the scaling of deep neural networks. While randomized automatic differentiation attempts to mitigate this, it historically suffers from catastrophic variance. In this paper, we introduce BASIS (Balanced Activation Sketching with Invariant Scalars), an efficient backpropagation algorithm that fully decouples activation memory from the batch and sequence dimensions. BASIS propagates the exact error signal (dX) to preserve flawless gradient flow, but computes the weight updates (dW) using massively compressed rank-R tensors. To solve the foundational instability of sketched gradients, we propose two novel mechanisms: Balanced Hashing, which strictly eliminates off-diagonal collision variance, and Invariant Scalars, a principled bias-variance tradeoff that deterministically preserves the exact continuous energy norm of the spatial geometry. Theoretically, BASIS reduces activation memory to O(L * RN ) and heavily decreases the backward pass matrix-multiplication footprint. Empirically, training a GPT architecture for 50,000 steps validates our theoretical guarantees: at R = 32, BASIS achieves parity with (and marginally outperforms) exact backpropagation validation loss (6.575 vs. 6.616), acting as an implicit regularizer. Remarkably, the stabilized magnitude trajectory allows the model to converge smoothly even under extreme spatial compression (R = 1), proving the extreme robustness of the estimator. The code is available at https://github.com/VladimerKhasia/basis

</details>


### [477] [UniMamba: A Unified Spatial-Temporal Modeling Framework with State-Space and Attention Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16325)
*Xingsheng Chen,Xianpei Mu,Deyu Yi,Yilin Yuan,Xingwei He,Bo Gao,Regina Zhang,Pietro Lio,Siu-Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UniMamba: A unified spatial-temporal forecasting framework that combines efficient state-space dynamics with attention-based dependency learning for multivariate time series forecasting, achieving better accuracy and efficiency than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multivariate time series forecasting faces challenges with complex temporal dependencies and cross-variable interactions. Existing Transformer-based methods have quadratic computational costs, while state-space models like Mamba lack explicit temporal pattern recognition.

Method: UniMamba integrates state-space dynamics with attention-based learning: 1) Mamba Variate-Channel Encoding Layer with FFT-Laplace Transform and TCN for global temporal dependencies, 2) Spatial Temporal Attention Layer for inter-variate correlations and temporal evolution, 3) Feedforward Temporal Dynamics Layer to fuse continuous and discrete contexts.

Result: Comprehensive experiments on eight public benchmark datasets show UniMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting models in both forecasting accuracy and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: UniMamba establishes a scalable and robust solution for long-sequence multivariate time-series prediction by effectively combining the strengths of state-space models and attention mechanisms.

Abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting is fundamental to numerous domains such as energy, finance, and environmental monitoring, where complex temporal dependencies and cross-variable interactions pose enduring challenges. Existing Transformer-based methods capture temporal correlations through attention mechanisms but suffer from quadratic computational cost, while state-space models like Mamba achieve efficient long-context modeling yet lack explicit temporal pattern recognition. Therefore we introduce UniMamba, a unified spatial-temporal forecasting framework that integrates efficient state-space dynamics with attention-based dependency learning. UniMamba employs a Mamba Variate-Channel Encoding Layer enhanced with FFT-Laplace Transform and TCN to capture global temporal dependencies, and a Spatial Temporal Attention Layer to jointly model inter-variate correlations and temporal evolution. A Feedforward Temporal Dynamics Layer further fuses continuous and discrete contexts for accurate forecasting. Comprehensive experiments on eight public benchmark datasets demonstrate that UniMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting models in both forecasting accuracy and computational efficiency, establishing a scalable and robust solution for long-sequence multivariate time-series prediction.

</details>


### [478] [Annotation Entropy Predicts Per-Example Learning Dynamics in LoRA Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16332)
*Brady Steele*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRA fine-tuning causes un-learning on contested examples (high annotator disagreement), unlike full fine-tuning, across six models tested.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) differs from full fine-tuning in handling examples with high annotator disagreement, and whether it exhibits un-learning behavior on contested data.

Method: Correlate annotation entropy (from ChaosNLI's 100 labels per example) with per-example area under the loss curve (AULC) on SNLI and MNLI datasets across six models (four encoder, two decoder-only) using LoRA fine-tuning.

Result: Positive correlation between annotation entropy and AULC in all 25 conditions (Spearman ρ=0.06-0.43), with decoder-only models showing stronger correlations. The effect persists after partial-correlation controls and replicates across seeds and datasets. Noise-injection experiments support findings.

Conclusion: LoRA fine-tuning exhibits un-learning on contested examples (high annotator disagreement), a distinct pattern not seen in full fine-tuning, suggesting parameter-efficient methods handle ambiguous data differently than full fine-tuning approaches.

Abstract: We find that LoRA fine-tuning exhibits un-learning on contested examples: items with high annotator disagreement show increasing loss during training, a qualitatively distinct pattern largely absent under full fine-tuning and consistent across all six models tested (four encoder, two decoder-only). This discovery emerges from correlating annotation entropy, computed from ChaosNLI's 100 labels per example, with per-example area under the loss curve (AULC) on SNLI and MNLI. The correlation is positive in all 25 conditions tested (Spearman $ρ= 0.06$-$0.43$), with decoder-only models showing stronger correlations than encoders at matched LoRA rank. The effect survives partial-correlation controls and replicates across seeds and datasets. A preliminary noise-injection experiment is consistent with these findings.

</details>


### [479] [A Discordance-Aware Multimodal Framework with Multi-Agent Clinical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16333)
*Pegah Ahadian,Mingrui Yang,Sixu Chen,Xiaojuan Li,Qiang Guan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposed a discordance-aware multimodal framework combining ML prediction models with multi-agent reasoning to address mismatch between structural damage and pain in knee osteoarthritis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knee osteoarthritis shows discordance between imaging-based structural damage and patient-reported pain, complicating clinical interpretation and patient stratification, which existing decision support systems fail to adequately model.

Method: Multimodal framework with three modality-specific experts (CatBoost tabular model, MRI image embeddings via ResNet18, X-ray embeddings via same architecture) fused via stacking ensemble. Residual-based models estimate expected pain from structural features to compute pain-structure discordance score. Multi-agent reasoning layer interprets signals to assign OA phenotypes and generate management recommendations.

Result: Framework developed using FNIH Osteoarthritis Biomarkers Consortium baseline data to predict two progression tasks: joint space loss only progression vs non-progression, and pain only progression vs non-progression.

Conclusion: The proposed discordance-aware multimodal framework with multi-agent reasoning addresses the clinical challenge of pain-structure mismatch in knee osteoarthritis, enabling better patient stratification and phenotype-specific management recommendations.

Abstract: Knee osteoarthritis frequently exhibits discordance between structural damage observed in imaging and patient-reported symptoms such as pain. This mismatch complicates clinical interpretation and patient stratification and remains insufficiently modeled in existing decision support systems. We propose a discordance aware multimodal framework that combines machine learning prediction models with a tool grounded multi agent reasoning system. Using baseline data from the FNIH Osteoarthritis Biomarkers Consortium, we trained multimodal models to predict two progression tasks, joint space loss only progression versus non progression, and pain only progression versus non progression. The predictive system integrates three modality specific experts: a CatBoost tabular model using demographic, radiographic, MRI-derived scalar, and biomarker features; MRI image embeddings extracted using a ResNet18 backbone; and Xray embeddings derived from the same architecture. Expert predictions are fused using a stacking ensemble. Residual based models estimate expected pain from structural features, enabling the computation of a pain structure discordance score between observed and expected symptoms. A multi-agent reasoning layer interprets these signals to assign clinically interpretable OA phenotypes and generate phenotype specific management recommendations.

</details>


### [480] [Preventing overfitting in deep learning using differential privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16334)
*Alizishaan Anwar Hussein Khatri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Differential privacy approach to improve generalization in DNNs by reducing overfitting to training noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DNNs achieve SOTA performance but are vulnerable to overfitting, learning noise from training data which hurts generalization. In practice, analysts have limited data and need models that generalize well to unseen data.

Method: Using a differential-privacy based approach to improve generalization in Deep Neural Networks.

Result: Not specified in abstract (paper likely presents experimental results showing improved generalization with differential privacy approach).

Conclusion: Differential privacy can help address overfitting and improve generalization in DNNs when training data is limited.

Abstract: The use of Deep Neural Network based systems in the real world is growing. They have achieved state-of-the-art performance on many image, speech and text datasets. They have been shown to be powerful systems that are capable of learning detailed relationships and abstractions from the data. This is a double-edged sword which makes such systems vulnerable to learning the noise in the training set, thereby negatively impacting performance. This is also known as the problem of \emph{overfitting} or \emph{poor generalization}. In a practical setting, analysts typically have limited data to build models that must generalize to unseen data. In this work, we explore the use of a differential-privacy based approach to improve generalization in Deep Neural Networks.

</details>


### [481] [Beyond Verifiable Rewards: Rubric-Based GRM for Reinforced Fine-Tuning SWE Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16335)
*Jiawei Huang,Qingping Yang,Renjie Zheng,Jiaze Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a rubric-based Generative Reward Model (GRM) that provides richer learning signals for LLM agents in software engineering tasks, outperforming terminal-score-only approaches by better shaping intermediate behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current end-to-end fine-tuning for LLM agents in software engineering relies on binary terminal rewards (e.g., unit test pass/fail), which provide little guidance for shaping intermediate behaviors during multi-step interactions, limiting improvements in overall resolution process quality.

Method: Introduces a rubric-based Generative Reward Model (GRM) with human-designed rubrics indicating criteria for encouraging/discouraging specific behavioral patterns. Uses this feedback for high-quality training data collection via trajectory filtration, and applies it for Reinforced Fine-Tuning (RFT) on SWE tasks.

Result: Outperforms terminal-score-only rejection sampling: more effectively suppresses undesirable patterns while promoting beneficial ones (confirmed by case analyses), and ultimately improves final test accuracy.

Conclusion: Rubric-based GRM provides richer learning signals that better shape intermediate behaviors in LLM agents for software engineering tasks, leading to improved overall performance compared to binary terminal reward approaches.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in Large Language Model (LLM) Agents for Software Engineering (SWE) tasks, end-to-end fine-tuning typically relies on verifiable terminal rewards such as whether all unit tests pass. While these binary signals reflect whether the final solution is correct, they provide little guidance for shaping intermediate behaviors during multi-step interactions, thereby limiting improvements in the overall quality of the resolution process. To address this, we introduce a rubric-based Generative Reward Model (GRM) that provides richer learning signals. The GRM is equipped with human-designed rubrics that indicate criteria for encouraging or discouraging specific behavioral patterns, and we leverage this feedback for high-quality training data collection via trajectory filtration. When used for Reinforced Fine-Tuning (RFT) on SWE Tasks, our approach outperforms terminal-score-only rejection sampling: it more effectively suppresses undesirable patterns while promoting beneficial ones, as confirmed by case analyses, and it ultimately improves final test accuracy.

</details>


### [482] [SaFeR-Steer: Evolving Multi-Turn MLLMs via Synthetic Bootstrapping and Feedback Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16358)
*Haolong Hu,Hanyu Li,Tiancheng He,Huahui Yi,An Zhang,Qiankun Li,Kun Wang,Yang Liu,Zhigang Zeng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SaFeR-Steer is a progressive multi-turn alignment framework for MLLMs that addresses safety decay in long-context conversations through staged synthetic bootstrapping and tutor-in-the-loop GRPO training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLM safety alignment focuses on single-turn data and fixed dialogues, creating a mismatch with real-world multi-turn deployments where attackers can exploit evolving visual-text history and long-context safety decay.

Method: Progressive multi-turn alignment combining staged synthetic bootstrapping with tutor-in-the-loop GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) to train a single student model under adaptive on-policy attacks, plus TCSR (Trajectory Cumulative Safety Reward) that propagates late-turn failures to earlier turns.

Result: Substantial improvements on Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B models: single-turn safety/helpfulness improved from 48.30/45.86 to 81.84/70.77 (3B) and 56.21/60.32 to 87.89/77.40 (7B); multi-turn improved from 12.55/27.13 to 55.58/70.27 (3B) and 24.66/46.48 to 64.89/72.35 (7B), shifting failures to later turns.

Conclusion: SaFeR-Steer effectively bridges the training-deployment gap for MLLM safety in multi-turn settings, providing robustness beyond model scaling alone and releasing the STEER dataset for future research.

Abstract: MLLMs are increasingly deployed in multi-turn settings, where attackers can escalate unsafe intent through the evolving visual-text history and exploit long-context safety decay. Yet safety alignment is still dominated by single-turn data and fixed-template dialogues, leaving a mismatch between training and deployment.To bridge this gap, we propose SaFeR-Steer, a progressive multi-turn alignment framework that combines staged synthetic bootstrapping with tutor-in-the-loop GRPO to train a single student under adaptive, on-policy attacks. We also introduce TCSR, which uses trajectory minimum/average safety to propagate late-turn failures to earlier turns.I. Dataset. We release STEER, a multi-turn multimodal safety dataset with STEER-SFT (12,934), STEER-RL (2,000), and STEER-Bench (3,227) dialogues spanning 2~10 turns.II. Experiment. Starting from Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B, SaFeR-Steer substantially improves Safety/Helpfulness on both single-turn (48.30/45.86 -> 81.84/70.77 for 3B; 56.21/60.32 -> 87.89/77.40 for 7B) and multi-turn benchmarks (12.55/27.13 -> 55.58/70.27 for 3B; 24.66/46.48 -> 64.89/72.35 for 7B), shifting failures to later turns and yielding robustness beyond scaling alone.Codes are available at https://github.com/Ed-Bg/SaFeR-Steer

</details>


### [483] [SetFlow: Generating Structured Sets of Representations for Multiple Instance Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16362)
*Nikola Jovišić,Milica Škipina,Vanja Švenda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SetFlow is a generative model that creates synthetic MIL bag representations using flow matching and Set Transformers, improving performance in data-scarce tasks like mammography.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity and weak supervision limit ML performance in real-world applications like mammography. Existing MIL augmentation methods fail to capture intra-bag dependencies as they operate at instance level rather than set level.

Method: SetFlow uses flow matching paradigm with Set Transformer-inspired design to model entire MIL bags directly in representation space. It handles permutation-invariant inputs while capturing instance interactions, and is conditioned on class labels and input scale.

Result: Generated samples closely match original data distribution and improve downstream performance when used for augmentation. Training on synthetic data alone shows competitive results on large-scale mammography benchmark using MIL-PF classification pipeline.
