While uncertainty quantification can provide a means to assess the quality of adapted data, the standard framework for evaluating the quality of predicted uncertainties may not easily extend to generative models due to the common lack of ground truths (among other reasons). Even with ground truths, this evaluation is agnostic to how the generated outputs are used on the downstream task, limiting the extent to which the uncertainty reliability analysis provides insights about the utility of the uncertainties with respect to the intended use case of the adapted examples. Here, we describe how decision-theoretic uncertainty quantification can address these concerns and provide a convenient framework for evaluating the trustworthiness of generated outputs, in particular, for domain adaptation. We consider a case study in photoplethysmography time series denoising for Atrial Fibrillation classification. This formalises a well-known heuristic method of using a downstream classifier to assess the quality of generated outputs.

</details>


### [589] [A Probabilistic Consensus-Driven Approach for Robust Counterfactual Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17494)
*Marcin Kostrzewa,Maciej Zięba,Jerzy Stefanowski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel method for generating robust counterfactual explanations that remain valid across model changes using conditional normalizing flows and model ensemble consensus.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counterfactual explanations often become invalid when models change slightly, and existing robust CFE methods are limited to specific models, require costly tuning, or have inflexible robustness controls.

Method: Jointly models data distribution and plausible model decisions using probabilistic consensus over model ensemble. Trains conditional normalizing flow to capture data density under varying classifier agreement levels. Uses interpretable parameter to control robustness level at inference.

Result: Achieves superior empirical robustness while maintaining good performance across other evaluation measures compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively generates counterfactual explanations that are both plausible and stable across model changes with flexible robustness control through a single interpretable parameter.

Abstract: Counterfactual explanations (CFEs) are essential for interpreting black-box models, yet they often become invalid when models are slightly changed. Existing methods for generating robust CFEs are often limited to specific types of models, require costly tuning, or inflexible robustness controls. We propose a novel approach that jointly models the data distribution and the space of plausible model decisions to ensure robustness to model changes. Using a probabilistic consensus over a model ensemble, we train a conditional normalizing flow that captures the data density under varying levels of classifier agreement. At inference time, a single interpretable parameter controls the robustness level; it specifies the minimum fraction of models that should agree on the target class without retraining the generative model. Our method effectively pushes CFEs toward regions that are both plausible and stable across model changes. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves superior empirical robustness while also maintaining good performance across other evaluation measures.

</details>


### [590] [Contraction and Hourglass Persistence for Learning on Graphs, Simplices, and Cells](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17548)
*Mattie Ji,Indradyumna Roy,Vikas Garg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hourglass Persistence introduces a new topological descriptor that interleaves graph inclusions and contractions to overcome limitations of traditional persistent homology methods in graph neural networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional persistent homology methods in GNNs use inclusion sequences of subgraphs, which have limitations in capturing topological information. The authors identify shortcomings of this approach and seek to incorporate contractions as a complementary topological operation for better graph representation learning.

Method: The paper introduces Contraction Homology (CH) to study persistence of contraction sequences, then proposes Hourglass Persistence - a class of topological descriptors that interleave inclusion and contraction sequences. The framework extends to simplicial and cellular networks, with efficient algorithms designed for differentiable GNN pipelines.

Result: Hourglass Persistence demonstrates superior expressivity, learnability, and stability compared to traditional PH methods. The approach shows consistent empirical improvements across standard real-world graph datasets when integrated into GNN pipelines.

Conclusion: By combining inclusion and contraction sequences, Hourglass Persistence provides a more powerful topological framework for graph representation learning that addresses limitations of traditional persistent homology methods while maintaining computational efficiency and integration with neural networks.

Abstract: Persistent homology (PH) encodes global information, such as cycles, and is thus increasingly integrated into graph neural networks (GNNs). PH methods in GNNs typically traverse an increasing sequence of subgraphs. In this work, we first expose limitations of this inclusion procedure. To remedy these shortcomings, we analyze contractions as a principled topological operation, in particular, for graph representation learning. We study the persistence of contraction sequences, which we call Contraction Homology (CH). We establish that forward PH and CH differ in expressivity. We then introduce Hourglass Persistence, a class of topological descriptors that interleave a sequence of inclusions and contractions to boost expressivity, learnability, and stability. We also study related families parametrized by two paradigms. We also discuss how our framework extends to simplicial and cellular networks. We further design efficient algorithms that are pluggable into end-to-end differentiable GNN pipelines, enabling consistent empirical improvements over many PH methods across standard real-world graph datasets. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/Aalto-QuML/Hourglass}{this https URL}.

</details>


### [591] [SVL: Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning as Survival Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17551)
*Franki Nguimatsia Tiofack,Fabian Schramm,Théotime Le Hellard,Justin Carpentier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SVL reframes goal-conditioned RL as survival learning by modeling time-to-goal distributions, enabling stable value estimation via hazard models trained with maximum likelihood.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard temporal-difference approaches to goal-conditioned RL are unstable and sample-inefficient due to bootstrapping. The paper aims to provide a more stable probabilistic alternative.

Method: Survival Value Learning (SVL) models time-to-goal as probability distributions, expresses value functions as discounted sums of survival probabilities, and trains hazard models via maximum likelihood on both event and right-censored trajectories. Introduces three practical value estimators including finite-horizon truncation and binned infinite-horizon approximations.

Result: SVL combined with hierarchical actors matches or surpasses strong hierarchical TD and Monte Carlo baselines on offline GCRL benchmarks, particularly excelling on complex, long-horizon tasks.

Conclusion: SVL provides a stable, probabilistic alternative to TD-based GCRL that effectively handles long-horizon objectives through survival learning formulations and hazard modeling.

Abstract: Standard approaches to goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) that rely on temporal-difference learning can be unstable and sample-inefficient due to bootstrapping. While recent work has explored contrastive and supervised formulations to improve stability, we present a probabilistic alternative, called survival value learning (SVL), that reframes GCRL as a survival learning problem by modeling the time-to-goal from each state as a probability distribution. This structured distributional Monte Carlo perspective yields a closed-form identity that expresses the goal-conditioned value function as a discounted sum of survival probabilities, enabling value estimation via a hazard model trained via maximum likelihood on both event and right-censored trajectories. We introduce three practical value estimators, including finite-horizon truncation and two binned infinite-horizon approximations to capture long-horizon objectives. Experiments on offline GCRL benchmarks show that SVL combined with hierarchical actors matches or surpasses strong hierarchical TD and Monte Carlo baselines, excelling on complex, long-horizon tasks.

</details>


### [592] [Diverse Dictionary Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17568)
*Yujia Zheng,Zijian Li,Shunxing Fan,Andrew Gordon Wilson,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces "diverse dictionary learning" to recover identifiable components of latent variables from observational data when full identifiability is impossible, using set-theoretic operations and structural diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for recovering latent variables from observational data require strong assumptions (linearity, auxiliary supervision, functional constraints) that are rarely verifiable in practice and break down under mild violations, creating uncertainty about reliable understanding of hidden variables.

Method: Proposes diverse dictionary learning framework that shows intersections, complements, and symmetric differences of latent variables linked to arbitrary observations, along with latent-to-observed dependency structure, are identifiable up to appropriate indeterminacies without strong assumptions. Uses set algebra to construct structured views of hidden world (e.g., genus-differentia definitions).

Result: Theoretical results show identifiable components can be recovered with guarantees, and when sufficient structural diversity is present, full identifiability of all latent variables is achievable. A simple inductive bias during estimation enables these identifiability benefits and can be integrated into most models.

Conclusion: The approach makes identifiability actionable in real-world scenarios by focusing on what can be recovered with guarantees when full identifiability is unattainable, providing a practical framework for understanding hidden variables from observational data.

Abstract: Given only observational data $X = g(Z)$, where both the latent variables $Z$ and the generating process $g$ are unknown, recovering $Z$ is ill-posed without additional assumptions. Existing methods often assume linearity or rely on auxiliary supervision and functional constraints. However, such assumptions are rarely verifiable in practice, and most theoretical guarantees break down under even mild violations, leaving uncertainty about how to reliably understand the hidden world. To make identifiability actionable in the real-world scenarios, we take a complementary view: in the general settings where full identifiability is unattainable, what can still be recovered with guarantees, and what biases could be universally adopted? We introduce the problem of diverse dictionary learning to formalize this view. Specifically, we show that intersections, complements, and symmetric differences of latent variables linked to arbitrary observations, along with the latent-to-observed dependency structure, are still identifiable up to appropriate indeterminacies even without strong assumptions. These set-theoretic results can be composed using set algebra to construct structured and essential views of the hidden world, such as genus-differentia definitions. When sufficient structural diversity is present, they further imply full identifiability of all latent variables. Notably, all identifiability benefits follow from a simple inductive bias during estimation that can be readily integrated into most models. We validate the theory and demonstrate the benefits of the bias on both synthetic and real-world data.

</details>


### [593] [Recovery Guarantees for Continual Learning of Dependent Tasks: Memory, Data-Dependent Regularization, and Data-Dependent Weights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17578)
*Liangzu Peng,Uday Kiran Reddy Tadipatri,Ziqing Xu,Eric Eaton,René Vidal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides theoretical analysis of continual learning with statistical recovery guarantees for various CL paradigms under explicit task dependency modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual learning theory remains underdeveloped despite empirical advances. The core challenge is handling varying data distributions across tasks, which requires understanding task dependency. Current theoretical work often provides vacuous bounds in practical scenarios.

Method: Models task dependency explicitly using nonlinear regression tasks, assuming current task data is a nonlinear transformation of previous data. Proves statistical recovery guarantees (estimation error bounds) for several practical CL paradigms including experience replay with data-independent regularization, data-independent loss balancing weights, replay with data-dependent weights, and continual learning with data-dependent regularization like knowledge distillation.

Result: Provides informative statistical recovery guarantees (estimation error bounds) for multiple CL paradigms under natural assumptions. These bounds are non-vacuous in cases where prior theoretical work gives vacuous bounds.

Conclusion: Explicit modeling of task dependency enables meaningful theoretical analysis of continual learning. The proposed framework yields informative statistical guarantees for practical CL methods, advancing the theoretical understanding of continual learning beyond vacuous bounds.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) is concerned with learning multiple tasks sequentially without forgetting previously learned tasks. Despite substantial empirical advances over recent years, the theoretical development of CL remains in its infancy. At the heart of developing CL theory lies the challenge that the data distribution varies across tasks, and we argue that properly addressing this challenge requires understanding this variation--dependency among tasks. To explicitly model task dependency, we consider nonlinear regression tasks and propose the assumption that these tasks are dependent in such a way that the data of the current task is a nonlinear transformation of previous data. With this model and under natural assumptions, we prove statistical recovery guarantees (more specifically, bounds on estimation errors) for several CL paradigms in practical use, including experience replay with data-independent regularization and data-independent weights that balance the losses of tasks, replay with data-dependent weights, and continual learning with data-dependent regularization (e.g., knowledge distillation). To the best of our knowledge, our bounds are informative in cases where prior work gives vacuous bounds.

</details>


### [594] [How Much Data is Enough? The Zeta Law of Discoverability in Biomedical Data, featuring the enigmatic Riemann zeta function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17581)
*Paul M. Thompson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a theoretical framework using spectral analysis and Riemann zeta function to predict when additional data will improve scientific discovery in biomedical AI, explaining cross-over phenomena where simpler models work better with small data while complex models excel with large data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current biomedical AI development relies on empirical scaling curves without theoretical guidance on when performance improves, saturates, or exhibits cross-over behavior. There's a need for principled understanding of data scaling effects on discovery.

Method: A scaling-law framework based on spectral structure of data covariance operators, task-aligned signal projections, and learned representations. Performance metrics like AUC are expressed as cumulative signal-to-noise energy across spectral modes, following zeta-like scaling laws governed by power-law decay of covariance spectra.

Result: The framework predicts cross-over regimes where simpler models perform best at small sample sizes, while higher-capacity or multimodal encoders outperform with sufficient data. Representation learning methods improve sample efficiency by concentrating signal into earlier stable modes.

Conclusion: The zeta law provides a principled way to anticipate when scaling data, improving representations, or adding modalities will accelerate discovery, with applications in multimodal disease classification, imaging genetics, fMRI, and topological data analysis.

Abstract: How much data is enough to make a scientific discovery? As biomedical datasets scale to millions of samples and AI models grow in capacity, progress increasingly depends on predicting when additional data will substantially improve performance. In practice, model development often relies on empirical scaling curves measured across architectures, modalities, and dataset sizes, with limited theoretical guidance on when performance should improve, saturate, or exhibit cross-over behavior.
  We propose a scaling-law framework for cross-modal discoverability based on spectral structure of data covariance operators, task-aligned signal projections, and learned representations. Many performance metrics, including AUC, can be expressed in terms of cumulative signal-to-noise energy accumulated across identifiable spectral modes of an encoder and cross-modal operator. Under mild assumptions, this accumulation follows a zeta-like scaling law governed by power-law decay of covariance spectra and aligned signal energy, leading naturally to the appearance of the Riemann zeta function. Representation learning methods such as sparse models, low-rank embeddings, and multimodal contrastive objectives improve sample efficiency by concentrating useful signal into earlier stable modes, effectively steepening spectral decay and shifting scaling curves.
  The framework predicts cross-over regimes in which simpler models perform best at small sample sizes, while higher-capacity or multimodal encoders outperform them once sufficient data stabilizes additional degrees of freedom. Applications include multimodal disease classification, imaging genetics, functional MRI, and topological data analysis. The resulting zeta law provides a principled way to anticipate when scaling data, improving representations, or adding modalities is most likely to accelerate discovery.

</details>


### [595] [STEP-PD: Stage-Aware and Explainable Parkinson's Disease Severity Classification Using Multimodal Clinical Assessments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17611)
*Md Mezbahul Islam,John Michael Templeton,Christian Poellabauer,Ananda Mohan Mondal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STEP-PD is a severity-aware ML framework that classifies Parkinson's disease severity into three categories using multimodal clinical data, achieving high accuracy with XGBoost and providing interpretable explanations via SHAP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current computational studies focus mainly on binary PD detection and don't fully utilize repeated follow-up assessments for stage-aware prediction, which is essential for clinical monitoring and treatment planning as PD symptom burden evolves over time.

Method: Proposes STEP-PD framework using all available PPMI visits, integrating subjective questionnaires and objective clinician-assessed measures. Defines severity using Hoehn and Yahr staging grouped into three categories. Evaluates three binary classification tasks and a three-class severity task with stratified cross-validation and imbalance-aware training. Uses XGBoost for classification and SHAP for interpretability.

Result: XGBoost achieved strong performance: 95.48% accuracy (Healthy vs. Mild), 99.44% (Healthy vs. Moderate-to-Severe), 96.78% (Mild vs. Moderate-to-Severe), and 94.14% accuracy with 0.8775 Macro-F1 for three-class severity classification. SHAP analysis revealed feature importance shifts from early motor features to progression-related axial and balance impairments.

Conclusion: Multimodal clinical assessments from the PPMI cohort can support accurate and interpretable visit-level PD severity stratification, providing a valuable tool for clinical monitoring and treatment planning.

Abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a progressive disorder in which symptom burden and functional impairment evolve over time, making severity staging essential for clinical monitoring and treatment planning. However, many computational studies emphasize binary PD detection and do not fully use repeated follow-up clinical assessments for stage-aware prediction. This study proposes STEP-PD, a severity-aware machine learning framework to classify PD severity using clinically interpretable boundaries. It leverages all available visits from the Parkinson's Progression Markers Initiative (PPMI) and integrates routinely collected subjective questionnaires and objective clinician-assessed measures. Disease severity is defined using Hoehn and Yahr staging and grouped into three clinically meaningful categories: Healthy, Mild PD (stages 1-2), and Moderate-to-Severe PD (stages 3-5). Three binary classification problems and a three-class severity task were evaluated using stratified cross-validation with imbalance-aware training. To enhance interpretability, SHAP was used to provide global explanations and local patient-level waterfall explanations. Across all tasks, XGBoost achieved the strongest and most stable performance, with accuracies of 95.48% (Healthy vs. Mild), 99.44% (Healthy vs. Moderate-to-Severe), and 96.78% (Mild vs. Moderate-to-Severe), and 94.14% accuracy with 0.8775 Macro-F1 for three-class severity classification. Explainability results highlight a shift from early motor features to progression-related axial and balance impairments. These findings show that multimodal clinical assessments within the PPMI cohort can support accurate and interpretable visit-level PD severity stratification.

</details>


### [596] [Conditional Attribution for Root Cause Analysis in Time-Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17616)
*Shashank Mishra,Karan Patil,Cedric Schockaert,Didier Stricker,Jason Rambach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conditional attribution framework for time-series anomaly detection that explains anomalies relative to contextually similar normal states, improving root-cause analysis accuracy and reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RCA methods for time-series anomaly detection often use unrealistic feature perturbations and ignore temporal/cross-feature dependencies, leading to unreliable attributions. There's a need for dependency-preserving, operationally meaningful explanations.

Method: Proposes conditional attribution framework that retrieves representative normal instances conditioned on anomalous observations. Uses learned low-dimensional representations (VAE latent spaces and UMAP embeddings) for contextual retrieval to avoid out-of-distribution artifacts. Introduces confidence-aware and temporal evaluation metrics.

Result: Experiments on SWaT and MSDS benchmarks show consistent improvements in root-cause identification accuracy, temporal localization, and robustness across multiple anomaly detection models.

Conclusion: Conditional attribution provides practical utility for explainable anomaly diagnosis in complex time-series systems by enabling dependency-preserving, contextually grounded explanations.

Abstract: Root cause analysis (RCA) for time-series anomaly detection is critical for the reliable operation of complex real-world systems. Existing explanation methods often rely on unrealistic feature perturbations and ignore temporal and cross-feature dependencies, leading to unreliable attributions. We propose a conditional attribution framework that explains anomalies relative to contextually similar normal system states. Instead of using marginal or randomly sampled baselines, our method retrieves representative normal instances conditioned on the anomalous observation, enabling dependency-preserving and operationally meaningful explanations. To support high-dimensional time-series data, contextual retrieval is performed in learned low-dimensional representations using both variational autoencoder latent spaces and UMAP manifold embeddings. By grounding the retrieval process in the system's learned manifold, this strategy avoids out-of-distribution artifacts and ensures attribution fidelity while maintaining computational efficiency. We further introduce confidence-aware and temporal evaluation metrics for assessing explanation reliability and responsiveness. Experiments on the SWaT and MSDS benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently improves root-cause identification accuracy, temporal localization, and robustness across multiple anomaly detection models. These results highlight the practical utility of conditional attribution for explainable anomaly diagnosis in complex time-series systems. Code and models will be publicly released.

</details>


### [597] [STRIKE: Additive Feature-Group-Aware Stacking Framework for Credit Default Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17622)
*Swattik Maiti,Ritik Pratap Singh,Fardina Fathmiul Alam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STRIKE is a feature-group-aware stacking framework for credit risk prediction that partitions features into semantic groups, trains independent learners per group, and aggregates predictions via meta-learner, outperforming traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ML models for credit risk prediction face challenges with high-dimensional, heterogeneous, noisy datasets that increase overfitting risk and reduce robustness under distributional shift. There's a need for more robust, interpretable approaches that can handle modern credit data characteristics.

Method: STRIKE partitions feature space into semantically coherent groups, trains independent learners within each group, and integrates group-specific predictions through a meta-learner that aggregates signals while maintaining robustness and modularity.

Result: STRIKE consistently outperforms strong tree-based baselines and conventional stacking approaches in AUC-ROC across three real-world datasets spanning corporate bankruptcy and consumer lending scenarios. Ablation studies confirm performance gains stem from meaningful feature decomposition rather than increased model complexity.

Conclusion: STRIKE is a stable, scalable, and interpretable framework for credit risk default prediction that demonstrates the value of feature-group-aware decomposition and structured aggregation for handling modern credit datasets.

Abstract: Credit risk default prediction remains a cornerstone of risk management in the financial industry. The task involves estimating the likelihood that a borrower will fail to meet debt obligations, an objective critical for lending decisions, portfolio optimization, and regulatory compliance. Traditional machine learning models such as logistic regression and tree-based ensembles are widely adopted for their interpretability and strong empirical performance. However, modern credit datasets are high-dimensional, heterogeneous, and noisy, increasing overfitting risk in monolithic models and reducing robustness under distributional shift. We introduce STRIKE (Stacking via Targeted Representations of Isolated Knowledge Extractors), a feature-group-aware stacking framework for structured tabular credit risk data. Rather than training a single monolithic model on the complete dataset, STRIKE partitions the feature space into semantically coherent groups and trains independent learners within each group. This decomposition is motivated by an additive perspective on risk modeling, where distinct feature sources contribute complementary evidence that can be combined through a structured aggregation. The resulting group-specific predictions are integrated through a meta-learner that aggregates signals while maintaining robustness and modularity. We evaluate STRIKE on three real-world datasets spanning corporate bankruptcy and consumer lending scenarios. Across all settings, STRIKE consistently outperforms strong tree-based baselines and conventional stacking approaches in terms of AUC-ROC. Ablation studies confirm that performance gains stem from meaningful feature decomposition rather than increased model complexity. Our findings demonstrate that STRIKE is a stable, scalable, and interpretable framework for credit risk default prediction tasks.

</details>


### [598] [SLO-Guard: Crash-Aware, Budget-Consistent Autotuning for SLO-Constrained LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17627)
*Christian Lysenstøen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SLO-Guard is a crash-aware autotuner for vLLM serving that treats crashes as first-class observations, combining feasible-first exploration with warm-started exploitation to achieve more predictable tuning budget usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Serving large language models under latency SLOs is challenging due to many configurations crashing or missing targets, and standard optimizers treat crashes as wasted trials rather than useful information.

Method: SLO-Guard combines Thermal Budget Annealing (TBA) exploration with warm-started Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) exploitation, encodes crashes as extreme constraint violations, and includes configuration-repair pass and GPU-aware KV-cache memory guard.

Result: SLO-Guard achieves similar feasibility and best latency as random search, but shows superior budget consistency: more trials in fast-serving regime (10.20 vs 7.40), higher post-handoff consistency (0.876 vs 0.539), and 4.4x tighter cross-seed standard deviation on best latency.

Conclusion: SLO-Guard's main advantage is not finding better final configurations, but spending fixed tuning budgets more predictably once fast regimes are found, making it more reliable for production serving systems.

Abstract: Serving large language models under latency service-level objectives (SLOs) is a configuration-heavy systems problem with an unusually failure-prone search space: many plausible configurations crash outright or miss user-visible latency targets, and standard black-box optimizers treat these failures as wasted trials. We present SLO-Guard, a crash-aware autotuner for vLLM serving that treats crashes as first-class observations. SLO-Guard combines a feasible-first Thermal Budget Annealing (TBA) exploration phase with a warm-started Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) exploitation phase; the handoff replays all exploration history, including crashes encoded as extreme constraint violations. We additionally contribute a configuration-repair pass, a GPU-aware KV-cache memory guard, and a four-category crash taxonomy.
  We evaluate SLO-Guard on Qwen2-1.5B served with vLLM 0.19 on an NVIDIA A100 40GB. Across a pre-specified five-seed study, both SLO-Guard and uniform random search attain 75/75 feasibility with zero crashes under the corrected concurrent harness, and are statistically tied on best-achieved latency (Mann-Whitney two-sided p=0.84). SLO-Guard's advantage is in budget consistency: more trials in the fast-serving regime (10.20 vs. 7.40 out of 15; one-sided p=0.014) and higher post-handoff consistency (0.876 vs. 0.539; p=0.010). Under concurrent load, SLO-Guard's cross-seed standard deviation on best latency is 4.4x tighter than random search's (2.26 ms vs. 10.00 ms). A harness-replication analysis shows that the consistency findings survive an independent sequential-dispatch measurement condition.
  The central claim is not that SLO-Guard finds a better final configuration, but that it spends a fixed tuning budget more predictably once the fast regime has been found.

</details>


### [599] [ATLAS: Constitution-Conditioned Latent Geometry and Redistribution Across Language Models and Neural Perturbation Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17663)
*Gareth Seneque,Lap-Hang Ho,Nafise Erfanian Saeedi,Jeffrey Molendijk,Tim Elson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ATLAS is a geometry-first method that analyzes constitution-conditioned post-training as structured perturbation of representational geometry, tracing hidden-state structure across models and substrates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how written constitutions induce recoverable latent geometry that remains detectable across model and substrate changes, moving beyond single behaviors, neurons, vectors, or patches as analysis units.

Method: ATLAS tests local charts with measurable tangent structure, occupancy distribution, and behavioral coupling under system change, analyzing geometry across charts, models, and substrates.

Result: On Gemma: anchored source-local chart captures 310/320 source rows and all 84/84 score-flip rows. In unadapted Phi model: target-local realization separates with AUC 0.984. In mouse cortex data: source-defined family supported across 5/5 folds with mean AUC 0.72.

Conclusion: Written constitutions induce recoverable latent geometry whose organization remains detectable across model/substrate changes, though local coordinates, occupancy, and behavioral expression shift. The correspondence is geometric recurrence under redistribution, not coordinate/site identity.

Abstract: Constitution-conditioned post-training can be analysed as a structured perturbation of a model's learned representational geometry. We introduce ATLAS, a geometry-first program that traces constitution-induced hidden-state structure across charts, models, and substrates. Instead of treating the relevant unit as a single behaviour, neuron, vector, or patch, ATLAS tests a local chart whose tangent structure, occupancy distribution, and behavioural coupling can be measured under system change. On Gemma, the anchored source-local chart captures 310 / 320 reviewed source rows and all 84 / 84 reviewed score-flip rows, but compact exact-patch sufficiency does not close, so the exportable unit is the broader source-defined family. Freezing that family, we re-identify a target-local realisation in an unadapted Phi model, where the fully adjudicated confirmatory contrast separates with AUC 0.984 and mean gap 5.50. In held-out ALM8 mouse frontal-cortex perturbation data, the same source-defined family receives support across 5/5 folds, with mean held-out AUC 0.72 and mean fold gap 4.50. A multiple-choice analysis provides the main boundary: nearby target-local signals can appear without source-faithful closure. The resulting correspondence is not coordinate identity, site identity, or a target-side mediation theorem. It is geometric recurrence under redistribution: written constitutions can induce recoverable latent geometry whose organisation remains detectable across model and substrate changes while its local coordinates, occupancy, and behavioural expression shift.

</details>


### [600] [Prior-Fitted Functional Flow: In-Context Generative Models for Pharmacokinetics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17670)
*César Ojeda,Niklas Hartung,Wilhelm Huisinga,Tim Jahn,Purity Kamene Kavwele,Marian Klose,Piyush Kumar,Ramsés J. Sánchez,Darius A. Faroughy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PFFlow: A generative foundation model for pharmacokinetics that enables zero-shot population synthesis and individual forecasting without manual parameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a generative model for pharmacokinetics that can handle sparse, irregular patient data and enable both population-level synthesis and individual forecasting without requiring manual parameter tuning.

Method: Learn functional vector fields explicitly conditioned on sparse, irregular data from entire study populations. Use a new open-access literature corpus to inform priors.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art predictive accuracy on extensive real-world datasets. Enables generation of coherent virtual cohorts and forecasting of partially observed patient trajectories with calibrated uncertainty.

Conclusion: Prior-Fitted Functional Flows provides a powerful generative foundation model for pharmacokinetics that supports zero-shot population synthesis and individual forecasting with calibrated uncertainty.

Abstract: We introduce Prior-Fitted Functional Flows, a generative foundation model for pharmacokinetics that enables zero-shot population synthesis and individual forecasting without manual parameter tuning. We learn functional vector fields, explicitly conditioned on the sparse, irregular data of an entire study population. This enables the generation of coherent virtual cohorts as well as forecasting of partially observed patient trajectories with calibrated uncertainty. We construct a new open-access literature corpus to inform our priors, and demonstrate state-of-the-art predictive accuracy on extensive real-world datasets.

</details>


### [601] [Grokking of Diffusion Models: Case Study on Modular Addition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17673)
*Joon Hyeok Kim,Yong-Hyun Park,Mattis Dalsætra Østby,Jiatao Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion models trained with flow-matching show grokking on modular addition, revealing how they implement discrete arithmetic through periodic representations and iterative sampling phases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite empirical success, the mechanistic understanding of how diffusion models generalize remains limited. The paper aims to uncover the internal computational mechanisms behind diffusion models' ability to perform algorithmic tasks like modular addition.

Method: Study diffusion models trained with flow-matching objectives on modular addition across two data regimes: (1) single-image regime for mechanistic dissection, and (2) diverse-image regime with high intraclass variability. Analyze grokking phenomenon and internal representations through iterative sampling process analysis.

Result: In single-image regime, models implement modular addition by composing periodic representations of individual operands. In diverse-image regime, models partition tasks into arithmetic computation phase followed by visual denoising phase, separated by a critical timestep threshold.

Conclusion: The work provides mechanistic decomposition of algorithmic learning in diffusion models, revealing how these models bridge continuous pixel-space generation and discrete symbolic reasoning through their iterative sampling process and internal representations.

Abstract: Despite their empirical success, how diffusion models generalize remains poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective. We demonstrate that diffusion models trained with flow-matching objectives exhibit grokking--delayed generalization after overfitting--on modular addition, enabling controlled analysis of their internal computations. We study this phenomenon across two levels of data regime. In a single-image regime, mechanistic dissection reveals that the model implements modular addition by composing periodic representations of individual operands. In a diverse-image regime with high intraclass variability, we find that the model leverages its iterative sampling process to partition the task into an arithmetic computation phase followed by a visual denoising phase, separated by a critical timestep threshold. Our work provides the mechanistic decomposition of algorithmic learning in diffusion models, revealing how these models bridge continuous pixel-space generation and discrete symbolic reasoning.

</details>


### [602] [SafeAnchor: Preventing Cumulative Safety Erosion in Continual Domain Adaptation of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17691)
*Dongxin Guo,Jikun Wu,Siu Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SafeAnchor is a framework that preserves safety alignment in LLMs during continual multi-domain adaptation by identifying safety subspaces, constraining domain updates to orthogonal complements, and monitoring for safety drift with corrective replay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current safety alignment in LLMs is shallow and fragile, easily eroded during sequential adaptation across multiple domains (medicine, law, code). Existing safety-preserving methods only address single-task fine-tuning, leaving the critical multi-domain sequential setting unaddressed.

Method: SafeAnchor: 1) Identifies low-rank safety subspaces in LoRA parameter space using Fisher Information eigendecomposition, 2) Constrains domain-specific gradient updates to the orthogonal complement of these safety subspaces, 3) Monitors for residual safety drift with threshold-triggered corrective replay.

Result: Evaluated on Llama-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct across a three-domain pipeline and eight benchmarks, SafeAnchor retains 93.2% of original safety alignment, outperforming all baselines by 18-42 points, while matching unconstrained fine-tuning to within 1.5 points on domain tasks.

Conclusion: SafeAnchor effectively addresses the critical problem of safety erosion during continual multi-domain adaptation, providing a robust framework that maintains safety alignment while preserving domain task performance.

Abstract: Safety alignment in large language models is remarkably shallow: it is concentrated in the first few output tokens and reversible by fine-tuning on as few as 100 adversarial examples. This fragility becomes critical in real-world deployment, where models undergo sequential adaptation across domains such as medicine, law, and code, causing safety guardrails to erode cumulatively. Yet all existing safety-preserving methods target only single-task fine-tuning, leaving the multi-domain sequential setting entirely unaddressed.
  We introduce SafeAnchor, a framework that anchors safety in place throughout continual adaptation. SafeAnchor first identifies low-rank safety subspaces in LoRA parameter space via Fisher Information eigendecomposition, then constrains domain-specific gradient updates to the orthogonal complement of these subspaces, and finally monitors for residual safety drift with threshold-triggered corrective replay. Evaluated on Llama-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct across a three-domain pipeline and eight benchmarks, SafeAnchor retains 93.2% of original safety alignment, outperforming all baselines by 18-42 points, while matching unconstrained fine-tuning to within 1.5 points on domain tasks.

</details>


### [603] [CAPO: Counterfactual Credit Assignment in Sequential Cooperative Teams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17693)
*Shripad Deshmukh,Jayakumar Subramanian,Raghavendra Addanki,Nikos Vlassis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SeqAU is a novel per-agent learning signal for sequential cooperative teams that maximizes individual learnability, leading to CAPO - a critic-free policy-gradient algorithm that decomposes team rewards into per-agent contributions without extra environment calls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In sequential cooperative teams where agents act in fixed order and share a single team reward, it's difficult to attribute individual contributions, especially when agents are updated one at a time, making earlier collected data obsolete for evaluating new policies.

Method: Introduces Sequential Aristocrat Utility (SeqAU), extending Wolpert and Tumer's framework to sequential settings, then derives CAPO (Counterfactual Advantage Policy Optimization) - a critic-free policy-gradient algorithm that fits per-agent reward decomposition from group rewards and computes per-agent advantage in closed form with minimal forward passes through current policy.

Result: CAPO requires no extra environment calls beyond initial batch, provides analytic bias and variance bounds, and shows growing advantage over standard baselines with team size in controlled sequential bandit experiments. The framework is general and applicable to multi-LLM pipelines.

Conclusion: SeqAU and CAPO provide an effective solution for credit assignment in sequential cooperative teams, enabling efficient per-agent learning without critics or additional environment interactions, with particular promise for large-scale applications like multi-LLM pipelines.

Abstract: In cooperative teams where agents act in a fixed order and share a single team reward, it is hard to know how much each agent contributed, and harder still when agents are updated one at a time because data collected earlier no longer reflects the new policies. We introduce the Sequential Aristocrat Utility (SeqAU), the unique per-agent learning signal that maximizes the individual learnability of each agent's action, extending the classical framework of Wolpert and Tumer (2002) to this sequential setting. From SeqAU we derive CAPO (Counterfactual Advantage Policy Optimization), a critic-free policy-gradient algorithm. CAPO fits a per-agent reward decomposition from group rewards and computes the per-agent advantage in closed form plus a handful of forward passes through the current policy, requiring no extra environment calls beyond the initial batch. We give analytic bias and variance bounds and validate them on a controlled sequential bandit, where CAPO's advantage over standard baselines grows with the team size. The framework is general; multi-LLM pipelines are a natural deployment target.

</details>


### [604] [MoE-nD: Per-Layer Mixture-of-Experts Routing for Multi-Axis KV Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17695)
*Libo Sun,Peixiong He,Po-Wei Harn,Xiao Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoE-nD: A mixture-of-experts framework for heterogeneous KV cache compression that optimizes per-layer eviction and quantization under global memory budget, achieving 14x compression with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV cache memory is the dominant bottleneck for long-context LLM inference. Existing compression methods apply uniform recipes across all layers, but different layers respond differently to compression, leaving accuracy on the table.

Method: Propose MoE-nD framework that routes each layer to its own (eviction-ratio, K-bits, V-bits) tuple under global memory budget. Uses offline-calibrated greedy solver to choose routing minimizing predicted quality loss, applied through single attention patch at inference.

Result: On LongBench-v1 subset, MoE-nD matches uncompressed baseline at 14x compression (136MB vs 1.9GB) while other baselines score under 8/100. Gains hold on AIME reasoning benchmarks (+6 to +27 pts over strongest per-layer-quantization baseline).

Conclusion: Per-layer heterogeneous compression significantly outperforms uniform approaches. The method cleanly characterizes when per-layer eviction routing has headroom (long inputs) versus when it doesn't (short inputs where solver picks keep=1.0).

Abstract: KV cache memory is the dominant bottleneck for long-context LLM inference. Existing compression methods each act on a single axis of the four-dimensional KV tensor -- token eviction (sequence), quantization (precision), low-rank projection (head dimension), or cross-layer sharing -- but apply the same recipe to every layer. We show that this homogeneity leaves accuracy on the table: different layers respond very differently to each compression operation, and the optimal per-layer mix of eviction and quantization is far from uniform. We propose MoE-nD, a mixture-of-experts framework that routes each layer to its own (eviction-ratio, K-bits, V-bits) tuple under a global memory budget. An offline-calibrated greedy solver chooses the routing that minimizes predicted quality loss; at inference time, per-layer heterogeneous eviction and quantization are applied jointly through a single attention patch. On a 4-task subset of LongBench-v1 (16k inputs, n=50 per task, adapted reasoning-model protocol; see section Experiments), MoE-nD's hetero variant matches our uncompressed 1.9~GB baseline at 14x compression (136~MB) while every other compressed baseline we tested (1d, 2d_uniform, 2d) at comparable or smaller memory stays under 8/100. The gains hold on AIME reasoning benchmarks (+6 to +27 pts over the strongest per-layer-quantization baseline across eight configurations). Two null results -- MATH-500 and LongBench's TREC -- share a principled cause (short inputs, solver picks keep=1.0 on most layers), cleanly characterizing when per-layer eviction routing has headroom to help.

</details>


### [605] [The Geometric Canary: Predicting Steerability and Detecting Drift via Representational Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17698)
*Prashant C. Raju*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geometric stability (consistency of representation distances) predicts model controllability and detects degradation. Supervised stability predicts steerability, unsupervised stability detects drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable LLM deployment requires predicting whether models accept behavioral control and detecting when internal structure degrades. These appear distinct but share geometric foundations.

Method: Proposed geometric stability measures: supervised Shesha variants for task-aligned stability predict linear steerability; unsupervised stability for drift detection. Tested across 35-69 embedding models and three NLP tasks.

Result: Supervised stability predicts steerability with near-perfect accuracy (ρ=0.89-0.97), capturing unique variance beyond class separability. Unsupervised stability excels at drift detection, showing 2× greater geometric change than CKA during alignment, earlier warnings in 73% of models, and 6× lower false alarm rate.

Conclusion: Supervised and unsupervised geometric stability form complementary diagnostics: supervised for pre-deployment controllability assessment, unsupervised for post-deployment monitoring. Task alignment is essential for controllability prediction.

Abstract: Reliable deployment of language models requires two capabilities that appear distinct but share a common geometric foundation: predicting whether a model will accept targeted behavioral control, and detecting when its internal structure degrades. We show that geometric stability, the consistency of a representation's pairwise distance structure, addresses both. Supervised Shesha variants that measure task-aligned geometric stability predict linear steerability with near-perfect accuracy ($ρ= 0.89$-$0.97$) across 35-69 embedding models and three NLP tasks, capturing unique variance beyond class separability (partial $ρ= 0.62$-$0.76$). A critical dissociation emerges: unsupervised stability fails entirely for steering on real-world tasks ($ρ\approx 0.10$), revealing that task alignment is essential for controllability prediction. However, unsupervised stability excels at drift detection, measuring nearly $2\times$ greater geometric change than CKA during post-training alignment (up to $5.23\times$ in Llama) while providing earlier warning in 73\% of models and maintaining a $6\times$ lower false alarm rate than Procrustes. Together, supervised and unsupervised stability form complementary diagnostics for the LLM deployment lifecycle: one for pre-deployment controllability assessment, the other for post-deployment monitoring.

</details>


### [606] [Modeling Higher-Order Brain Interactions via a Multi-View Information Bottleneck Framework for fMRI-based Psychiatric Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17713)
*Kunyu Zhang,Qiang Li,Vince D. Calhoun,Shujian Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces O-information for characterizing higher-order brain interactions and integrates it into a multi-view information bottleneck framework for fMRI-based psychiatric diagnosis, achieving significant computational speedup and outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fMRI analysis methods for psychiatric diagnosis rely on pairwise brain connectivities that overlook higher-order interactions central to complex brain dynamics. Existing hypergraph methods use heuristic similarity metrics and don't characterize whether interactions are synergy- or redundancy-dominated.

Method: Introduces O-information as a signed measure for characterizing informational nature of higher-order interactions. Develops two acceleration strategies: Gaussian analytical approximation and randomized matrix-based Rényi entropy estimator (30x speedup). Creates tri-view architecture fusing pairwise, triadic, and tetradic brain interactions in a multi-view information bottleneck framework.

Result: Extensive evaluation across four benchmark datasets (REST-meta-MDD, ABIDE, UCLA, ADNI) shows consistent improvements, outperforming 11 baseline methods including state-of-the-art GNN and hypergraph approaches. Achieves interpretable region-level synergy-redundancy patterns not captured by conventional hypergraph formulations.

Conclusion: The proposed O-information based multi-view framework effectively captures higher-order brain interactions for psychiatric diagnosis, providing computational efficiency, superior performance, and interpretable insights into synergy-redundancy patterns in brain connectivity.

Abstract: Resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has emerged as a cornerstone for psychiatric diagnosis, yet most approaches rely on pairwise brain cortical or sub-cortical connectivities that overlooks higher-order interactions (HOIs) central to complex brain dynamics. While hypergraph methods encode HOIs through predefined hyperedges, their construction typically relies on heuristic similarity metrics and does not explicitly characterize whether interactions are synergy- or redundancy-dominated. In this paper, we introduce $O$-information, a signed measure that characterizes the informational nature of HOIs, and integrate third- and fourth-order $O$-information into a unified multi-view information bottleneck framework for fMRI-based psychiatric diagnosis. To enable scalable $O$-information estimation, we further develop two independent acceleration strategies: a Gaussian analytical approximation and a randomized matrix-based Rényi entropy estimator, achieving over a 30-fold computational speedup compared with conventional estimators. Our tri-view architecture systematically fuses pairwise, triadic, and tetradic brain interactions, capturing comprehensive brain connectivity while explicitly penalizing redundancy. Extensive evaluation across four benchmark datasets (REST-meta-MDD, ABIDE, UCLA, ADNI) demonstrates consistent improvements, outperforming 11 baseline methods including state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN) and hypergraph based approaches. Moreover, our method reveals interpretable region-level synergy-redundancy patterns which are not explicitly characterized by conventional hypergraph formulations.

</details>


### [607] [FlashFPS: Efficient Farthest Point Sampling for Large-Scale Point Clouds via Pruning and Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17720)
*Yuzhe Fu,Hancheng Ye,Cong Guo,Junyao Zhang,Qinsi Wang,Yueqian Lin,Changchun Zhou,Hai,Li,Yiran Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlashFPS is a hardware-agnostic framework that accelerates Farthest Point Sampling in point-based neural networks by eliminating computational redundancies, achieving 5.16× GPU speedup with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) is a major bottleneck in point-based neural networks, causing significant inference latency despite existing optimizations. The authors identify three key redundancies in FPS that hinder scalability for large-scale point cloud processing.

Method: FlashFPS consists of two components: FPS-Prune (with candidate pruning and iteration pruning to reduce redundant computations while preserving sampling quality) and FPS-Cache (which eliminates layer-wise redundancy via cache-and-reuse). The framework is hardware-agnostic and plug-and-play.

Result: FlashFPS achieves 5.16× speedup over standard CUDA baseline on GPU and 2.69× speedup on PNN accelerators, with negligible accuracy loss. The framework enables efficient and scalable PNN inference.

Conclusion: FlashFPS successfully addresses FPS bottlenecks in PNNs by eliminating computational redundancies through pruning and caching techniques, making point cloud processing more efficient and scalable without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Point-based Neural Networks (PNNs) have become a key approach for point cloud processing. However, a core operation in these models, Farthest Point Sampling (FPS), often introduces significant inference latency, especially for large-scale processing. Despite existing CUDA- and hardware-level optimizations, FPS remains a major bottleneck due to exhaustive computations across multiple network layers in PNNs, which hinders scalability. Through systematic analysis, we identify three substantial redundancies in FPS, including unnecessary full-cloud computations, redundant late-stage iterations, and predictable inter-layer outputs that make later FPS computations avoidable. To address these, we propose \textbf{\textit{FlashFPS}}, a hardware-agnostic, plug-and-play framework for FPS acceleration, composed of \textit{FPS-Prune} and \textit{FPS-Cache}. \textit{FPS-Prune} introduces candidate pruning and iteration pruning to reduce redundant computations in FPS while preserving sampling quality, and \textit{FPS-Cache} eliminates layer-wise redundancy via cache-and-reuse. Integrated into existing CUDA libraries and state-of-the-art PNN accelerators, \textit{FlashFPS} achieves 5.16$\times$ speedup over the standard CUDA baseline on GPU and 2.69$\times$ on PNN accelerators, with negligible accuracy loss, enabling efficient and scalable PNN inference. Codes are released at https://github.com/Yuzhe-Fu/FlashFPS.

</details>


### [608] [Tool Learning Needs Nothing More Than a Free 8B Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17739)
*Chenming Tang,Hsiu-Yuan Huang,Weijie Liu,Junqiang Zheng,Saiyong Yang,Yunfang Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRUSTEE is a data-free method that trains tool-calling agents using dynamic environments simulated entirely by free open-source language models (as small as 8B), with adaptive curriculum learning for task difficulty control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL approaches for training tool-calling agents require either expensive ground truth annotations or rely on commercial LMs to create static environments. There's a need for a more accessible, resource-efficient method that doesn't require external resources.

Method: TRUSTEE uses free open-source LMs (as small as 8B) to dynamically simulate four components: task generation, user simulation, tool simulation, and trajectory evaluation. It incorporates adaptive curriculum learning that dynamically controls task difficulty during training.

Result: TRUSTEE consistently improves performance across various domains and outperforms baselines that require external resources. It demonstrates that even simulated environments with a local 8B LM can set strong baselines for tool learning.

Conclusion: The work shows that sophisticated design with simulated environments using free open-source LMs can enable effective tool learning without expensive annotations, human interactions, executable tools, or commercial LM environments, inspiring future research on environment scaling with limited resources.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a prevalent paradigm for training tool calling agents, which typically requires online interactive environments. Existing approaches either rely on training data with ground truth annotations or require advanced commercial language models (LMs) to synthesize environments that keep fixed once created. In this work, we propose TRUSTEE, a data-free method training tool calling agents with dynamic environments fully simulated by free open-source LMs that can be as small as 8B, including task generation, user simulation, tool simulation and trajectory evaluation, paired with an adaptive curriculum learning mechanism that controls various aspects of the task difficulty dynamically during training. Our empirical results show that TRUSTEE brings consistent improvements across various domains and outperforms all the baselines which require extra external resources for training. These confirm that, with a sufficiently sophisticated design, even simulated environments with a local 8B LM as the backbone could set a strong baseline for tool learning, without expensive annotated data, realistic human interactions, executable tools or costly verifiable environments from human experts or commercial LMs. We hope our proposed paradigm could inspire future research on environment scaling with limited resources.

</details>


### [609] [Efficient Federated RLHF via Zeroth-Order Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17747)
*Deyi Wang,Qining Zhang,Lei Ying*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Par-S²ZPO, a federated RLHF algorithm for resource-constrained agents using zeroth-order optimization with binary perturbation for low complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses reinforcement learning from human feedback in federated learning settings with resource-constrained edge devices, aiming to reduce communication, computation, and memory overhead.

Method: Partitioned, Sign-based Stochastic Zeroth-order Policy Optimization (Par-S²ZPO) built on zeroth-order optimization with binary perturbation for low complexity.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows Par-S²ZPO matches centralized counterpart in sample complexity but converges faster in policy updates; experimental results outperform FedAvg-based RLHF on four MuJoCo tasks.

Conclusion: Par-S²ZPO provides an efficient federated RLHF solution that maintains performance while significantly reducing resource requirements for edge devices.

Abstract: This paper considers reinforcement learning from human feedback in a federated learning setting with resource-constrained agents, such as edge devices. We propose an efficient federated RLHF algorithm, named Partitioned, Sign-based Stochastic Zeroth-order Policy Optimization (Par-S$^2$ZPO). The algorithm is built on zeroth-order optimization with binary perturbation, resulting in low communication, computation, and memory complexity by design. Our theoretical analysis establishes an upper bound on the convergence rate of Par-S$^2$ZPO, revealing that it is as efficient as its centralized counterpart in terms of sample complexity but converges faster in terms of policy update iterations. Our experimental results show that it outperforms a FedAvg-based RLHF on four MuJoCo RL tasks.

</details>


### [610] [HiP-LoRA: Budgeted Spectral Plasticity for Robust Low-Rank Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17751)
*Lixian Chen,Jianhong Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HiP-LoRA is a spectrum-aware adaptation framework that addresses LoRA's spectral interference by decomposing updates into principal and residual channels with stability budgeting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA suffers from spectral interference where low-rank updates concentrate on leading singular directions of pretrained weights, causing catastrophic forgetting, fragile multi-adapter merging, and degradation of general capabilities.

Method: Uses cached SVD of pretrained layers to decompose updates into: 1) principal channel within dominant singular subspace, and 2) residual low-rank channel in orthogonal complement. Applies singular-value-weighted stability budget on principal channel to balance preservation and plasticity.

Result: On Llama-3.1-8B, HiP-LoRA drastically reduces pretraining degradation and multi-adapter MergeFail, robustly outperforming baselines in interference-sensitive tasks like continual tuning and knowledge editing under matched budgets.

Conclusion: HiP-LoRA effectively resolves LoRA's spectral interference problem through spectrum-aware adaptation, enabling better preservation of pretrained capabilities while maintaining task-specific adaptation efficiency.

Abstract: Adapting foundation models under resource budgets relies heavily on Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), with LoRA being a standard modular solution. However, LoRA suffers from spectral interference. Low-rank updates often concentrate energy on the leading singular directions of pretrained weights, perturbing general capabilities and causing catastrophic forgetting and fragile multi-adapter merging. To resolve this, we propose HiP-LoRA, a spectrum-aware adaptation framework. Utilizing the cached singular value decomposition (SVD) of pretrained layers, HiP-LoRA decomposes updates into two channels: a principal channel within the dominant singular subspace, and a residual low-rank channel in the orthogonal complement. A singular-value-weighted stability budget on the principal channel continuously balances pretrained behavior preservation with task-specific plasticity. Experiments on Llama-3.1-8B demonstrate that under matched budgets, HiP-LoRA drastically reduces pretraining degradation and multi-adapter MergeFail, robustly outperforming baselines in interference-sensitive tasks like continual tuning and knowledge editing.

</details>


### [611] [LLM-AUG: Robust Wireless Data Augmentation with In-Context Learning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17770)
*Pranshav Gajjar,Manan Tiwari,Sayanta Seth,Vijay K. Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-AUG: A data augmentation framework using large language models' in-context learning to generate synthetic RF training samples in embedding space, outperforming traditional methods in low-data wireless communication tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity is a major bottleneck for deep learning in wireless communications due to expensive and time-consuming RF data collection. There's a need for efficient data augmentation methods that work well with limited labeled data.

Method: LLM-AUG leverages in-context learning of LLMs to generate synthetic training samples directly in learned embedding space through structured prompting, without requiring task-specific model training. It enables rapid adaptation in low-shot regimes.

Result: LLM-AUG consistently outperforms traditional augmentation and deep generative baselines, reaching near oracle performance with only 15% labeled data. It shows 29.4% relative gain over diffusion-based augmentation at lower SNR, and 67.6%/35.7% gains on RadioML/IC datasets. t-SNE visualizations confirm better class structure preservation.

Conclusion: LLMs can serve as effective and practical data augmenters for wireless machine learning, enabling robust and data-efficient learning in evolving wireless environments through in-context learning-based synthetic data generation.

Abstract: Data scarcity remains a fundamental bottleneck in applying deep learning to wireless communication problems, particularly in scenarios where collecting labeled Radio Frequency (RF) data is expensive, time-consuming, or operationally constrained. This paper proposes LLM-AUG, a data augmentation framework that leverages in-context learning in large language models (LLMs) to generate synthetic training samples directly in a learned embedding space. Unlike conventional generative approaches that require training task-specific models, LLM-AUG performs data generation through structured prompting, enabling rapid adaptation in low-shot regimes. We evaluate LLM-AUG on two representative tasks: modulation classification and interference classification using the RadioML 2016.10A dataset, and the Interference Classification (IC) dataset respectively. Results show that LLM-AUG consistently outperforms traditional augmentation and deep generative baselines across low-shot settings and reaches near oracle performance using only 15% labeled data. LLM-AUG further demonstrates improved robustness under distribution shifts, yielding a 29.4% relative gain over diffusion-based augmentation at a lower SNR value. On the RadioML and IC datasets, LLM-AUG yields a relative gain of 67.6% and 35.7% over the diffusion-based baseline. The t-SNE visualizations further validate that synthetic samples generated by better preserve class structure in the embedding space, leading to more consistent and informative augmentations. These results demonstrate that LLMs can serve as effective and practical data augmenters for wireless machine learning, enabling robust and data-efficient learning in evolving wireless environments.

</details>


### [612] [TeleEmbedBench: A Multi-Corpus Embedding Benchmark for RAG in Telecommunications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17778)
*Pranshav Gajjar,Vijay K Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TeleEmbedBench is the first large-scale embedding benchmark for telecommunications, evaluating 8 embedding models across 3 telecom corpora with 9,000 question-chunk pairs, showing LLM-based embedders outperform traditional sentence-transformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current embedding model evaluations fail to capture the dense, acronym-heavy, cross-referential nature of telecommunications corpora, creating a gap in assessing RAG pipelines for telecom LLM deployments.

Method: Created TeleEmbedBench with 3 telecom corpora (O-RAN, 3GPP, srsRAN) using automated LLM pipeline for query generation and validation. Evaluated 8 embedding models across 3 chunk sizes with TeleEmbedBench-Clean for noisy query robustness.

Result: LLM-based embedders (Qwen3, EmbeddingGemma) consistently outperform sentence-transformers in retrieval accuracy and cross-domain robustness. Domain-specific instructions help for code but degrade performance for natural language specifications.

Conclusion: TeleEmbedBench fills critical evaluation gap for telecom RAG systems, showing LLM-based embedders are superior for telecom applications, with nuanced findings about domain-specific instructions.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in the telecommunications domain for critical tasks, relying heavily on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to adapt general-purpose models to continuously evolving standards. However, a significant gap exists in evaluating the embedding models that power these RAG pipelines, as general-purpose benchmarks fail to capture the dense, acronym-heavy, and highly cross-referential nature of telecommunications corpora. To address this, we introduce TeleEmbedBench, the first large-scale, multi-corpus embedding benchmark designed specifically for telecommunications. The benchmark spans three heterogeneous corpora: O-RAN Alliance specifications, 3GPP release documents, and the srsRAN open-source codebase, comprising 9,000 question-chunk pairs across three standard chunk sizes (512, 1024, and 2048 tokens). To construct this dataset at scale without manual annotation bottlenecks, we employ a novel automated pipeline where one LLM generates specific queries from text chunks and a secondary LLM validates them across strict criteria. We comprehensively evaluate eight embedding models, spanning standard sentence-transformers and LLM-based embedders. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based embedders, such as Qwen3 and EmbeddingGemma, consistently and significantly outperform traditional sentence-transformers in both retrieval accuracy and robustness against cross-domain interference. Additionally, we introduce TeleEmbedBench-Clean to evaluate model robustness against noisy, incomplete user queries. Finally, our analysis reveals that while domain-specific task instructions improve embedder performance for raw source code, they paradoxically degrade retrieval performance for natural language telecommunications specifications.

</details>


### [613] [Ranking Abuse via Strategic Pairwise Data Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17805)
*Junyi Yao,Zihao Zheng,Jiayu Long*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MLE-based ranking systems like Bradley-Terry are vulnerable to strategic data manipulation, with a phase-transition effect where small perturbation budgets allow significant ranking alterations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the robustness of widely-used MLE-based pairwise ranking systems (e.g., Bradley-Terry) against strategic data manipulation, as their vulnerability remains insufficiently studied despite widespread adoption.

Method: Formulate manipulation as constrained combinatorial optimization and propose Adaptive Subset Selection Attack (ASSA) to efficiently identify high-impact perturbations for strategic ranking alteration.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world election data show MLE rankings exhibit sharp phase-transition: beyond small perturbation budgets, few strategic voters can significantly alter global rankings. ASSA consistently outperforms random and greedy baselines.

Conclusion: MLE-based ranking mechanisms are fundamentally sensitive to structured perturbations, revealing need for more robust aggregation methods in collective decision-making systems.

Abstract: Pairwise ranking systems based on Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), such as the Bradley-Terry model, are widely used to aggregate preferences from pairwise comparisons. However, their robustness under strategic data manipulation remains insufficiently understood.
  In this paper, we study the vulnerability of MLE-based ranking systems to adversarial perturbations. We formulate the manipulation task as a constrained combinatorial optimization problem and propose an Adaptive Subset Selection Attack (ASSA) to efficiently identify high-impact perturbations.
  Experimental results on both synthetic data and real-world election datasets show that MLE-based rankings exhibit a sharp phase-transition behavior: beyond a small perturbation budget, a limited number of strategic voters can significantly alter the global ranking. In particular, our method consistently outperforms random and greedy baselines under constrained budgets.
  These findings reveal a fundamental sensitivity of MLE-based ranking mechanisms to structured perturbations and highlight the need for more robust aggregation methods in collective decision-making systems.

</details>


### [614] [Efficient Diffusion Models under Nonconvex Equality and Inequality constraints via Landing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17838)
*Kijung Jeon,Michael Muehlebach,Molei Tao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified framework for constrained diffusion models on nonconvex feasible sets that enforces equality and inequality constraints using efficient landing mechanisms instead of projections, with underdamped dynamics to accelerate mixing and reduce computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative modeling within constrained sets is essential for scientific and engineering applications with physical, geometric, or safety requirements (e.g., molecular generation, robotics). Existing methods struggle with nonconvex feasible sets and computationally expensive projections.

Method: A unified framework for constrained diffusion models that incorporates both overdamped and underdamped dynamics. Key innovation: a computationally efficient landing mechanism that replaces costly projections onto nonconvex feasible sets Σ, ensuring feasibility without iterative Newton solves. Uses underdamped dynamics to accelerate mixing toward prior distribution.

Result: Empirically reduces function evaluations and memory usage during both training and inference while preserving sample quality. On benchmarks with equality and mixed constraints, achieves comparable sample quality to state-of-the-art baselines while significantly reducing computational cost.

Conclusion: Provides a practical and scalable solution for diffusion on nonconvex feasible sets, addressing the high simulation costs typically associated with constrained diffusion models through efficient landing mechanisms and underdamped dynamics.

Abstract: Generative modeling within constrained sets is essential for scientific and engineering applications involving physical, geometric, or safety requirements (e.g., molecular generation, robotics). We present a unified framework for constrained diffusion models on generic nonconvex feasible sets $Σ$ that simultaneously enforces equality and inequality constraints throughout the diffusion process. Our framework incorporates both overdamped and underdamped dynamics for forward and backward sampling. A key algorithmic innovation is a computationally efficient landing mechanism that replaces costly and often ill-defined projections onto $Σ$, ensuring feasibility without iterative Newton solves or projection failures. By leveraging underdamped dynamics, we accelerate mixing toward the prior distribution, effectively alleviating the high simulation costs typically associated with constrained diffusion. Empirically, this approach reduces function evaluations and memory usage during both training and inference while preserving sample quality. On benchmarks featuring equality and mixed constraints, our method achieves comparable sample quality to state-of-the-art baselines while significantly reducing computational cost, providing a practical and scalable solution for diffusion on nonconvex feasible sets.

</details>


### [615] [M100: An Orchestrated Dataflow Architecture Powering General AI Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17862)
*Yan Xie,Changkui Mao,Changsong Wu,Chao Lu,Chao Suo,Cheng Qian,Chun Yang,Danyang Zhu,Hengchang Xiong,Hongzhan Lu,Hongzhen Liu,Jiafu Liu,Jie Chen,Jie Dai,Junfeng Tang,Kai Liu,Kun Li,Lipeng Ge,Meng Sun,Min Luo,Peng Chen,Peng Wang,Shaodong Yang,Shibin Tang,Shibo Chen,Weikang Zhang,Xiao Ling,Xiaobo Du,Xin Wu,Yang Liu,Yi Jiang,Yihua Jin,Yin Huang,Yuli Zhang,Zhen Yuan,Zhiyuan Man,Zhongxiao Yao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M100 is Li Auto's dataflow parallel architecture for efficient AI inference across autonomous driving, LLMs, and human interaction applications, using compiler-architecture co-design to optimize data movement and eliminate caching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI computing architectures face limitations: GPGPUs lack efficiency and cost-effectiveness for diverse AI workloads, while Domain-Specific Architectures (DSAs) are too narrow and can't adapt to rapidly evolving AI applications. There's a need for a performant, cost-effective architecture that can handle the demanding requirements of modern automobile platforms including autonomous driving, large language models, and intelligent human interactions.

Method: M100 employs a dataflow parallel architecture with compiler-architecture co-design that orchestrates both computation and data movement across time and space. The system eliminates traditional caching by using compiler- and runtime-managed data streams flowing between computing elements and memories.
