We demonstrate the generalized clinical forecasting potential of Apollo embeddings, including predicting new disease onset risk up to five years in advance (95 tasks), disease progression (78 tasks), treatment response (59 tasks), risk of treatment-related adverse events (17 tasks), and hospital operations endpoints (12 tasks). Using feature attribution techniques, we show that model predictions align with clinically-interpretable multimodal biomarkers. We evaluate semantic similarity search on 61 retrieval tasks, and moreover demonstrate the potential of Apollo as a multimodal medical search engine using text and image queries. Together, these modeling capabilities establish the foundation for computable medicine, where the full context of patient care becomes accessible to computational reasoning.

</details>


### [670] [When Can LLMs Learn to Reason with Weak Supervision?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18574)
*Salman Rahman,Jingyan Shen,Anna Mordvina,Hamid Palangi,Saadia Gabriel,Pavel Izmailov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR generalization under weak supervision depends on training reward saturation dynamics and reasoning faithfulness, not just output diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM capabilities grow, constructing high-quality reward signals becomes difficult, making it essential to understand when RLVR can succeed under weaker forms of supervision (scarce data, noisy rewards, self-supervised proxy rewards).

Method: Systematic empirical study across diverse model families and reasoning domains under three weak supervision settings, analyzing training reward saturation dynamics, reasoning faithfulness, and disentangling contributions of continual pre-training vs supervised fine-tuning.

Result: Generalization is governed by training reward saturation dynamics: models that generalize exhibit prolonged pre-saturation phase where training reward and downstream performance climb together, while models that saturate rapidly memorize. Reasoning faithfulness (extent to which intermediate steps logically support final answer) predicts which regime a model falls into, while output diversity alone is uninformative.

Conclusion: SFT on explicit reasoning traces is necessary for generalization under weak supervision, while continual pre-training on domain data amplifies the effect. Applied together to Llama3.2-3B-Base, these interventions enable generalization across all three weak supervision settings where the base model previously failed.

Abstract: Large language models have achieved significant reasoning improvements through reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). Yet as model capabilities grow, constructing high-quality reward signals becomes increasingly difficult, making it essential to understand when RLVR can succeed under weaker forms of supervision. We conduct a systematic empirical study across diverse model families and reasoning domains under three weak supervision settings: scarce data, noisy rewards, and self-supervised proxy rewards. We find that generalization is governed by training reward saturation dynamics: models that generalize exhibit a prolonged pre-saturation phase during which training reward and downstream performance climb together, while models that saturate rapidly memorize rather than learn. We identify reasoning faithfulness, defined as the extent to which intermediate steps logically support the final answer, as the pre-RL property that predicts which regime a model falls into, while output diversity alone is uninformative. Motivated by these findings, we disentangle the contributions of continual pre-training and supervised fine-tuning, finding that SFT on explicit reasoning traces is necessary for generalization under weak supervision, while continual pre-training on domain data amplifies the effect. Applied together to Llama3.2-3B-Base, these interventions enable generalization across all three settings where the base model previously failed.

</details>


### [671] [Bounded Ratio Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18578)
*Yunke Ao,Le Chen,Bruce D. Lee,Assefa S. Wahd,Aline Czarnobai,Philipp Fürnstahl,Bernhard Schölkopf,Andreas Krause*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BRRL framework bridges trust region methods with PPO's clipped objective, introducing Bounded Policy Optimization (BPO) that ensures monotonic improvement and connects trust region optimization with CEM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a disconnect between theoretical trust region methods and PPO's heuristic clipped objective. The paper aims to bridge this gap with a principled framework.

Method: Introduces Bounded Ratio Reinforcement Learning (BRRL) framework with regularized/constrained policy optimization, derives analytic optimal solution, then develops BPO algorithm that minimizes advantage-weighted divergence between policy and analytic solution. Extends to Group-relative BPO (GBPO) for LLM fine-tuning.

Result: BPO ensures monotonic performance improvement, provides theoretical interpretation of PPO success, connects trust region optimization with CEM. Empirical evaluations show BPO/GBPO match or outperform PPO/GRPO in MuJoCo, Atari, IsaacLab, and LLM fine-tuning tasks.

Conclusion: BRRL framework successfully bridges theoretical trust region methods with practical PPO objectives, providing both theoretical guarantees and empirical performance improvements across diverse domains.

Abstract: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) has become the predominant algorithm for on-policy reinforcement learning due to its scalability and empirical robustness across domains. However, there is a significant disconnect between the underlying foundations of trust region methods and the heuristic clipped objective used in PPO. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing the Bounded Ratio Reinforcement Learning (BRRL) framework. We formulate a novel regularized and constrained policy optimization problem and derive its analytical optimal solution. We prove that this solution ensures monotonic performance improvement. To handle parameterized policy classes, we develop a policy optimization algorithm called Bounded Policy Optimization (BPO) that minimizes an advantage-weighted divergence between the policy and the analytic optimal solution from BRRL. We further establish a lower bound on the expected performance of the resulting policy in terms of the BPO loss function. Notably, our framework also provides a new theoretical lens to interpret the success of the PPO loss, and connects trust region policy optimization and the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM). We additionally extend BPO to Group-relative BPO (GBPO) for LLM fine-tuning. Empirical evaluations of BPO across MuJoCo, Atari, and complex IsaacLab environments (e.g., Humanoid locomotion), and of GBPO for LLM fine-tuning tasks, demonstrate that BPO and GBPO generally match or outperform PPO and GRPO in stability and final performance.

</details>


### [672] [Sessa: Selective State Space Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18580)
*Liubomyr Horbatko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sessa is a new decoder architecture that combines attention with feedback paths to achieve power-law memory decay, outperforming Transformers and Mamba on long-context tasks while remaining competitive on short-context language modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sequence models have limitations: Transformers dilute token influence as O(1/ℓ) in long contexts, while selective state-space models like Mamba suffer from exponential decay of long-range sensitivity. Both architectures either retrieve from the past in a single read or propagate through a single feedback chain, limiting their long-term memory capabilities.

Method: Sessa places attention inside a feedback path, enabling recurrent many-path aggregation within a layer. This creates a feedback loop where attention operates within a recurrent structure, allowing for multiple paths of information propagation rather than single-chain feedback.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows Sessa achieves power-law memory decay O(ℓ^{-β}) for 0<β<1, which is asymptotically slower than Transformers' 1/ℓ decay. Empirically, Sessa achieves strongest performance on long-context benchmarks while remaining competitive with Transformer and Mamba baselines on short-context language modeling under matched architectures and training budgets.

Conclusion: Sessa represents a novel architecture that combines attention with feedback to overcome limitations of existing models, achieving superior long-context performance with power-law memory decay while maintaining competitive short-context capabilities.

Abstract: Modern sequence models are dominated by Transformers, where self-attention mixes information from the visible context in an input-dependent way. However, when retrieval is not sharp and attention remains diffuse over an effective support $S_{\mathrm{eff}}(t)$, the influence of any individual token is diluted, typically scaling as $O(1/S_{\mathrm{eff}}(t))$ and reaching $O(1/\ell)$ for old tokens in full-prefix settings. Structured state-space models process sequences recurrently through an explicit feedback path; selective variants such as Mamba make this feedback input-dependent, yet when freeze time cannot be sustained over long intervals, their long-range sensitivity decays exponentially with lag. Existing architectures therefore either retrieve from the past in a single read or propagate information through a single feedback chain. We introduce Sessa, a decoder that places attention inside a feedback path, enabling recurrent many-path aggregation within a layer. Under stated assumptions, Sessa admits regimes with a power-law memory tail in lag $\ell$ of order $O(\ell^{-β})$ for $0<β<1$, which is asymptotically slower than $1/\ell$; moreover, this rate is tight in an explicit diffuse uniform-routing setting where the influence is $Θ(\ell^{-β})$. Under the same conditions, only Sessa among the compared model classes realizes flexible selective retrieval, including non-decaying profiles. Empirically, under matched architectures and training budgets, Sessa achieves the strongest performance on our long-context benchmarks while remaining competitive with Transformer and Mamba style baselines on short-context language modeling.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [673] [A Two-Stage Multi-Modal MRI Framework for Lifespan Brain Age Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16655)
*Dingyi Zhang,Ruiying Liu,Yun Wang*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A two-stage multimodal MRI framework for brain age estimation across lifespan using morphology and white matter organization


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing brain age approaches are limited by narrow age ranges and single-modality MRI, failing to capture coordinated macro- and microstructural changes across lifespan

Method: Two-stage architecture with independent modality processing and late fusion: first classifies subjects into six developmental stages, then estimates age within predicted stage

Result: Developed unified lifespan-spanning assessment of brain maturity across diverse developmental periods

Conclusion: Multi-modal brain age framework enables comprehensive characterization of integrated brain evolution across human lifespan

Abstract: The accurate quantification of brain age from MRI has emerged as an important biomarker of brain health. However, existing approaches are often restricted to narrow age ranges and single-modality MRI data, limiting their capacity to capture the coordinated macro- and microstructural changes that unfold across the human lifespan. To address these limitations, we developed a multi-modal brain age framework to characterize the integrated evolution of brain morphology and white matter organization. Our model adopts a two-stage architecture, where modalities are processed independently and integrated via late fusion in both stages: first to classify each subject into one of six developmental stages, and then to estimate age within the predicted stage. This design enables a unified and lifespan-spanning assessment of brain maturity across diverse developmental periods.

</details>


### [674] [Structured 3D-SVD: A Practical Framework for the Compression and Reconstruction of Biological Volumetric Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16947)
*Mario Aragonés Lozano,Oscar Romero,Antonio León*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Structured 3D-SVD is a framework for biological volumetric data processing that achieves near-Tucker decomposition quality with faster computation, outperforming CPD in both accuracy and speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an efficient framework for reconstruction, compression, and analysis of biological volumetric data that combines the mathematical elegance of SVD with practical computational efficiency.

Method: Extends matrix SVD logic to third-order volumetric data in spatial domain, using ordered quasi-singular coefficients for progressive reconstruction. Evaluated on fish and brain volumetric datasets.

Result: Achieves reconstruction quality close to Tucker decomposition with shorter computation times, outperforms CPD in both accuracy and runtime. Progressive analysis shows low truncation preserves main structures while higher truncation adds details.

Conclusion: Structured 3D-SVD provides a practical and efficient alternative to existing tensor decomposition methods for biological volumetric data processing, balancing quality and computational performance.

Abstract: This work introduces Structured 3D-SVD as a practical framework for the reconstruction, compression, and analysis of biological volumetric data. Inspired by the logic of matrix singular value decomposition (SVD), the proposed approach represents third-order volumetric data in the spatial domain and supports progressive reconstruction through ordered quasi-singular coeffients. The experimental evaluation was carried out on two biological volumetric datasets: one full-volume scan of a fish and another of a brain. The results show that Structured 3D-SVD achieves reconstruction quality close to that of Tucker decomposition while requiring shorter computation times and outperforms canonical polyadic decomposition (CPD) in both accuracy and runtime. In addition, a progressive reconstruction analysis shows that relatively low truncation levels are sufficient to preserve the main volumetric structures, while higher truncation levels lead to more detailed reconstructions.

</details>


### [675] [A Two-Stage Deep Learning Framework for Segmentation of Ten Gastrointestinal Organs from Coronal MR Enterography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17118)
*Ashiqur Rahman,Md. Abu Sayed,Md Sharjis Ibne Wadud,Md. Abu Asad Al-Hafiz,Adam Mushtak,Muhammad E. H. Chowdhury*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A dual-stage deep learning framework for GI organ segmentation from MRE images achieves high accuracy using coarse-to-fine approach with DenseNet-UNet++ and DenseNet-SelfONN-UNet models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate segmentation of gastrointestinal organs in MRE is critical for IBD diagnosis, but anatomical variability, class imbalance, and low tissue contrast hinder reliable automation.

Method: Two-stage approach: 1) DenseNet201-UNet++ for coarse ROI extraction, 2) DenseNet121-SelfONN-UNet for organ-specific segmentation on patches. Used data augmentation, normalization, 5-fold cross-validation, and class-specific weighting to address class imbalance.

Result: Framework achieved mDSC of 88.99%, mIoU of 84.76%, and mHD95 of 6.94 mm, outperforming baselines. Class weighting improved appendix DSC from 6.76% to 85.76%. Second stage significantly enhanced segmentation across all GI structures.

Conclusion: The coarse-to-fine, organ-aware segmentation strategy is effective for intestinal MRE, showing strong potential for clinical translation despite higher computational cost.

Abstract: Accurate segmentation of gastrointestinal (GI) organs in magnetic resonance enterography (MRE) is critical for diagnosing inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). However, anatomical variability, class imbalance, and low tissue contrast hinder reliable automation. This study proposes a dual-stage deep learning framework for organ-specific segmentation of GI structures from coronal MRE images to address these challenges.
  A publicly available MRE dataset of 3,195 coronal T2-weighted HASTE slices from 114 IBD patients was used. Initially, a DenseNet201-UNet++ model generated coarse masks for ROI extraction. A DenseNet121-SelfONN-UNet model was then trained on organ-specific patches. Extensive data augmentation, normalization, five-fold cross-validation, and class-specific weighting were applied to mitigate severe class imbalance, particularly for the appendix.
  The initial stage achieved strong organ localization but underperformed for the appendix; class weighting improved its DSC from 6.76% to 85.76%. The second-stage DenseNet121-SelfONN-UNet significantly enhanced segmentation across all GI structures, with notable DSC gains (cecum +23.62%, sigmoid +18.57%, rectum +17.99%, small intestine +16.06%). Overall, the framework achieved mDSC of 88.99%, mIoU of 84.76%, and mHD95 of 6.94 mm, outperforming all baselines.
  This framework demonstrates the effectiveness of a coarse-to-fine, organ-aware segmentation strategy for intestinal MRE. Despite higher computational cost, it shows strong potential for clinical translation and enables anatomically informed diagnostic tools in gastroenterology.

</details>


### [676] [Chaos-Enhanced Prototypical Networks for Few-Shot Medical Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17300)
*Chinhtakuntla Meghan Sai,Murarisetty V Sai Kartheek,Sita Devi Bharatula,Karthik Seemakurthy*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: CE-ProtoNet integrates a Logistic Chaos Module into ResNet-18 to stabilize prototypes in few-shot brain tumor classification by injecting controlled chaotic perturbations during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Prototypical Networks struggle with prototype instability caused by morphological noise and high intra-class variance in brain tumor scans, especially in data-scarce clinical oncology settings.

Method: Integrates a non-linear Logistic Chaos Module into fine-tuned ResNet-18 backbone (CE-ProtoNet). Uses deterministic ergodicity of logistic chaos map to inject controlled perturbations into support features during episodic training, effectively "stress testing" the embedding space to learn noise-invariant representations.

Result: Achieved 84.52% test accuracy on 4-way 5-shot brain tumor classification, outperforming standard ProtoNet. Found 15% chaotic injection level efficiently stabilizes high-dimensional clusters and reduces class dispersion.

Conclusion: Chaotic perturbation serves as an efficient, low-overhead regularization tool for data-scarce regimes, helping stabilize prototypes in few-shot learning for medical imaging applications.

Abstract: The scarcity of labeled clinical data in oncology makes Few-Shot Learning (FSL) a critical framework for Computer Aided Diagnostics, but we observed that standard Prototypical Networks often struggle with the "prototype instability" caused by morphological noise and high intra-class variance in brain tumor scans. Our work attempts to minimize this by integrating a non-linear Logistic Chaos Module into a fine-tuned ResNet-18 backbone creating the Chaos-Enhanced ProtoNet(CE-ProtoNet). Using the deterministic ergodicity of the logistic chaos map we inject controlled perturbations into support features during episodic training-essentially for "stress testing" the embedding space. This process makes the model to converge on noise-invariant representations without increasing computational overhead. Testing this on a 4-way 5-shot brain tumor classification task, we found that a 15% chaotic injection level worked efficiently to stabilize high-dimensional clusters and reduce class dispersion. Our method achieved a peak test accuracy of 84.52%, outperforming standard ProtoNet. Our results suggest the idea of using chaotic perturbation as an efficient, low-overhead regularization tool, for the data-scarce regimes.

</details>


### [677] [BreathAI: Transfer Learning-Based Thermal Imaging for Automated Breathing Pattern Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17442)
*Hamza Kheddar,Yassine Himeur,Abbes Amira*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: ATL-TDLM uses thermal imaging, adaptive multi-thresholding, and knowledge distillation to achieve 98.8% accuracy in breathing pattern recognition, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a more accurate and efficient automated breathing pattern recognition system using thermal imaging instead of conventional sound-based methods, with applications in respiratory disorder detection like sleep apnea and asthma.

Method: Adaptive Transfer Learning and Thresholding-based Deep Learning Model (ATL-TDLM) that combines hierarchical deep feature extraction, adaptive multi-thresholding (AMT) for feature segmentation, knowledge distillation-based fine-tuning (KD-FT) for transfer learning optimization, and contrastive representation learning (CRL) to improve separation between inhalation and exhalation phases.

Result: Achieved 98.8% accuracy in breathing pattern recognition, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art models while maintaining computational efficiency.

Conclusion: The ATL-TDLM framework provides an effective thermal imaging-based solution for breathing pattern recognition with high accuracy and efficiency, showing strong potential for clinical applications in respiratory disorder monitoring and detection.

Abstract: This study presents an Adaptive Transfer Learning and Thresholding-based Deep Learning Model (ATL-TDLM) for automated breathing pattern recognition using thermal imaging. Unlike conventional methods that rely on sound-based respiratory data, our approach leverages hierarchical deep feature extraction and adaptive multi-thresholding (AMT) to enhance feature segmentation. The model integrates knowledge distillation-based fine-tuning (KD-FT) to optimize learning transfer and contrastive representation learning (CRL) to improve inter-class separability between inhalation (INH) and exhalation (EXH) phases. The ATL-TDLM framework achieves an accuracy of 98.8%, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art models while ensuring computational efficiency. This approach has potential applications in respiratory disorder detection, including sleep apnea and asthma monitoring.

</details>


### [678] [Learned Nonlocal Feature Matching and Filtering for RAW Image Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17453)
*Marco Sánchez-Beeckman,Antoni Buades*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A novel neural network architecture for RAW-to-RAW denoising that incorporates classical self-similarity principles into a learnable design, achieving state-of-the-art results with fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern deep learning denoisers often ignore classical denoising knowledge, rely on large parameter counts, and struggle with interpretability and deployment on resource-constrained devices with diverse noise profiles.

Method: Proposes a RAW-to-RAW denoising architecture with a novel nonlocal block that mimics classical patch-based methods (neighbor matching, collaborative filtering, aggregation) but operates on learned multiscale features. Uses single block per scale with moderate neighbors, trained on clean real RAW data with synthetic noise, conditioned on noise level maps.

Result: Achieves competitive results with state-of-the-art convolutional and transformer-based denoisers while using significantly fewer parameters. Generalizes effectively to unseen devices and performs well on benchmarks and real-world photographs.

Conclusion: The method provides a practical, interpretable solution for real-world RAW denoising that bridges classical self-similarity principles with modern deep learning, offering efficient deployment and good generalization across devices.

Abstract: Being one of the oldest and most basic problems in image processing, image denoising has seen a resurgence spurred by rapid advances in deep learning. Yet, most modern denoising architectures make limited use of the technical knowledge acquired researching the classical denoisers that came before the mainstream use of neural networks, instead relying on depth and large parameter counts. This poses a challenge not only for understanding the properties of such networks, but also for deploying them on real devices which may present resource constraints and diverse noise profiles. Tackling both issues, we propose an architecture dedicated to RAW-to-RAW denoising that incorporates the interpretable structure of classical self-similarity-based denoisers into a fully learnable neural network. Our design centers on a novel nonlocal block that parallels the established pipeline of neighbor matching, collaborative filtering and aggregation popularized by nonlocal patch-based methods, operating on learned multiscale feature representations. This built-in nonlocality efficiently expands the receptive field, sufficing a single block per scale with a moderate number of neighbors to obtain high-quality results. Training the network on a curated dataset with clean real RAW data and modeled synthetic noise while conditioning it on a noise level map yields a sensor-agnostic denoiser that generalizes effectively to unseen devices. Both quantitative and visual results on benchmarks and in-the-wild photographs position our method as a practical and interpretable solution for real-world RAW denoising, achieving results competitive with state-of-the-art convolutional and transformer-based denoisers while using significantly fewer parameters. The code is available at https://github.com/MIA-UIB/nonlocal-matchfilter .

</details>


### [679] [VIDS: A Verified Imaging Dataset Standard for Medical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17525)
*Joan S. Muthu,John Shalen*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: VIDS is a new open standard for medical imaging AI datasets that validates dataset structure, annotation provenance, quality documentation, and ML readiness with machine-enforceable rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current standards like DICOM and BIDS don't address dataset curation aspects - who annotated what, when, with what tools, and to what quality standards. There's no comprehensive framework for validating medical imaging datasets for AI development.

Method: VIDS defines folder layout, file naming, annotation provenance schemas, quality documentation, and 21 machine-enforceable validation rules across two compliance profiles. It uses NIfTI as working format while preserving DICOM metadata in sidecars for traceability.

Result: Major public datasets (LIDC-IDRI, BraTS, CheXpert, Medical Segmentation Decathlon) only satisfy 20-39% of VIDS compliance dimensions. LIDC-Hybrid-100 was released as a 100-subject VIDS-compliant CT dataset with consensus segmentations from four radiologists (mean Dice 0.7765), validating 21/21 on Full compliance.

Conclusion: VIDS provides the first comprehensive standard for medical imaging dataset validation, addressing systematic gaps in provenance and quality documentation. It's fully open source with tools available for adoption.

Abstract: Medical imaging AI development is fundamentally dependent on annotated datasets, yet no existing standard provides machine-enforceable validation across dataset structure, annotation provenance, quality documentation, and ML readiness within a single framework. DICOM standardizes image acquisition, storage, and communication at the individual study level. BIDS organizes neuroimaging research datasets with consistent naming conventions. Neither addresses the curation layer, viz., who annotated what, when, with what tool, and to what quality standard.
  This paper presents VIDS (Verified Imaging Dataset Standard), an open specification that defines folder layout, file naming, annotation provenance schemas, quality documentation, and 21 machine-enforceable validation rules across two compliance profiles. VIDS uses NIfTI as a canonical working format while preserving full DICOM metadata in sidecars for traceability, and supports export to any downstream ML framework (nnU-Net, MONAI, COCO, flat NIfTI) without loss of provenance.
  Twenty-two compliance dimensions are defined and four major public datasets -- LIDC-IDRI, BraTS, CheXpert, and the Medical Segmentation Decathlon -- are benchmarked against these dimensions. Even widely used datasets satisfy only 20--39% of these dimensions, with provenance and quality documentation as the largest systematic gaps. LIDC-Hybrid-100 is released as a 100-subject VIDS-compliant reference CT dataset with consensus segmentation masks from four radiologist annotations (mean pairwise Dice 0.7765), validating 21/21 on the Full compliance profile.
  VIDS is fully open source: the specification is CC BY 4.0, all tools are Apache 2.0, the reference validator is available on PyPI (pip install vids-validator), and LIDC-Hybrid-100 is published on Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19582717).

</details>


### [680] [Optimally Bridging Semantics and Data: Generative Semantic Communication via Schrödinger Bridge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17802)
*Dahua Gao,Ruichao Liu,Minxi Yang,Shuai Ma,Youlong Wu,Guangming Shi*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: SBGSC framework uses Schrödinger Bridges for direct semantic-to-image generation, reducing hallucinations and computational costs in generative semantic communication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GSC methods suffer from severe hallucination and high computational cost due to long, indirect transport trajectories from Gaussian to image distributions guided by semantics.

Method: Propose Schrödinger Bridge-based GSC (SBGSC) framework using SB for optimal transport between arbitrary distributions. Design Diffusion SB-based GSC (DSBGSC) reconstructing nonlinear drift terms with Schrödinger potentials, and add self-consistency objective to learn nonlinear velocity field for accelerated generation.

Result: DSBGSC outperforms state-of-the-art GSC methods, improving FID by at least 38% and SSIM by 49.3%, while accelerating inference speed by over 8 times.

Conclusion: SBGSC framework enables direct generative decoding from semantics to images, breaking Gaussian limitations and achieving superior performance with significantly reduced computational overhead.

Abstract: Generative Semantic Communication (GSC) is a promising solution for image transmission over narrow-band and high-noise channels. However, existing GSC methods rely on long, indirect transport trajectories from a Gaussian to an image distribution guided by semantics, causing severe hallucination and high computational cost. To address this, we propose a general framework named Schrödinger Bridge-based GSC (SBGSC). By leveraging the Schrödinger Bridge (SB) to construct optimal transport trajectories between arbitrary distributions, SBGSC breaks Gaussian limitations and enables direct generative decoding from semantics to images. Within this framework, we design Diffusion SB-based GSC (DSBGSC). DSBGSC reconstructs the nonlinear drift term of diffusion models using Schrödinger potentials, achieving direct optimal distribution transport to reduce hallucinations and computational overhead. To further accelerate generation, we propose a self-consistency-based objective guiding the model to learn a nonlinear velocity field pointing directly toward the image, bypassing Markovian noise prediction to significantly reduce sampling steps. Simulation results demonstrate that DSBGSC outperforms state-of-the-art GSC methods, improving FID by at least 38% and SSIM by 49.3%, while accelerating inference speed by over 8 times.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [681] [BrainMem: Brain-Inspired Evolving Memory for Embodied Agent Task Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16331)
*Xiaoyu Ma,Lianyu Hu,Wenbing Tang,Zixuan Hu,Zeqin Liao,Zhizhen Wu,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BrainMem is a training-free hierarchical memory system for embodied AI agents that combines working, episodic, and semantic memory inspired by human cognition to improve long-horizon task planning without model fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based embodied planners are stateless and reactive, lacking persistent memory which causes them to repeat errors and struggle with spatial/temporal dependencies in complex 3D environments.

Method: BrainMem transforms interaction histories into structured knowledge graphs and distilled symbolic guidelines, providing a plug-and-play memory system that integrates with arbitrary multi-modal LLMs without fine-tuning or additional training.

Result: Significantly enhances task success rates across four benchmarks (EB-ALFRED, EB-Navigation, EB-Manipulation, EB-Habitat), with largest gains on long-horizon and spatially complex tasks.

Conclusion: Evolving memory is a promising and scalable mechanism for generalizable embodied intelligence, reducing reliance on task-specific prompt engineering while improving performance across diverse models and difficulty levels.

Abstract: Embodied task planning requires agents to execute long-horizon, goal-directed actions in complex 3D environments, where success depends on both immediate perception and accumulated experience across tasks. However, most existing LLM-based planners are stateless and reactive, operating without persistent memory and therefore repeating errors and struggling with spatial or temporal dependencies. We propose BrainMem(Brain-Inspired Evolving Memory), a training-free hierarchical memory system that equips embodied agents with working, episodic, and semantic memory inspired by human cognition. BrainMem continuously transforms interaction histories into structured knowledge graphs and distilled symbolic guidelines, enabling planners to retrieve, reason over, and adapt behaviors from past experience without any model fine-tuning or additional training. This plug-and-play design integrates seamlessly with arbitrary multi-modal LLMs and greatly reduces reliance on task-specific prompt engineering. Extensive experiments on four representative benchmarks, including EB-ALFRED, EB-Navigation, EB-Manipulation, and EB-Habitat, demonstrate that BrainMem significantly enhances task success rates across diverse models and difficulty subsets, with the largest gains observed on long-horizon and spatially complex tasks. These results highlight evolving memory as a promising and scalable mechanism for generalizable embodied intelligence.

</details>


### [682] [Interdisciplinary Workshop on Mechanical Intelligence: Summary Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16381)
*Victoria A. Webster-Wood,Nicholas Gravish,Amir Alavi,Andres F Arrieta,Sarah Bergbreiter,Anthony Bloch,Laura Blumenschein,C. Chase Cao,Aja Mia Carter,Paolo Celli,Tony Chen,Margaret Coad,Mark Cutkosky,Michael Dickey,Brian Do,Robert Full,Mahdi Haghshenas-Jaryani,Kaushik Jayaram,Aaron Johnson,Eva Kanso,Emma Lejeune,Chen Li,Suyi Li,Jeffrey Lipton,Rob MacCurdy,Matt McHenry,Jean-Michel Mongeau,Todd Murphey,Mark Plecnik,Jordan Raney,Ryan D. Sochol,Hannah Stuart,Zeynep Temel,Michael Tolley,Barry Trimmer,T. J. Wallin,Kon-Well Wang,Wenzhong Yan,Mark Yim,Wenlong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Summary of 2024 Interdisciplinary Workshop on Mechanical Intelligence outcomes, defining MI as structural intelligence through responsiveness, adaptivity, memory, and learning in physical systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore and define Mechanical Intelligence (MI) as an emerging field distinct from computational intelligence, bringing together interdisciplinary experts to discuss how physical structures themselves can encode intelligence through mechanical properties rather than electronic computation.

Method: Two-day workshop at NSF headquarters with 38 academic researchers and 8 NSF program officers, structured around active small (4-5 people) and large (9-10 people) group discussions addressing topical questions on MI. Groups documented discussions in shared presentation slides and presented outcomes on final day.

Result: Workshop successfully brought together interdisciplinary experts to discuss Mechanical Intelligence, produced collaborative documentation of discussions and outcomes, and established foundational understanding of MI as structural intelligence through responsiveness, adaptivity, memory, and learning in material/biological/robotic systems.

Conclusion: The workshop provided a platform for defining and advancing the field of Mechanical Intelligence, establishing it as a distinct paradigm from computational intelligence, and creating collaborative foundations for future research in this emerging interdisciplinary area.

Abstract: This report provides a summary of the outcomes of the Interdisciplinary Workshop on Mechanical Intelligence held in 2024. Mechanical Intelligence (MI) represents the phenomenon that novel structural features of material/biological/robotic systems can encode intelligence through responsiveness, adaptivity, memory, and learning in the mechanical structure itself. This is in contrast to computational intelligence, wherein the intelligence functions occur through electrical signaling and computer code. The two-day workshop was held at NSF headquarters on May 30-31 and included 38 invited academic researcher participants, and 8 program officers from the NSF. The workshop was structured around active small and large group discussions in groups of 4-5 and 9-10 with the goal of addressing topical questions on MI. Working groups entered notes into shared presentation slides for each discussion session and presented their outcomes in a final presentation on the last day. Here we summarize the overall outcomes of the workshop.

</details>


### [683] [RHINO-AR: An Augmented Reality Exhibit for Teaching Mobile Robotics Concepts in Museums](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16384)
*Nils Dengler,Tim Graf,Leif Van Holland,Patrick Stotko,Reinhard Klein,Maren Bennewitz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RHINO-AR is an AR museum exhibit that places a virtual reconstruction of the historical RHINO robot into the real museum space, making robotics processes visible and understandable to visitors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous VR reconstruction (RHINO-VR) separated visitors from the real exhibition space and physical robot, creating a "reality gap." The goal is to create a more physically grounded experience that connects visitors to the actual museum environment.

Method: Implemented on Magic Leap 2 headset using Unity, combining real-time environment meshing with interactive visualizations of LiDAR sensing, traversability, and path planning to make invisible robotics processes visible.

Result: Two-day museum study with 22 participants showed RHINO-AR was well received, effectively conveyed key navigation concepts, and generally preferred over VR due to stronger physical grounding and increased realism.

Conclusion: AR can effectively bridge the reality gap in museum exhibits, providing more authentic and engaging experiences that connect visitors to physical spaces while making complex technical concepts accessible to non-experts.

Abstract: We present RHINO-AR, an interactive Augmented Reality (AR) museum exhibit that reintroduces the historical mobile robot RHINO into its original exhibition environment at the Deutsches Museum Bonn. The system builds on our previous work RHINO-VR, which reconstructed the robot and the environment in virtual reality. Although this created an engaging experience, it also revealed an important limitation, because visitors were separated from the real exhibition space and from the physical robot on display. RHINO-AR addresses this reality gap by placing a virtual reconstruction of the robot directly into the real museum space. Implemented on a Magic Leap~2 headset using Unity, our system combines real-time environment meshing with interactive visualizations of LiDAR sensing, traversability, and path planning to make otherwise invisible robotics processes understandable to non-expert visitors. We evaluated RHINO-AR in a two-day museum study with 22 participants, assessing usability, technical performance, satisfaction, conceptual understanding, and preference comparison to RHINO-VR. The results show that RHINO-AR was well received, effectively conveyed key navigation concepts, and generally preferred over the VR exhibit due to its stronger physical grounding and increased realism.

</details>


### [684] [Visual-RRT: Finding Paths toward Visual-Goals via Differentiable Rendering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16388)
*Sebin Lee,Jumin Lee,Taeyeon Kim,Younju Na,Woobin Im,Sung-Eui Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: vRRT enables robot motion planning to visual goals (images/videos) by combining differentiable rendering gradients with RRT sampling, using adaptive frontier exploration and inertial gradient expansion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RRT planners require precise numerical joint configurations as goals, but real-world applications often provide visual observations (images/demonstration videos) where exact goal configurations are unknown. There's a gap between sampling-based planning and vision-centric robot applications.

Method: Visual-RRT (vRRT) unifies gradient-based exploitation from differentiable robot rendering with sampling-based exploration from RRTs. It introduces: (1) frontier-based exploration-exploitation strategy that adaptively prioritizes visually promising search regions, and (2) inertial gradient tree expansion that inherits optimization states across tree branches for momentum-consistent gradient exploitation.

Result: Extensive experiments across various robot manipulators (Franka, UR5e, Fetch) demonstrate that vRRT achieves effective visual-goal planning in both simulated and real-world settings, successfully bridging the gap between sampling-based planning and vision-centric applications.

Conclusion: vRRT enables motion planning to visual goals by integrating differentiable rendering with RRT sampling, providing a practical solution for vision-based robot applications where precise goal configurations are unavailable.

Abstract: Rapidly-exploring random trees (RRTs) have been widely adopted for robot motion planning due to their robustness and theoretical guarantees. However, existing RRT-based planners require explicit goal configurations specified as numerical joint angles, while many practical applications provide goal specifications through visual observations such as images or demonstration videos where precise goal configurations are unavailable. In this paper, we propose visual-RRT (vRRT), a motion planner that enables visual-goal planning by unifying gradient-based exploitation from differentiable robot rendering with sampling-based exploration from RRTs. We further introduce (i) a frontier-based exploration-exploitation strategy that adaptively prioritizes visually promising search regions, and (ii) inertial gradient tree expansion that inherits optimization states across tree branches for momentum-consistent gradient exploitation. Extensive experiments across various robot manipulators including Franka, UR5e, and Fetch demonstrate that vRRT achieves effective visual-goal planning in both simulated and real-world settings, bridging the gap between sampling-based planning and vision-centric robot applications. Our code is available at https://sgvr.kaist.ac.kr/Visual-RRT.

</details>


### [685] [Disentangled Robot Learning via Separate Forward and Inverse Dynamics Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16391)
*Wenyao Zhang,Bozhou Zhang,Zekun Qi,Wenjun Zeng,Xin Jin,Li Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DeFI decouples visual forward and inverse dynamics pretraining to address VLA model limitations, using separate models for video prediction and action inference that are later integrated for end-to-end fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models face misalignment between 2D image forecasting and 3D action prediction, and their vision-action entangled training limits learning from action-free web video data.

Method: Proposes DeFI framework with two components: GFDM (General Forward Dynamics Model) pretrained on diverse videos for future prediction, and GIDM (General Inverse Dynamics Model) trained via self-supervised learning to infer latent actions from unlabeled video transitions. These are later integrated for end-to-end fine-tuning.

Result: State-of-the-art performance: average task length of 4.51 for CALVIN, 51.2% success rate on SimplerEnv-Fractal benchmark, and 81.3% success rate in real-world deployment, significantly outperforming prior methods.

Conclusion: Decoupling forward and inverse dynamics pretraining enables better exploitation of respective data sources and leads to superior performance in vision-language-action tasks for robotics.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown great potential in building generalist robots, but still face a dilemma-misalignment of 2D image forecasting and 3D action prediction. Besides, such a vision-action entangled training manner limits model learning from large-scale, action-free web video data. To address these issues, we propose DeFI, a novel framework that Decouples visual Forward and Inverse dynamics pretraining to exploit respective data sources, wherein video generation and action prediction are disentangled. We introduce the General Forward Dynamics Model (GFDM), pretrained on diverse human and robot videos for future prediction, and the General Inverse Dynamics Model (GIDM), trained via self-supervised learning to infer latent actions from unlabeled video transitions. These models are then integrated into a unified architecture for end-to-end finetuning on downstream tasks. In this manner, GFDM and GIDM first shine separately and then cooperate for mutual benefit. Extensive experiments on CALVIN ABC-D and SimplerEnv demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with DeFI achieving an average task length of 4.51 for CALVIN, 51.2% success rate on SimplerEnv-Fractal benchmark and 81.3% success rate in real-world deployment, significantly outperforming prior methods.

</details>


### [686] [ICAT: Incident-Case-Grounded Adaptive Testing for Physical-Risk Prediction in Embodied World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16405)
*Zhenglin Lai,Sirui Huang,Yuteng Li,Changxin Huang,Jianqiang Li,Bingzhe Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ICAT framework tests video-generative world models for safety by grounding evaluation in real incident reports and safety manuals to assess their ability to predict physical risks and severe consequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video-generative world models used as neural simulators for embodied planning often fail to accurately predict physical risks and severe outcomes, potentially leading to unsafe preferences during planning and policy training on imagined rollouts.

Method: ICAT (Incident-based Causal Assessment of Trajectories) builds structured risk memories from real incident reports and safety manuals, then retrieves and composes them to constrain generation of risk cases with causal chains and severity labels for systematic testing.

Result: Experiments on an ICAT-based benchmark show mainstream world models frequently miss risk mechanisms and triggering conditions, miscalibrate severity assessments, and fall short of reliability needed for safety-critical embodied deployment.

Conclusion: Current video-generative world models have significant safety limitations in predicting physical risks, highlighting the need for systematic safety evaluation frameworks like ICAT before deployment in safety-critical embodied AI applications.

Abstract: Video-generative world models are increasingly used as neural simulators for embodied planning and policy learning, yet their ability to predict physical risk and severe consequences is rarely evaluated.We find that these models often downplay or omit key danger cues and severe outcomes for hazardous actions, which can induce unsafe preferences during planning and training on imagined rollouts. We propose ICAT, which grounds testing in real incident reports and safety manuals by building structured risk memories and retrieving/composing them to constrain the generation of risk cases with causal chains and severity labels. Experiments on an ICAT-based benchmark show that mainstream world models frequently miss mechanisms and triggering conditions and miscalibrate severity, falling short of the reliability required for safety-critical embodied deployment.

</details>


### [687] [An Edge-Host-Cloud Architecture for Robot-Agnostic, Caregiver-in-the-Loop Personalized Cognitive Exercise: Multi-Site Deployment in Dementia Care](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16408)
*Wenzheng Zhao,Ruth Palan Lopez,Shu Fen Wung,Fengpei Yuan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Speaking Memories is a distributed robotic platform for personalized cognitive exercise support that integrates caregiver knowledge, edge computing, and robots to create emotion-aware dialogues while preserving privacy and enabling scalable deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the need for personalized cognitive exercise support systems that can integrate stakeholder knowledge, maintain privacy, and work across different robotic platforms while providing emotion-aware interactions.

Method: The system uses a distributed architecture with: 1) caregiver-authored knowledge via secure cloud portal, 2) local edge interaction server for multimodal perception and reasoning, 3) embodied robotic agents, and 4) automated multimodal evaluation layer for continuous analysis of user responses and engagement patterns.

Result: The platform achieves sub-6-second response latency, robust multimodal synchronization, and consistently positive feedback from participants and caregivers. It demonstrates scalable deployment across heterogeneous robotic embodiments with privacy-preserving operation.

Conclusion: Speaking Memories presents a successful distributed robotics architecture for cognitive exercise support that effectively integrates stakeholder knowledge, enables emotion-aware personalized dialogue, and provides scalable, privacy-preserving operation with promising real-world performance.

Abstract: We present Speaking Memories, a distributed, stakeholder-in-the-loop robotic interaction platform for personalized cognitive exercise support. Rather than a single robot-centric system, Speaking Memories is designed as a generalizable robotics architecture that integrates caregiver-authored knowledge, local edge intelligence, and embodied robotic agents into a unified socio-technical loop. The platform fuses auditory, visual, and textual signals to enable emotion-aware, personalized dialogue, while decoupling multimodal perception and reasoning from robot-specific hardware through a local edge interaction server. This design achieves low-latency, privacy-preserving operation and supports scalable deployment across heterogeneous robotic embodiments. Caregivers and family members contribute structured biographical knowledge via a secure cloud portal, which conditions downstream dialogue policies and enables longitudinal personalization across interaction sessions. Beyond real-time interaction, the system incorporates an automated multimodal evaluation layer that continuously analyzes user responses, affective cues, and engagement patterns, producing structured interaction metrics at scale. These metrics support systematic assessment of interaction quality, enable data-driven model fine-tuning, and lay the foundation for future clinician- and caregiver-informed personalization and intervention planning. We evaluate the platform through real-world deployments, measuring end-to-end latency, dialogue coherence, interaction stability, and stakeholder-reported usability and engagement. Results demonstrate sub-6-second response latency, robust multimodal synchronization, and consistently positive feedback from both participants and caregivers. Furthermore, subsets of the dataset can be shared upon request, subject to participant consent and IRB constraints.

</details>


### [688] [LatentMimic: Terrain-Adaptive Locomotion via Latent Space Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16440)
*Zhiquan Wang,Yunyu Liu,Dipam Patel,Ayush Kumar,Aniket Bera,Bedrich Benes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LatentMimic: A locomotion learning framework that decouples stylistic fidelity from geometric constraints for quadruped robots, enabling terrain-adaptive locomotion while preserving motion style.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing imitation-based methods face a trade-off: strict adherence to motion capture references penalizes geometric deviations needed for terrain adaptability, while terrain-centric policies compromise stylistic fidelity.

Method: Introduces LatentMimic framework that minimizes marginal latent divergence between policy's state-action distribution and learned mocap prior, providing conditional relaxation of rigid pose-tracking. Includes terrain adaptation module with dynamic replay buffer to resolve distribution shifts across terrains.

Result: Validated across four locomotion styles and four terrains, achieving higher terrain traversal success rates than state-of-the-art motion-tracking methods while maintaining high stylistic fidelity.

Conclusion: LatentMimic enables effective terrain-adaptive locomotion for quadruped robots by decoupling stylistic fidelity from geometric constraints, overcoming the fundamental optimization trade-off in existing imitation-based methods.

Abstract: Developing natural and diverse locomotion controllers for quadruped robots that can adapt to complex terrains while preserving motion style remains a significant challenge. Existing imitation-based methods face a fundamental optimization trade-off: strict adherence to motion capture (mocap) references penalizes the geometric deviations required for terrain adaptability, whereas terrain-centric policies often compromise stylistic fidelity. We introduce LatentMimic, a novel locomotion learning framework that decouples stylistic fidelity from geometric constraints. By minimizing the marginal latent divergence between the policy's state-action distribution and a learned mocap prior, our approach provides a conditional relaxation of rigid pose-tracking objectives. This formulation preserves gait topology while permitting independent end-effector adaptations for irregular terrains. We further introduce a terrain adaptation module with a dynamic replay buffer to resolve the policy's distribution shifts across different terrains. We validate our method across four locomotion styles and four terrains, demonstrating that LatentMimic enables effective terrain-adaptive locomotion, achieving higher terrain traversal success rates than state-of-the-art motion-tracking methods while maintaining high stylistic fidelity.

</details>


### [689] [Compiling OpenSCENARIO 2.1 for Scenario-Based Testing in CARLA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16452)
*Thoshitha Gamage,Lasanthi Gamage*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A modern compiler architecture translates OpenSCENARIO 2.1 DSL directly into executable CARLA behaviors using ANTLR4 frontend, semantic middle-end, and runtime backend with py_trees behavior trees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While OpenSCENARIO 2.1 DSL enables declarative, intent-driven authoring for scenario-based testing, its integration into open-source simulators like CARLA remains limited by legacy parsers, creating a need for better compilation approaches.

Method: Multi-pass compiler architecture with ANTLR4 frontend for AST generation, semantic middle-end, and runtime backend that synthesizes deterministic py_trees behavior trees. Maps standardized domain ontology directly to CARLA's procedural API via custom method registry, eliminating need for external logic solvers.

Result: Successfully demonstrated with multi-actor cut-in and evasive maneuver scenario, confirming compiler's ability to process concurrent actions, dynamic mathematical expressions, and asynchronous signaling. Establishes functional baseline for reproducible, large-scale scenario-based testing.

Conclusion: The framework provides a functional baseline for reproducible, large-scale scenario-based testing and paves the way for future C++ optimizations to mitigate current Python-based computational overhead.

Abstract: While the ASAM OpenSCENARIO 2.1 Domain-Specific Language (DSL) enables declarative, intent-driven authoring for Scenario-Based Testing (SBT), its integration into open-source simulators like CARLA remains limited by legacy parsers. We propose a multi-pass modern compiler architecture that translates the OpenSCENARIO 2.1 DSL directly into executable CARLA behaviors. The pipeline features an ANTLR4 frontend for Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) generation, a semantic middle-end, and a runtime backend that synthesizes deterministic py_trees behavior trees. Mapping the standardized domain ontology directly to CARLA's procedural API via a custom method registry eliminates the need for external logic solvers. A demonstrative multi-actor cut-in and evasive maneuver, selected from a wider suite of validated scenarios, confirms the compiler's ability to process concurrent actions, dynamic mathematical expressions, and asynchronous signaling. This framework establishes a functional baseline for reproducible, large-scale SBT, paving the way for future C++ optimizations to mitigate current Python-based computational overhead.

</details>


### [690] [Learning-Based Sparsification of Dynamic Graphs in Robotic Exploration Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16509)
*Adithya V. Sastry,Bibek Poudel,Weizi Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Transformer-based RL framework prunes exploration graphs to reduce redundancy, achieving up to 96% size reduction while maintaining consistent exploration performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph-based robotic exploration algorithms accumulate redundant information as graphs grow rapidly, impacting performance. Current methods lack intelligent pruning mechanisms to limit this growth while maintaining exploration effectiveness.

Method: Transformer-based framework trained with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to prune exploration graphs during frontier-based exploration using RRT. The policy learns to make pruning decisions based on exploration outcomes despite sparse, delayed rewards.

Result: Achieves up to 96% reduction in graph size. While intelligent pruning shows slightly lower exploration rate than baselines, it yields the lowest standard deviation, producing most consistent exploration across varied environments. First evidence of RL viability for dynamic graph sparsification in robotic exploration.

Conclusion: Transformer-based RL framework successfully prunes exploration graphs, reducing redundancy while maintaining consistent exploration performance. Demonstrates the viability of reinforcement learning for dynamic graph sparsification in robotic exploration algorithms.

Abstract: Many robotic exploration algorithms rely on graph structures for frontier-based exploration and dynamic path planning. However, these graphs grow rapidly, accumulating redundant information and impacting performance. We present a transformer-based framework trained with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to prune these graphs during exploration, limiting their growth and reducing the accumulation of excess information. The framework was evaluated on simulations of a robotic agent using Rapidly Exploring Random Trees (RRT) to carry out frontier-based exploration, where the learned policy reduces graph size by up to 96%. We find preliminary evidence that our framework learns to associate pruning decisions with exploration outcomes despite sparse, delayed reward signals. We also observe that while intelligent pruning achieves a lower rate of exploration compared to baselines, it yields the lowest standard deviation, producing the most consistent exploration across varied environments. To the best of our knowledge, these results are the first suggesting the viability of RL in sparsification of dynamic graphs used in robotic exploration algorithms.

</details>


### [691] [On-Orbit Space AI: Federated, Multi-Agent, and Collaborative Algorithms for Satellite Constellations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16518)
*Ziyang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Survey paper on AI for satellite constellations, covering federated learning, multi-agent algorithms, and collaborative sensing for constellation-scale autonomy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Satellite constellations are evolving into networked platforms requiring AI for autonomy, but existing AI research focuses on single satellites, missing the unique challenges of constellation-scale coordination.

Method: Three-paradigm framework: 1) Federated learning for cross-satellite training and secure aggregation, 2) Multi-agent algorithms for cooperative planning and control, 3) Collaborative sensing and distributed inference for multi-satellite fusion and split inference.

Result: Provides a comprehensive taxonomy unifying collaboration architectures, temporal mechanisms, and trust models, with ongoing community resources at GitHub repository.

Conclusion: Establishes the emerging field of on-orbit space AI, offering a systematic framework to address constellation-scale autonomy challenges and enabling future networked space systems.

Abstract: Satellite constellations are transforming space systems from isolated spacecraft into networked, software-defined platforms capable of on-orbit perception, decision making, and adaptation. Yet much of the existing AI studies remains centered on single-satellite inference, while constellation-scale autonomy introduces fundamentally new algorithmic requirements: learning and coordination under dynamic inter-satellite connectivity, strict SWaP-C limits, radiation-induced faults, non-IID data, concept drift, and safety-critical operational constraints. This survey consolidates the emerging field of on-orbit space AI through three complementary paradigms: (i) {federated learning} for cross-satellite training, personalization, and secure aggregation; (ii) {multi-agent algorithms} for cooperative planning, resource allocation, scheduling, formation control, and collision avoidance; and (iii) {collaborative sensing and distributed inference} for multi-satellite fusion, tracking, split/early-exit inference, and cross-layer co-design with constellation networking. We provide a system-level view and a taxonomy that unifies collaboration architectures, temporal mechanisms, and trust models. To support community development and keep this review actionable over time, we continuously curate relevant papers and resources at https://github.com/ziyangwang007/AI4Space.

</details>


### [692] [Human Cognition in Machines: A Unified Perspective of World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16592)
*Timothy Rupprecht,Pu Zhao,Amir Taherin,Arash Akbari,Arman Akbari,Yumei He,Sean Duffy,Juyi Lin,Yixiao Chen,Rahul Chowdhury,Enfu Nan,Yixin Shen,Yifan Cao,Haochen Zeng,Weiwei Chen,Geng Yuan,Jennifer Dy,Sarah Ostadabbas,Silvia Zhang,David Kaeli,Edmund Yeh,Yanzhi Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a unified framework for evaluating world models based on Cognitive Architecture Theory, identifies gaps in motivation and meta-cognition research, introduces Epistemic World Models for scientific discovery, and provides a taxonomy across different world model domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for proper evaluation of claims about "human-like" cognitive capabilities in world models by grounding them in Cognitive Architecture Theory principles, and to identify research gaps that previous taxonomies have missed.

Method: Developed a conceptual unified framework incorporating all cognitive functions from Cognitive Architecture Theory (memory, perception, language, reasoning, imagining, motivation, meta-cognition), analyzed existing works through this framework, and created a taxonomy applied across video, embodied, and epistemic world models.

Result: Identified that motivation (especially intrinsic motivation) and meta-cognition remain drastically under-researched in world models, introduced Epistemic World Models as a new category for scientific discovery frameworks, and proposed concrete research directions informed by active inference and global workspace theory.

Conclusion: The unified framework provides better evaluation criteria for world models, reveals critical research gaps in motivation and meta-cognition, and the new taxonomy offers novel research directions that previous classifications have overlooked, particularly for epistemic world models operating on structured knowledge.

Abstract: This comprehensive report distinguishes prior works by the cognitive functions they innovate. Many works claim an almost "human-like" cognitive capability in their world models. To evaluate these claims requires a proper grounding in first principles in Cognitive Architecture Theory (CAT). We present a conceptual unified framework for world models that fully incorporates all the cognitive functions associated with CAT (i.e. memory, perception, language, reasoning, imagining, motivation, and meta-cognition) and identify gaps in the research as a guide for future states of the art. In particular, we find that motivation (especially intrinsic motivation) and meta-cognition remain drastically under-researched, and we propose concrete directions informed by active inference and global workspace theory to address them. We further introduce Epistemic World Models, a new category encompassing agent frameworks for scientific discovery that operate over structured knowledge. Our taxonomy, applied across video, embodied, and epistemic world models, suggests research directions where prior taxonomies have not.

</details>


### [693] [Emergency Stopping for Liquid-manipulating Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16667)
*Samuli Hynninen,Ville Kyrki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Emergency stop system for robots manipulating liquids in open containers that generates time-optimal, spill-free stopping trajectories using model predictive control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emergency stopping during liquid manipulation is challenging because abrupt braking can cause hazardous spills, yet this problem has received little attention despite its importance for safety.

Method: Formulates emergency stopping as an optimal control problem and solves it in a model predictive control framework to generate time-optimal, spill-free stopping trajectories. Acts as a plug-and-play safety layer on top of existing slosh-free motion planning methods.

Result: Demonstrated through simulation and on a 7-DoF Franka Emika Panda robot that the approach achieves fast emergency stopping without spilling.

Conclusion: The proposed emergency stop system enables immediate reaction to detected hazards while accounting for nonlinear liquid dynamics, providing a safety layer for liquid manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Manipulating open liquid containers is challenging because liquids are highly sensitive to vessel accelerations and jerks. Although spill-free liquid manipulation has been widely studied, emergency stopping under unexpected hazards has received little attention, despite the fact that abrupt braking may cause hazardous spills. This letter presents an emergency stop system for robots manipulating liquids in open containers. We formulate emergency stopping as an optimal control problem and solve it in a model predictive control framework to generate time-optimal, spill-free stopping trajectories. The method operates as a plug-and-play safety layer on top of existing slosh-free motion planning methods, enabling immediate reaction to detected hazards while accounting for nonlinear liquid dynamics. We demonstrate, through simulation and on a 7-DoF Franka Emika Panda robot, that the proposed approach achieves fast emergency stopping without spilling.

</details>


### [694] [Diffusion-Based Optimization for Accelerated Convergence of Redundant Dual-Arm Minimum Time Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16670)
*Jushan Chen,Jonathan Fried,Santiago Paternain*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based framework for dual-arm robots that reduces runtime 35x and Cartesian error 34% compared to prior gradient-based optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous gradient-based optimization for dual-arm path following had high computation overhead and couldn't effectively impose L∞ Cartesian error constraints due to gradient sparsity issues.

Method: Uses a novel variant of model-based diffusion algorithm with probabilistic sampling to solve the nonconvex high-level optimization problem for dual-arm robot path following.

Result: Achieves 35x reduction in runtime and 34% less Cartesian error compared to prior gradient-based approach.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based probabilistic sampling effectively overcomes limitations of gradient-based optimization for dual-arm robot path following with significant performance improvements.

Abstract: We present a framework leveraging a novel variant of the model-based diffusion algorithm to minimize the time required for a redundant dual-arm robot configuration to follow a desired relative Cartesian path. Our prior work proposed a bi-level optimization approach for the dual-arm problem, where we derived the analytical solution to the lower-level convex sub-problem and solved the high-level nonconvex problem using a primal-dual approach. However, the gradient-based nature leads to a large computation overhead, and it prohibits directly imposing an $L_{\infty}$ Cartesian error constraint along the joint trajectory due to the sparsity of the gradient. In this work, we propose a diffusion-based framework that relies on probabilistic sampling to tackle the aforementioned challenges in the nonconvex high-level problem, leading to a 35x reduction in the runtime and 34\% less Cartesian error compared to our prior work.

</details>


### [695] [ReconVLA: An Uncertainty-Guided and Failure-Aware Vision-Language-Action Framework for Robotic Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16677)
*Lingling Chen,Zongyao Lyu,William J. Beksi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReconVLA adds conformal prediction to VLA models to provide calibrated uncertainty estimates for robotic control, improving failure anticipation and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models lack calibrated confidence measures for their action predictions, limiting reliability in real-world settings where uncertainty and failures must be anticipated.

Method: Apply conformal prediction directly to action token outputs of pretrained VLA policies to get calibrated uncertainty estimates. Also extend conformal prediction to robot state space to detect unsafe states before failures occur.

Result: Conformalized action predictions consistently improve failure anticipation, reduce catastrophic errors, and provide calibrated confidence measures without retraining or modifying the underlying VLA model.

Conclusion: ReconVLA provides a simple yet effective approach to make VLA controllers more reliable by adding conformal prediction for uncertainty quantification and failure detection.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have emerged as generalist robotic controllers capable of mapping visual observations and natural language instructions to continuous action sequences. However, VLAs provide no calibrated measure of confidence in their action predictions, thus limiting their reliability in real-world settings where uncertainty and failures must be anticipated. To address this problem we introduce ReconVLA, a reliable conformal model that produces uncertainty-guided and failure-aware control signals. Concretely, our approach applies conformal prediction directly to the action token outputs of pretrained VLA policies, yielding calibrated uncertainty estimates that correlate with execution quality and task success. Furthermore, we extend conformal prediction to the robot state space to detect outliers or unsafe states before failures occur, providing a simple yet effective failure detection mechanism that complements the action-level uncertainty. We evaluate ReconVLA in both simulation and real robot experiments across diverse manipulation tasks. Our results show that conformalized action predictions consistently improve failure anticipation, reduce catastrophic errors, and provide a calibrated measure of confidence without retraining or modifying the underlying VLA.

</details>


### [696] [Rewind-IL: Online Failure Detection and State Respawning for Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16683)
*Gehan Zheng,Sanjay Seenivasan,Matthew Johnson-Roberson,Weiming Zhi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Rewind-IL is a training-free online safeguard framework for action-chunked imitation policies that detects failures via temporal discrepancy monitoring and recovers by rewinding to verified safe states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Action-chunked imitation policies often fail to recover from execution drift, continuing to produce locally plausible actions after deviating from demonstration manifolds. Existing runtime monitors either need failure data, over-trigger, or lack recovery mechanisms.

Method: Combines zero-shot failure detection using Temporal Inter-chunk Discrepancy Estimate (TIDE) calibrated with split conformal prediction, with state-respawning to return to semantically verified safe intermediate states. Uses vision-language models offline to identify recovery checkpoints and builds a checkpoint feature database from frozen policy encoder.

Result: Experiments on real-world and simulated long-horizon manipulation tasks, including transfer to flow-matching action-chunked policies, demonstrate improved reliability through policy-internal consistency monitoring and semantically grounded respawning.

Conclusion: Rewind-IL offers a practical route to improved reliability in imitation learning by combining self-consistency monitoring with semantically verified state recovery, without requiring additional training or failure data.

Abstract: Imitation learning has enabled robots to acquire complex visuomotor manipulation skills from demonstrations, but deployment failures remain a major obstacle, especially for long-horizon action-chunked policies. Once execution drifts off the demonstration manifold, these policies often continue producing locally plausible actions without recovering from the failure. Existing runtime monitors either require failure data, over-trigger under benign feature drift, or stop at failure detection without providing a recovery mechanism. We present Rewind-IL, a training-free online safeguard framework for generative action-chunked imitation policies. Rewind-IL combines a zero-shot failure detector based on Temporal Inter-chunk Discrepancy Estimate (TIDE), calibrated with split conformal prediction, with a state-respawning mechanism that returns the robot to a semantically verified safe intermediate state. Offline, a vision-language model identifies recovery checkpoints in demonstrations, and the frozen policy encoder is used to construct a compact checkpoint feature database. Online, Rewind-IL monitors self-consistency in overlapping action chunks, tracks similarity to the checkpoint library, and, upon failure, rewinds execution to the latest verified safe state before restarting inference from a clean policy state. Experiments on real-world and simulated long-horizon manipulation tasks, including transfer to flow-matching action-chunked policies, demonstrate that policy-internal consistency coupled with semantically grounded respawning offers a practical route to improved reliability in imitation learning. Supplemental materials are available at https://sjay05.github.io/rewind-il

</details>


### [697] [Autonomous Vehicle Collision Avoidance With Racing Parameterized Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16702)
*Shathushan Sivashangaran,Vihaan Dutta,Apoorva Khairnar,Sepideh Gohari,Azim Eskandarian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DRL-based collision avoidance policy for autonomous vehicles outperforms MPC-APF baseline with 31x fewer FLOPS and 64x lower latency, successfully transferring from simulation to hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Road traffic accidents cause significant fatalities and economic costs, with human error responsible for 94% of crashes. Autonomous vehicles need emergency collision avoidance systems that operate at vehicle dynamics limits while being computationally efficient, especially for adverse conditions and mixed traffic environments.

Method: Parameterizes Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) collision avoidance policy using race car overtaking scenarios without explicit trajectory guidance. Uses physics-informed, simulator exploit-aware reward to encode nonlinear vehicle kinodynamics. Evaluates two policies: default uni-direction and reversed heading variant that navigates opposite to other cars.

Result: Both DRL policies outperform MPC-APF baseline, with zero-shot transfer to proportionally scaled hardware across three intersection collision scenarios. Achieves 31x fewer FLOPS and 64x lower inference latency. Reversed heading policy outperforms default policy by 30% and baseline by 50% in head-to-head collisions, matches default in side collisions, with both DRL policies evading 10% more than numerical optimal control.

Conclusion: DRL-based collision avoidance with physics-informed rewards provides computationally efficient, high-performance emergency response for autonomous vehicles, demonstrating superior performance over traditional control methods while maintaining real-time feasibility.

Abstract: Road traffic accidents are a leading cause of fatalities worldwide. In the US, human error causes 94% of crashes, resulting in excess of 7,000 pedestrian fatalities and $500 billion in costs annually. Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) with emergency collision avoidance systems that operate at the limits of vehicle dynamics at a high frequency, a dual constraint of nonlinear kinodynamic accuracy and computational efficiency, further enhance safety benefits during adverse weather and cybersecurity breaches, and to evade dangerous human driving when AVs and human drivers share roads.
