This paper parameterizes a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) collision avoidance policy Out-Of-Distribution (OOD) utilizing race car overtaking, without explicit geometric mimicry reference trajectory guidance, in simulation, with a physics-informed, simulator exploit-aware reward to encode nonlinear vehicle kinodynamics. Two policies are evaluated, a default uni-direction and a reversed heading variant that navigates in the opposite direction to other cars, which both consistently outperform a Model Predictive Control and Artificial Potential Function (MPC-APF) baseline, with zero-shot transfer to proportionally scaled hardware, across three intersection collision scenarios, at 31x fewer Floating Point Operations (FLOPS) and 64x lower inference latency. The reversed heading policy outperforms the default racing overtaking policy in head-to-head collisions by 30% and the baseline by 50%, and matches the former in side collisions, where both DRL policies evade 10% greater than numerical optimal control.

</details>


### [698] [LiDAR-based Crowd Navigation with Visible Edge Group Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16741)
*Allan Wang,Aaron Steinfeld*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a simplified visible edge-based group representation for robot navigation in crowded pedestrian environments, showing that group prediction accuracy has minimal impact on navigation performance in dense crowds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches for group-based robot navigation either work only in limited scenarios without high-density crowds, or rely on external detection modules that are prone to noise and errors due to occlusions in crowded environments.

Method: Proposes a visible edge-based group representation that simplifies group detection. The navigation framework integrates this simplified representation and is evaluated through simulation experiments and real-world deployment.

Result: The simplified group representation performs comparably to more complex methods in terms of safety and socialness in dense crowds, while achieving faster computation speed. Real-world deployment demonstrates practical benefits.

Conclusion: Group prediction accuracy has only marginal impact on navigation performance in crowded environments, making simplified visible edge-based group representations practical for real-world deployment in dense pedestrian settings.

Abstract: Robot navigation in crowded pedestrian environments is a well-known challenge and we explore the practical deployment of group-based representations in this setting. Pedestrian groups have been empirically shown to enable a mobile robot's navigation behavior to be safer and more social. However, existing approaches either explored groups only in limited scenarios with no high-density crowds or depended on external detection modules to track individuals, which are prone to noise and errors due to occlusions in crowds. We show that group prediction accuracy affects navigation performance only marginally in crowded environments. Based on this observation, we propose the visible edge-based group representation. We additionally demonstrate via simulation experiments that our navigation framework, integrated with the simplified group representation, performs comparatively in terms of safety and socialness in dense crowds, while achieving faster computation speed. Finally, we deploy our navigation framework on a real robot to explore the benefits of practically deploying group-based representations in the real world.

</details>


### [699] [LongBench: Evaluating Robotic Manipulation Policies on Real-World Long-Horizon Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16788)
*Xueyao Chen,Jingkai Jia,Tong Yang,Yibo Fu,Wei Li,Wenqiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LongBench is a real-world benchmark with 1,000+ episodes for evaluating long-horizon robotic manipulation, distinguishing between context-independent (fully observable) and context-dependent (ambiguity-driven) regimes to analyze execution robustness and temporal consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to explain why robotic manipulation policies degrade over extended horizons, as they are either simulation-based or report only aggregate success, making it difficult to identify specific sources of temporal difficulty in real-world execution.

Method: Created LongBench with over 1,000 real-world episodes covering two regimes: Context-Independent (fully observable) and Context-Dependent (ambiguity-driven). Organized tasks into capability- and ambiguity-specific subsets to enable mechanism-aware evaluation of execution robustness, temporal consistency, and context-dependent reasoning.

Result: Evaluation of six state-of-the-art policies revealed that long-horizon performance isn't governed by a single factor. Performance in fully observable settings correlates more with execution robustness, while contextual difficulty varies across tasks and isn't consistently improved by memory-based methods.

Conclusion: LongBench serves as a useful benchmark for studying long-horizon manipulation and developing policies with stronger robustness across both execution and contextual challenges, addressing the limitations of existing evaluation approaches.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation policies often degrade over extended horizons, yet existing benchmarks provide limited insight into why such failures occur. Most prior benchmarks are either simulation-based or report aggregate success, making it difficult to disentangle the distinct sources of temporal difficulty in real-world execution. We introduce LongBench, a real-world benchmark for evaluating long-horizon manipulation. LongBench consists of over 1,000 real-world episodes, covering two complementary regimes: Context-Independent (fully observable) and Context-Dependent (ambiguity-driven). By organizing tasks into capability- and ambiguity-specific subsets, LongBench enables mechanism-aware evaluation of execution robustness, temporal consistency, and context-dependent reasoning. Evaluating six state-of-the-art policies reveals that long-horizon performance is not governed by a single factor. We observe that performance in fully observable settings is more strongly associated with execution robustness, while contextual difficulty varies across tasks and is not consistently improved by memory-based methods. We hope that LongBench serves as a useful benchmark for studying long-horizon manipulation and for developing policies with stronger robustness across both execution and contextual challenges.

</details>


### [700] [Refinement of Accelerated Demonstrations via Incremental Iterative Reference Learning Control for Fast Contact-Rich Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16850)
*Koki Yamane,Cristian C. Beltran-Hernandez,Steven Oh,Masashi Hamaya,Sho Sakaino*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A method called Incremental Iterative Reference Learning Control (I2RLC) refines time-accelerated demonstrations for imitation learning by gradually increasing speed while updating reference trajectories, achieving up to 10x faster demonstrations with improved fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fast execution of contact-rich manipulation is critical for practical deployment, but obtaining fast demonstrations for imitation learning is challenging. Human demonstrations cannot be performed at high speeds, and simply accelerating demonstrations alters contact dynamics and causes large tracking errors.

Method: The paper proposes Incremental Iterative Reference Learning Control (I2RLC), which builds on Iterative Reference Learning Control (IRLC) but gradually increases speed while updating the reference trajectory. This addresses issues with direct high-speed IRLC that produces larger early-iteration errors and unstable transients. The method uses a teleoperation setup with a compliance-controlled follower robot and a 3D-printed haptic leader.

Result: Both IRLC and I2RLC achieve up to 10x faster demonstrations with reduced tracking error. I2RLC improves spatial similarity to original trajectories by 22.5% on average over IRLC across three tasks and multiple speeds (3x-10x). IL policies trained on refined trajectories execute faster than demonstrations and achieve 100% success rates in peg-in-hole tasks at both seen and unseen positions, with I2RLC-trained policies exhibiting lower contact forces.

Conclusion: Gradual speed scheduling coupled with reference adaptation provides a practical path to fast, contact-rich imitation learning. I2RLC enables high-fidelity accelerated demonstrations that can be used to train effective IL policies for contact-rich manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Fast execution of contact-rich manipulation is critical for practical deployment, yet providing fast demonstrations for imitation learning (IL) remains challenging: humans cannot demonstrate at high speed, and naively accelerating demonstrations alters contact dynamics and induces large tracking errors. We present a method to autonomously refine time-accelerated demonstrations by repurposing Iterative Reference Learning Control (IRLC) to iteratively update the reference trajectory from observed tracking errors. However, applying IRLC directly at high speed tends to produce larger early-iteration errors and less stable transients. To address this issue, we propose Incremental Iterative Reference Learning Control (I2RLC), which gradually increases the speed while updating the reference, yielding high-fidelity trajectories. We validate on real-robot whiteboard erasing and peg-in-hole tasks using a teleoperation setup with a compliance-controlled follower and a 3D-printed haptic leader. Both IRLC and I2RLC achieve up to 10x faster demonstrations with reduced tracking error; moreover, I2RLC improves spatial similarity to the original trajectories by 22.5% on average over IRLC across three tasks and multiple speeds (3x-10x). We then use the refined trajectories to train IL policies; the resulting policies execute faster than the demonstrations and achieve 100% success rates in the peg-in-hole task at both seen and unseen positions, with I2RLC-trained policies exhibiting lower contact forces than those trained on IRLC-refined demonstrations. These results indicate that gradual speed scheduling coupled with reference adaptation provides a practical path to fast, contact-rich IL.

</details>


### [701] [Greedy Kalman-Swarm: Improving State Estimation in Robot Swarms in Harsh Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16868)
*Phunyapa Suksomboon,Paulo Garcia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Greedy Kalman-Swarm: A decentralized state estimation approach for robotic swarms that uses localized, greedy integration of neighbor data without requiring global communication or full data availability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional swarm state estimation requires centralized processing or heavy communication protocols, which are impractical in real-world deployments with communication constraints. There's a need for decentralized approaches that maintain accuracy without global data sharing.

Method: A localized "greedy" distributed state estimation approach where individual robots leverage relative inter-robot sensing to integrate all currently available neighbor data at each iteration, without requiring full data availability or global communication.

Result: Simulations show robots can effectively integrate neighbor data to refine internal states while remaining robust when data is missing. The approach balances low overhead of independent estimation with high accuracy of centralized systems, particularly under harsh/dynamic conditions.

Conclusion: Global state awareness can emerge rather than be enforced, providing a scalable framework for swarm cohesion in unpredictable terrains. This decentralized methodology serves as a foundation for resilient autonomous systems in communication-constrained environments like search-and-rescue or space exploration.

Abstract: State estimation is a fundamental requirement in robotics, where the accurate determination of a robot's state is essential for stable operation despite inherent process disturbances and sensor noise. Traditionally, this is achieved through Kalman filtering, providing a statistically optimal estimate by balancing predictive models with noisy measurements. In the context of robotic swarms, the challenge shifts from individual accuracy to collective coordination, where the integration of global dynamics can significantly enhance the precision of the entire group. Existing estimation techniques rely on centralized processing or heavy communication protocols to reach a global consensus, which are frequently impractical in real-world deployments. Here we show that a localized, "greedy" approach to distributed state estimation (termed "Greedy Kalman-Swarm") allows individual robots to leverage relative inter-robot sensing for improved accuracy without requiring full data availability or global communication. Simulations in communication-constrained environments show robots can effectively integrate all currently available neighbor data at each iteration to refine their internal states, yet remain robust and functional even when data is missing. This results in a performance profile that strikes a balance between the low overhead of independent estimation and the high accuracy of centralized systems, specifically under harsh or dynamic environmental conditions. Our results demonstrate that global state awareness can be emergent rather than enforced, providing a scalable framework for maintaining swarm cohesion in unpredictable terrains. We anticipate that this decentralized methodology will serve as a foundation for more resilient autonomous systems, particularly in search-and-rescue or space exploration missions where reliable, high-bandwidth communication cannot be guaranteed.

</details>


### [702] [Memory Centric Power Allocation for Multi-Agent Embodied Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17810)
*Chengyang Li,Shuai Wang,Kejiang Ye,Weijie Yuan,Boyu Zhou,Yik-Chung Wu,Chengzhong Xu,Huseyin Arslan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a memory-centric approach for multi-agent embodied question answering, introducing Quality of Memory (QoM) model and Memory Centric Power Allocation (MCPA) to optimize robot team performance under communication constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent resource management methods focus on sensing, communication, or computation metrics, but MA-EQA requires emphasis on memory qualities since it involves querying robot teams about what they've seen over long horizons.

Method: Proposes a Quality of Memory (QoM) model based on Generative Adversarial Exam (GAE) that uses forward simulation to assess memory retrieval and compute QoM values. Then introduces Memory Centric Power Allocation (MCPA) that maximizes QoM function under communication resource constraints.

Result: Through asymptotic analysis, transmit powers are found to be proportional to GAE error probability, prioritizing high-QoM robots. Extensive experiments show MCPA achieves significant improvements over benchmarks across diverse metrics in various scenarios.

Conclusion: The memory-centric approach with QoM modeling and MCPA allocation effectively addresses the unique requirements of MA-EQA, outperforming traditional resource management methods that don't account for memory qualities.

Abstract: This paper considers multi-agent embodied question answering (MA-EQA), which aims to query robot teams on what they have seen over a long horizon. In contrast to existing edge resource management methods that emphasize sensing, communication, or computation performance metrics, MA-EQA emphasizes the memory qualities. To cope with this paradigm shift, we propose a quality of memory (QoM) model based on generative adversarial exam (GAE), which leverages forward simulation to assess memory retrieval and uses the resulting exam scores to compute QoM values. Then we propose memory centric power allocation (MCPA), which maximizes the QoM function under communication resource constraints. Through asymptotic analysis, it is found that the transmit powers are proportional to the GAE error probability, thus prioritizing towards high-QoM robots. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MCPA achieves significant improvements over extensive benchmarks in terms of diverse metrics in various scenarios.

</details>


### [703] [Chain Of Interaction Benchmark (COIN): When Reasoning meets Embodied Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16886)
*Xianhao Wang,Xiaojian Ma,Haozhe Hu,Rongpeng Su,Yutian Cheng,Zhou Ziheng,Hangxin Liu,Lei Liu,Bin Li,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: COIN is a benchmark for evaluating interactive reasoning in robotic manipulation, featuring 50 realistic tasks, a teleoperation system, and systematic metrics revealing current methods' limitations in causally-dependent reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to systematically evaluate interactive reasoning capabilities needed for real-world robotic manipulation, where agents must perform sequential interactions under partial observability to solve long-horizon tasks.

Method: 1) Created COIN-50 with 50 interactive tasks in daily scenarios, plus COIN-Primitive for causally-dependent tasks and COIN-Composition for skill generalization; 2) Developed low-cost mobile AR teleoperation system to collect 1,000 demonstrations; 3) Established systematic evaluation metrics for execution stability and generalization robustness.

Result: Comprehensive evaluation of CodeAsPolicy, VLA, and language-conditioned H-VLA approaches reveals critical limitations: models struggle with interactive reasoning tasks due to significant gaps between visual understanding and motor execution.

Conclusion: The COIN benchmark exposes fundamental weaknesses in current embodied AI methods for interactive reasoning, highlighting the need for better integration of visual understanding with motor execution capabilities for real-world robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Generalist embodied agents must perform interactive, causally-dependent reasoning, continually interacting with the environment, acquiring information, and updating plans to solve long-horizon tasks before they could be adopted in real-life scenarios. For instance, retrieving an apple from a cabinet may require opening multiple doors and drawers before the apple becomes visible and reachable, demanding sequential interaction under partial observability. However, existing benchmarks fail to systematically evaluate this essential capability. We introduce COIN, a benchmark designed to assess interactive reasoning in realistic robotic manipulation through three key contributions. First, we construct COIN-50: 50 interactive tasks in daily scenarios, and create COIN-Primitive required by causally-dependent tasks, and COIN-Composition with mid-term complexity for skill learning and generalization evaluation. Second, we develop a low-cost mobile AR teleoperation system and collect the COIN-Primitive Dataset with 50 demonstrations per primitive task (1,000 in total). Third, we develop systematic evaluation metrics about execution stability and generalization robustness to evaluate CodeAsPolicy, VLA, and language-conditioned H-VLA approaches. Our comprehensive evaluation reveals critical limitations in current methods: models struggle with interactive reasoning tasks due to significant gaps between visual understanding and motor execution. We provide fine-grained analysis of these limitations.

</details>


### [704] [Time-Division Multiplexing Actuation in Tendon-Driven Arms: Lightweight Design and Fault Tolerance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16887)
*Shoujie Li,Changqing Guo,Jianle Xu,Hong Luo,Xueqian Wang,Wenbo Ding,Bin Liang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TDMA enables tendon-driven robots to reduce actuator count while maintaining high torque and fault tolerance, demonstrated in the lightweight MuxArm with 2.17kg self-weight and 10kg load capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aerospace robotic manipulators need lightweight construction for weight limitations and fault-tolerant operation for reliability in remote, hazardous environments, requiring a balance between these competing demands.

Method: Time-Division Multiplexing Actuation (TDMA) with vertically-stacked rotational selection structure, self-rotating TDM motors, electromagnetic clutches (<0.1s engagement), worm gear reducer, dual-encoder system, and actuation space trajectory planning algorithm.

Result: MuxArm achieves 2.17kg self-weight, 10kg actuator driving capacity, 1% end-effector accuracy even under partial servo failure, and up to 50% tendon load reduction compared to conventional methods.

Conclusion: TDMA provides a practical solution for tendon-driven robots to achieve lightweight construction with fault tolerance, demonstrated through MuxArm's robust performance in various environments including free-space, cluttered, and confined settings.

Abstract: Robotic manipulators for aerospace applications require a delicate balance between lightweight construction and fault-tolerant operation to satisfy strict weight limitations and ensure reliability in remote, hazardous environments. This paper presents Time-Division Multiplexing Actuation (TDMA), a practical approach for tendon-driven robots that significantly reduces actuator count while preserving high torque output and intrinsic fault tolerance. The key hardware employs a vertically-stacked rotational selection structure that integrates self-rotating TDM motors for rapid configuration, electromagnetic clutches enabling sub-0.1 second engagement, a worm gear reducer for enhanced load capacity and self-locking capability, and a dual-encoder system for precise, long-term positioning. Leveraging TDMA, the proposed MuxArm achieves a self-weight of 2.17 kg, supports an actuator driving capacity of 10 kg, and maintains end-effector accuracy up to 1% of its length, even under partial servo failure. Additionally, an actuation space trajectory planning algorithm is developed, enabling fault-tolerant control and reducing tendon load by up to 50% compared to conventional methods. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate MuxArm's robust performance in diverse settings, including free-space, cluttered, and confined environments.

</details>


### [705] [Leveraging VR Robot Games to Facilitate Data Collection for Embodied Intelligence Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16903)
*Yihan Zhang,Ziyun Huang,Linqi Ye*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A gamified VR framework for collecting embodied interaction data using procedural scene generation and humanoid robot control, validated with a trash pick-and-place task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collecting embodied interaction data at scale is costly and difficult with conventional interfaces, so the authors propose a more accessible solution.

Method: Developed a Unity-based gamified framework with procedural scene generation, VR-based humanoid robot control, automatic task evaluation, and trajectory logging. Validated with a trash pick-and-place task prototype.

Result: Collected demonstrations show broad coverage of state-action space, with increased task difficulty leading to higher motion intensity and more extensive arm workspace exploration.

Conclusion: Game-oriented virtual environments can serve as an effective and extensible solution for embodied data collection.

Abstract: Collecting embodied interaction data at scale remains costly and difficult due to the limited accessibility of conventional interfaces. We present a gamified data collection framework based on Unity that combines procedural scene generation, VR-based humanoid robot control, automatic task evaluation, and trajectory logging. A trash pick-and-place task prototype is developed to validate the full workflow.Experimental results indicate that the collected demonstrations exhibit broad coverage of the state-action space, and that increasing task difficulty leads to higher motion intensity as well as more extensive exploration of the arm's workspace. The proposed framework demonstrates that game-oriented virtual environments can serve as an effective and extensible solution for embodied data collection.

</details>


### [706] [Multi-stage Planning for Multi-target Surveillance using Aircrafts Equipped with Synthetic Aperture Radars Aware of Target Visibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16962)
*Daniel Fuertes,Carlos R. del-Blanco,Fernando Jaureguizar,Juan José Navarro-Corcuera,Narciso García*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multi-stage planning system for SAR aircraft trajectories that optimizes straight-flight segments for multi-target imaging while considering 3D terrain and target visibility constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SAR trajectory planning methods don't adapt to target visibility based on 3D terrain and aircraft orientation, and don't scale well for multi-target scenarios requiring real-time operations.

Method: Three-stage approach: 1) Waypoint sequencing to visit all targets, 2) Neural network with deep reinforcement learning to predict straight-flight segments maximizing target visibility considering 3D terrain, 3) Optimization connecting segments using 3D Dubins curves.

Result: System demonstrates robustness for SAR missions, ensuring high-quality multi-target SAR image acquisition while being aware of 3D terrain and target visibility, with real-time performance.

Conclusion: Proposed multi-stage planning system effectively addresses the challenges of SAR trajectory planning by integrating waypoint sequencing, neural network-based segment prediction, and optimization for real-time multi-target operations.

Abstract: Generating trajectories for synthetic aperture radar (SAR)-equipped aircraft poses significant challenges due to terrain constraints, and the need for straight-flight segments to ensure high-quality imaging. Related works usually focus on trajectory optimization for predefined straight-flight segments that do not adapt to the target visibility, which depends on the 3D terrain and aircraft orientation. In addition, this assumption does not scale well for the multi-target problem, where multiple straight-flight segments that maximize target visibility must be defined for real-time operations. For this purpose, this paper presents a multi-stage planning system. First, the waypoint sequencing to visit all the targets is estimated. Second, straight-flight segments maximizing target visibility according to the 3D terrain are predicted using a novel neural network trained with deep reinforcement learning. Finally, the segments are connected to create a trajectory via optimization that imposes 3D Dubins curves. Evaluations demonstrate the robustness of the system for SAR missions since it ensures high-quality multi-target SAR image acquisition aware of 3D terrain and target visibility, and real-time performance.

</details>


### [707] [NaviFormer: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Transformer-like Model to Holistically Solve the Navigation Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16967)
*Daniel Fuertes,Andrea Cavallaro,Carlos R. del-Blanco,Fernando Jaureguizar,Narciso García*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NaviFormer is a Transformer-based deep reinforcement learning model that simultaneously solves both high-level route planning and low-level trajectory prediction for global navigation problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world navigation requires solving both route planning (waypoint sequencing) and path planning (collision-free trajectory prediction) simultaneously, but existing approaches typically address these subproblems separately rather than holistically.

Method: NaviFormer uses a Transformer architecture with deep reinforcement learning to predict both high-level routes and low-level trajectories in an integrated manner.

Result: NaviFormer shows competitive accuracy by understanding constraints of both subproblems, and demonstrates superior computation speed suitable for real-time missions.

Conclusion: The proposed NaviFormer provides an efficient holistic solution to global navigation by simultaneously addressing route and path planning, making it suitable for real-time applications.

Abstract: Path planning is usually solved by addressing either the (high-level) route planning problem (waypoint sequencing to achieve the final goal) or the (low-level) path planning problem (trajectory prediction between two waypoints avoiding collisions). However, real-world problems usually require simultaneous solutions to the route and path planning subproblems with a holistic and efficient approach. In this paper, we introduce NaviFormer, a deep reinforcement learning model based on a Transformer architecture that solves the global navigation problem by predicting both high-level routes and low-level trajectories. To evaluate NaviFormer, several experiments have been conducted, including comparisons with other algorithms. Results show competitive accuracy from NaviFormer since it can understand the constraints and difficulties of each subproblem and act consequently to improve performance. Moreover, its superior computation speed proves its suitability for real-time missions.

</details>


### [708] [Neural Network-Based Adaptive Event-Triggered Control for Dual-Arm Unmanned Aerial Manipulator Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17048)
*Yang Wang,Hai Yu,Wei He,Jianda Han,Yongchun Fang,Xiao Liang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive event-triggered control with neural network approximation for dual-arm UAV manipulators to handle coupling, disturbances, and communication constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dual-arm unmanned aerial manipulator systems face challenges from strong coupling between arms and platform, unmodeled dynamics, and external disturbances, making stable and accurate operation difficult. Communication constraints further complicate control implementation.

Method: Proposes adaptive event-triggered control with neural network approximation: 1) Derives DAUAM dynamic model, 2) Constructs command-filter-based backstepping framework with error compensation, 3) Uses neural network to approximate external frictions, 4) Designs event-triggered mechanism to reduce control update transmission frequency.

Result: Lyapunov analysis shows all closed-loop signals remain bounded with tracking error converging to neighborhood of desired trajectory within fixed time. Experiments on self-built DAUAM platform demonstrate accurate trajectory tracking.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive event-triggered control scheme effectively addresses coupling, disturbances, and communication constraints in DAUAM systems, achieving stable and accurate operation while reducing communication and energy burdens.

Abstract: This paper investigates the control problem of dual-arm unmanned aerial manipulator systems (DAUAMs). Strong coupling between the dual-arm and the multirotor platform, together with unmodeled dynamics and external disturbances, poses significant challenges to stable and accurate operation. An adaptive event-triggered control scheme with neural network-based approximation is proposed to address these issues while explicitly considering communication constraints. First, a dynamic model of the DAUAM system is derived, and a command-filter-based backstepping framework with error compensation is constructed. Then, a neural network is employed to approximate external frictions, and an event-triggered mechanism is designed to reduce the transmission frequency of control updates, thereby alleviating communication and energy burdens. Lyapunov-based analysis shows that all closed-loop signals remain bounded and that the tracking error converges to a neighborhood of the desired trajectory within a fixed time. Finally, experiments on a self-built DAUAM platform demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves accurate trajectory tracking.

</details>


### [709] [Web-Gewu: A Browser-Based Interactive Playground for Robot Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17050)
*Kaixuan Chen,Linqi Ye*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Web-Gewu is a browser-based robotics education platform using WebRTC cloud-edge-client architecture to enable low-latency, installation-free robot interaction and RL training visualization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotics education faces challenges with high computational barriers and cumbersome environment setup. Cloud solutions are expensive for large-scale deployment, while local computing is limited by learners' hardware constraints.

Method: Built on WebRTC cloud-edge-client collaborative architecture. Offloads physics simulation and RL training to edge nodes, uses cloud server as lightweight signaling relay for browser-based P2P real-time streaming. Includes predefined command communication protocol.

Result: Enables learners to interact with multi-form robots at low end-to-end latency directly in web browsers without installation, with real-time visualization of multi-dimensional monitoring data including RL reward curves.

Conclusion: Provides highly scalable, out-of-the-box, barrier-free teaching infrastructure for embodied intelligence, significantly lowering entry barriers for cutting-edge robotics technology.

Abstract: With the rapid development of embodied intelligence, robotics education faces a dual challenge: high computational barriers and cumbersome environment configuration. Existing centralized cloud simulation solutions incur substantial GPU and bandwidth costs that preclude large-scale deployment, while pure local computing is severely constrained by learners' hardware limitations. To address these issues, we propose \href{http://47.76.242.88:8080/receiver/index.html}{Web-Gewu}, an interactive robotics education platform built on a WebRTC cloud-edge-client collaborative architecture. The system offloads all physics simulation and reinforcement learning (RL) training to the edge node, while the cloud server acts exclusively as a lightweight signaling relay, enabling extremely low-cost browser-based peer-to-peer (P2P) real-time streaming. Learners can interact with multi-form robots at low end-to-end latency directly in a web browser without any local installation, and simultaneously observe real-time visualization of multi-dimensional monitoring data, including reinforcement learning reward curves. Combined with a predefined robust command communication protocol, Web-Gewu provides a highly scalable, out-of-the-box, and barrier-free teaching infrastructure for embodied intelligence, significantly lowering the barrier to entry for cutting-edge robotics technology.

</details>


### [710] [Shepherding UAV Swarm with Action Prediction Based on Movement Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17189)
*Yusuke Tsunoda,Yusuke Goto,Takao Sato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a sheepdog-inspired UAV swarm control method with motion constraint-aware behavior prediction using Dynamic Window Approach principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sheepdog-inspired guidance methods treat agents as point masses without considering real robot motion constraints (velocity/acceleration limits) and lack predictive capabilities for more efficient swarm guidance.

Method: 3D guidance control law using behavior prediction under motion constraints, inspired by Dynamic Window Approach. Navigator agents generate feasible motion candidates satisfying constraints, predict short-horizon swarm evolution using internal models, and evaluate candidates based on target progress, positioning strategy, and safety margins.

Result: Numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed guidance control law for safe and efficient UAV swarm guidance.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses motion constraints of real UAVs and incorporates predictive swarm behavior modeling, enabling more practical and efficient sheepdog-inspired swarm guidance compared to conventional approaches.

Abstract: In this study, we propose a new sheepdog-inspired control method for a swarm of small unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), which predicts the swarm behavior while explicitly accounting for the motion constraints of real robots. Sheepdog-inspired guidance control refers to a framework in which a small number of navigator agents (sheepdog agents) indirectly drive a large number of autonomous agents (a flock of sheep agents) so as to steer the group toward a target position. In conventional studies on sheepdog-inspired guidance, both types of agents have typically been modeled as point masses, and the guidance law for the navigator agents has been designed using simple interaction vectors based on the instantaneous relative positions between the agents. However, when implementing such methods on real robots such as drones, it is necessary to consider each agent's motion constraints, including upper bounds on velocity and acceleration. Moreover, we argue that guidance can be made more efficient by predicting the future behavior of the autonomous swarm that is observable to the navigator agents. To this end, we propose a three-dimensional guidance control law based on behavior prediction of autonomous agents under motion constraints, inspired by the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA). At each control cycle, the navigator agent generates a set of feasible motion candidates that satisfy its motion constraints, and predicts the short-horizon swarm evolution using an internal model of the autonomous agents maintained within the navigator agent. The motion candidates are then evaluated according to criteria such as the progress velocity toward the target, the positioning strategy with respect to the swarm, and safety margins, and the optimal motion is selected to achieve safe and efficient guidance. Numerical simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed guidance control law.

</details>


### [711] [Modeling, Control and Self-sensing of Dielectric Elastomer Soft Actuators: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17199)
*Y. Zhao,G. Meng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper reviews modeling, control, and self-sensing methods for dielectric elastomer actuators (DEAs) in soft robotics, addressing challenges like nonlinear elasticity, viscoelastic creep, hysteresis, and vibrational dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DEAs have advantages for soft robotics (lightweight, large strain, fast response, high energy density) but face challenges in modeling, control, and self-sensing due to nonlinear elasticity, viscoelastic creep, hysteresis, and vibrational dynamics, hindering practical applications.

Method: Review paper that presents and discusses: 1) physics-based and phenomenological modeling methods for predicting DEA electromechanical response, 2) control methods (open-loop feedforward, feedback, feedforward-feedback, adaptive feedforward), 3) physics-based and data-driven self-sensing methods for displacement reconstruction without additional sensors.

Result: The review comprehensively surveys existing approaches to address DEA challenges, providing a systematic classification of modeling, control, and self-sensing techniques developed in the field.

Conclusion: The paper summarizes existing problems and identifies new opportunities for further studies in DEA modeling, control, and self-sensing to advance practical applications in soft robotics.

Abstract: Dielectric elastomer actuators (DEAs) have garnered extensive attention especially in soft robotic applications over the past few decades owing to the advantages of lightweight, large strain, fast response and high energy density. However, because the DEAs suffer from nonlinear elasticity, inherent viscoelastic creep, hysteresis and vibrational dynamics, the modeling, control and self-sensing of DEAs are challenging, thereby hindering the practical applications of DEAs. In order to address these challenges, numerous studies have been conducted. In this review, various physics-based modeling methods and phenomenological modeling methods for predicting the electromechanical response of DEAs are presented and discussed. Different control methods for DEAs are reviewed, which are classified into open-loop feedforward control, feedback control, feedforward-feedback control and adaptive feedforward control. Physics-based self-sensing methods and data-driven self-sensing methods for reconstructing the DEA displacement without the need for additional sensors are discussed. Finally, the existing problems and new opportunities for the further studies are summarized.

</details>


### [712] [Planning Smooth and Safe Control Laws for a Unicycle Robot Among Obstacles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17212)
*Aref Amiri,Basak Sakcak,Steven M. LaValle*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A framework for safe navigation of unicycle robots using smooth vector fields and nonlinear feedback control that guarantees safety without online optimization, achieving faster arrival with lower control effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable safe navigation of unicycle robots from almost any admissible state to a goal position in obstacle-populated environments while respecting input limits, without requiring computationally expensive online optimization.

Method: Introduces a novel QP formulation to create C∞-smooth vector fields with reduced bending/turning, then designs an analytic nonlinear feedback controller that inherently satisfies Nagumo's theorem conditions for forward invariance of safe sets.

Result: The controller safely converges to goal position even under hard input limits, achieving twice faster arrival time with over 50% lower angular control effort compared to baseline in simulations.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a computationally efficient, safe navigation solution for unicycle robots that guarantees forward invariance without online optimization, significantly improving performance metrics.

Abstract: This paper presents a framework for safe navigation of a unicycle point robot to a goal position in an environment populated with obstacles from almost any admissible state, considering input limits. We introduce a novel QP formulation to create a Cinfinity-smooth vector field with reduced total bending and total turning. Then we design an analytic, non-linear feedback controller that inherently satisfies the conditions of Nagumo's theorem, ensuring forward invariance of the safe set without requiring any online optimization. We have demonstrated that our controller, even under hard input limits, safely converges to the goal position. Simulations confirm the effectiveness of the proposed framework, resulting in a twice faster arrival time with over 50\% lower angular control effort compared to the baseline.

</details>


### [713] [GaLa: Hypergraph-Guided Visual Language Models for Procedural Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17241)
*Kun Wang,Yiming Li,Mingcheng Qu,Aqiang Zhang,Guang Yang,Tonghua Su*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GaLa is a vision-language framework that uses hypergraph representations to capture implicit spatial relations and semantic structures for better procedural planning in embodied AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches over-rely on VLMs' reasoning capabilities while overlooking structured semantic information in multimodal inputs, causing models to struggle with understanding functional spatial relationships in complex scenes.

Method: GaLa introduces a hypergraph-based representation where object instances are nodes, and region-level hyperedges aggregate objects by attributes/functional semantics. It uses a TriView HyperGraph Encoder with contrastive learning across node, area, and node-area association views.

Result: Extensive experiments on ActPlan1K and ALFRED benchmarks show GaLa significantly outperforms existing methods in execution success rate, LCS, and planning correctness.

Conclusion: GaLa effectively exploits implicit spatial relations and deep semantic structures in multimodal data through hypergraph representations, improving procedural planning performance in embodied AI systems.

Abstract: Implicit spatial relations and deep semantic structures encoded in object attributes are crucial for procedural planning in embodied AI systems. However, existing approaches often over rely on the reasoning capabilities of vision language models (VLMs) themselves, while overlooking the rich structured semantic information that can be mined from multimodal inputs. As a result, models struggle to effectively understand functional spatial relationships in complex scenes. To fully exploit implicit spatial relations and deep semantic structures in multimodal data, we propose GaLa, a vision language framework for multimodal procedural planning. GaLa introduces a hypergraph-based representation, where object instances in the image are modeled as nodes, and region-level hyperedges are constructed by aggregating objects according to their attributes and functional semantics. This design explicitly captures implicit semantic relations among objects as well as the hierarchical organization of functional regions. Furthermore, we design a TriView HyperGraph Encoder that enforces semantic consistency across the node view, area view, and node area association view via contrastive learning, enabling hypergraph semantics to be more effectively injected into downstream VLM reasoning. Extensive experiments on the ActPlan1K and ALFRED benchmarks demonstrate that GaLa significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of execution success rate, LCS, and planning correctness.

</details>


### [714] [MM-Hand: A 21-DOF Multi-modal Modular Dexterous Robotic Hand with Remote Actuation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17245)
*Zhuoheng Li,Qingquan Lin,Checheng Yu,Qiangyu Chen,Zhiqian Lan,Lutong Zhang,Hongyang Li,Ping Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MM-Hand is a 21-DOF modular dexterous hand using remote tendon-driven actuation with multimodal sensing and open-source design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional dexterous hand designs occupy valuable in-hand space, increase mass, and suffer from heat accumulation near the hand. Remote tendon-driven actuation can free fingers and palm for more DOFs, sensing, and maintainable structures.

Method: Developed MM-Hand with spring-return tendon-driven fingers, modular 3D-printed structures, quick tendon connectors, and multimodal sensing (joint angle, tactile, motor feedback, in-palm stereo vision). Analyzed tendon-sheath length variation and friction loss for routing design.

Result: Fingertip force reaches 25N under 1m remote sheath transmission. Validated transmission, output force, sensing, and control capabilities. Closed-loop joint tracking works with static arm and during arm motion.

Conclusion: MM-Hand provides lightweight, sensor-rich, maintainable platform for dexterous manipulation research. All hardware designs and software are open-sourced to support community.

Abstract: High-DOF dexterous hands require compact actuation, rich sensing, and reliable thermal behavior, but conventional designs often occupy valuable in-hand space, increase end-effector mass, and suffer from heat accumulation near the hand. Remote tendon-driven actuation offers an alternative by relocating motors to the robot base or an external motor hub, thereby freeing the fingers and palm for additional degrees of freedom, sensing modules, and maintainable mechanical structures. This paper presents MM-Hand, a 21-DOF Multimodal Modular dexterous hand based on remote tendon-driven actuation. The hand integrates spring-return tendon-driven fingers, modular 3D-printed finger and palm structures, quick tendon connectors for maintenance, and a multimodal sensing system including joint angle sensors, tactile sensors, motor-side feedback, and in-palm stereo vision. We further analyze tendon-sheath length variation and friction loss to guide the design of the routing, motor hub, and closed-loop joint control. Experiments validate the transmission, output force, sensing, and control capability of the system. The fingertip force reaches 25N under a 1m remote sheath transmission, demonstrating practical load capacity despite long-distance tendon routing. Closed-loop joint-level experiments further evaluate command tracking with a static arm and during arm motion. These results show that MM-Hand provides a lightweight, sensor-rich, and maintainable hardware platform for dexterous manipulation research. To support the community, all hardware designs and software frameworks are made fully open-source at https://mmlab.hk/research/MM-Hand.

</details>


### [715] [A Rapid Deployment Pipeline for Autonomous Humanoid Grasping Based on Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17258)
*Yifei Yan,Yankai Liao,Linqi Ye*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: End-to-end pipeline reduces humanoid robot deployment time for new objects from 1-2 days to ~30 minutes using foundation models for automatic annotation, 3D reconstruction, and zero-shot pose tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional humanoid robot deployment for new objects requires 1-2 days of manual work including data collection, annotation, 3D modeling, and training, creating a significant barrier to practical adoption.

Method: Integrated pipeline with three foundation-model components: (1) Roboflow-based automatic annotation for YOLOv8 object detector training, (2) Meta SAM 3D for 3D reconstruction without laser scanners, and (3) FoundationPose for zero-shot 6-DoF pose tracking using SAM 3D-generated mesh. Unity-based IK planner sends commands via UDP to Unitree G1 humanoid.

Result: Achieved detection accuracy of mAP@0.5 = 0.995, pose tracking precision σ < 1.05 mm, successful grasping at five workspace positions, and verified on automobile-window glue-application task.

Conclusion: Combining foundation models for perception with everyday imaging devices (like smartphones) can substantially lower deployment barriers for humanoid manipulation tasks, enabling rapid onboarding of new objects.

Abstract: Deploying a humanoid robot to manipulate a new object has traditionally required one to two days of effort: data collection, manual annotation, 3D model acquisition, and model training. This paper presents an end-to-end rapid deployment pipeline that integrates three foundation-model components to shorten the onboarding cycle for a new object to approximately 30 minutes: (i) Roboflow-based automatic annotation to assist in training a YOLOv8 object detector; (ii) 3D reconstruction based on Meta SAM 3D, which eliminates the need for a dedicated laser scanner; and (iii) zero-shot 6-DoF pose tracking based on FoundationPose, using the SAM~3D-generated mesh directly as the template. The estimated pose drives a Unity-based inverse kinematics planner, whose joint commands are streamed via UDP to a Unitree~G1 humanoid and executed through the Unitree SDK. We demonstrate detection accuracy of mAP@0.5 = 0.995, pose tracking precision of $σ< 1.05$ mm, and successful grasping on a real robot at five positions within the workspace. We further verify the generality of the pipeline on an automobile-window glue-application task. The results show that combining foundation models for perception with everyday imaging devices (e.g., smartphones) can substantially lower the deployment barrier for humanoid manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [716] [Learning Whole-Body Humanoid Locomotion via Motion Generation and Motion Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17335)
*Zewei Zhang,Kehan Wen,Michael Xu,Junzhe He,Chenhao Li,Takahiro Miki,Clemens Schwarke,Chong Zhang,Xue Bin Peng,Marco Hutter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A whole-body humanoid locomotion framework combining diffusion-based terrain-aware motion generation with RL-based reference tracking for robust, adaptive locomotion on various terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of existing humanoid locomotion approaches: RL with reward shaping leads to lower-body-dominated behaviors, while imitation-based RL lacks online adaptation to terrain from perception.

Method: Two-stage approach: 1) Train diffusion model on retargeted human motions for real-time terrain-aware reference motion generation. 2) Train RL-based whole-body reference tracker using this motion data, then fine-tune tracker with frozen motion generator in closed-loop for robustness.

Result: Successfully deployed on Unitree G1 humanoid with onboard perception/computation, demonstrating traversal over boxes, hurdles, stairs, and mixed terrain combinations. Quantitative results show improved generalization and robustness from online motion generation and tracker fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Proposed framework enables terrain-aware whole-body humanoid locomotion with directional goal-reaching control, combining the benefits of imitation-based skills with online perceptual adaptation for robust real-world deployment.

Abstract: Whole-body humanoid locomotion is challenging due to high-dimensional control, morphological instability, and the need for real-time adaptation to various terrains using onboard perception. Directly applying reinforcement learning (RL) with reward shaping to humanoid locomotion often leads to lower-body-dominated behaviors, whereas imitation-based RL can learn more coordinated whole-body skills but is typically limited to replaying reference motions without a mechanism to adapt them online from perception for terrain-aware locomotion. To address this gap, we propose a whole-body humanoid locomotion framework that combines skills learned from reference motions with terrain-aware adaptation. We first train a diffusion model on retargeted human motions for real-time prediction of terrain-aware reference motions. Concurrently, we train a whole-body reference tracker with RL using this motion data. To improve robustness under imperfectly generated references, we further fine-tune the tracker with a frozen motion generator in a closed-loop setting. The resulting system supports directional goal-reaching control with terrain-aware whole-body adaptation, and can be deployed on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot with onboard perception and computation. The hardware experiments demonstrate successful traversal over boxes, hurdles, stairs, and mixed terrain combinations. Quantitative results further show the benefits of incorporating online motion generation and fine-tuning the motion tracker for improved generalization and robustness.

</details>


### [717] [Think before Go: Hierarchical Reasoning for Image-goal Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17407)
*Pengna Li,Kangyi Wu,Shaoqing Xu,Fang Li,Lin Zhao,Long Chen,Zhi-Xin Yang,Nanning Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HRNav is a hierarchical framework for image-goal navigation that decomposes the task into high-level planning using vision-language models and low-level execution using reinforcement learning, with a wandering suppression penalty to improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing end-to-end navigation policies fail when targets are distant or in other rooms because they can't extract informative visual cues, causing agents to wander. The paper is motivated by human cognitive principles where high-level reasoning guides reactive execution in complex tasks.

Method: HRNav decomposes image-goal navigation into two levels: 1) High-level planning where a vision-language model trained on self-collected data generates short-horizon plans (e.g., "walk through door" or "down hallway"), and 2) Low-level execution where an online RL policy decides actions conditioned on the short-horizon plan. A novel Wandering Suppression Penalty (WSP) is also introduced to reduce wandering.

Result: Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world environments demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method over existing approaches.

Conclusion: The hierarchical reasoning framework effectively addresses the limitations of end-to-end methods for image-goal navigation, particularly for long-horizon tasks where targets are distant or in different rooms, by leveraging human-inspired cognitive principles of deliberate planning guiding reactive execution.

Abstract: Image-goal navigation steers an agent to a target location specified by an image in unseen environments. Existing methods primarily handle this task by learning an end-to-end navigation policy, which compares the similarities of target and observation images and directly predicts the actions. However, when the target is distant or lies in another room, such methods fail to extract informative visual cues, leading the agent to wander around. Motivated by the human cognitive principle that deliberate, high-level reasoning guides fast, reactive execution in complex tasks, we propose Hierarchical Reasoning Navigation (HRNav), a framework that decomposes image-goal navigation into high-level planning and low-level execution. In high-level planning, a vision-language model is trained on a self-collected dataset to generate a short-horizon plan, such as whether the agent should walk through the door or down the hallway. This downgrades the difficulty of the long-horizon task, making it more amenable to the execution part. In low-level execution, an online reinforcement learning policy is utilized to decide actions conditioned on the short-horizon plan. We also devise a novel Wandering Suppression Penalty (WSP) to further reduce the wandering problem. Together, these components form a hierarchical framework for Image-Goal Navigation. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world environments demonstrate the superiority of our method.

</details>


### [718] [FLASH: Fast Learning via GPU-Accelerated Simulation for High-Fidelity Deformable Manipulation in Minutes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17513)
*Siyuan Luo,Bingyang Zhou,Chong Zhang,Xin Liu,Zhenhao Huang,Gang Yang,Zhengtao Han,Xiaotian Hu,Eric Yang,Rymon Yu,Ziqiu Zeng,Fan Shi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FLASH is a GPU-native simulation framework for contact-rich deformable manipulation that scales to 3M+ degrees of freedom at 30 FPS, enabling efficient sim-to-real transfer for deformable object tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing simulation frameworks struggle with contact-rich deformable object manipulation due to continuously changing geometry, large vertex counts, and contact constraints, making it difficult to achieve the accuracy, speed, and stability needed for large-scale interactive learning.

Method: FLASH is built from the ground up as a GPU-native framework with an accurate NCP-based solver that enforces strict contact and deformation constraints. It's explicitly designed for fine-grained GPU parallelism with optimized collision handling and memory layouts, rather than porting conventional SIMD solvers to GPUs.

Result: FLASH scales to over 3 million degrees of freedom at 30 FPS on a single RTX 5090 while accurately simulating physical interactions. Policies trained solely on FLASH-generated synthetic data achieve robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on physical robots for challenging deformable manipulation tasks like towel folding and garment folding.

Conclusion: FLASH provides a practical alternative to labor-intensive real-world data collection by enabling efficient simulation of contact-rich deformable manipulation at scale, with successful sim-to-real transfer validated on physical robots without any real-world demonstrations.

Abstract: Simulation frameworks such as Isaac Sim have enabled scalable robot learning for locomotion and rigid-body manipulation; however, contact-rich simulation remains a major bottleneck for deformable object manipulation. The continuously changing geometry of soft materials, together with large numbers of vertices and contact constraints, makes it difficult to achieve high accuracy, speed, and stability required for large-scale interactive learning. We present FLASH, a GPU-native simulation framework for contact-rich deformable manipulation, built on an accurate NCP-based solver that enforces strict contact and deformation constraints while being explicitly designed for fine-grained GPU parallelism. Rather than porting conventional single-instruction-multiple-data (SIMD) solvers to GPUs, FLASH redesigns the physics engine from the ground up to leverage modern GPU architectures, including optimized collision handling and memory layouts. As a result, FLASH scales to over 3 million degrees of freedom at 30 FPS on a single RTX 5090, while accurately simulating physical interactions. Policies trained solely on FLASH-generated synthetic data in minutes achieve robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, which we validate on physical robots performing challenging deformable manipulation tasks such as towel folding and garment folding, without any real-world demonstration, providing a practical alternative to labor-intensive real-world data collection.

</details>


### [719] [Safer Trajectory Planning with CBF-guided Diffusion Model for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17527)
*Peiwen Yang,Shiyu Bai,Weisong Wen,Yixin Gao,Jiahao Hu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AeroTrajGen: Diffusion-based trajectory generation for UAVs with CBF-guided sampling that reduces collisions by 94.7% while maintaining agility across 14 aerobatic maneuvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safety concerns in autonomous systems during complex aerobatic maneuvers by mitigating unpredictability of diffusion models and reducing reliance on extensively safety-verified training data.

Method: Uses diffusion-based trajectory generation with CBF-guided sampling during inference, featuring obstacle-aware diffusion transformer architecture with multi-modal conditioning (trajectory history, obstacles, maneuver styles, goal).

Result: CBF-guided sampling reduces collision rates by 94.7% compared to unguided diffusion baselines while preserving trajectory agility and diversity in multi-obstacle environments.

Conclusion: AeroTrajGen successfully integrates diffusion models with CBF guidance to generate safe, agile trajectories for UAV aerobatics, demonstrating significant safety improvements without compromising performance.

Abstract: Safe and agile trajectory planning is essential for autonomous systems, especially during complex aerobatic maneuvers. Motivated by the recent success of diffusion models in generative tasks, this paper introduces AeroTrajGen, a novel framework for diffusion-based trajectory generation that incorporates control barrier function (CBF)-guided sampling during inference, specifically designed for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). The proposed CBF-guided sampling addresses two critical challenges: (1) mitigating the inherent unpredictability and potential safety violations of diffusion models, and (2) reducing reliance on extensively safety-verified training data. During the reverse diffusion process, CBF-based guidance ensures collision-free trajectories by seamlessly integrating safety constraint gradients with the diffusion model's score function. The model features an obstacle-aware diffusion transformer architecture with multi-modal conditioning, including trajectory history, obstacles, maneuver styles, and goal, enabling the generation of smooth, highly agile trajectories across 14 distinct aerobatic maneuvers. Trained on a dataset of 2,000 expert demonstrations, AeroTrajGen is rigorously evaluated in simulation under multi-obstacle environments. Simulation results demonstrate that CBF-guided sampling reduces collision rates by 94.7% compared to unguided diffusion baselines, while preserving trajectory agility and diversity. Our code is open-sourced at https://github.com/RoboticsPolyu/CBF-DMP.

</details>


### [720] [Novel Algorithms for Smoothly Differentiable and Efficiently Vectorizable Contact Manifold Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17538)
*Onur Beker,Andreas René Geist,Anselm Paulus,Georg Martius*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A method for generating smoothly differentiable collision detection in robot simulation pipelines using analytical SDF primitives and novel contact manifold generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robot behavior generation in contact-rich settings relies on zeroth-order methods, but first/second-order methods could improve speed and efficiency. The bottleneck is obtaining useful gradients/Hessians due to simulation pipeline pathologies in collision detection, contact dynamics, and time integration.

Method: Two contributions: 1) A highly expressive class of analytical Signed Distance Function (SDF) primitives that can efficiently represent complex 3D surfaces, 2) A novel contact manifold generation routine that uses this geometry representation to achieve smoothly differentiable and massively vectorizable collision detection.

Result: The method addresses the collision detection part of the simulation pipeline in a manner that is smoothly differentiable and massively vectorizable, enabling the use of first/second-order optimization methods.

Conclusion: The proposed approach provides a solution to the collision detection bottleneck in simulation pipelines, paving the way for more efficient gradient-based optimization methods in contact-rich robot behavior generation.

Abstract: Generating intelligent robot behavior in contact-rich settings is a research problem where zeroth-order methods currently prevail. Developing methods that make use of first/second order information about the dynamics holds great promise in terms of increasing the solution speed and computational efficiency. The main bottleneck in this research direction is the difficulty in obtaining useful gradients and Hessians, due to pathologies in all three steps of a common simulation pipeline: i) collision detection, ii) contact dynamics, iii) time integration. This abstract proposes a method that can address the collision detection part of the puzzle in a manner that is smoothly differentiable and massively vectorizable. This is achieved via two contributions: i) a highly expressive class of analytical SDF primitives that can efficiently represent complex 3D surfaces, ii) a novel contact manifold generation routine that makes use of this geometry representation.

</details>


### [721] [A Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability-Guided Search Framework for Efficient and Safe Indoor Planar Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17679)
*Hanyang Hu,Cameron Siu,Mo Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Combines offline Hamilton-Jacobi reachability with online graph search for efficient, safe autonomous navigation in dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph search algorithms provide theoretical guarantees but become computationally expensive in high-dimensional dynamic environments, while HJ reachability requires full environment knowledge. Need to combine strengths of both for real-time planning.

Method: Precomputes HJ value functions offline to use as informative heuristics and proactive safety constraints for online graph search. This amortizes computation while enabling reachability-based reasoning in online planning.

Result: Extensive simulations and real-world experiments show the approach consistently outperforms baseline methods in planning efficiency and navigation safety, including in environments with humans.

Conclusion: The hybrid approach successfully leverages complementary strengths of HJ reachability and graph search, enabling efficient real-time planning with safety guarantees in complex dynamic environments.

Abstract: Autonomous navigation requires planning to reach a goal safely and efficiently in complex and potentially dynamic environments. Graph search-based algorithms are widely adopted due to their generality and theoretical guarantees when equipped with admissible heuristics. However, the computational complexity of graph search grows rapidly with the dimensionality of the search space, often making real-time planning in dynamic environments intractable. In this paper, we combine offline Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability with online graph search to leverage the complementary strengths of both. Precomputed HJ value functions, used as informative heuristics and proactive safety constraints, amortize online computation of the graph search process. At the same time, graph search enables reachability-based reasoning to be incorporated into online planning, overcoming the long-standing challenge of HJ reachability requiring full knowledge of the environment. Extensive simulation studies and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms baseline methods in terms of planning efficiency and navigation safety, in environments with and without human presence.

</details>


### [722] [OmniVLA-RL: A Vision-Language-Action Model with Spatial Understanding and Online RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17706)
*Haoxiang Jie,Yaoyuan Yan,Xiangyu Wei,Kailin Wang,Hongjie Yan,Zhiyou Heng,Daocheng Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OmniVLA-RL: A new Visual-Language-Action model using Mix-of-Transformers and Flow-GSPO to address spatial perception, multimodal fusion, and RL instability issues in embodied AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models struggle with imprecise spatial perception, suboptimal multimodal fusion, and instability in reinforcement learning, limiting their effectiveness in embodied AI applications.

Method: Proposes OmniVLA-RL with Mix-of-Transformers (MoT) design integrating reasoning, spatial, and action experts. Introduces Flow-GSPO that reformulates flow matching as an SDE process and integrates it with Group Segmented Policy Optimization for enhanced action precision and training robustness.

Result: Extensive evaluations on LIBERO and LIBERO-Plus benchmarks show OmniVLA-RL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, effectively overcoming fundamental limitations of current VLA models.

Conclusion: OmniVLA-RL represents an advancement in VLA models by addressing key challenges through novel architectural design and optimization techniques, demonstrating superior performance on established benchmarks.

Abstract: Visual-Language-Action (VLA) models represent a paradigm shift in embodied AI, yet existing frameworks often struggle with imprecise spatial perception, suboptimal multimodal fusion, and instability in reinforcement learning. To bridge these gaps, we propose OmniVLA-RL, a novel architecture that leverages a Mix-of-Transformers (MoT) design to synergistically integrate reasoning, spatial, and action experts. Furthermore, we introduce Flow-GSPO, which reformulates flow matching as a Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) process and integrates it with Group Segmented Policy Optimization (GSPO) to enhance action precision and training robustness. Extensive evaluations on the LIBERO and LIBERO-Plus benchmarks demonstrate that OmniVLA-RL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, effectively overcoming the fundamental limitations of current VLA models.

</details>


### [723] [AnchorRefine: Synergy-Manipulation Based on Trajectory Anchor and Residual Refinement for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17787)
*Tingzheng Jia,Kan Guo,Lanping Qian,Yongli Hu,Daxin Tian,Guixian Qu,Chunmian Lin,Baocai Yin,Jiapu Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AnchorRefine: A hierarchical VLA framework that separates manipulation into coarse trajectory planning and fine execution refinement, improving precision-critical tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA policies use a monolithic action space that forces both large transport motions and small corrective adjustments to be optimized under the same objective, causing large motions to dominate learning while suppressing critical fine adjustments. Human manipulation naturally separates global planning from continuous local adjustment during execution.

Method: AnchorRefine factorizes VLA action modeling into two components: 1) Anchor planner predicts coarse motion scaffolds (global trajectories), and 2) Refinement module corrects execution-level deviations to improve geometric and contact precision. Includes a decision-aware gripper refinement mechanism for discrete, boundary-sensitive gripper control.

Result: Experiments on LIBERO, CALVIN, and real-robot tasks show consistent improvements for both regression-based and diffusion-based VLA backbones, with gains up to 7.8% in simulation success rate and 18% in real-world success rate.

Conclusion: Hierarchical factorization of VLA action modeling into anchor planning and residual refinement better captures the structure of precision-critical manipulation, addressing the limitations of monolithic approaches and improving both simulated and real-world performance.

Abstract: Precision-critical manipulation requires both global trajectory organization and local execution correction, yet most vision-language-action (VLA) policies generate actions within a single unified space. This monolithic formulation forces macro-level transport and micro-level refinement to be optimized under the same objective, causing large motions to dominate learning while suppressing small but failure-critical corrective signals. In contrast, human manipulation is structured by global movement planning together with continuous local adjustment during execution. Motivated by this principle, we propose AnchorRefine, a hierarchical framework that factorizes VLA action modeling into trajectory anchor and residual refinement. The anchor planner predicts a coarse motion scaffold, while the refinement module corrects execution-level deviations to improve geometric and contact precision. We further introduce a decision-aware gripper refinement mechanism to better capture the discrete and boundary-sensitive nature of gripper control. Experiments on LIBERO, CALVIN, and real-robot tasks demonstrate that AnchorRefine consistently improves both regression-based and diffusion-based VLA backbones, yielding gains of up to 7.8% in simulation success rate and 18% in real-world success rate.

</details>


### [724] [ReFineVLA: Multimodal Reasoning-Aware Generalist Robotic Policies via Teacher-Guided Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17800)
*Tuan Van Vo,Tan Q. Nguyen,Khang Nguyen,Nhat Xuan Tran,Duy H. M. Nguyen,An T. Le,Ngo Anh Vien,Minh Nhat Vu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReFineVLA enhances Vision-Language-Action models by incorporating explicit reasoning through teacher-guided rationales, improving interpretability and generalization for complex robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models lack explicit reasoning capabilities, learning only functional input-action mappings without logical steps, which limits interpretability and generalization for complex, long-horizon manipulation tasks.

Method: Augment robotic datasets with reasoning rationales generated by an expert teacher model, then fine-tune pre-trained VLAs with the reasoning-enriched datasets using the ReFineVLA framework while maintaining generalization abilities.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on simulated manipulation benchmarks (SimplerEnv with WidowX and Google Robot tasks), with highest success rates over second-best methods on both benchmarks.

Conclusion: ReFineVLA enhances multimodal understanding and generalization by incorporating explicit reasoning, leading to improved performance and better alignment between vision-language observations and robotic actions.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have gained much attention from the research community thanks to their strength in translating multimodal observations with linguistic instructions into desired robotic actions. Despite their advancements, VLAs often overlook explicit reasoning and learn the functional input-action mappings, omitting crucial logical steps, which are especially pronounced in interpretability and generalization for complex, long-horizon manipulation tasks. In this work, we propose ReFineVLA, a multimodal reasoning-aware framework that fine-tunes VLAs with teacher-guided reasons. We first augment robotic datasets with reasoning rationales generated by an expert teacher model, guiding VLA models to learn to reason about their actions. Then, we fine-tune pre-trained VLAs with the reasoning-enriched datasets with ReFineVLA, while maintaining the underlying generalization abilities and boosting reasoning capabilities. We also conduct attention map visualization to analyze the alignment among visual observation, linguistic prompts, and to-be-executed actions of ReFineVLA, reflecting the model is ability to focus on relevant tasks and actions. Through this additional step, we explore that ReFineVLA-trained models exhibit a meaningful agreement between vision-language and action domains, highlighting the enhanced multimodal understanding and generalization. Evaluated across a suite of simulated manipulation benchmarks on SimplerEnv with both WidowX and Google Robot tasks, ReFineVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance, in success rate over the second-best method on the both the WidowX benchmark and Google Robot Tasks.

</details>


### [725] [SYMBOLIZER: Symbolic Model-free Task Planning with VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17830)
*Sami Azirar,Zlatan Ajanovic,Hermann Blum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A framework combining Visual Language Models with domain-independent heuristic search for Task and Motion Planning, eliminating need for handcrafted symbolic models while achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional TAMP systems require meticulously handcrafted symbolic models which are resource-intensive and domain-specific, limiting scalability. VLMs offer zero-shot visual understanding but limited planning capabilities. Integrating VLMs with classical planning could enable long-horizon reasoning without domain-specific engineering.

Method: Uses VLMs to ground lifted predicates from images to obtain symbolic states, then performs planning with domain-independent heuristic search (goal-count and width-based heuristics) without requiring action models. Symbolic search operates over VLM-grounded state-space.

Result: Outperforms direct VLM-based planning and performs on par with approaches using VLM-derived heuristics. Achieves state-of-the-art results on ProDG and ViPlan benchmarks, showing domain-independent search can effectively solve problems across domains with large combinatorial state spaces.

Conclusion: The framework successfully integrates VLMs' visual understanding with classical planning's reasoning capabilities, eliminating the need for handcrafted symbolic models while maintaining strong performance across domains, demonstrating the viability of domain-independent search for TAMP problems.

Abstract: Traditional Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) systems depend on physics models for motion planning and discrete symbolic models for task planning.
